## Agent 4 report — dual left-null O3 attack

I followed the Agent 4 instruction: audit the canonical multiplier, test whether multiplier variation plus \(L(1)=0\) proves O3, examine plus/dual structure, and formulate the family-valued dual condition. The coordinator’s current target is whether a dimension-sufficient upstream theorem can still certify the same canonical source \(q''_{\can}\). 

## 1. Dual O3 setup

Let

\[
F=Z_\alpha,\qquad G=q''_{\can},
\qquad \mathcal D=\lambda\partial_m.
\]

At an admissible zero

\[
F(m_0)=0,
\]

write the zero-conditioned complex moment functional as

\[
L(H)=\sum_n c_n(m_0,\alpha)H(x_n)+\text{dual/bg/tail terms},
\qquad x_n=\lambda\log n.
\]

The canonical curvature source has schematic multiplier form

\[
G \sim mF,
\]

so the \(G\)-Krylov block corresponds to the subspace

\[
m\mathbb C_R[x].
\]

Assume O2 gives a well-conditioned block \(K\). Then there is a left-null polynomial

\[
U\in \mathbb C_{R+1}[x]
\]

satisfying

\[
L(U\,mP)=0
\qquad
\forall P\in\mathbb C_R[x].
\]

Equivalently,

\[
U\perp m\mathbb C_R[x].
\]

O3 is exactly

\[
\boxed{
|L(U)|\ge Q^{-A}\|U\|.
}
\]

So O3 is the nonorthogonality of the left-null polynomial \(U\) against the constant direction \(1\).

---

## 2. Canonical multiplier audit

The first necessary analytic check is whether the canonical multiplier \(m\) is scalar, or close to scalar, on the ordinary packet.

On the ordinary support,

\[
x=\lambda\log n\in[c_-,c_+],
\]

the prime-side part of \(q''_{\can}\) is not a scalar multiple of \(Z_\alpha\). Differentiating the AFE terms introduces nonconstant powers of \(\log n\), and after normalized scaling those become nonconstant functions of \(x\). The dual side has its own differentiated \(\chi\)- and folded-weight symbols. The background \(B_2\) and regularized completion \(E_2\) are not prime-log scalar multiples of \(Z_\alpha\) either.

So the canonical multiplier should pass the weakest analytic test:

\[
\operatorname{osc}_{[c_-,c_+]} m \ge Q^{-A_0}
\]

for some fixed \(A_0\), unless the canonical symbol has an unexpected cancellation.

But this is not the right sufficient condition. The actual analytic condition needed for O3 is stronger:

\[
\delta_R(m)
:=
\inf_{\deg A\le R}
\|1-A(x)m(x)\|_{\mathcal X}
\ge Q^{-A_1},
\]

for a norm \(\mathcal X\) that controls the moment map. If

\[
1/m
\]

were exactly a degree-\(R\) polynomial, then there would be some \(A\) with

\[
1-Am=0,
\]

and the Hermite–Padé/O3 residual would collapse.

For the canonical multiplier, exact reciprocal-polynomial collapse is highly unlikely. On a fixed compact interval away from multiplier zeros, ordinary analytic approximation gives errors like

\[
e^{-cR}
=
Q^{-c/\log\log Q},
\]

which is still much larger than \(Q^{-A}\) for any fixed polynomial exponent \(A\). Thus analytic multiplier separation is plausibly green.

But this only rules out a trivial scalar/low-degree collapse. It does not prove O3.

---

## 3. Why multiplier variation plus \(L(1)=0\) is insufficient

The scalar zero equation gives

\[
F(m_0)=0,
\]

which in moment language is

\[
L(1)=0.
\]

O3 needs

\[
L(U)\ne0
\]

where \(U\) is the polynomial satisfying

\[
L(UmP)=0
\qquad
\forall P\in\mathbb C_R[x].
\]

There is no implication

\[
L(1)=0
\quad\text{and}\quad
m\text{ nonconstant}
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
L(U)\ne0.
\]

Reason: \(L\) is complex and oscillatory. In a positive-measure setting, one might try a Chebyshev or orthogonal-polynomial argument. But here \(L\) has signs/phases:

\[
c_n(m_0,\alpha)\sim n^{-1/2}W(n,m_0,\alpha)e^{-im_0\log n},
\]

plus folded-dual and completion terms.

For separated nodes \(x_i\), the equations

\[
\sum_i c_i U(x_i)m(x_i)x_i^j=0,
\qquad 0\le j\le R,
\]

and

\[
\sum_i c_iU(x_i)=0
\]

are only \(R+2\) homogeneous complex linear constraints on the weights \(c_i\). With \(N\ge R+3\) nodes, phase-relaxed nonzero solutions exist even when \(m\) is genuinely nonconstant.

So:

\[
\boxed{
\text{non-scalar }m + L(1)=0
\text{ does not give O3.}
}
\]

It gives no lower bound for \(L(U)\).

---

## 4. Plus/dual sign and parity audit

The two-branch AFE structure can be modelled as support on two components:

\[
x>0
\quad\text{and}\quad
x<0.
\]

The ordinary zero equation is a folded cancellation:

\[
L_+(1)+e^{i\Theta_Z}L_-(1)+L_{\rm bg/tail}(1)=0.
\]

The canonical source has different multipliers on the two components:

\[
m_+(x),\qquad m_-(-x).
\]

This branch mismatch helps O1b at the cellwise two-jet level, because plus and folded-dual symbols differ. But for O3, the left-null polynomial condition is already allowed to use the full two-branch multiplier:

\[
U\perp m_+\mathbb C_R[x] \oplus m_-\mathbb C_R[-x].
\]

The signs do not force

\[
L(U)\ne0.
\]

Parity also does not rescue O3. If the model were exactly symmetric, then one could split \(U\) into even/odd parts. But complex weights and the folded phase \(e^{i\Theta_Z}\) destroy positivity. Even in a parity-symmetric phase-relaxed model, one can choose \(U\) and weights so that the \(m\mathbb C_R\)-moments and the constant moment both vanish.

Thus:

\[
\boxed{
\text{plus/dual branch structure gives symbol richness, but not O3 nonorthogonality.}
}
\]

It may improve analytic multiplier separation; it does not prove the arithmetic frame lower bound.

---

## 5. Family-valued version

If the scalar seed \(1\) is replaced by a seed space

\[
\Phi=\operatorname{span}\{\phi_\nu:\nu\in\mathcal I\},
\]

then scalar O3 becomes:

\[
\boxed{
\left\|
\bigl(L(U\phi_\nu)\bigr)_{\nu\in\mathcal I}
\right\|_2
\ge
Q^{-A}\|U\|.
}
\]

This is the correct dual condition.

Dimensionally, to beat random pointwise failures over roughly

\[
e^Q Q
\]

zeros, one needs

\[
|\mathcal I|\gtrsim \frac{Q}{\log Q}.
\]

So a family-valued \(\Phi\) with dimension \(\asymp Q/\log Q\) is statistically plausible, unlike the current scalar seed.

But \(\Phi\) must satisfy two structural constraints.

First, it must frame the refined cell scale. It cannot be another \(O(R)\)-dimensional projection. It must see enough independent packet directions to avoid the Agent 1 nullspace obstruction.

Second, it must remain compatible with the canonical source \(q''_{\can}\). If the \(\phi_\nu\) are arbitrary probes, they may certify only an enlarged family energy, not the scalar canonical curvature source that downstream D4 consumes.

The family-valued theorem needed is:

\[
\boxed{
\Phi + m\mathbb C_R[x]
\text{ has a zero-conditioned complex moment frame lower bound.}
}
\]

Equivalently, for every nonzero left-null polynomial \(U\) of \(m\mathbb C_R[x]\),

\[
\boxed{
\|(L(U\phi_\nu))_\nu\|_2
\ge Q^{-A}\|U\|.
}
\]

This fixes the dimension count if \(\Phi\) is large enough, but it is still a new actual prime-log arithmetic theorem.

---

## 6. Final dual theorem needed

The scalar-current architecture requires the following theorem.

\[
\boxed{
\textbf{Zero-conditioned left-null-polynomial nonorthogonality.}
}
\]

At every admissible zero \(Z_\alpha(m_0)=0\), let \(L=L_{m_0,\alpha}\) be the actual zero-conditioned prime-log functional and let \(m\) be the full canonical curvature multiplier for

\[
q''_{\can}=B_2+P_2+D_2+E_2.
\]

Assume \(U\in\mathbb C_{R+1}[x]\) satisfies

\[
L(UmP)=0
\qquad
\forall P\in\mathbb C_R[x].
\]

Then

\[
|L(U)|\ge Q^{-A}\|U\|.
\]

For a family-valued repair, replace the final line by

\[
\|(L(U\phi_\nu))_{\nu\in\mathcal I}\|_2
\ge Q^{-A}\|U\|.
\]

---

## 7. Classification

No proof of O3 emerges from multiplier variation, the zero equation, plus/dual signs, or parity.

The analytic side is probably manageable:

\[
m\text{ is non-scalar and not degree-}R\text{ reciprocally collapsible}.
\]

The arithmetic side remains open:

\[
\boxed{
L(U)\text{ must not vanish for the actual zero-conditioned prime-log functional.}
}
\]

So Agent 4 classification is:

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{O3}\text{ remains Red as a scalar finite-row input.}
}
\]

The best replacement is the family-valued dual frame theorem

\[
\boxed{
\|(L(U\phi_\nu))_\nu\|_2\ge Q^{-A}\|U\|,
\qquad
|\mathcal I|\gtrsim Q/\log Q,
}
\]

but that only helps the RH architecture if the seed family certifies the same canonical source \(q''_{\can}\) rather than producing unrelated high-dimensional observables.

## Agent 4 report — dual left-null polynomial attack

I followed the Agent 4 instruction: prove the dual equivalence, audit the canonical multiplier, test whether multiplier variation plus \(L(1)=0\) helps, analyze plus/dual structure, and formulate the family-valued repair. 

## Verdict

\[
\boxed{\mathrm{O3}\text{ remains Red.}}
\]

The exact missing theorem is now sharply isolated:

\[
\boxed{
\text{zero-conditioned left-null-polynomial nonorthogonality.}
}
\]

More concretely, after O2 gives a well-conditioned canonical \(q''_{\can}\)-Krylov block, O3 is equivalent to:

\[
U\perp m\mathbb C_R[x]
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
|L(U)|\ge Q^{-A}\|U\|,
\]

where \(L=L_{m_0,\alpha}\) is the actual prime-log moment functional at a zero \(Z_\alpha(m_0)=0\), \(m\) is the canonical curvature multiplier, and \(U\) is the left-null polynomial of the \(q''_{\can}\)-moment block.

The analytic multiplier part is probably manageable. The arithmetic nonorthogonality part is not supplied by current tools.

---

# 1. Dual equivalence: O3 as left-null nonorthogonality

Let

\[
F=Z_\alpha,\qquad G=q''_{\can},\qquad \mathcal D=\lambda\partial_m.
\]

At an admissible zero

\[
F(m_0)=0,
\]

write

\[
f_k=(\mathcal D^kF)(m_0),
\qquad
g_k=(\mathcal D^kG)(m_0).
\]

In monomial jet coordinates, define

\[
b=(f_0,\ldots,f_{R+1})^T
\]

and

\[
K=(g_{j+k})_{\substack{0\le k\le R+1\\0\le j\le R}}
\in\mathbb C^{(R+2)\times(R+1)}.
\]

Then O3 is the distance estimate

\[
\operatorname{dist}(b,\operatorname{col}K)
\ge
Q^{-A}\|b\|.
\]

Assume O2 gives

\[
\sigma_{\min}(K)\ge Q^{-A_2},
\]

so \(K\) has rank \(R+1\). The left nullspace of \(K\) is one-dimensional. Let

\[
u=(u_0,\ldots,u_{R+1})\neq0
\]

span it:

\[
u^*K=0.
\]

Define the polynomial

\[
U(x)=\sum_{k=0}^{R+1}\overline{u_k}x^k.
\]

Let \(L\) denote the zero-conditioned prime-log moment functional, so schematically

\[
f_k=L(x^k),
\qquad
g_k=L(m(x)x^k).
\]

Then

\[
u^*K=0
\]

is equivalent to

\[
L(U(x)m(x)x^j)=0,
\qquad
0\le j\le R.
\]

Thus

\[
\boxed{U\perp m\mathbb C_R[x].}
\]

Also,

\[
u^*b
=
\sum_{k=0}^{R+1}\overline{u_k}f_k
=
L(U).
\]

Since \(u\) spans the orthogonal complement of \(\operatorname{col}K\),

\[
\operatorname{dist}(b,\operatorname{col}K)
=
\frac{|u^*b|}{\|u\|}
=
\frac{|L(U)|}{\|u\|}.
\]

Therefore O3 is exactly:

\[
\boxed{
U\perp m\mathbb C_R[x]
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
|L(U)|\ge Q^{-A}\|U\|.
}
\]

This is not a heuristic reformulation. It is the exact Schur-complement form of O3 after O2 supplies rank.

---

# 2. Canonical multiplier audit

The first analytic danger is scalar collapse.

If

\[
m(x)\equiv c,
\]

then

\[
G=cF,
\]

hence

\[
g_k=cf_k.
\]

The first column of \(K\) is then

\[
(g_0,\ldots,g_{R+1})^T
=
cb.
\]

So

\[
b\in\operatorname{col}K,
\]

and therefore

\[
\operatorname{dist}(b,\operatorname{col}K)=0.
\]

Thus:

\[
\boxed{
m\text{ scalar } \Longrightarrow \mathrm{O3}\text{ fails identically.}
}
\]

For the actual canonical source

\[
q''_{\can}=B_2+P_2+D_2+E_2,
\]

the prime-side and folded-dual symbols are not scalar on the ordinary packet. Differentiating AFE terms introduces powers of \(\log n\), and after normalized scaling those become nonconstant functions of

\[
x=\lambda\log n\in[c_-,c_+].
\]

At the schematic level, the prime-side multiplier contains nonconstant pieces such as

\[
x,\quad x^2,\quad \partial_t W/W,\quad \chi'\text{- and }\chi''\text{-weighted folded terms},
\]

while \(B_2\) and \(E_2\) are not scalar prime-log multipliers of \(Z_\alpha\).

So the canonical multiplier should satisfy a weak non-scalar condition of the form

\[
\operatorname{osc}_{[c_-,c_+]}m
\ge Q^{-A_0}.
\]

A stronger analytic condition is the reciprocal approximation lower bound:

\[
\delta_R(m)
:=
\inf_{\deg A\le R}
\|1-A(x)m(x)\|_{\mathcal X}
\ge Q^{-A_1}.
\]

On a fixed ordinary packet away from multiplier zeros, if \(1/m\) has only fixed-distance singularities, then best polynomial approximation is exponential in \(R\):

\[
\delta_R(m)\asymp \rho^{-R}.
\]

Since

\[
R\asymp\frac{\log Q}{\log\log Q},
\]

this gives

\[
\rho^{-R}
=
Q^{-O(1/\log\log Q)},
\]

which is much larger than \(Q^{-A}\) for any fixed large \(A\). So the analytic multiplier separation is plausibly green after an explicit symbol audit.

But this does not prove O3.

---

# 3. Non-scalar \(m\) plus \(L(1)=0\) gives no O3 lower bound

At a zero,

\[
F(m_0)=0.
\]

In moment language,

\[
L(1)=0.
\]

O3 asks for

\[
|L(U)|\ge Q^{-A}\|U\|
\]

where

\[
U\perp m\mathbb C_R[x].
\]

There is no implication

\[
L(1)=0
\quad+\quad
m\text{ non-scalar}
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
L(U)\ne0.
\]

The obstruction is that \(L\) is complex and oscillatory, not positive.

A concrete phase-relaxed countermodel:

Choose separated nodes

\[
x_1,\ldots,x_N\in[c_-,c_+]
\]

and complex weights \(a_i\). Fix a non-scalar multiplier \(m\) and a degree-\((R+1)\) polynomial \(U\) nonzero at the nodes. Impose the constraints

\[
\sum_i a_i U(x_i)m(x_i)x_i^j=0,
\qquad
0\le j\le R,
\]

\[
\sum_i a_iU(x_i)=0,
\]

and

\[
\sum_i a_i=0.
\]

These encode:

\[
U\perp m\mathbb C_R[x],
\qquad
L(U)=0,
\qquad
L(1)=0.
\]

There are \(R+3\) homogeneous linear constraints. With

\[
N\ge R+4,
\]

there are nonzero solutions.

For generic nodes and generic solution weights inside this nullspace, the \(K\)-block still has rank \(R+1\), so O2 can hold while O3 fails.

This does not disprove zeta-O3, because zeta weights are not arbitrary. But it proves that the following are insufficient:

\[
m\text{ non-scalar},
\qquad
L(1)=0,
\qquad
\text{broad separated support},
\qquad
\text{finite-dimensional moment rank}.
\]

One needs actual prime-log arithmetic.

---

# 4. Plus/dual branch analysis

The real AFE structure has two components:

\[
x>0,\qquad x<0.
\]

Schematic form:

\[
F
=
F_+
+
e^{i\Theta_Z}F_-
+
F_{\rm bg/tail},
\]

and

\[
G
=
m_+F_+
+
e^{i\Theta_G}m_-F_-
+
G_{\rm bg/tail}.
\]

In the dual language,

\[
U\perp
\left(
m_+\mathbb C_R[x]
\oplus
m_-\mathbb C_R[-x]
\right)
\]

with respect to the folded complex moment functional.

The plus/dual split helps with O1b because the two branch symbols differ at the cellwise two-jet level. But for O3 it does not force

\[
L(U)\neq0.
\]

Why not?

Because the branch phases are complex and the moment functional is indefinite. The two-branch conditions are still homogeneous complex linear constraints on the weights. With enough nodes on the two components, one can satisfy:

\[
L(Um_+x^j)+L(Um_-(-x)^j)=0,
\qquad
0\le j\le R,
\]

while also arranging

\[
L(U)=0
\]

and

\[
L(1)=0.
\]

Parity also does not rescue the argument. Even if \(m_+\) and \(m_-\) had exact even/odd structure, complex folded weights destroy positivity. Odd/even cancellation can be enforced rather than prevented in a phase-relaxed model.

So:

\[
\boxed{
\text{plus/dual branch structure improves symbol richness, but not O3 nonorthogonality.}
}
\]

---

# 5. Family-valued dual repair

Replace the scalar seed \(1\) by a seed space

\[
\Phi=\operatorname{span}\{\phi_\nu:\nu\in\mathcal I\}.
\]

Then scalar O3 becomes the family condition:

\[
\boxed{
\left\|
\bigl(L(U\phi_\nu)\bigr)_{\nu\in\mathcal I}
\right\|_2
\ge
Q^{-A}\|U\|.
}
\]

Dimensionally, to beat pointwise random failures across roughly

\[
e^Q Q
\]

zeros, one wants

\[
|\mathcal I|
\gtrsim
\frac{Q}{\log Q}.
\]

So a family-valued \(\Phi\) of this size is statistically plausible, whereas the scalar seed is not.

But \(\Phi\) must satisfy two strict conditions.

First, it must be a real frame for the refined cell scale. If \(\Phi\) has only \(O(R)\) effective directions, it reproduces the same nullspace obstruction.

Second, it must certify the canonical source \(q''_{\can}\), not arbitrary unrelated observables. Downstream D4 consumes the scalar canonical source

\[
u=q''_{\can}.
\]

A high-dimensional \(\Phi\)-energy theorem does not imply

\[
\|J_R(q''_{\can})(m_0)\|\ge Q^{-A}
\]

unless \(\Phi\) is linked to the same canonical source map.

In complex-frame language, the family-valued replacement theorem is:

\[
\boxed{
\Phi+m\mathbb C_R[x]
\text{ has a zero-conditioned complex moment frame lower bound.}
}
\]

Equivalently, for the left-null polynomial \(U\),

\[
\boxed{
\|(L(U\phi_\nu))_\nu\|_2
\ge
Q^{-A}\|U\|.
}
\]

This fixes the dimension count only if

\[
\dim\Phi\asymp Q/\log Q.
\]

It does not automatically preserve compatibility with the repaired scalar downstream engine.

---

# 6. Final theorem needed

The scalar finite-row architecture requires:

\[
\boxed{
\textbf{Zero-conditioned left-null-polynomial nonorthogonality.}
}
\]

At every admissible zero \(Z_\alpha(m_0)=0\), let \(L=L_{m_0,\alpha}\) be the actual zero-conditioned prime-log functional for the full canonical source

\[
q''_{\can}=B_2+P_2+D_2+E_2.
\]

Let \(m\) be the canonical curvature multiplier. If

\[
U\in\mathbb C_{R+1}[x],
\qquad
L(UmP)=0
\quad\forall P\in\mathbb C_R[x],
\]

then

\[
\boxed{
|L(U)|\ge Q^{-A}\|U\|.
}
\]

The family-valued version is:

\[
\boxed{
\|(L(U\phi_\nu))_{\nu\in\mathcal I}\|_2
\ge Q^{-A}\|U\|,
\qquad
|\mathcal I|\gtrsim Q/\log Q.
}
\]

---

# 7. Agent 4 classification

The analytic side:

\[
m\text{ is non-scalar and probably polynomially separated from reciprocal degree-}R\text{ collapse.}
\]

The arithmetic side:

\[
L(U)\text{ must not vanish for the actual zero-conditioned prime-log functional.}
\]

No known piece of the current architecture proves the arithmetic side.

Therefore:

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{O3}\text{ remains Red as a scalar finite-row input.}
}
\]

And the best high-dimensional repair is not “more local algebra”; it is the family-valued complex-frame theorem

\[
\boxed{
\Phi+m\mathbb C_R[x]
\text{ has a zero-conditioned complex moment frame lower bound}
}
\]

with enough seed dimension and explicit compatibility with the canonical source \(q''_{\can}\).

## Agent 4 report — plausibility of the augmented O2/O3 complex-frame theorem

I followed the Agent 4 fork-decision prompt: assess whether the augmented O2/O3 theorem is a plausible counter-random input for Path A, or whether heuristics predict failures. The relevant theorem is the zero-conditioned augmented complex-frame lower bound for the actual prime-log functional \(L\) and canonical multiplier \(m\). 

## Verdict

\[
\boxed{
\text{Path A remains possible only as a new counter-random arithmetic axiom.}
}
\]

More sharply:

\[
\boxed{
\text{random matrix / random Euler-product heuristics predict many failures of the pointwise augmented frame.}
}
\]

So I would classify the Path A augmented complex-frame theorem as:

\[
\boxed{
\text{possible in the logical sense, but too strong to treat as a natural zeta estimate.}
}
\]

It is closer to a new RH-scale rigidity theorem than to a standard large-sieve, universality, or random-model consequence.

---

# 1. The theorem being tested

The Path A theorem is:

\[
\inf_{(c,P)\ne0}
\left\|
\left(
L((c+mP)x^k)
\right)_{0\le k\le R+1}
\right\|_2
\ge
Q^{-A}(|c|+\|P\|).
\]

Equivalently, define the square matrix

\[
T=
\left[
b\;\;K
\right],
\]

where

\[
b_k=L(x^k),
\qquad
K_{k,j}=L(mx^{j+k}),
\qquad
0\le k\le R+1,\quad 0\le j\le R.
\]

Then the theorem asks for

\[
\sigma_{\min}(T)\ge Q^{-A}.
\]

The \(c=0\) block is O2. The Schur complement of that block is O3.

So the question is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Can the actual zero-conditioned zeta functional force }
\sigma_{\min}(T)\ge Q^{-A}
\text{ at every admissible zero?}
}
\]

---

# 2. Random matrix heuristic: pointwise theorem is statistically mis-sized

Let

\[
n=R+2\asymp \frac{\log Q}{\log\log Q}.
\]

After normalization, the augmented matrix \(T\) is an \(n\times n\) complex matrix of low-order moments sampled at a zero.

In a basic complex Gaussian model, the small singular value tail behaves polynomially:

\[
\mathbb P(\sigma_{\min}(T)\le \varepsilon)
\asymp \varepsilon^2
\]

up to \(n\)-dependent factors. The exact exponent is not the important point; the important point is that it is **polynomial in \(\varepsilon\)**, not exponentially small in \(Q\).

Taking

\[
\varepsilon=Q^{-A},
\]

one expects

\[
\mathbb P(\sigma_{\min}(T)\le Q^{-A})
\approx Q^{-C A}.
\]

But the number of zeros up to height \(T\sim e^Q\) is

\[
N(T)\asymp e^Q Q.
\]

Hence the expected number of bad zeros is heuristically

\[
e^Q Q\cdot Q^{-CA},
\]

which still tends to infinity rapidly.

Therefore, a universal pointwise theorem

\[
\sigma_{\min}(T(m_0))\ge Q^{-A}
\quad\text{for every admissible zero }m_0
\]

is not what the random matrix model predicts. It predicts many polynomially bad singular values unless the matrix dimension is raised to roughly

\[
d\gtrsim \frac{Q}{\log Q},
\]

matching the earlier dimension count.

So under random matrix heuristics:

\[
\boxed{
\text{the Path A augmented frame theorem is counter-random.}
}
\]

---

# 3. Random Euler-product / Dirichlet polynomial heuristic

A more zeta-like model writes the zero-conditioned functional as

\[
L(H)
=
\sum_n a_n(m_0,\alpha)H(\lambda\log n)
+
\text{dual/bg/tail},
\]

with

\[
a_n(m_0,\alpha)
\sim n^{-1/2}W(n,m_0,\alpha)e^{-im_0\log n}.
\]

Conditioning on

\[
Z_\alpha(m_0)=0
\]

imposes essentially one complex linear constraint:

\[
L(1)=0.
\]

The augmented frame failure asks for the existence of \((c,P)\ne0\) such that

\[
L((c+mP)x^k)\approx0,
\qquad
0\le k\le R+1.
\]

This is an additional \(R+2\)-component small-value event. Under a Gaussian regression model, conditioning on \(L(1)=0\) changes the covariance of the other moment coordinates by a rank-one update. It does **not** turn an \(O(R)\)-dimensional random moment map into a dimension-\(Q/\log Q\) frame.

More explicitly, if the vector

\[
\mathcal V
=
\left(
L(x^k),\,
L(mx^{j+k})
\right)
\]

is modeled as a joint complex Gaussian vector, then conditioning on \(L(1)=0\) produces another Gaussian vector with conditional covariance

\[
\Sigma_{\rm cond}
=
\Sigma
-
\Sigma_{\cdot,1}\Sigma_{1,1}^{-1}\Sigma_{1,\cdot}.
\]

That operation can remove one direction. It does not supply an exponentially strong lower bound for \(\sigma_{\min}(T)\).

Thus the zero condition does not statistically rescue Path A.

---

# 4. Arithmetic discreteness does not obviously rescue it

The phase-relaxed model allows arbitrary complex cell weights. The actual weights are not arbitrary; they are structured:

\[
n^{-1/2}e^{-im_0\log n}
\]

with multiplicative relations among \(\log n\).

However, this structure seems to hurt Path A more than help it.

The phases

\[
m_0\log n
\]

over a fixed-width packet behave like a high-dimensional quasi-random torus orbit. The zero condition cuts one complex hyperplane. There is no evident arithmetic reason that the remaining orbit should avoid the low-dimensional near-kernel of the augmented moment matrix at polynomial scale for every zero.

The discreteness of \(n\) gives many terms, not fewer. On a packet of width comparable to \(Q\), the number of contributing integers is exponentially large in \(Q\). After cell aggregation, the refined dimension is still about

\[
\frac{Q}{\log Q}.
\]

So the arithmetic support has enough freedom to realize near-null designs unless a strong anti-concentration theorem is proved.

The theorem needed would have to say not merely that prime-log phases are random-like, but that at zeros they are **less capable of producing low-dimensional cancellations than random phases are**. That is the opposite of the usual heuristic direction.

So:

\[
\boxed{
\text{arithmetic discreteness does not make the theorem naturally plausible.}
}
\]

---

# 5. Functional equation / plus-dual coupling

The functional equation couples the plus and folded-dual branches:

\[
F=F_+ + e^{i\Theta_Z}F_-+\text{completion}.
\]

This reduces some freedom and creates deterministic anti-lock effects at the cellwise two-jet level, which helps O1b.

But for the augmented complex frame, the plus-dual system still has many degrees of freedom. The failure equations become branch-coupled moment equations:

\[
L_+\bigl((c+m_+P)x^k\bigr)
+
e^{i\Theta}
L_-\bigl((c+m_-P)(-x)^k\bigr)
+
\text{completion}
\approx0.
\]

This is still a complex linear system with only \(O(R)\) observed equations. The folded relation does not raise the observed dimension to \(Q/\log Q\). It also does not create positivity.

The functional equation therefore gives structure, but not the kind of coercivity needed for the pointwise augmented frame.

---

# 6. Critical-line reality

On the critical line, the completed function can be made real-valued after phase normalization. This may reduce complex degrees of freedom by a factor of roughly two.

But the dimension gap is not a factor of two. It is

\[
\frac{Q/\log Q}{\log Q/\log\log Q}
=
\frac{Q\log\log Q}{(\log Q)^2},
\]

which tends to infinity.

So real symmetry does not repair the dimension count. At most it changes complex random tails into real random tails. The small singular value probability remains polynomial in \(Q^{-A}\), not exponentially small in \(Q\).

---

# 7. Is the theorem weaker or stronger than RH?

The augmented complex-frame theorem is not literally stronger than RH in a logical sense, because it is a different finite-dimensional statement at zeros and is designed to feed the proof architecture.

But as an arithmetic input, it is **RH-scale or stronger in difficulty**.

Why?

RH is a global zero-location statement. The augmented frame theorem is a pointwise anti-cancellation statement at every admissible zero, involving structured Dirichlet polynomial moments conditioned on vanishing. It asserts polynomial anti-concentration of a low-dimensional projection across exponentially many points.

That is much stronger than standard zero-density, mean-square, or large-sieve information. Those are aggregate or averaged estimates. This theorem is uniform pointwise and counter-random.

So I would classify it as:

\[
\boxed{
\text{not an innocuous named input; it is a central conjectural arithmetic theorem.}
}
\]

---

# 8. Fork-relevant judgment

The Path A augmented complex-frame theorem is mathematically clean:

\[
\mathbb C\cdot1+m\mathbb C_R[x]
\]

must have a zero-conditioned complex moment frame lower bound.

But its plausibility is poor as a universal pointwise theorem. The random matrix and random Euler-product models predict many bad zeros at polynomial thresholds because the observed dimension is only

\[
O(R)
=
O\!\left(\frac{\log Q}{\log\log Q}\right).
\]

Therefore:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Path A should not proceed by treating the augmented complex-frame theorem as likely provable by known zeta technology.}
}
\]

The real options are:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Path A as a conditional theorem with a new counter-random axiom;}
}
\]

or

\[
\boxed{
\text{move to aggregate/high-dimensional architecture.}
}
\]

My recommendation from Agent 4’s side:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Path A-complex-frame is logically minimal but heuristically counter-random.}
}
\]

If the project continues on Path A, the theorem should be named explicitly as the major upstream conjecture, not treated as a local lemma awaiting refinement.

## Agent 4 report — aggregate O2/O3 in Path C

I followed the Agent 4 instruction: decide whether O2/O3 should remain pointwise on the good subset, or be converted to aggregate inputs with extraction, in the new Path C architecture. The prompt explicitly shifts the main route to aggregate canonical Gate–1 plus good-subset downstream extraction, while retaining the unified augmented O2/O3 complex-frame obstruction. 

## Verdict

\[
\boxed{
\text{Path C should not keep O2/O3 as pointwise hypotheses.}
}
\]

Pointwise O2/O3 is the same kind of low-dimensional counter-random assertion that made pointwise Gate–1 implausible. If Path C relaxes Gate–1 to aggregate form but keeps O2/O3 pointwise at every good center, it only moves the bottleneck.

The better stack is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{aggregate Gate--1}
+
\text{aggregate augmented O2/O3}
+
\text{joint good-subset extraction}
+
\text{pointwise downstream on the extracted subset}.
}
\]

However, separate aggregate Gate–1 and separate aggregate O2/O3 are not enough. One needs either a **joint aggregate theorem** or a weighted aggregate O2/O3 theorem over the Gate–1-visible mass. Otherwise the two good subsets can be disjoint.

---

# 1. Local O2/O3 energy

At each routed ordinary center \(m\), define the augmented moment map

\[
\mathcal A_m(c,P)
=
\left(
L_m((c+m_{\can}P)x^k)
\right)_{0\le k\le R+1},
\]

with normalization

\[
|c|^2+\|P\|^2=1.
\]

Define the local augmented-frame energy

\[
E_{23}(m)
:=
\sigma_{\min}(\mathcal A_m)^2
=
\inf_{|c|^2+\|P\|^2=1}
\|\mathcal A_m(c,P)\|_2^2.
\]

Then local O2/O3 is

\[
E_{23}(m)\ge Q^{-A}.
\]

This single local condition includes:

\[
c=0 \quad\Rightarrow\quad \text{O2},
\]

and the Schur complement / constant-direction condition

\[
c\ne0 \quad\Rightarrow\quad \text{O3}.
\]

The local downstream chain only needs this condition at centers where we actually run the local argument.

---

# 2. Pointwise O2/O3 is too strong for Path C

If we keep pointwise O2/O3 on the extracted Gate–1 good subset, the requirement is:

\[
m\in \mathcal G_1
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
E_{23}(m)\ge Q^{-A}.
\]

This is still a universal pointwise low-dimensional complex-frame lower bound.

Random matrix heuristics predict polynomially many small singular values among exponentially many routed centers. Thus, unless the Gate–1 good subset has some special correlation with O2/O3 good behavior, pointwise O2/O3 remains counter-random.

So the correct relaxation is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{make O2/O3 aggregate too.}
}
\]

---

# 3. Simple aggregate O2/O3 theorem

The most direct aggregate version is:

\[
\sum_{m\in\mathcal C_{\ord}}
\rho(m)E_{23}(m)
\ge
Q^{-A_{23}}
\sum_{m\in\mathcal C_{\ord}}\rho(m).
\]

Assume also a polynomial upper bound

\[
E_{23}(m)\le Q^{C_{23}}.
\]

Then by the usual extraction lemma, the set

\[
\mathcal G_{23}
=
\{m:E_{23}(m)\ge \tfrac12 Q^{-A_{23}}\}
\]

has polynomial total weight:

\[
\sum_{m\in\mathcal G_{23}}\rho(m)
\ge
Q^{-A_{23}-C_{23}-O(1)}
\sum_m\rho(m).
\]

So aggregate O2/O3 gives a polynomial-weight subset on which pointwise augmented-frame holds.

This is a substantial improvement over pointwise O2/O3.

---

# 4. But separate aggregate inputs do not guarantee intersection

Let \(E_1(m)\) denote the normalized Gate–1 energy, for example

\[
E_1(m)
=
\frac{|T_{\can}X(m)|^2}{|X(m)|^2}
\]

when \(X(m)\neq0\).

Aggregate Gate–1 gives

\[
\sum_m\rho(m)E_1(m)
\ge
Q^{-A_1}\sum_m\rho(m).
\]

Aggregate O2/O3 gives

\[
\sum_m\rho(m)E_{23}(m)
\ge
Q^{-A_{23}}\sum_m\rho(m).
\]

These separately imply polynomial-weight good sets

\[
\mathcal G_1=\{E_1\ge Q^{-A_1'}\},
\]

\[
\mathcal G_{23}=\{E_{23}\ge Q^{-A_{23}'}\}.
\]

But polynomial-weight subsets can be disjoint. Therefore separate aggregate theorems do not by themselves produce centers where both Gate–1 and O2/O3 hold.

This is a critical point.

To run downstream locally, we need

\[
m\in \mathcal G_1\cap \mathcal G_{23}.
\]

So Path C needs either:

\[
\boxed{
\text{pointwise O2/O3 on }\mathcal G_1,
}
\]

or

\[
\boxed{
\text{aggregate O2/O3 weighted by Gate--1 mass},
}
\]

or

\[
\boxed{
\text{a joint aggregate lower bound.}
}
\]

The second or third option is preferable.

---

# 5. Correct joint aggregate theorem

The cleanest Path C ordinary theorem is a joint lower bound:

\[
\sum_{m\in\mathcal C_{\ord}}
\rho(m)\,
E_1(m)\,
E_{23}(m)
\ge
Q^{-A}
\sum_{m\in\mathcal C_{\ord}}\rho(m).
\]

More invariantly, if \(E_1\) is normalized by \(|X|^2\), use

\[
\sum_m
\rho(m)
\frac{|T_{\can}X(m)|^2}{|X(m)|^2}
E_{23}(m)
\ge
Q^{-A}
\sum_m\rho(m).
\]

Or avoid division by small \(X\) by using the weighted form:

\[
\sum_m
\rho(m)
|T_{\can}X(m)|^2
E_{23}(m)
\ge
Q^{-A}
\sum_m
\rho(m)|X(m)|^2.
\]

This is the best formulation.

Then define the joint good set

\[
\mathcal G
=
\left\{
m:
E_1(m)\ge Q^{-B_1},
\quad
E_{23}(m)\ge Q^{-B_{23}}
\right\}.
\]

Assume polynomial upper bounds

\[
E_1(m)\le Q^{C_1},
\qquad
E_{23}(m)\le Q^{C_{23}}.
\]

From the product lower bound, one extracts a polynomial-weight joint good subset:

\[
\sum_{m\in\mathcal G}\rho(m)
\ge
Q^{-C}
\sum_m\rho(m),
\]

after choosing \(B_1,B_{23}\) large enough relative to the aggregate exponent and upper bounds.

This is the precise replacement for pointwise O2/O3.

---

# 6. Why aggregate O2/O3 is more plausible

Pointwise augmented-frame failure is a small singular value event. Random matrix heuristics predict many failures among exponentially many centers.

But aggregate O2/O3 only asks that the average augmented-frame energy is not too small:

\[
\mathbb E_\rho E_{23}(m)\ge Q^{-A}.
\]

For a random \(n\times n\) complex matrix with normalized entries,

\[
\mathbb E\,\sigma_{\min}^2
\]

is small in \(n\), but \(n=R+2\) grows only like

\[
\frac{\log Q}{\log\log Q}.
\]

So the expected value is at worst sub-polynomial or polynomially small in \(Q\), not exponentially small. An aggregate lower bound at scale \(Q^{-A}\) is therefore heuristically plausible.

This is analogous to aggregate Gate–1: random projection gives average visible energy roughly

\[
\frac{R}{Q/\log Q}=Q^{-1+o(1)},
\]

which is too small for pointwise but acceptable for aggregate.

Thus:

\[
\boxed{
\text{aggregate O2/O3 is plausibly lower-frame/second-moment in nature.}
}
\]

Pointwise O2/O3 is counter-random.

---

# 7. What theorem would prove aggregate O2/O3?

The aggregate augmented-frame lower bound is nonlinear because of the smallest singular value. A direct second-moment theorem controls trace or Frobenius norm, not \(\sigma_{\min}\).

So the right aggregate theorem should be stated as a **restricted lower frame**:

For every normalized \((c,P)\),

\[
\sum_{m\in\mathcal C_{\ord}}
\rho(m)
\left\|
\left(
L_m((c+m_{\can}P)x^k)
\right)_{0\le k\le R+1}
\right\|_2^2
\ge
Q^{-A}
\sum_m\rho(m).
\]

But this is not enough uniformly over all \((c,P)\) unless we control the finite-dimensional net. The dimension is \(R+2=Q^{o(1)}\), so a polynomial or sub-polynomial net is acceptable if the estimates have slack.

A stronger and cleaner formulation is:

\[
\inf_{|c|^2+\|P\|^2=1}
\sum_m
\rho(m)
\left\|
\left(
L_m((c+m_{\can}P)x^k)
\right)_{0\le k\le R+1}
\right\|_2^2
\ge
Q^{-A}
\sum_m\rho(m).
\]

This is an aggregate lower-frame theorem for the augmented O2/O3 space.

However, note the order of operations:

\[
\inf_{v}
\sum_m \|\mathcal A_m v\|^2
\]

is weaker than

\[
\sum_m
\sigma_{\min}(\mathcal A_m)^2.
\]

The former says every fixed vector is seen somewhere on average. The latter says each center is locally well-conditioned on average.

For good-subset extraction, we need the latter or a way to upgrade the former to many locally good centers. The fixed-vector aggregate frame can fail if different \(m\)'s see different directions but no single \(m\) sees all directions.

Thus the best theorem is a local-conditioning aggregate:

\[
\sum_m\rho(m)\sigma_{\min}(\mathcal A_m)^2
\ge
Q^{-A}\sum_m\rho(m).
\]

This is harder than a standard lower frame, but it is the theorem that directly supports extraction.

A compromise is to prove lower bounds for all exterior minors:

\[
\sum_m\rho(m)
|\det \mathcal A_m|^2
\ge
Q^{-A}\sum_m\rho(m),
\]

together with polynomial upper bounds for singular values. Since

\[
|\det \mathcal A_m|^2
=
\prod_i\sigma_i(m)^2,
\]

and upper bounds control the large singular values, determinant energy can imply small-singular-value energy:

\[
\sigma_{\min}(m)^2
\ge
\frac{|\det\mathcal A_m|^2}{\|\mathcal A_m\|^{2(n-1)}}.
\]

So an aggregate determinant lower bound may be the most accessible O2/O3 aggregate formulation.

---

# 8. Minimal Path C stack

I recommend:

\[
\boxed{
\text{aggregate Gate--1}
+
\text{joint aggregate augmented-frame/determinant}
+
\text{joint good-subset extraction}
+
\text{pointwise downstream}.
}
\]

Concretely, ordinary Path C should use:

### C-G1

\[
\sum_m\rho(m)|T_{\can}X(m)|^2
\ge
Q^{-A_1}
\sum_m\rho(m)|X(m)|^2.
\]

### C-AugDet or C-AugSV

Either determinant form:

\[
\sum_m\rho(m)
|\det \mathcal A_m|^2
\ge
Q^{-A_{23}}
\sum_m\rho(m),
\]

with operator norm upper bounds, or directly:

\[
\sum_m\rho(m)\sigma_{\min}(\mathcal A_m)^2
\ge
Q^{-A_{23}}
\sum_m\rho(m).
\]

### C-Joint

Preferably the joint form:

\[
\sum_m\rho(m)
\frac{|T_{\can}X(m)|^2}{|X(m)|^2}
\sigma_{\min}(\mathcal A_m)^2
\ge
Q^{-A}
\sum_m\rho(m).
\]

This gives a subset where both Gate–1 and augmented O2/O3 hold pointwise.

---

# 9. Final recommendation

\[
\boxed{
\text{Do not keep pointwise O2/O3 in Path C.}
}
\]

The minimal viable Path C is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{aggregate Gate--1 + aggregate augmented O2/O3 + joint extraction.}
}
\]

If forced to simplify, the next-best stack is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{aggregate Gate--1 + pointwise O2/O3 assumed on extracted subset,}
}
\]

but this is less coherent, because pointwise O2/O3 remains counter-random.

The mathematically clean Path C theorem is therefore:

\[
\boxed{
\sum_m\rho(m)
\frac{|T_{\can}X(m)|^2}{|X(m)|^2}
\sigma_{\min}(\mathcal A_m)^2
\ge
Q^{-A}
\sum_m\rho(m).
}
\]

From this, with polynomial upper bounds, one extracts a polynomial-weight subset on which both canonical Gate–1 and augmented O2/O3 hold pointwise, and the repaired downstream engine can run locally.

## Agent 4 report — joint aggregate Gate–1 and O2/O3 theorem

I handled the Stack C2 question: how to prevent Gate–1-good and O2/O3-good sets from being disjoint. The current coordinator target is explicitly **AggG1\(_{\can}\) + AggFrame\(_{23}\) + GoodSubsetIntersection + GoodSubsetDownstream**. 

## Verdict

\[
\boxed{
\text{Stack C2 needs a joint aggregate theorem, not merely two separate aggregate theorems.}
}
\]

Separate aggregate bounds for \(E_1\) and \(E_{23}\) do not guarantee a usable intersection. A product-energy theorem, or an equivalent joint determinant theorem, is the right mathematical object.

---

# 1. Product energy is sufficient for joint extraction

Let

\[
E_1(m)=\frac{|T_{\can}X(m)|^2}{|X(m)|^2}
\]

and

\[
E_{23}(m)=\sigma_{\min}(\mathcal A_m)^2,
\]

where

\[
\mathcal A_m(c,P)
=
\left(
L_m((c+m_{\can}P)x^k)
\right)_{0\le k\le R+1}.
\]

Assume polynomial upper bounds

\[
0\le E_1(m)\le B_1,\qquad 0\le E_{23}(m)\le B_{23},
\]

with

\[
B_1,B_{23}\le Q^C.
\]

Suppose we have the joint aggregate lower bound

\[
\sum_{m\in C} d\mu(m)\,E_1(m)E_{23}(m)
\ge
\eta\,\mu(C),
\qquad
\eta=Q^{-A}.
\]

Define thresholds

\[
a=\frac{\eta}{4B_{23}},
\qquad
b=\frac{\eta}{4B_1},
\]

and

\[
G=\{m:E_1(m)\ge a,\ E_{23}(m)\ge b\}.
\]

On the complement \(G^c\), either \(E_1<a\) or \(E_{23}<b\), hence

\[
E_1E_{23}
\le
\max(aB_{23},B_1b)
=
\frac{\eta}{4}.
\]

Therefore

\[
\eta\mu(C)
\le
\sum_G d\mu\,E_1E_{23}
+
\sum_{G^c}d\mu\,E_1E_{23}
\le
B_1B_{23}\mu(G)
+
\frac{\eta}{4}\mu(C).
\]

So

\[
\mu(G)
\ge
\frac{3\eta}{4B_1B_{23}}\mu(C).
\]

Since \(B_1,B_{23}\le Q^C\), this gives

\[
\boxed{
\mu(G)\ge Q^{-C'}\mu(C),
}
\]

and on \(G\),

\[
E_1(m)\ge Q^{-C'},\qquad
E_{23}(m)\ge Q^{-C'}.
\]

Thus the product aggregate theorem is exactly strong enough to extract a polynomial-weight subset where both local Gate–1 and local augmented O2/O3 hold.

---

# 2. Separate aggregate bounds are insufficient

Separate bounds

\[
\sum_C d\mu\,E_1\ge \eta_1\mu(C),
\]

and

\[
\sum_C d\mu\,E_{23}\ge \eta_{23}\mu(C)
\]

do not imply that the good sets intersect.

One can have

\[
E_1\text{ large on }C_1,\qquad
E_{23}\text{ large on }C_2,
\]

with

\[
C_1\cap C_2=\varnothing,
\]

and both aggregate estimates still hold.

So separate aggregate Gate–1 and aggregate O2/O3 require an additional correlation theorem:

\[
\mu(\{E_1\ge Q^{-A_1},\ E_{23}\ge Q^{-A_{23}}\})
\ge Q^{-C}\mu(C).
\]

The product theorem is precisely one clean way to encode that correlation.

---

# 3. A more natural joint determinant

The product

\[
E_1E_{23}
\]

is sufficient, but it is not obviously the easiest object to estimate because \(E_{23}=\sigma_{\min}(\mathcal A_m)^2\) is nonlinear.

A better algebraic proxy is determinant energy.

Let

\[
n=R+2.
\]

Assume

\[
\|\mathcal A_m\|\le Q^C.
\]

Then

\[
|\det \mathcal A_m|^2
=
\prod_{j=1}^n\sigma_j(\mathcal A_m)^2
\le
\sigma_{\min}(\mathcal A_m)^2\,\|\mathcal A_m\|^{2(n-1)}.
\]

Hence

\[
E_{23}(m)
=
\sigma_{\min}(\mathcal A_m)^2
\ge
\frac{|\det \mathcal A_m|^2}{\|\mathcal A_m\|^{2(n-1)}}.
\]

Because

\[
n=R+2\asymp\frac{\log Q}{\log\log Q},
\]

a polynomial operator norm bound gives

\[
\|\mathcal A_m\|^{2(n-1)}
\le
Q^{o(\log Q)}
\]

unless the norm bound is too crude. If the local normalization gives \(\|\mathcal A_m\|\le Q^{C_0}\), then the determinant-to-singular-value conversion costs

\[
Q^{O(R)}.
\]

This is not a fixed polynomial loss unless the normalization improves to

\[
\|\mathcal A_m\|\le Q^{O(1/R)}
\]

or the determinant lower bound has sufficient slack. Therefore, for fixed-polynomial bookkeeping, the cleanest condition is still a direct \(\sigma_{\min}\)-energy theorem.

But for actual estimation, the determinant form may be more algebraic:

\[
\sum_m d\mu(m)\,
E_1(m)\,
|\det\mathcal A_m|^2
\ge
Q^{-A}\mu(C).
\]

With strong enough uniform upper bounds on the other singular values, this implies the product theorem.

So there are two possible joint inputs:

### Direct joint singular-value input

\[
\boxed{
\sum_m d\mu(m)\,E_1(m)\sigma_{\min}(\mathcal A_m)^2
\ge Q^{-A}\mu(C).
}
\]

### Algebraic joint determinant input

\[
\boxed{
\sum_m d\mu(m)\,E_1(m)|\det\mathcal A_m|^2
\ge Q^{-A}\mu(C),
}
\]

plus upper bounds converting determinant control to \(\sigma_{\min}\)-control.

The first is exactly what the proof needs. The second may be more expandable into prime-log sums.

---

# 4. Expanded structure of the joint quantity

The Gate–1 factor is quadratic in the canonical projected cell vector:

\[
E_1(m)
\sim
\frac{|T_{\can}X(m)|^2}{|X(m)|^2}.
\]

The augmented-frame determinant is built from moments

\[
L_m((c+m_{\can}P)x^k).
\]

If one uses determinant energy, then

\[
|\det\mathcal A_m|^2
\]

expands into a degree-\(2n\) polynomial in the moment entries. Multiplying by \(|T_{\can}X(m)|^2\) yields a high-degree mixed moment over the zero/routed center set.

So the joint determinant theorem is a zero-restricted mixed moment lower bound of high but finite order:

\[
2n+2
=
2R+6
=
O\!\left(\frac{\log Q}{\log\log Q}\right).
\]

This order is \(Q^{o(1)}\), which is not absurd. But it is substantially harder than a second moment. It is closer to a finite high-moment lower large-sieve theorem.

A direct product with \(\sigma_{\min}\) is conceptually clean but less algebraically expandable.

Thus:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Joint product energy is proof-optimal; joint determinant energy is estimation-optimal.}
}
\]

---

# 5. Common-weight separate aggregates plus sequential bad-set control

There is an intermediate route.

Assume common measure \(d\mu\), and prove:

\[
\sum_C d\mu\,E_1\ge \eta_1\mu(C).
\]

Extract

\[
G_1=\{E_1\ge Q^{-A_1}\}
\]

with

\[
\mu(G_1)\ge Q^{-C}\mu(C).
\]

Then prove aggregate O2/O3 **restricted to \(G_1\)**:

\[
\sum_{m\in G_1}d\mu(m)E_{23}(m)
\ge
Q^{-A_{23}}\mu(G_1).
\]

This avoids estimating the product over all \(C\), but it is logically equivalent to a correlation input: O2/O3 must remain good on the Gate–1-visible subset.

This version may be easier if \(G_1\) has some structural definition from \(T_{\can}X\). But unless one can exploit that definition, it is no easier than product energy.

So the two viable formulations are:

\[
\boxed{
\text{global product theorem}}
\]

or

\[
\boxed{
\text{conditional aggregate O2/O3 on the Gate--1-good subset.}}
\]

Separate unconditional aggregate estimates are insufficient.

---

# 6. Plausibility

The product theorem is stronger than separate aggregate Gate–1 and aggregate O2/O3. But it is still more plausible than pointwise O2/O3.

Randomly, if \(E_1\) and \(E_{23}\) are not strongly anticorrelated, then

\[
\mathbb E(E_1E_{23})
\approx
\mathbb E(E_1)\mathbb E(E_{23}).
\]

The expected Gate–1 projection size is roughly

\[
\mathbb E E_1
\sim
\frac{R}{Q/\log Q}
=
Q^{-1+o(1)}.
\]

For a normalized \(n\times n\) random complex matrix with \(n=R+2\), one expects

\[
\mathbb E\sigma_{\min}^2
\]

to be polynomial or sub-polynomial in \(n\), hence \(Q^{-o(1)}\) or at worst \(Q^{-A_0}\) for some acceptable fixed \(A_0\).

So the product average should still be at least

\[
Q^{-1-o(1)}
\]

heuristically. That is compatible with a \(Q^{-A}\) aggregate theorem.

What would be implausible is demanding this lower bound pointwise at every center.

Thus the joint aggregate theorem is the right probabilistic scale.

---

# 7. Recommendation

For Stack C2, I recommend the following theorem as the central ordinary input:

\[
\boxed{
\sum_{m\in C}
d\mu(m)\,
E_1(m)\,
E_{23}(m)
\ge
Q^{-A}\mu(C).
}
\]

Where

\[
E_1(m)=\frac{|T_{\can}X(m)|^2}{|X(m)|^2},
\]

and

\[
E_{23}(m)=\sigma_{\min}(\mathcal A_m)^2.
\]

If singular values are too hard to estimate directly, use the determinant substitute:

\[
\boxed{
\sum_{m\in C}
d\mu(m)\,
E_1(m)
|\det\mathcal A_m|^2
\ge
Q^{-A}\mu(C),
}
\]

together with a carefully normalized operator-norm upper bound for \(\mathcal A_m\).

The product theorem gives the joint good subset directly. Separate aggregate theorems require an additional correlation theorem and should not be treated as sufficient.

Final classification:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Path C2 is viable only with a joint aggregate Gate--1/O2/O3 lower theorem.}
}
\]

This is a new arithmetic lower-frame/mixed-moment input, but it is dimensionally natural and far less counter-random than the old pointwise theorem.

_Thought for 13s_

## Agent 4 continuation — determinant proxy versus singular-value product

I continued the Stack C2 attack on the joint aggregate theorem. The current objective is to make

\[
\mathrm{AggG1}_{\can}
+
\mathrm{AggFrame}_{23}
+
\mathrm{GoodSubsetIntersection}
\]

usable without reintroducing pointwise counter-random assumptions. The previous instruction specifically put Agent 4 on the joint aggregate \(E_1E_{23}\) theorem. 

## 1. The immediate issue

The clean joint theorem is

\[
\sum_{m\in C} d\mu(m)\,E_1(m)E_{23}(m)
\ge Q^{-A}\mu(C),
\]

where

\[
E_1(m)=\frac{|T_{\can}X(m)|^2}{|X(m)|^2},
\]

and

\[
E_{23}(m)=\sigma_{\min}(\mathcal A_m)^2.
\]

This theorem is exactly suited for extraction, but hard to estimate because \(\sigma_{\min}\) is nonlinear and non-polynomial in the entries of \(\mathcal A_m\).

The algebraically expandable substitute is determinant energy:

\[
\sum_{m\in C} d\mu(m)\,E_1(m)|\det\mathcal A_m|^2
\ge Q^{-A}\mu(C).
\]

The question is whether determinant energy can replace singular-value energy without fatal losses.

---

# 2. Determinant energy only works under tight normalization

Let

\[
n=R+2.
\]

For each \(m\),

\[
|\det\mathcal A_m|^2
=
\prod_{j=1}^n \sigma_j(\mathcal A_m)^2.
\]

Thus

\[
\sigma_{\min}(\mathcal A_m)^2
\ge
\frac{|\det\mathcal A_m|^2}{\|\mathcal A_m\|^{2(n-1)}}.
\]

If all we know is

\[
\|\mathcal A_m\|\le Q^C,
\]

then

\[
\|\mathcal A_m\|^{2(n-1)}
\le Q^{2C(R+1)}.
\]

Since

\[
R\asymp \frac{\log Q}{\log\log Q},
\]

this loss is

\[
Q^{O(R)}
=
\exp\!\left(O\!\left(\frac{(\log Q)^2}{\log\log Q}\right)\right),
\]

which is **super-polynomial** in \(Q\).

So determinant energy does **not** imply usable \(E_{23}\)-energy under a generic polynomial operator-norm bound.

This is important. The determinant proxy is only viable if \(\mathcal A_m\) is normalized so that

\[
\|\mathcal A_m\|\le Q^{O(1/R)}
\]

or at worst

\[
\|\mathcal A_m\|\le e^{O(1)}.
\]

Then

\[
\|\mathcal A_m\|^{2(n-1)}
\le Q^{O(1)}
\]

or better, and determinant energy converts to singular-value energy with fixed-polynomial loss.

---

# 3. Correct normalization target

The augmented map should not be represented in raw monomial basis. Raw monomials on a packet create ill-conditioning.

Use instead:

\[
\mathcal P_0,\ldots,\mathcal P_R
\]

orthonormal or Riesz-normalized polynomials on the active packet interval

\[
[c_-,c_+],
\]

and normalize the output coordinates similarly. In that basis, the desired local operator norm is

\[
\|\mathcal A_m\|\le R^C
\]

or ideally

\[
\|\mathcal A_m\|\le C^R
\]

with a controlled base.

Now check the determinant conversion.

If

\[
\|\mathcal A_m\|\le R^C,
\]

then

\[
\|\mathcal A_m\|^{2(n-1)}
\le R^{C R}
=
\exp(CR\log R).
\]

Since

\[
R\sim\frac{\log Q}{\log\log Q},
\qquad
\log R\sim\log\log Q,
\]

we get

\[
R^{CR}
=
\exp(O(\log Q))
=
Q^{O(1)}.
\]

So **\(R^C\)-operator normalization is sufficient**.

This is the key repair.

The determinant proxy is viable if the augmented O2/O3 matrix is formulated in a polynomial/Riesz basis with operator norm

\[
\boxed{
\|\mathcal A_m\|\le R^C.
}
\]

Then determinant energy and singular-value energy are equivalent up to fixed-polynomial loss.

---

# 4. Revised aggregate O2/O3 theorem

The best Stack C2 input should therefore be stated in normalized exterior form.

Let

\[
\mathcal A_m:
\mathbb C\oplus \mathbb C_R[x]
\to
\mathbb C^{R+2}
\]

be written in a basis satisfying

\[
\|\mathcal A_m\|\le R^C
\]

uniformly.

Then use the determinant energy:

\[
\boxed{
\sum_{m\in C}
d\mu(m)\,
E_1(m)
|\det \mathcal A_m|^2
\ge
Q^{-A}\mu(C).
}
\]

This implies

\[
\sum_{m\in C}
d\mu(m)\,
E_1(m)
\sigma_{\min}(\mathcal A_m)^2
\ge
Q^{-A'}\mu(C),
\]

because

\[
\sigma_{\min}^2
\ge
|\det\mathcal A_m|^2 R^{-C R}
\ge
Q^{-C'}|\det\mathcal A_m|^2.
\]

Then product extraction gives a good subset with

\[
E_1(m)\ge Q^{-B_1},
\qquad
\sigma_{\min}(\mathcal A_m)^2\ge Q^{-B_{23}}.
\]

So determinant energy becomes usable after Riesz normalization.

---

# 5. Exterior-power formulation

The determinant is the top exterior power:

\[
|\det \mathcal A_m|^2
=
\|\Lambda^{n}\mathcal A_m\|^2,
\qquad n=R+2.
\]

Thus the joint theorem is:

\[
\sum_m d\mu(m)\,
E_1(m)
\|\Lambda^n\mathcal A_m\|^2
\ge
Q^{-A}\mu(C).
\]

This is algebraically cleaner than singular values and expands into finite mixed moments.

The Gate–1 factor \(E_1\) is quadratic. The exterior determinant factor has degree \(2n\) in the augmented moments. Total degree is

\[
2n+2=2R+6.
\]

Since

\[
R=Q^{o(1)},
\]

this is a high but slowly growing moment. That is not obviously impossible, but it must be treated as a serious zero-restricted high-moment lower bound, not a routine second-moment estimate.

---

# 6. Can product determinant energy vanish by sign cancellation?

The determinant energy is nonnegative:

\[
E_1(m)|\det\mathcal A_m|^2\ge0.
\]

This avoids the main complex cancellation issue after expansion. Of course, proving a lower bound for its average is still hard, but it is not a signed average.

This is better than trying to lower-bound raw mixed moments that may cancel.

So the recommended object for estimation is:

\[
\boxed{
E_1(m)|\det\mathcal A_m|^2.
}
\]

Not:

\[
E_1(m)\det\mathcal A_m
\]

or any signed determinant expression.

---

# 7. What a proof would need

A proof of the joint determinant theorem would likely require:

\[
\sum_m d\mu(m)
E_1(m)
|\det\mathcal A_m|^2
\not\ll Q^{-A}\mu(C).
\]

Expand \(\det\mathcal A_m\). It is an alternating sum over \(R+2\) columns, i.e. over the basis

\[
\mathbb C\cdot 1+m\mathbb C_R[x].
\]

After squaring, we get sums over two exterior configurations. The average over \(m\) becomes a zero-restricted correlation sum of the form

\[
\sum_m d\mu(m)
\prod_{\nu=1}^{R+2}
L_m(H_\nu)
\overline{
\prod_{\nu=1}^{R+2}
L_m(H'_\nu)
}
\cdot E_1(m).
\]

A diagonal contribution should exist when the two exterior configurations match. The needed lower-bound theorem is that the diagonal exterior mass survives and off-diagonal terms do not cancel it.

So the missing theorem is an **aggregate exterior lower-large-sieve**:

\[
\boxed{
\text{zero-restricted exterior-power lower frame for }
\mathbb C\cdot1+m\mathbb C_R[x],
\text{ weighted by }E_1.
}
\]

This is much more plausible than pointwise O2/O3 because it asks for average exterior mass, not uniform avoidance of small singular values.

---

# 8. Is product with \(E_1\) too nonlinear?

\(E_1\) itself is

\[
E_1(m)=\frac{|T_{\can}X(m)|^2}{|X(m)|^2}.
\]

The denominator is inconvenient in expansion. The better measure choice is:

\[
d\nu(m)=\rho(m)|X(m)|^2,
\]

and

\[
\widetilde E_1(m)=\frac{|T_{\can}X(m)|^2}{|X(m)|^2}.
\]

Then

\[
d\nu(m)\widetilde E_1(m)
=
\rho(m)|T_{\can}X(m)|^2.
\]

So the joint theorem should be formulated as:

\[
\boxed{
\sum_m
\rho(m)
|T_{\can}X(m)|^2
|\det\mathcal A_m|^2
\ge
Q^{-A}
\sum_m
\rho(m)|X(m)|^2.
}
\]

This avoids division by \(|X|\) in the estimated quantity.

For extraction, define the base measure

\[
d\nu(m)=\rho(m)|X(m)|^2.
\]

Then the product energy is

\[
\widetilde E_1(m)|\det\mathcal A_m|^2.
\]

If \(|X(m)|\) has bad variation, extraction is still valid in \(\nu\)-measure, which is exactly the natural Gate–1 mass measure.

Downstream then needs to accept \(\nu\)-weighted good subsets, not necessarily raw \(\rho\)-weighted ones. That is likely acceptable if row-energy summation is also weighted by the same local mass.

---

# 9. Updated Stack C2 theorem

The strongest usable formulation is:

\[
\boxed{
\sum_{m\in C}
\rho(m)
|T_{\can}X(m)|^2
|\det \mathcal A_m|^2
\ge
Q^{-A}
\sum_{m\in C}
\rho(m)|X(m)|^2.
}
\]

Assume also:

\[
\|\mathcal A_m\|\le R^C,
\]

\[
\frac{|T_{\can}X(m)|^2}{|X(m)|^2}\le R^C
\quad\text{or}\quad Q^C,
\]

and local nondegeneracy of \(X\) on the support, or else use the \(\nu=\rho|X|^2\) measure.

Then extraction yields a subset \(G\) with

\[
\nu(G)\ge Q^{-C}\nu(C),
\]

and on \(G\),

\[
\frac{|T_{\can}X(m)|^2}{|X(m)|^2}\ge Q^{-C},
\]

\[
\sigma_{\min}(\mathcal A_m)^2\ge Q^{-C}.
\]

This is exactly what the pointwise downstream needs.

---

# 10. Remaining risk

The joint determinant theorem is still a major arithmetic input. It is not automatic from aggregate Gate–1 plus aggregate O2/O3 separately.

The main remaining mathematical question is whether one can prove the nonnegative high-moment lower bound

\[
\sum_m
\rho(m)
|T_{\can}X(m)|^2
|\det \mathcal A_m|^2
\]

by a zero-restricted lower-large-sieve or explicit-formula averaging argument.

That is now the right attack surface.

---

# Final recommendation

Use determinant energy, not raw \(\sigma_{\min}\), but only after normalizing \(\mathcal A_m\) so that

\[
\|\mathcal A_m\|\le R^C.
\]

The Stack C2 ordinary input should be:

\[
\boxed{
\sum_m
\rho(m)
|T_{\can}X(m)|^2
|\det \mathcal A_m|^2
\ge
Q^{-A}
\sum_m
\rho(m)|X(m)|^2.
}
\]

This is the cleanest joint aggregate theorem. It is algebraically expandable, nonnegative, extraction-compatible, and dimensionally plausible.

It replaces three pointwise counter-random inputs with one aggregate exterior lower-frame theorem.

## Agent 4 report — JointAgg\(_{1,23,\mathrm{wall}}\)

I followed the Agent 4 instruction: identify the best joint aggregate quantity combining Gate–1, augmented O2/O3, and wall energy; test determinant/exterior and harmonic-exterior versions; and isolate the remaining arithmetic lower-bound problem. The current Stack C2 target is explicitly wall-inclusive joint aggregation plus good-subset extraction and downstream row mobility. 

## Verdict

\[
\boxed{
\text{The best central input is a capped or harmonic-exterior joint aggregate theorem.}
}
\]

The raw determinant product is algebraically expandable, but only safe after aggressive \(R^C\)-normalization of \(\mathcal A_m\). The raw singular-value product is extraction-optimal but analytically hard to estimate. The harmonic exterior ratio is the best compromise:

\[
\boxed{
\sum_{m\in C}
d\mu(m)\,
E_1(m)\,
\mathcal H_{23}(m)\,
\widetilde E_{\rm wall}(m)
\ge
Q^{-A}\mu(C).
}
\]

Here

\[
\mathcal H_{23}(m)
=
\frac{\det(\mathcal A_m^*\mathcal A_m)}
{e_{n-1}(\mathcal A_m^*\mathcal A_m)},
\qquad n=R+2,
\]

and

\[
\frac1n\sigma_{\min}(\mathcal A_m)^2
\le
\mathcal H_{23}(m)
\le
\sigma_{\min}(\mathcal A_m)^2.
\]

This quantity is pointwise comparable to the O2/O3 singular-value energy without requiring determinant-to-\(\sigma_{\min}\) conversion through a potentially super-polynomial operator-norm loss.

---

# 1. Ranking the joint quantities

There are four candidates.

## A. Capped product

\[
\int F_1F_{23}F_{\rm wall}\,d\mu
\ge Q^{-A}\mu(C).
\]

This is the best extraction object. It directly implies a polynomial-weight subset where all three local conditions hold.

But it is not algebraically natural. The caps introduce nonlinear cutoffs that are hard to expand into prime-log moments. This is best used as the final extraction hypothesis, not the first arithmetic object to estimate.

## B. Raw product

\[
\int E_1E_{23}E_{\rm wall}\,d\mu
\ge Q^{-A}\mu(C).
\]

This is clean, but \(E_{23}=\sigma_{\min}(\mathcal A_m)^2\) is difficult to expand. It is better than pointwise O2/O3 but still not ideal as an analytic target.

## C. Determinant/exterior product

\[
\sum_m \rho(m)
|T_{\can}X_{\can}(m)|^2
|\det\mathcal A_m|^2
\widetilde E_{\rm wall}(m)
\ge
Q^{-A}
\sum_m\rho(m)|X_{\can}(m)|^2.
\]

This is algebraically expandable and nonnegative. However, it controls \(\sigma_{\min}\) only if the other singular values are tightly normalized. If merely

\[
\|\mathcal A_m\|\le Q^C,
\]

then determinant-to-\(\sigma_{\min}\) conversion loses

\[
Q^{O(R)}
=
\exp\!\left(O\!\left(\frac{(\log Q)^2}{\log\log Q}\right)\right),
\]

which is super-polynomial. The determinant version is safe only if \(\mathcal A_m\) is represented in a Riesz/orthogonal basis with

\[
\|\mathcal A_m\|\le R^C.
\]

Then

\[
R^{CR}=Q^{O(1)}
\]

and determinant energy is extraction-compatible.

## D. Harmonic exterior product

\[
\sum_m d\mu(m)
E_1(m)\mathcal H_{23}(m)\widetilde E_{\rm wall}(m)
\ge Q^{-A}\mu(C).
\]

This is the best local invariant. It is pointwise comparable to \(\sigma_{\min}^2\), but still expressible via exterior quantities:

\[
\mathcal H_{23}
=
\frac{|\Lambda^n\mathcal A_m|^2}
{|\Lambda^{n-1}\mathcal A_m|_{\mathrm{HS}}^2}.
\]

It avoids the super-polynomial norm-loss problem of raw determinant energy.

So the recommendation is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Use harmonic exterior energy as the conceptual theorem,}
}
\]

and use determinant energy only on dyadic strata where

\[
|\Lambda^{n-1}\mathcal A_m|_{\mathrm{HS}}^2
\]

is controlled.

---

# 2. Moment order of the determinant/exterior product

Let

\[
n=R+2.
\]

The Gate–1 factor

\[
|T_{\can}X(m)|^2
\]

is quadratic in the canonical packet moments.

The determinant factor

\[
|\det\mathcal A_m|^2
\]

has degree

\[
2n=2R+4
\]

in the augmented O2/O3 moments.

The wall factor is built from

\[
|H(m)|^2(\Gamma_G(m)^2+\Gamma_{G,2}(m)^2),
\]

after normalization and capping. Its raw polynomial degree depends on how \(H,\Gamma_G,\Gamma_{G,2}\) are represented in the local source variables. In the D4 model,

\[
H\sim q_0u
\]

or equivalently in the associated graded D4 term

\[
H_{\rm D4}\sim q_0u^3
\]

depending on which normalized wall energy is used. At minimum, wall contributes a fixed finite polynomial degree in the canonical jet variables. Call this degree \(d_{\rm wall}=O(1)\) if the normalized wall observable is used.

Thus the total raw determinant product has degree

\[
2R+6+d_{\rm wall}
=
O(R).
\]

Since

\[
R\asymp\frac{\log Q}{\log\log Q},
\]

the total moment order is

\[
Q^{o(1)}.
\]

That is high, but still far below \(Q/\log Q\). It is plausible as a finite high-moment lower-large-sieve target, not as a routine second-moment estimate.

---

# 3. Nonnegativity and cancellation

The following are nonnegative pointwise:

\[
|T_{\can}X|^2,
\qquad
|\det\mathcal A_m|^2,
\qquad
\mathcal H_{23}(m),
\qquad
\widetilde E_{\rm wall}(m),
\]

assuming \(\widetilde E_{\rm wall}\) is defined as a squared normalized wall quantity or capped squared wall quantity.

Therefore the joint aggregate theorem avoids global sign cancellation at the final summation level.

However, when one expands

\[
|\det\mathcal A_m|^2,
\]

there are many signed or complex cross terms internally. A proof by expansion needs to show that the diagonal/exterior mass survives those cross terms after averaging over routed centers.

So nonnegativity helps, but it does not trivialize the lower bound. The missing theorem is still a lower bound for a high-degree nonnegative exterior polynomial sampled on zeta/routed centers.

---

# 4. What the zero-sampling / lower-large-sieve object looks like

Expanding the determinant gives an alternating exterior sum over basis elements

\[
1,\quad m_{\can},\quad xm_{\can},\quad \ldots,\quad x^Rm_{\can}.
\]

Schematically,

\[
\det\mathcal A_m
=
\sum_{\pi}
\operatorname{sgn}(\pi)
\prod_{j=0}^{R+1}
L_m(H_{\pi(j)}x^j),
\]

where

\[
H_0=1,\qquad H_{j+1}=m_{\can}x^j.
\]

Then

\[
|\det\mathcal A_m|^2
\]

contains products of \(R+2\) moment factors and \(R+2\) conjugate moment factors.

Multiplying by \(|T_{\can}X(m)|^2\) and wall energy yields a zero-restricted correlation sum of order \(O(R)\). Frequency terms become subset-product frequencies involving products of \(O(R)\) packet integers.

Thus the proof target is not ordinary diagonal dominance in a simple band. It is an **exterior-power zero-restricted lower-large-sieve theorem**.

The dangerous issue is subset-frequency separation. Products of \(O(R)\) packet integers can have logarithms separated only as small as

\[
e^{-O(QR)}
\]

in crude worst-case bounds, which is much worse than polynomial. Therefore a proof must avoid direct inversion of subset-frequency Vandermonde matrices.

A viable proof must use averaging/nonnegativity/exterior diagonal mass, not frequency-gap inversion.

---

# 5. Does actual-coefficient structure help?

The joint aggregate theorem should be stated for the actual canonical packet, not arbitrary packets. This matters.

A universal exterior lower frame for all possible coefficient vectors is too strong for the same reason universal Gate–1 lower sampling is too strong: adversarial packets can localize in sampling gaps or exterior-null configurations.

The actual theorem should be:

\[
\sum_m
\rho(m)
|T_{\can}X_{\can}(m)|^2
\mathcal H_{23}(m)
\widetilde E_{\rm wall}(m)
\ge
Q^{-A}
\sum_m
\rho(m)|X_{\can}(m)|^2.
\]

The coefficient vector is the actual one induced by

\[
q''_{\can}=B_2+P_2+D_2+E_2.
\]

This gives the proof some structure: AFE weights, folded duality, gamma/background terms, and regularized tails. But it also means the theorem is no longer a clean universal sampling theorem. It is an actual-coefficient zero-sampling lower bound.

That is acceptable for Stack C2. It is exactly the intended move away from pointwise counter-random projection.

---

# 6. Sequential version versus one joint theorem

There are two ways to formulate the stack.

## One-shot joint theorem

\[
\int F_1F_{23}F_{\rm wall}\,d\mu
\ge Q^{-A}\mu(C).
\]

This is extraction-perfect, but hard to prove directly.

## Sequential conditional theorem

First prove aggregate Gate–1:

\[
\int E_1\,d\mu\ge Q^{-A_1}\mu(C).
\]

Extract

\[
G_1=\{E_1\ge Q^{-B_1}\}.
\]

Then prove aggregate augmented frame on \(G_1\):

\[
\int_{G_1}\mathcal H_{23}\,d\mu
\ge Q^{-A_{23}}\mu(G_1).
\]

Extract

\[
G_{1,23}.
\]

Then prove aggregate wall-goodness on \(G_{1,23}\):

\[
\int_{G_{1,23}}\widetilde E_{\rm wall}\,d\mu
\ge Q^{-A_{\rm wall}}\mu(G_{1,23}).
\]

This sequential version may be more natural because each theorem is conditioned on a previously extracted actual subset. But it hides correlation assumptions in each restricted aggregate theorem.

The one-shot joint theorem is cleaner logically. The sequential version is cleaner if each step can exploit the structure of the already-good subset.

My recommendation:

\[
\boxed{
\text{State the one-shot joint theorem as the main input,}
}
\]

but attack it via sequential restricted lower-frame estimates.

---

# 7. Recommended central theorem

The robust central theorem should be formulated with capped quantities:

\[
F_1=\min(E_1/\eta_1,1),
\]

\[
F_{23}=\min(\mathcal H_{23}/\eta_{23},1),
\]

\[
F_{\rm wall}=\min(\widetilde E_{\rm wall}/\eta_{\rm wall},1).
\]

Then require

\[
\boxed{
\int_C
F_1F_{23}F_{\rm wall}\,d\mu
\ge
Q^{-A}\mu(C).
}
\]

For arithmetic estimation, use the sufficient exterior version:

\[
\boxed{
\sum_{m\in C}
\rho(m)
|T_{\can}X_{\can}(m)|^2
\mathcal H_{23}(m)
\widetilde E_{\rm wall}(m)
\ge
Q^{-A}
\sum_{m\in C}\rho(m)|X_{\can}(m)|^2.
}
\]

Or, if \(\mathcal A_m\) is \(R^C\)-normalized:

\[
\boxed{
\sum_{m\in C}
\rho(m)
|T_{\can}X_{\can}(m)|^2
|\det\mathcal A_m|^2
\widetilde E_{\rm wall}(m)
\ge
Q^{-A}
\sum_{m\in C}\rho(m)|X_{\can}(m)|^2.
}
\]

The determinant version requires:

\[
\|\mathcal A_m\|\le R^C
\]

uniformly in the chosen basis.

The harmonic exterior version does not require that determinant-to-\(\sigma_{\min}\) conversion.

---

# 8. Final classification

\[
\boxed{
\text{JointAgg}_{1,23,\mathrm{wall}}
\text{ is the correct Stack C2 bottleneck.}
}
\]

The most attackable version is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{actual-coefficient aggregate exterior lower-large-sieve}
}
\]

for

\[
|T_{\can}X_{\can}|^2
\cdot
\mathcal H_{23}
\cdot
\widetilde E_{\rm wall}.
\]

The key remaining arithmetic theorem is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{zero-restricted exterior-power lower sampling for the actual canonical packet.}
}
\]

This is still a serious new theorem, but it is dimensionally plausible and substantially less counter-random than the old pointwise scalar projection or pointwise O2/O3 assumptions.

## Agent 4 report — wall-inclusive joint aggregate theorem

I followed the Agent 4 instruction: select the right form of \(\mathrm{JointAgg}_{1,23,\mathrm{wall}}\), choose the measure, define the wall factor, compare capped/harmonic/determinant/sequential forms, and isolate the remaining lower-sampling theorem. The coordinator’s target is now the corrected Stack C2 with wall-goodness built into extraction, not added afterward. 

## Verdict

The best central theorem is the **wall-inclusive harmonic exterior joint aggregate theorem**:

\[
\boxed{
\sum_{m\in C}
d\mu(m)\,
E_1(m)\,
\mathcal H_{23}(m)\,
E_{\mathrm{wall,full}}(m)
\ge
Q^{-A}\mu(C).
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\int_C F_1F_{23}F_{\mathrm{wall}}\,d\mu
\ge
Q^{-A}\mu(C).

The harmonic exterior version is the best mathematical object; the capped version is the best extraction object.

---

# 1. Choice of central quantity

There are four candidate formulations.

## A. Capped product

\[
\int_C F_1F_{23}F_{\mathrm{wall}}\,d\mu
\ge Q^{-A}\mu(C),
\]

where

\[
F_1=\min(E_1/\eta_1,1),
\qquad
F_{23}=\min(\mathcal H_{23}/\eta_{23},1),
\qquad
F_{\mathrm{wall}}=\min(E_{\mathrm{wall,full}}/\eta_{\mathrm{wall}},1).
\]

This is ideal for extraction, because it is robust against outliers and directly gives a polynomial-weight set where all three local conditions hold.

But it is not the best arithmetic target, because caps are nonlinear cutoffs.

## B. Raw product

\[
\sum_C d\mu\,E_1\mathcal H_{23}E_{\mathrm{wall,full}}
\ge Q^{-A}\mu(C).
\]

This is the cleanest invariant mathematical theorem. It is nonnegative, pointwise meaningful, and directly stronger than the capped theorem after dyadic truncation.

## C. Determinant/exterior product

\[
\sum_m
\rho(m)|T_{\can}X_{\can}(m)|^2
|\det\mathcal A_m|^2
E_{\mathrm{wall,full}}(m)
\ge
Q^{-A}
\sum_m\rho(m)|X_{\can}(m)|^2.
\]

This is algebraically expandable. But it is safe only if \(\mathcal A_m\) is written in a Riesz-normalized basis with

\[
\|\mathcal A_m\|\le R^C.
\]

Otherwise determinant-to-\(\sigma_{\min}\) conversion loses

\[
\|\mathcal A_m\|^{2(R+1)},
\]

which is super-polynomial if \(\|\mathcal A_m\|\le Q^C\).

## D. Sequential restricted estimates

\[
\mathrm{AggG1}
\quad\Rightarrow\quad
G_1,
\]

then

\[
\mathrm{AggHarm}_{23}\text{ on }G_1
\quad\Rightarrow\quad
G_{1,23},
\]

then

\[
\mathrm{AggWall}\text{ on }G_{1,23}
\quad\Rightarrow\quad
G_{1,23,\mathrm{wall}}.
\]

This may be the best proof strategy, but it hides correlation assumptions in the restricted estimates. It is logically equivalent to needing joint non-avoidance.

### Recommendation

Use the one-shot theorem as the formal target:

\[
\boxed{
\sum_C d\mu\,E_1\mathcal H_{23}E_{\mathrm{wall,full}}
\ge Q^{-A}\mu(C).
}
\]

Attack it sequentially if the Gate–1-good subset has exploitable structure.

---

# 2. Correct measure

For normalized Gate–1,

\[
E_1(m)=\frac{|T_{\can}X_{\can}(m)|^2}{|X_{\can}(m)|^2}.
\]

This suggests the natural measure

\[
d\mu(m)=\rho(m)|X_{\can}(m)|^2.
\]

Then

\[
d\mu(m)E_1(m)
=
\rho(m)|T_{\can}X_{\can}(m)|^2,
\]

so no division by \(|X_{\can}(m)|\) appears in the arithmetic theorem.

Thus the preferred raw theorem is:

\[
\boxed{
\sum_{m\in C}
\rho(m)
|T_{\can}X_{\can}(m)|^2
\mathcal H_{23}(m)
E_{\mathrm{wall,full}}(m)
\ge
Q^{-A}
\sum_{m\in C}\rho(m)|X_{\can}(m)|^2.
}
\]

This is the best formulation. Extraction is then performed in the measure

\[
d\mu=\rho|X_{\can}|^2.
\]

Returning to raw \(\rho\)-measure would require comparability or lower/upper bounds for \(|X_{\can}|^2\), which should not be assumed unless independently proved.

---

# 3. Definition of \(E_{\mathrm{wall,full}}\)

The wall factor must include every local eligibility condition needed after extraction. A single scalar anisotropy factor is insufficient.

Define it as a capped product:

\[
F_{\mathrm{wall}}
=
F_{\mathrm{aniso}}
F_{\mathrm{Gram}}
F_{\mathrm{gauge}}
F_{\mathrm{car}}
F_{\mathrm{chart}}
F_{\mathrm{route}}.
\]

The uncapped ingredients should correspond to:

\[
E_{\mathrm{aniso}}
\sim
\frac{|H|^2(\Gamma_G^2+\Gamma_{G,2}^2)}
{\text{local normalization}},
\]

plus eligibility indicators or quantitative energies for:

\[
\text{Gram-good},
\quad
\text{gauge-good},
\quad
\text{carrier-frame-good},
\quad
\text{chart-good},
\quad
\text{carrier-routing-good}.
\]

For extraction, the safest definition is capped:

\[
F_{\mathrm{wall}}
=
\prod_{\nu}
\min(E_\nu/\eta_\nu,1).
\]

This prevents one very good wall variable from masking a failure of Gram/gauge/carrier/chart eligibility.

So the extraction theorem should use

\[
F_1F_{23}F_{\mathrm{aniso}}
F_{\mathrm{Gram}}
F_{\mathrm{gauge}}
F_{\mathrm{car}}
F_{\mathrm{chart}}
F_{\mathrm{route}}.
\]

---

# 4. Algebraic determinant version and moment degree

If \(\mathcal A_m\) is \(R^C\)-normalized, one may use

\[
|\det \mathcal A_m|^2
\]

instead of \(\mathcal H_{23}\).

The total degree is manageable:

\[
|T_{\can}X|^2
\]

is degree \(2\) in canonical packet moments.

\[
|\det \mathcal A_m|^2
\]

has degree

\[
2(R+2)=2R+4.
\]

The anisotropy/wall energy is fixed finite degree in the local canonical jet variables if normalized correctly. Denote this degree by \(d_{\mathrm{wall}}=O(1)\).

Thus the total moment degree is

\[
2R+6+d_{\mathrm{wall}}
=
O(R).
\]

Since

\[
R\asymp\frac{\log Q}{\log\log Q},
\]

this is \(Q^{o(1)}\)-order. It is a serious high-moment lower-sampling problem, but not dimensionally absurd.

The factors

\[
|T_{\can}X|^2,\qquad
|\det\mathcal A_m|^2,\qquad
E_{\mathrm{wall,full}}
\]

are nonnegative. After expansion, individual determinant cross terms are signed/complex, but the full squared exterior quantity is nonnegative. This is important: the final averaged object is not a signed moment.

---

# 5. Exact arithmetic theorem needed

The remaining arithmetic theorem is:

\[
\boxed{
\textbf{Wall-inclusive exterior lower sampling for the actual canonical packet.}
}
\]

A clean statement is:

\[
\sum_{m\in C}
\rho(m)
|T_{\can}X_{\can}(m)|^2
\mathcal H_{23}(m)
E_{\mathrm{wall,full}}(m)
\ge
Q^{-A}
\sum_{m\in C}\rho(m)|X_{\can}(m)|^2.
\]

Here:

\[
X_{\can}
\]

must be the actual canonical packet coming from

\[
q''_{\can}=B_2+P_2+D_2+E_2,
\]

not an arbitrary ordinary-band packet.

And

\[
\mathcal H_{23}
\]

must be formed from the full augmented O2/O3 map

\[
\mathcal A_m(c,P)
=
\left(
L_m((c+m_{\can}P)x^k)
\right)_{0\le k\le R+1},
\]

including plus/folded-dual/background/tail effects either inside \(m_{\can}\) or as explicit canonical affine corrections.

This theorem is weaker and more plausible than the old pointwise projection theorem because it is aggregate and actual-coefficient. But it is still the core new arithmetic input.

---

# 6. One-shot versus sequential attack

The one-shot theorem is cleaner:

\[
\sum_C d\mu\,E_1\mathcal H_{23}E_{\mathrm{wall,full}}
\ge Q^{-A}\mu(C).
\]

It guarantees a single good subset after capped extraction.

The sequential approach may be more attackable:

1. Prove aggregate Gate–1:
   \[
   \int E_1\,d\mu\ge Q^{-A_1}\mu(C).
   \]

2. Extract \(G_1\).

3. Prove aggregate harmonic O2/O3 on \(G_1\):
   \[
   \int_{G_1}\mathcal H_{23}\,d\mu\ge Q^{-A_{23}}\mu(G_1).
   \]

4. Extract \(G_{1,23}\).

5. Prove wall-goodness on \(G_{1,23}\):
   \[
   \int_{G_{1,23}}E_{\mathrm{wall,full}}\,d\mu
   \ge Q^{-A_{\mathrm{wall}}}\mu(G_{1,23}).
   \]

This is valid only if the restricted estimates are actually proved on the previously extracted sets. Without that, separate aggregate estimates do not guarantee intersection.

### Recommendation

State the one-shot theorem as the official Stack C2 input, but try to prove it through sequential restricted estimates.

---

# Final proposed JointAgg theorem

\[
\boxed{
\textbf{JointAgg}_{1,23,\mathrm{wall}}:
}
\]

There exists \(A>0\) such that, for every routed branch-complete center set \(C\),

\[
\boxed{
\sum_{m\in C}
\rho(m)
|T_{\can}X_{\can}(m)|^2
\mathcal H_{23}(m)
E_{\mathrm{wall,full}}(m)
\ge
Q^{-A}
\sum_{m\in C}\rho(m)|X_{\can}(m)|^2.
}
\]

With capped extraction, this yields a polynomial-weight subset where:

\[
E_1(m)\ge Q^{-A'},
\qquad
E_{23}(m)\ge Q^{-A'},
\qquad
E_{\mathrm{wall,full}}(m)\ge Q^{-A'}.
\]

Then the existing local Gate–2/D4 machinery can run pointwise on that subset, subject to RowMobility\(_{\rm lower}\) and GlobalRowEnergyUpper\(_{\rm agg}\).

## Final status

\[
\boxed{
\text{JointAgg}_{1,23,\mathrm{wall}}
\text{ is now the central upstream bottleneck for Stack C2.}
}
\]

The exact missing theorem is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{zero-restricted, wall-weighted, exterior-power lower sampling}
}
\]

for the actual canonical packet \(q''_{\can}\).
