## Agent 4 continuation — decomposing \(\mathcal H_{23}\) into Schur-complement energies

I pushed the wall-inclusive joint theorem further. The current central object is

\[
\sum_{m\in C}
\rho(m)
|T_{\can}X_{\can}(m)|^2
\mathcal H_{23}(m)
E_{\mathrm{wall,full}}(m)
\ge
Q^{-A}
\sum_{m\in C}\rho(m)|X_{\can}(m)|^2,
\]

where \(\mathcal H_{23}\) is the harmonic exterior ratio for the augmented O2/O3 matrix. This is the current Stack C2 bottleneck. 

The next useful move is to replace the opaque local quantity \(\mathcal H_{23}\) by **sequential Schur-complement residuals**. This gives a more estimable version of aggregate O2/O3.

---

# 1. Column model for the augmented O2/O3 map

Let

\[
\mathcal A_m:\mathbb C^{R+2}\to\mathbb C^{R+2}
\]

be the augmented map with columns corresponding to

\[
h_0(x)=1,
\qquad
h_{j+1}(x)=m_{\can}(x)x^j,\quad 0\le j\le R.
\]

The \(j\)-th column is

\[
v_j(m)=
\left(
L_m(h_jx^k)
\right)_{0\le k\le R+1}.
\]

So

\[
\mathcal A_m=[v_0(m)\ v_1(m)\ \cdots\ v_{R+1}(m)].
\]

Local O2/O3 asks for

\[
\sigma_{\min}(\mathcal A_m)^2\ge Q^{-A}.
\]

The harmonic exterior ratio is

\[
\mathcal H_{23}(m)
=
\frac{\det(\mathcal A_m^*\mathcal A_m)}
{e_{n-1}(\mathcal A_m^*\mathcal A_m)},
\qquad n=R+2.
\]

It is pointwise comparable to \(\sigma_{\min}^2\), but still difficult to estimate directly.

---

# 2. Sequential Schur residuals

Fix an ordering of the columns. Define

\[
V_j(m)=\operatorname{span}\{v_0(m),\ldots,v_{j-1}(m)\},
\]

and

\[
\Delta_j(m)
=
\operatorname{dist}(v_j(m),V_j(m))^2,
\qquad
0\le j\le R+1.
\]

By Gram–Schmidt,

\[
|\det\mathcal A_m|^2
=
\prod_{j=0}^{R+1}\Delta_j(m).
\]

Thus full augmented-frame nondegeneracy is equivalent to saying that none of these Schur residuals is too small, after normalization.

The first residual

\[
\Delta_0=\|v_0\|^2
\]

is the constant-direction moment energy.

The later residuals measure O2-type independence of the canonical multiplier columns

\[
m_{\can},\ xm_{\can},\ldots,x^Rm_{\can}.
\]

The specific O3 obstruction is visible as the residual of the constant column against the \(m_{\can}\mathbb C_R[x]\) block. For that purpose, reorder the columns as

\[
v_1,\ldots,v_{R+1},v_0.
\]

Then the final residual is

\[
\Delta_{\mathrm{O3}}(m)
=
\operatorname{dist}
\left(
v_0,
\operatorname{span}\{v_1,\ldots,v_{R+1}\}
\right)^2.
\]

This is exactly O3 after O2.

So the augmented frame naturally splits into:

\[
\boxed{
\text{O2 residuals for }m_{\can}\mathbb C_R[x]
}
\]

and

\[
\boxed{
\text{O3 residual for the constant direction.}
}
\]

---

# 3. Why raw determinant residuals are not enough

A determinant lower bound controls the product

\[
\prod_j\Delta_j.
\]

But for local extraction we need each relevant residual polynomially large.

If

\[
\prod_j\Delta_j\ge Q^{-A}
\]

and

\[
\Delta_j\le R^C,
\]

then

\[
\Delta_j\ge Q^{-A}R^{-CR}
\ge Q^{-A-C'}
\]

for at least one residual, but **not for all** residuals unless we have product plus upper bounds on the other residuals.

Actually, from

\[
\prod_j\Delta_j\ge Q^{-A}
\]

and

\[
\Delta_j\le R^C
\]

for all \(j\), each individual residual satisfies

\[
\Delta_j
\ge
Q^{-A}R^{-C R}
=
Q^{-A-C'}
\]

because if one residual were smaller than this, even with all others at their maximum the product would fall below \(Q^{-A}\).

Thus determinant works **only in a normalized basis** where the residual upper bounds are \(R^C\), not \(Q^C\).

This confirms the prior conclusion:

\[
\boxed{
\text{determinant/exterior energy is viable only with }R^C\text{-normalization.}
}
\]

---

# 4. A better aggregate target: capped Schur residual product

Instead of \(\mathcal H_{23}\) or raw determinant, define normalized residuals

\[
\widehat\Delta_j(m)
=
\frac{\Delta_j(m)}
{\|v_j(m)\|^2+\varepsilon_{\rm norm}},
\]

or use a Riesz-normalized basis so that column sizes are \(R^C\)-controlled.

Then define capped residual factors

\[
F_j(m)=\min\left(\frac{\widehat\Delta_j(m)}{\eta_j},1\right).
\]

The augmented O2/O3 good condition is

\[
F_0F_1\cdots F_{R+1}\approx1.
\]

So the joint Stack C2 extraction condition becomes

\[
\boxed{
\int_C
F_1^{\rm Gate}
\left(\prod_{j=0}^{R+1}F_j^{23}\right)
F_{\rm wall}
\,d\mu
\ge
Q^{-A}\mu(C).
}
\]

This is extraction-perfect: it directly gives a polynomial-weight subset where every Schur residual is polynomially large.

But it is too many capped factors:

\[
R+2\asymp\frac{\log Q}{\log\log Q}.
\]

The extraction loss from requiring all factors above threshold remains polynomial because \(R=Q^{o(1)}\), but the arithmetic theorem becomes a high-order joint nondegeneracy theorem.

The advantage is conceptual: it avoids the determinant-to-singular-value ambiguity.

The disadvantage is analytic: capped Schur residuals are hard to expand.

---

# 5. Pivoted O2/O3 may reduce the burden

The fixed column ordering may be too rigid. O2 does not require every predetermined residual to be large; it requires the \(m_{\can}\mathbb C_R[x]\) block to be well-conditioned.

A better local formulation uses pivoted QR:

For the \(m_{\can}\mathbb C_R[x]\) block, choose pivots adaptively to maximize the next residual. Then O2 means all pivot residuals are large.

After O2 pivoting, O3 is the residual of \(v_0\) against the completed pivot span.

The aggregate theorem could use the product of pivoted residuals:

\[
\prod_{j=1}^{R+1}\Delta_j^{\rm piv}(m)
\cdot
\Delta_{\rm O3}(m).
\]

This equals the determinant of the augmented Gram up to column permutations, but the pivoted form is more stable and avoids bad fixed bases.

So the more robust local quantity is:

\[
\boxed{
\mathcal R_{23}(m)
=
\left(
\prod_{j=1}^{R+1}
\Delta_j^{\rm piv}(m)
\right)
\Delta_{\rm O3}(m).
}
\]

With \(R^C\)-normalization,

\[
\mathcal R_{23}(m)\ge Q^{-A}
\]

implies

\[
\sigma_{\min}(\mathcal A_m)^2\ge Q^{-A'}.
\]

The aggregate theorem becomes

\[
\sum_m d\mu(m)
E_1(m)
\mathcal R_{23}(m)
E_{\rm wall}(m)
\ge Q^{-A}\mu(C).
\]

This is essentially determinant energy, but interpreted via stable pivots.

---

# 6. Can \(\mathcal H_{23}\) be expressed as averaged Schur residual?

There is a useful identity.

For a positive definite matrix

\[
G=\mathcal A^*\mathcal A
\]

with eigenvalues \(\lambda_1,\ldots,\lambda_n\),

\[
\mathcal H_{23}
=
\frac{\prod_i\lambda_i}
{\sum_i\prod_{j\ne i}\lambda_j}
=
\frac{1}{\sum_i\lambda_i^{-1}}
=
\frac{1}{\operatorname{tr}(G^{-1})}.
\]

Also,

\[
\operatorname{tr}(G^{-1})
=
\sum_{j=1}^n
\operatorname{dist}(e_j,\operatorname{span\ of\ other\ columns})^{-2}
\]

in the coordinate geometry induced by the columns. More concretely, the diagonal entries of \(G^{-1}\) are inverse squared distances of each column to the span of the others:

\[
(G^{-1})_{jj}
=
\operatorname{dist}(v_j,\operatorname{span}\{v_i:i\ne j\})^{-2}.
\]

Therefore

\[
\mathcal H_{23}
=
\left(
\sum_{j=0}^{R+1}
\operatorname{dist}
(v_j,\operatorname{span}_{i\ne j}v_i)^{-2}
\right)^{-1}.
\]

This is valuable.

It says \(\mathcal H_{23}\) is controlled by **all leave-one-out Schur residuals**, not by an arbitrary column order.

Thus \(\mathcal H_{23}\) is the correct symmetric local quantity.

The joint theorem can be interpreted as:

\[
\sum_m d\mu(m)E_1(m)E_{\rm wall}(m)
\left(
\sum_j \Delta_j^{\rm loo}(m)^{-1}
\right)^{-1}
\ge Q^{-A}\mu(C).
\]

This avoids pivot ordering entirely.

---

# 7. Attackable version of JointAgg

The most precise theorem is now:

\[
\boxed{
\sum_{m\in C}
d\mu(m)\,
E_1(m)\,
E_{\rm wall,full}(m)
\left(
\sum_{j=0}^{R+1}
\Delta_j^{\rm loo}(m)^{-1}
\right)^{-1}
\ge
Q^{-A}\mu(C),
}
\]

where

\[
\Delta_j^{\rm loo}(m)
=
\operatorname{dist}
\left(
v_j(m),
\operatorname{span}\{v_i(m):i\ne j\}
\right)^2.
\]

This is exactly equivalent to the harmonic exterior formulation.

For proof attempts, dyadically decompose according to the leave-one-out residual profile. The bad set is where at least one essential direction has tiny residual. Thus the arithmetic problem becomes:

\[
\boxed{
\text{show that Gate--1 and wall mass do not concentrate on centers where an augmented column nearly lies in the span of the others.}
}
\]

That is a much more geometric formulation of the joint aggregate lower theorem.

---

# 8. Relation to O3

The leave-one-out residual corresponding to the constant column \(v_0\) is precisely the O3 residual:

\[
\Delta_0^{\rm loo}
=
\operatorname{dist}
\left(
v_0,
m_{\can}\mathbb C_R[x]
\right)^2.
\]

The other residuals are O2 cofactor residuals.

So the aggregate \(\mathcal H_{23}\) theorem says:

\[
\boxed{
\text{on average, weighted by Gate--1 and wall mass, neither O2 nor O3 degenerates.}
}
\]

This is exactly the aggregate replacement for pointwise O2/O3.

---

# 9. Remaining arithmetic theorem

The final irreducible input is:

\[
\boxed{
\textbf{Gate--1/wall-weighted leave-one-out complex-frame nondegeneracy.}
}
\]

For the actual canonical moment columns \(v_j(m)\),

\[
\sum_{m\in C}
d\mu(m)
E_1(m)
E_{\rm wall,full}(m)
\left(
\sum_{j=0}^{R+1}
\operatorname{dist}(v_j,\operatorname{span}_{i\ne j}v_i)^{-2}
\right)^{-1}
\ge
Q^{-A}\mu(C).
\]

This is equivalent to the harmonic exterior joint theorem and is more geometrically transparent.

It remains a serious aggregate arithmetic theorem, but it is no longer a pointwise counter-random assertion.

---

# 10. Final recommendation

Use three interchangeable forms:

### Extraction form

\[
\int F_1F_{23}F_{\rm wall}\,d\mu
\ge Q^{-A}\mu(C).
\]

### Invariant harmonic form

\[
\sum_C d\mu\,E_1\mathcal H_{23}E_{\rm wall}
\ge Q^{-A}\mu(C).
\]

### Leave-one-out residual form

\[
\sum_C d\mu\,E_1E_{\rm wall}
\left(
\sum_j(\Delta_j^{\rm loo})^{-1}
\right)^{-1}
\ge Q^{-A}\mu(C).
\]

The leave-one-out form is the best for mathematical attack, because it isolates the bad events:

\[
v_j\approx \operatorname{span}\{v_i:i\ne j\}.
\]

The next attack should try to show that the actual zeta/routed-center measure cannot place most Gate–1 and wall mass on these leave-one-out degeneracy tubes.

## Agent 4 continuation — tube-avoidance formulation of JointAgg

I continued the attack from the leave-one-out residual formulation. The current Stack C2 bottleneck is the wall-weighted joint aggregate theorem, and the previous reduction identified \(\mathcal H_{23}\) with a symmetric leave-one-out nondegeneracy quantity. 

The useful next reformulation is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{JointAgg}_{1,23,\mathrm{wall}}
\text{ is equivalent to a weighted sublevel-set estimate for degeneracy tubes.}
}
\]

This is more attackable than the raw determinant or singular-value formulation.

---

# 1. Local leave-one-out residuals

Let

\[
\mathcal A_m=[v_0(m),v_1(m),\ldots,v_{n-1}(m)],
\qquad n=R+2,
\]

where

\[
v_0(m)
=
\left(L_m(x^k)\right)_{0\le k\le R+1},
\]

and

\[
v_{j+1}(m)
=
\left(L_m(m_{\can}(x)x^{j+k})\right)_{0\le k\le R+1},
\qquad 0\le j\le R.
\]

Define the leave-one-out residuals

\[
\Delta_j(m)
=
\operatorname{dist}
\left(
v_j(m),
\operatorname{span}\{v_i(m):i\ne j\}
\right)^2.
\]

Then, with

\[
G_m=\mathcal A_m^*\mathcal A_m,
\]

one has

\[
(G_m^{-1})_{jj}
=
\Delta_j(m)^{-1}
\]

when \(G_m\) is invertible. Therefore

\[
\mathcal H_{23}(m)
=
\frac{1}{\operatorname{tr}(G_m^{-1})}
=
\left(
\sum_{j=0}^{n-1}\Delta_j(m)^{-1}
\right)^{-1}.
\]

Thus

\[
\frac{1}{n}\min_j\Delta_j(m)
\le
\mathcal H_{23}(m)
\le
\min_j\Delta_j(m).
\]

So aggregate O2/O3 reduces to keeping the weighted mass away from the union of tubes

\[
\mathcal T_j(\varepsilon)
=
\{m:\Delta_j(m)\le \varepsilon\}.
\]

---

# 2. Weighted measure for the joint theorem

The correct joint measure is not raw counting measure. It is

\[
d\nu(m)
=
\rho(m)|X_{\can}(m)|^2
E_1(m)
E_{\mathrm{wall,full}}(m)\,dm_{\mathrm{disc}},
\]

or equivalently, since

\[
E_1(m)=\frac{|T_{\can}X_{\can}(m)|^2}{|X_{\can}(m)|^2},
\]

\[
d\nu(m)
=
\rho(m)|T_{\can}X_{\can}(m)|^2
E_{\mathrm{wall,full}}(m)\,dm_{\mathrm{disc}}.
\]

Then the harmonic joint theorem becomes simply

\[
\boxed{
\int_C \mathcal H_{23}(m)\,d\nu(m)
\ge
Q^{-A}\nu_0(C),
}
\]

where

\[
\nu_0(C)=\sum_{m\in C}\rho(m)|X_{\can}(m)|^2
\]

or whichever base normalization is chosen.

If we instead normalize by the already weighted measure \(\nu\), the theorem is

\[
\boxed{
\int_C \mathcal H_{23}(m)\,d\nu(m)
\ge
Q^{-A}\nu(C).
}
\]

This is cleaner after Gate–1 and wall weighting have already been incorporated.

---

# 3. Sufficient tube sublevel theorem

A natural sufficient condition is the following.

For each \(j=0,\ldots,n-1\), suppose there are constants \(B,\beta>0\) such that for all

\[
0<\varepsilon\le 1
\]

one has

\[
\boxed{
\nu(\mathcal T_j(\varepsilon))
\le
Q^B\varepsilon^\beta \nu(C).
}
\]

Then we get a lower bound for \(\mathcal H_{23}\) on average.

Indeed, since

\[
\mathcal H_{23}(m)
\ge
\frac{1}{n}\min_j\Delta_j(m),
\]

define

\[
\Delta_{\min}(m)=\min_j\Delta_j(m).
\]

Then

\[
\{\Delta_{\min}\le\varepsilon\}
\subseteq
\bigcup_j\mathcal T_j(\varepsilon),
\]

so

\[
\nu(\Delta_{\min}\le\varepsilon)
\le
nQ^B\varepsilon^\beta\nu(C).
\]

Choose

\[
\varepsilon_0
=
Q^{-C_0}
\]

with \(C_0\) large enough that

\[
nQ^B\varepsilon_0^\beta
\le
\frac12.
\]

Then on a set of \(\nu\)-measure at least \(\frac12\nu(C)\),

\[
\Delta_{\min}\ge Q^{-C_0}.
\]

Therefore

\[
\mathcal H_{23}\ge \frac{1}{n}Q^{-C_0}
\]

on that set, and hence

\[
\int_C\mathcal H_{23}\,d\nu
\ge
\frac12 n^{-1}Q^{-C_0}\nu(C).
\]

Since

\[
n=R+2=Q^{o(1)},
\]

this gives

\[
\boxed{
\int_C\mathcal H_{23}\,d\nu
\ge Q^{-C}\nu(C).
}
\]

Thus a polynomial sublevel estimate for each leave-one-out tube is sufficient.

---

# 4. What is each degeneracy tube?

The condition

\[
\Delta_j(m)\le \varepsilon
\]

means there exists a coefficient vector \(a=(a_i)_{i\ne j}\) such that

\[
\left\|
v_j(m)-\sum_{i\ne j}a_i v_i(m)
\right\|^2
\le \varepsilon.
\]

Equivalently, there exists a polynomial relation among the basis functions.

For \(j=0\), this is the O3 tube:

\[
1
\approx
m_{\can}(x)P(x)
\]

in the zero-conditioned moment geometry, with

\[
\deg P\le R.
\]

More exactly,

\[
\left\|
\left(
L_m((1-m_{\can}P)x^k)
\right)_{0\le k\le R+1}
\right\|
\le \varepsilon^{1/2}.
\]

For \(j\ge1\), this is an O2 tube:

\[
m_{\can}(x)x^{j-1}
\approx
c
+
m_{\can}(x)P_{\widehat{j}}(x)
\]

in the same moment geometry, where \(P_{\widehat{j}}\) omits the \(x^{j-1}\) basis direction.

So all degeneracy tubes are of the same type:

\[
\boxed{
L_m(H(x)x^k)\approx0,\qquad 0\le k\le R+1,
}
\]

for some nonzero

\[
H\in
\mathbb C\cdot1+m_{\can}\mathbb C_R[x].
\]

Thus tube avoidance is a uniform anti-concentration statement over the projective space

\[
\mathbb P(\mathbb C\cdot1+m_{\can}\mathbb C_R[x]).
\]

---

# 5. Projective formulation

Let

\[
\mathcal V
=
\mathbb C\cdot1+m_{\can}\mathbb C_R[x],
\qquad
\dim\mathcal V=n=R+2.
\]

For \(H\in\mathcal V\), define

\[
\Phi_m(H)
=
\left(
L_m(Hx^k)
\right)_{0\le k\le R+1}.
\]

Then

\[
\mathcal A_m
\]

is the matrix of \(\Phi_m\) in a chosen basis of \(\mathcal V\).

The aggregate O2/O3 theorem can be attacked through the projective sublevel estimate:

\[
\boxed{
\nu\left(
\left\{
m:
\|\Phi_m(H)\|
\le \varepsilon \|H\|
\right\}
\right)
\le
Q^B\varepsilon^\beta\nu(C)
}
\]

uniformly for all

\[
0\ne H\in\mathcal V.
\]

If this holds uniformly over \(H\), then a net argument over

\[
\mathbb P(\mathcal V)
\]

gives a lower bound for

\[
\sigma_{\min}(\mathcal A_m)
\]

on a positive-weight subset.

But we must be careful: the net size in complex dimension \(n=R+2\) is roughly

\[
(1/\varepsilon)^{O(n)}.
\]

If

\[
\varepsilon=Q^{-C},
\]

then the net size is

\[
Q^{O(CR)}
=
\exp\!\left(O\!\left(\frac{(\log Q)^2}{\log\log Q}\right)\right),
\]

which is super-polynomial. This is too costly unless the sublevel estimate has exponential strength in \(R\), or unless we use exterior/harmonic quantities that avoid net union bounds.

Therefore:

\[
\boxed{
\text{individual }H\text{-sublevel estimates are not enough unless they have }e^{-O(R\log Q)}\text{ slack.}
}
\]

The leave-one-out residual formulation avoids a full projective net because it uses only \(n\) canonical degeneracy directions **after the matrix is formed**. But estimating those directions is hard, because they themselves depend on \(m\).

---

# 6. Moving-degeneracy issue

The tube

\[
\Delta_j(m)\le \varepsilon
\]

is not defined by a fixed \(H\). The best approximating dependence relation changes with \(m\). This is the main complication.

In algebraic terms,

\[
\Delta_j(m)\le\varepsilon
\]

is equivalent to smallness of a Schur complement, or smallness of a cofactor/minor ratio. It is not just one fixed moment vector being small.

So there are two possible routes.

## Route A: exterior ratio directly

Work with

\[
\mathcal H_{23}
=
\frac{|\Lambda^n\mathcal A_m|^2}
{|\Lambda^{n-1}\mathcal A_m|_{\mathrm{HS}}^2}.
\]

This avoids moving choices of \(H\). The arithmetic theorem is a lower bound for this ratio averaged with Gate–1/wall weight.

## Route B: dyadic denominator + determinant lower bound

Stratify by

\[
D_{n-1}(m)=|\Lambda^{n-1}\mathcal A_m|_{\mathrm{HS}}^2.
\]

On a slice

\[
D_{n-1}\sim \Lambda,
\]

one has

\[
\mathcal H_{23}\sim
\frac{|\det\mathcal A_m|^2}{\Lambda}.
\]

So it suffices to show that, on at least one dyadic slice,

\[
\sum_{m:D_{n-1}\sim\Lambda}
d\nu(m)
|\det\mathcal A_m|^2
\ge
Q^{-A}\Lambda\,\nu(C).
\]

This is an exterior determinant lower bound with denominator control.

This seems the most attackable algebraic route.

---

# 7. Wall factor interaction

The wall factor should be included in the measure:

\[
d\nu_{\rm wall}(m)
=
\rho(m)|T_{\can}X_{\can}(m)|^2
E_{\mathrm{wall,full}}(m).
\]

Then the O2/O3 aggregate theorem is

\[
\int_C \mathcal H_{23}(m)\,d\nu_{\rm wall}(m)
\ge
Q^{-A}
\sum_C\rho|X_{\can}|^2.
\]

The wall factor is not merely a downstream nuisance. It biases the sampling measure. Therefore the exterior lower-sampling theorem must be proved **under wall-weighted sampling**, not under raw zero counting.

This could help or hurt:

- It helps if wall energy correlates with nondegenerate O2/O3 frames.
- It hurts if wall energy concentrates on augmented-frame degeneracy tubes.

Thus the precise arithmetic theorem is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{wall-weighted exterior-ratio lower sampling.}
}
\]

Not just unweighted aggregate O2/O3.

---

# 8. Local algebraic sufficient condition

Suppose we can prove two estimates.

### Upper denominator bound

\[
D_{n-1}(m)
=
|\Lambda^{n-1}\mathcal A_m|_{\mathrm{HS}}^2
\le R^C
\]

on the wall-good support, after Riesz normalization.

### Joint determinant lower bound

\[
\sum_C
\rho(m)
|T_{\can}X_{\can}(m)|^2
E_{\mathrm{wall,full}}(m)
|\det\mathcal A_m|^2
\ge
Q^{-A}
\sum_C\rho(m)|X_{\can}(m)|^2.
\]

Then

\[
\mathcal H_{23}
=
\frac{|\det\mathcal A_m|^2}{D_{n-1}(m)}
\ge
R^{-C}|\det\mathcal A_m|^2,
\]

so

\[
\sum_C
\rho|T_{\can}X|^2
E_{\mathrm{wall,full}}
\mathcal H_{23}
\ge
Q^{-A'}
\sum_C\rho|X|^2.
\]

This closes the joint aggregate theorem.

Thus we can avoid full harmonic-ratio estimation if we can normalize so that

\[
|\Lambda^{n-1}\mathcal A_m|_{\mathrm{HS}}^2\le R^C.
\]

This is slightly weaker than bounding \(\|\mathcal A_m\|\le R^C\), since

\[
|\Lambda^{n-1}\mathcal A|_{\mathrm{HS}}^2
\le
\binom{n}{n-1}\|\mathcal A\|^{2(n-1)}.
\]

A direct exterior normalization may be better.

---

# 9. Final reduced theorem

The cleanest current target is:

\[
\boxed{
\textbf{Wall-weighted exterior determinant lower sampling.}
}
\]

After Riesz/exterior normalization of \(\mathcal A_m\), prove

\[
\sum_{m\in C}
\rho(m)
|T_{\can}X_{\can}(m)|^2
E_{\mathrm{wall,full}}(m)
|\det\mathcal A_m|^2
\ge
Q^{-A}
\sum_{m\in C}\rho(m)|X_{\can}(m)|^2.
\]

Together with

\[
|\Lambda^{n-1}\mathcal A_m|_{\mathrm{HS}}^2\le R^C
\]

on the same support, this implies the harmonic exterior joint theorem and hence extraction.

This is more concrete than the \(\mathcal H_{23}\) statement.

---

# 10. What remains genuinely hard

The determinant expands into \(O(R)\)-degree prime-log moments with subset-product frequencies. Direct frequency separation is hopeless because subset-product frequencies can be too close.

Therefore the proof must use:

\[
\boxed{
\text{nonnegative exterior mass + actual coefficients + zero/routed-center averaging,}
}
\]

not Vandermonde inversion.

The hard theorem is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{the wall-weighted zero/routed-center average of the exterior determinant is not small.}
}
\]

That is now the exact arithmetic target for Agent 4’s piece of Stack C2.

---

# Final status

The best current attack surface is no longer the raw harmonic ratio. It is the pair:

\[
\boxed{
|\Lambda^{n-1}\mathcal A_m|_{\mathrm{HS}}^2\le R^C
}
\]

after proper normalization, and

\[
\boxed{
\sum_m
\rho|T_{\can}X_{\can}|^2
E_{\mathrm{wall,full}}
|\det\mathcal A_m|^2
\ge
Q^{-A}
\sum_m\rho|X_{\can}|^2.
}
\]

If both hold, Stack C2 obtains a polynomial-weight good subset with Gate–1, O2/O3, and wall-goodness simultaneously.

I’ll continue by expanding the exterior determinant itself. The key question is whether its Cauchy–Binet structure has an intrinsic diagonal mass, or whether it still needs a new zero-sampling theorem.

_Thought for 1m 5s_

## Agent 4 continuation — Cauchy–Binet expansion of the exterior determinant

I pushed the wall-weighted exterior determinant target down to the coefficient level. The central Stack C2 bottleneck is still the wall-inclusive joint aggregate theorem, but the determinant expansion now separates the obstruction into three pieces: analytic multiplier noncollapse, multiplicative collision/noncancellation, and zero-sampling lower mass. The prompt’s current architecture is the corrected Stack C2 with \(\mathrm{JointAgg}_{1,23,\mathrm{wall}}\) as the central aggregate input. 

---

# 1. Model the augmented matrix

Ignore dual/background/tail for one pass. Write the prime-side contribution as

\[
L_m(H)
=
\sum_{n\in\mathcal B}
a_n e^{-im\log n}H(x_n),
\qquad
x_n=\lambda\log n.
\]

The augmented O2/O3 basis is

\[
h_0(x)=1,
\qquad
h_{j+1}(x)=m_{\can}(x)x^j,
\quad 0\le j\le R.
\]

Let

\[
N=R+2.
\]

The augmented matrix has entries

\[
(\mathcal A_m)_{k,j}
=
L_m(h_jx^k),
\qquad
0\le k,j\le N-1.
\]

So

\[
(\mathcal A_m)_{k,j}
=
\sum_{n\in\mathcal B}
a_ne^{-im\log n}x_n^k h_j(x_n).
\]

This can be written as

\[
\mathcal A_m
=
V^T D_m H,
\]

where

\[
V_{n,k}=x_n^k,
\qquad
D_m(n,n)=a_ne^{-im\log n},
\qquad
H_{n,j}=h_j(x_n).
\]

---

# 2. Cauchy–Binet determinant formula

By Cauchy–Binet,

\[
\det\mathcal A_m
=
\sum_{\substack{S\subset\mathcal B\\ |S|=N}}
\left(\prod_{n\in S}a_ne^{-im\log n}\right)
\det V_S
\det H_S.
\]

Here

\[
\det V_S
=
\prod_{n<n'\in S}(x_{n'}-x_n)
\]

is the usual Vandermonde determinant.

The second determinant is the key analytic multiplier determinant:

\[
\det H_S
=
\det
\begin{pmatrix}
1&m(x_1)&m(x_1)x_1&\cdots&m(x_1)x_1^R\\
1&m(x_2)&m(x_2)x_2&\cdots&m(x_2)x_2^R\\
\vdots&\vdots&\vdots&&\vdots\\
1&m(x_N)&m(x_N)x_N&\cdots&m(x_N)x_N^R
\end{pmatrix}.
\]

If \(m(x_i)\neq0\) on the packet, factor \(m(x_i)\) from each row except the first column by rewriting the first column as \(1/m(x_i)\):

\[
\det H_S
=
\left(\prod_{i=1}^N m(x_i)\right)
\det
\begin{pmatrix}
1/m(x_1)&1&x_1&\cdots&x_1^R\\
1/m(x_2)&1&x_2&\cdots&x_2^R\\
\vdots&\vdots&\vdots&&\vdots\\
1/m(x_N)&1&x_N&\cdots&x_N^R
\end{pmatrix}.
\]

Therefore

\[
\det H_S
=
\left(\prod_{i=1}^N m(x_i)\right)
\det V_S\,
[x_1,\ldots,x_N]\left(\frac1m\right),
\]

where

\[
[x_1,\ldots,x_N]\left(\frac1m\right)
\]

is the divided difference of order \(N-1=R+1\).

Thus

\[
\boxed{
\det\mathcal A_m
=
\sum_{|S|=N}
C_S e^{-im\log N_S},
}
\]

where

\[
N_S=\prod_{n\in S}n,
\]

and

\[
\boxed{
C_S
=
\left(\prod_{n\in S}a_n\right)
\left(\det V_S\right)^2
\left(\prod_{n\in S}m(x_n)\right)
[x_n:n\in S]\left(\frac1m\right).
}
\]

This is the cleanest structural formula so far.

---

# 3. Interpretation of the determinant coefficient

The coefficient \(C_S\) splits into four parts:

\[
C_S
=
\text{amplitude}
\times
\text{Vandermonde}^2
\times
\text{multiplier product}
\times
\text{reciprocal-multiplier divided difference}.
\]

The last factor is exactly the analytic O3 content.

If \(m\) is scalar, then

\[
1/m
\]

is constant, and the divided difference of order \(R+1\) is zero. Hence

\[
\det\mathcal A_m=0.
\]

This recovers scalar multiplier collapse.

More generally, if

\[
1/m
\]

is extremely well approximated by a degree-\(R\) polynomial on the packet, then the divided differences are small, and the determinant exterior energy is weak.

Thus the analytic part of O3 is exactly:

\[
\boxed{
[x_1,\ldots,x_N]\left(\frac1{m_{\can}}\right)
\text{ is not too small on enough separated }N\text{-tuples.}
}
\]

This is much more precise than saying “\(m\) is non-scalar.”

---

# 4. Analytic multiplier noncollapse is probably not the main blocker

For a fixed analytic multiplier \(m_{\can}\) on a compact ordinary packet, if \(1/m_{\can}\) is not a degree-\(R\) polynomial and has no near singularity pathologically arranged with \(R\), then one expects polynomially acceptable divided-difference mass on separated tuples.

A strong enough analytic statement would be:

\[
\sum_{\substack{S:|S|=N\\ S\text{ separated}}}
\left|
\left(\det V_S\right)^2
\left(\prod_{n\in S}m(x_n)\right)
[x_n:n\in S]\left(\frac1m\right)
\right|^2
\ge
e^{-O(R\log R)}
\sum_{S\text{ separated}}w_S,
\]

with appropriate weights.

Since

\[
R\asymp\frac{\log Q}{\log\log Q},
\]

the loss

\[
e^{-O(R\log R)}
\]

is only polynomial in \(Q\).

So after Riesz-normalization, analytic multiplier noncollapse is plausibly green.

The real obstruction is not analytic. It is the exterior Dirichlet polynomial sampling problem.

---

# 5. Exterior determinant becomes a long Dirichlet polynomial

The determinant is a Dirichlet polynomial in the product variable:

\[
D(m):=\det\mathcal A_m
=
\sum_{S} C_S N_S^{-im}.
\]

The product length is

\[
N_S=\prod_{n\in S}n.
\]

If each packet integer \(n\) has

\[
\log n\asymp Q,
\]

and

\[
|S|=R+2,
\]

then

\[
\log N_S\asymp RQ.
\]

So the exterior determinant has effective length

\[
\exp(O(RQ)).
\]

This is much longer than the original packet length.

This creates a serious issue for lower sampling. Standard mean-square or large-sieve technology over height \(T=e^Q\) does not automatically give a lower frame for a Dirichlet polynomial of length

\[
e^{RQ}.
\]

The product frequencies

\[
\log N_S
\]

can be separated only as poorly as

\[
e^{-O(RQ)}
\]

in worst case. Direct frequency-gap inversion is therefore hopeless.

This confirms that the determinant formulation is not a routine lower large sieve.

---

# 6. Multiplicative collision problem

There is another obstruction.

Different subsets \(S\) may have the same product:

\[
N_S=N_{S'}.
\]

Then their coefficients combine:

\[
C_N=\sum_{S:\,N_S=N}C_S.
\]

The true determinant polynomial is

\[
D(m)=\sum_N C_N N^{-im}.
\]

A lower-bound proof needs mass in the grouped coefficients:

\[
\sum_N |C_N|^2,
\]

not just in

\[
\sum_S |C_S|^2.
\]

Thus we need a multiplicative collision noncancellation theorem:

\[
\boxed{
\sum_N
\left|
\sum_{S:\,N_S=N}C_S
\right|^2
\ge
Q^{-A}
\sum_S|C_S|^2
}
\]

or an actual-coefficient substitute.

This is nontrivial because subset products of integers have many collisions.

A prime-supported model would avoid most collisions, but the AFE/canonical packet is over integers, not primes. So this cannot be ignored.

---

# 7. Zero-sampling lower bound for the exterior determinant

Even after analytic noncollapse and collision noncancellation, one still needs a zero/routed-center lower bound:

\[
\sum_{m\in C}
\rho(m)
|D(m)|^2
\ge
Q^{-A}
\sum_N |C_N|^2
\]

or, more directly in the joint theorem,

\[
\sum_{m\in C}
\rho(m)
|T_{\can}X_{\can}(m)|^2
E_{\mathrm{wall,full}}(m)
|D(m)|^2
\ge
Q^{-A}
\sum_m\rho(m)|X_{\can}(m)|^2.
\]

This is the exterior determinant lower-sampling theorem.

It is weaker than a universal lower frame because \(D\) is the actual determinant polynomial coming from \(q''_{\can}\), not an arbitrary length-\(e^{RQ}\) Dirichlet polynomial. But it is still a serious new arithmetic statement.

---

# 8. Gate–1 and wall weights complicate but also help

The actual Stack C2 quantity is not simply

\[
\sum_C\rho|D|^2.
\]

It is

\[
\sum_C
\rho
|T_{\can}X_{\can}|^2
E_{\mathrm{wall,full}}
|D|^2.
\]

The Gate–1 and wall factors are nonnegative. They prevent the determinant-good set from being disjoint from the Gate–1/wall-good set.

But they also raise the moment order.

If

\[
|T_{\can}X_{\can}|^2
\]

is quadratic, and

\[
|D|^2
\]

has degree

\[
2(R+2),
\]

then before wall factors the moment order is

\[
2R+6.
\]

Wall factors add only fixed degree if normalized locally. So the total degree remains

\[
O(R)=O\!\left(\frac{\log Q}{\log\log Q}\right).
\]

This is high but still \(Q^{o(1)}\). It is not dimensionally impossible.

---

# 9. Clean decomposition of the remaining theorem

The wall-weighted determinant approach reduces to three subtheorems.

## A. Analytic exterior multiplier mass

For enough separated \(N\)-tuples,

\[
\left|
\left(\det V_S\right)^2
\left(\prod m(x_n)\right)
[x_n]\left(1/m\right)
\right|
\]

has at least polynomially weighted mass after Riesz normalization.

This is an analytic theorem about \(m_{\can}\).

## B. Multiplicative collision noncancellation

After grouping subsets by product,

\[
\sum_N |C_N|^2
\]

does not collapse relative to the ungrouped exterior mass.

This is an arithmetic-combinatorial theorem about the actual canonical coefficients.

## C. Wall-weighted zero-sampling lower bound

The exterior determinant polynomial satisfies

\[
\sum_C
\rho
|T_{\can}X_{\can}|^2
E_{\mathrm{wall,full}}
|D(m)|^2
\ge
Q^{-A}
\sum_N |C_N|^2
\]

or the directly normalized Stack C2 RHS.

This is a zero/routed-center lower-sampling theorem.

Together with the exterior denominator bound

\[
|\Lambda^{n-1}\mathcal A_m|_{\mathrm{HS}}^2\le R^C,
\]

these imply the harmonic joint theorem.

---

# 10. The main no-go risk

The biggest risk is **not** the divided difference. It is the exterior Dirichlet length and multiplicative collisions.

The determinant has length

\[
e^{RQ},
\]

and ordinary zero sampling at height \(e^Q\) cannot see arbitrary such polynomials. Thus any proof must use the actual determinant structure very strongly.

A universal theorem of the form

\[
\sum_{\gamma\le T}|D(\gamma)|^2
\ge Q^{-A}\sum_N |C_N|^2
\]

for all exterior determinant-type polynomials of length \(T^R\) is probably false or at least far beyond standard tools.

The viable theorem must be actual-coefficient:

\[
D=\det\mathcal A_m
\]

built from the actual canonical packet.

So the new exact bottleneck is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{actual-coefficient wall-weighted exterior determinant lower sampling.}
}
\]

---

# 11. Final status of this continuation

The determinant route is more concrete than the harmonic ratio, but it exposes new hard arithmetic subproblems.

The best Stack C2 input can now be decomposed as:

\[
\boxed{
\text{analytic multiplier exterior mass}
}
\]

\[
+
\boxed{
\text{multiplicative collision noncancellation}
}
\]

\[
+
\boxed{
\text{wall-weighted zero-sampling lower bound for the exterior determinant}
}
\]

\[
+
\boxed{
|\Lambda^{n-1}\mathcal A_m|_{\mathrm{HS}}^2\le R^C.
}
\]

If these hold, then the wall-inclusive joint aggregate theorem follows, capped extraction gives a good subset, and local downstream can run.

The most important mathematical target is therefore:

\[
\boxed{
\sum_m
\rho(m)
|T_{\can}X_{\can}(m)|^2
E_{\mathrm{wall,full}}(m)
|\det\mathcal A_m|^2
\ge
Q^{-A}
\sum_m\rho(m)|X_{\can}(m)|^2,
}
\]

with \(\mathcal A_m\) in an \(R^C\)-normalized basis and built from the full canonical source

\[
q''_{\can}=B_2+P_2+D_2+E_2.
\]

That is the sharpened Agent 4 target.

## Agent 4 continuation — collision-safe exterior determinant mass

I continued from the Cauchy–Binet expansion of the augmented determinant. The current Stack C2 bottleneck is the wall-weighted exterior determinant lower sampling theorem, and the previous reduction showed that the main new dangers are exterior length, multiplicative collisions, and zero/routed-center sampling. 

The most useful next attack is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Can we remove or control multiplicative collisions in }
\det \mathcal A_m?
}
\]

The answer is: **partially, but only after changing the determinant expansion to a collision-safe subpacket.** This gives a possible route for the exterior determinant theorem, but it is not yet a proof.

---

# 1. Recall the determinant expansion

For one prime-log branch, we had

\[
\det \mathcal A_m
=
\sum_{\substack{S\subset \mathcal B\\ |S|=N}}
C_S e^{-im\log N_S},
\qquad
N=R+2,
\]

where

\[
N_S=\prod_{n\in S}n,
\]

and

\[
C_S
=
\left(\prod_{n\in S}a_n\right)
(\det V_S)^2
\left(\prod_{n\in S}m(x_n)\right)
[x_n:n\in S]\left(\frac1m\right).
\]

The problem is that different subsets \(S\neq S'\) may have

\[
N_S=N_{S'}.
\]

Then the true coefficient at frequency \(\log N\) is

\[
C_N=\sum_{S:N_S=N}C_S.
\]

So the determinant mass is

\[
\sum_N |C_N|^2,
\]

not

\[
\sum_S |C_S|^2.
\]

Without controlling this grouping, exterior mass can collapse.

---

# 2. Collision-free model: primes or unique labels

If \(\mathcal B\) consisted only of primes, then

\[
\prod_{p\in S}p
=
\prod_{p\in S'}p
\]

would imply

\[
S=S'
\]

by unique factorization.

Then there are no subset-product collisions, and

\[
\sum_N |C_N|^2
=
\sum_S |C_S|^2.
\]

This suggests a route:

\[
\boxed{
\text{force the determinant expansion onto a collision-free arithmetic skeleton.}
}
\]

But the canonical AFE packet is over integers, not only primes. So we need a way to extract a prime-labelled or unique-factor-labelled subpacket from the actual canonical packet.

---

# 3. Large-prime-factor labelling

A plausible collision-safe construction is to select integers \(n\) in the packet with a unique large prime factor.

Write

\[
n=p r,
\]

where

\[
p\in \mathcal P_{\rm lab},
\]

and \(p\) lies in a large-prime label interval, while \(r\) lies in a smaller cofactor range. If the label prime \(p\) is unique to the selected atom and does not divide any other selected atom, then subset products remember the selected labels.

More precisely, build atoms

\[
\mathcal B_p=\{n=pr:\ r\in\mathcal R_p\},
\]

with disjoint label primes \(p\). Pick at most one atom from each \(\mathcal B_p\) in an exterior subset \(S\). Then

\[
N_S=\prod_{n\in S}n
\]

contains the label prime set

\[
\{p(n):n\in S\}.
\]

Thus if

\[
N_S=N_{S'},
\]

then the label-prime sets agree. If additionally each label fiber contributes a fixed representative or a collision-controlled cofactor, then \(S=S'\) or collisions are sharply bounded.

This gives a possible theorem:

\[
\sum_N |C_N|^2
\ge
Q^{-A}
\sum_{S\in\mathfrak S_{\rm lab}}|C_S|^2,
\]

where \(\mathfrak S_{\rm lab}\) is the family of exterior subsets using distinct labels.

The key question becomes whether the labelled subpacket carries polynomially much canonical mass.

---

# 4. Mass of labelled subpackets

The ordinary packet has

\[
\log n\asymp Q.
\]

Integers in this range are near size

\[
e^Q.
\]

A positive density of integers have a prime factor above, say,

\[
e^{\theta Q}
\]

for many fixed \(\theta\in(0,1)\). But requiring a **unique** large label and clean cofactor support may reduce the mass.

We do not need density one. We need only polynomially much mass relative to the canonical packet energy, because the architecture tolerates \(Q^{-A}\) losses.

So a viable arithmetic lemma would be:

\[
\boxed{
\sum_{n\in\mathcal B_{\rm lab}} |a_n|^2
\ge
Q^{-A}
\sum_{n\in\mathcal B}|a_n|^2.
}
\]

For \(a_n\sim n^{-1/2}\) times smooth AFE weights, this is plausible from rough-number / large-prime-factor estimates. The hard part is not counting; it is preserving the canonical determinant structure after restriction.

If the determinant is computed from the full matrix \(\mathcal A_m\), we cannot simply discard terms. But for a lower bound on

\[
|\det\mathcal A_m|^2,
\]

discarding terms inside the determinant is not legitimate because of possible cancellation with discarded terms.

So the labelled skeleton is useful only if we can either:

1. define a projected determinant \(\det\mathcal A_m^{\rm lab}\) that is still accepted by the O2/O3 frame, or  
2. prove the labelled contribution survives in the full determinant after averaging.

Option 1 changes the local augmented map and must be checked against downstream O2/O3. Option 2 is a hard noncancellation theorem.

---

# 5. Exterior minor projection route

Instead of the full determinant over all packet atoms, define a localized/filtered augmented map:

\[
\mathcal A_m^{\rm lab}
\]

using only the labelled subpacket.

Then

\[
\det \mathcal A_m^{\rm lab}
\]

has the same Cauchy–Binet expansion, but restricted to collision-safe \(S\).

If this determinant is large, does it imply the original O2/O3 condition for the full canonical map?

Not automatically.

The full augmented map is

\[
\mathcal A_m
=
\mathcal A_m^{\rm lab}
+
\mathcal A_m^{\rm rest}.
\]

Large determinant of one summand does not imply large determinant or singular value of the sum, because the rest can cancel.

However, if the labelled and rest parts are orthogonalized by frequency projection or by a positive energy decomposition, then one can use a block-frame argument.

This suggests a modified local O2/O3 quantity:

\[
\mathcal H_{23}^{\rm family}(m)
=
\sum_{\nu}
\mathcal H_{23}^{(\nu)}(m),
\]

where \(\nu\) ranges over collision-safe labels or subpackets.

But this starts to drift toward a family-valued upstream object. It remains acceptable only if the summed quantity still certifies the same scalar canonical \(q''_{\can}\) local source. That compatibility is nontrivial.

So the projected-minor route is not immediately compatible with the current scalar downstream.

---

# 6. Positivity route using sign-definite divided differences

There is another possible way to control collisions without restricting to primes.

Suppose on the ordinary packet:

1. \(a_n\) have essentially common phase;
2. \(m(x)\) has a reciprocal \(1/m(x)\) whose \((R+1)\)-st divided differences have a common sign;
3. \((\det V_S)^2\ge0\).

Then the coefficients \(C_S\) may have aligned phases:

\[
C_S\approx e^{i\theta}\,|C_S|.
\]

If so, then when multiple subsets collide,

\[
C_N=\sum_{S:N_S=N}C_S
\]

does not cancel. In fact,

\[
|C_N|
\approx
\sum_{S:N_S=N}|C_S|.
\]

Then

\[
\sum_N |C_N|^2
\ge
\sum_S |C_S|^2.
\]

This would solve the collision problem.

Can this happen?

For a single prime-side AFE branch, \(a_n\) may be positive or slowly varying after normalization. The factor

\[
(\det V_S)^2
\]

is nonnegative. If \(m\) is real and \(1/m\) has a fixed sign \((R+1)\)-st derivative on the interval, then the divided difference has fixed sign.

Thus, in a highly normalized single-branch real model, collision cancellation may be absent.

But for the full canonical source:

\[
q''_{\can}=B_2+P_2+D_2+E_2,
\]

we have plus/folded-dual phases, gamma/completion corrections, and complex weights. The common-phase condition is not automatic.

Still, this gives a concrete sub-attack:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Can the full canonical determinant be decomposed into sign-aligned exterior branches?}
}
\]

If yes, collisions are less dangerous.

If no, collision noncancellation remains a separate arithmetic input.

---

# 7. Divided-difference sign condition

The analytic coefficient

\[
[x_1,\ldots,x_N](1/m)
\]

has the Hermite integral representation

\[
[x_1,\ldots,x_N]f
=
\frac{1}{(N-1)!}
\int_{\Delta_{N-1}}
f^{(N-1)}
\left(
\sum_{i=1}^N t_i x_i
\right)
d\sigma(t),
\]

up to normalization.

Thus if

\[
f^{(R+1)}(x)
\]

has fixed phase/sign on the packet, then all divided differences have fixed phase/sign.

So the analytic sign-alignment condition is:

\[
\boxed{
\arg\left(\frac{d^{R+1}}{dx^{R+1}}\frac1{m_{\can}(x)}\right)
\text{ varies by }<\pi/4
\text{ on the packet.}
}
\]

This is much stronger than non-scalar multiplier variation.

Is it plausible? Maybe not for the full \(m_{\can}\), but possibly for a carefully chosen ordinary subpacket where the symbol is monotone and one branch dominates.

This suggests a **subpacket partition**:

Partition the ordinary frequency interval into \(O(Q^o(1))\) subintervals where

\[
(1/m_{\can})^{(R+1)}
\]

has controlled phase. On each such subinterval, exterior coefficients are phase-aligned. Then sum over subintervals.

The risk is that subintervals must be wide enough to support \(N=R+2\) exterior nodes and not destroy Vandermonde mass.

---

# 8. Collision control via squarefree kernel

Another route is to group by squarefree kernel or prime valuation vector.

Let

\[
\operatorname{rad}(n)
\]

be the squarefree kernel. If selected atoms have distinct large squarefree kernels, then subset products are partially labelled.

A possible combinatorial lemma:

There exists a subcollection

\[
\mathcal B_*
\subset\mathcal B
\]

with

\[
\sum_{n\in\mathcal B_*}|a_n|^2
\ge Q^{-A}
\sum_{n\in\mathcal B}|a_n|^2
\]

such that subset products of size \(N=R+2\) from \(\mathcal B_*\) have collision multiplicity at most

\[
Q^A.
\]

Then by Cauchy–Schwarz, even without phase alignment,

\[
\sum_N |C_N|^2
\ge
\frac{1}{M_{\rm coll}}
\sum_S |C_S|^2
\]

if the coefficients are not adversarially signed within each collision class. More precisely, bounded multiplicity alone gives

\[
\sum_N\left|\sum_{S:N_S=N}C_S\right|^2
\]

can still vanish by cancellation if phases are adversarial. But if coefficients have controlled phase, bounded multiplicity suffices.

So squarefree-kernel control helps only together with phase control.

---

# 9. Collision issue after averaging over \(m\)

Maybe exact product collisions are not the only issue. For non-equal products,

\[
N_S\neq N_{S'},
\]

averaging over \(m\in C\) gives kernels

\[
\sum_{m\in C}\rho(m)e^{-im\log(N_S/N_{S'})}.
\]

If \(C\) is dense enough, off-diagonal terms should cancel unless

\[
|\log(N_S/N_{S'})|
\]

is very small.

But for products of length \(R\), we can have

\[
|\log(N_S/N_{S'})|
\gtrsim e^{-O(RQ)}.
\]

This is far below the resolution of a height-\(e^Q\) zero sample. So near-collisions may behave almost like collisions.

Thus we need not only exact collision control, but **near-collision control** at sampling resolution.

A useful definition:

\[
S\sim S'
\quad\text{if}\quad
|\log(N_S/N_{S'})|\le e^{-Q}Q^B.
\]

Within each near-collision cluster, phases do not separate over height \(e^Q\). The determinant coefficient should be grouped over these clusters.

The needed theorem is then:

\[
\boxed{
\sum_{\mathcal C_{\rm near}}
\left|
\sum_{S\in\mathcal C_{\rm near}} C_S
\right|^2
\ge
Q^{-A}\sum_S|C_S|^2.
}
\]

This is stronger than exact collision noncancellation.

Prime-label or large-label constructions can help here, because distinct label products are separated at least by reciprocal of product size. But product size is \(e^{RQ}\), still giving \(e^{-RQ}\), not \(e^{-Q}\). To get sampling-scale separation \(e^{-Q}\), one needs stronger label spacing.

This is a serious issue.

---

# 10. A possible resolution: short exterior blocks

The exterior determinant uses

\[
N=R+2
\]

columns. That creates product length \(e^{RQ}\).

Can we reduce exterior length?

O2/O3 dimension is \(R+2\), so the full determinant naturally uses \(R+2\) sampled integers. But one might use a multiscale/block determinant where each block has size \(L\ll R\), and prove frame nondegeneracy by combining several smaller exterior certificates.

For example, split

\[
\mathbb C\cdot1+m\mathbb C_R[x]
\]

into \(J\) blocks of dimension \(L\), with

\[
JL\sim R.
\]

Each block determinant has product length \(e^{LQ}\). If \(L=O(1)\) or \(O(\log Q/Q)\) impossible; but if \(L\sim \log Q\), product length \(e^{Q\log Q}\) still large but less severe.

The question is whether blockwise nondegeneracy implies full augmented frame.

In general, no. A matrix can be nondegenerate on coordinate blocks but still have a global small singular vector spread across blocks.

But with an additional almost-orthogonality condition between blocks, it might work.

This suggests another possible theorem:

\[
\boxed{
\text{block-exterior frame decomposition for }\mathbb C\cdot1+m\mathbb C_R[x].
}
\]

It would trade one large determinant for several smaller determinants plus cross-block angle estimates.

This may be more accessible than the full exterior determinant, but it introduces new block-angle conditions.

---

# 11. Current best sub-attack

The best immediate target is not the full determinant lower bound. It is the analytic/collision-safe coefficient mass:

\[
\boxed{
\sum_N |C_N|^2
\ge Q^{-A}
\sum_S |C_S|^2
}
\]

or near-collision version, under actual canonical coefficients.

I see three plausible routes, in decreasing order of compatibility:

## Route 1: phase-aligned single-branch exterior mass

Prove that a dominant branch of \(q''_{\can}\) has exterior coefficients \(C_S\) with common phase on suitable subpackets. Then multiplicative collisions do not cancel.

Risk: full canonical plus/dual/completion may destroy dominance.

## Route 2: labelled subpacket exterior minor

Extract a large-mass labelled subpacket with controlled product collisions and prove its exterior determinant contributes to the full \(\mathcal H_{23}\) or a compatible localized frame.

Risk: localized determinant may not imply scalar canonical O2/O3 for the full map.

## Route 3: block-exterior frame

Replace one \(R+2\) determinant by smaller block determinants to reduce product length and collision severity.

Risk: block nondegeneracy may not imply full singular-value lower bound without new cross-block frame assumptions.

---

# 12. Final status

The determinant Cauchy–Binet expansion exposed a deeper arithmetic obstruction:

\[
\boxed{
\text{exterior determinant mass must survive product collisions and near-collisions.}
}
\]

The analytic multiplier factor is now relatively well understood:

\[
\det H_S
=
(\prod m(x_i))\det V_S
[x_i](1/m).
\]

The key analytic condition is nontrivial divided-difference mass for \(1/m_{\can}\).

The harder arithmetic condition is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{collision/near-collision noncancellation of the exterior coefficients }C_S.
}
\]

The most promising next attack is the **phase-aligned branch route**:

show that on a suitable ordinary subpacket, the dominant canonical branch has exterior coefficients with controlled phase, so collision classes add rather than cancel. If that works, the determinant theorem becomes much closer to an actual lower-sampling theorem.

## Agent 4 report — exterior determinant collision / phase alignment

I read the update carefully. The main change for my work is that the determinant route is now subordinate to corrected Stack C2: it is useful only insofar as it feeds the **wall-inclusive aggregate exterior theorem** for the actual canonical source, while the downstream contradiction must be through a forbidden channel \(\Lambda\). So the determinant analysis should not be treated as a standalone O2/O3 proof; it is an upstream component of

\[
\mathrm{JointAgg}_{1,23,\mathrm{forb}}.
\]

The current Agent 4 task is to attack multiplicative collision and phase alignment in the Cauchy–Binet determinant expansion. 

## Verdict

\[
\boxed{
\text{Phase alignment can plausibly remove exact collision cancellation on suitable single-branch subpackets.}
}
\]

But:

\[
\boxed{
\text{It does not by itself prove the full exterior determinant lower-sampling theorem.}
}
\]

The best route is a **prime-labelled, phase-aligned, single-branch exterior subpacket**. This is the most compatible with the scalar canonical downstream because it remains a substructure of the actual canonical determinant, not an arbitrary source-family object.

---

# 1. Cauchy–Binet coefficient structure

For the model prime-side augmented matrix,

\[
\det A_m
=
\sum_{|S|=N} C_S e^{-im\log N_S},
\qquad
N=R+2,
\qquad
N_S=\prod_{n\in S}n.
\]

The coefficient is

\[
C_S=
\left(\prod_{n\in S}a_n\right)
(\det V_S)^2
\left(\prod_{n\in S}m(x_n)\right)
[x_n:n\in S]\left(\frac1m\right).
\]

Here:

\[
(\det V_S)^2\ge0
\]

for real ordered \(x_n\), so it does not create phase variation.

Thus the phase of \(C_S\) comes from:

\[
\prod a_n,
\qquad
\prod m(x_n),
\qquad
[x_n](1/m).
\]

The analytic O3 content is exactly the divided difference

\[
[x_n:n\in S](1/m_{\can}).
\]

---

# 2. Phase-aligned branch route

The phase-aligned route asks for an ordinary subpacket \(I\) where:

1. the dominant branch coefficients \(a_n\) have controlled phase;
2. \(m_{\can}(x)\) has controlled phase;
3. the divided difference \([x_n](1/m_{\can})\) has controlled phase;
4. the full determinant terms selected from this subpacket survive exact and near-collision grouping.

If all \(C_S\) lie in a fixed sector of width \(<\pi/2\), then exact collision classes cannot cancel. Indeed, if

\[
C_S=e^{i\theta_S}|C_S|,
\qquad
|\theta_S-\theta_0|\le\vartheta<\pi/2,
\]

then for every exact collision class \(\mathcal C_N=\{S:N_S=N\}\),

\[
\left|
\sum_{S\in\mathcal C_N}C_S
\right|
\ge
\cos\vartheta
\sum_{S\in\mathcal C_N}|C_S|.
\]

Therefore

\[
\sum_N
\left|
\sum_{S:N_S=N}C_S
\right|^2
\ge
\cos^2\vartheta
\sum_N
\left(
\sum_{S:N_S=N}|C_S|
\right)^2
\ge
\cos^2\vartheta
\sum_S |C_S|^2.
\]

So exact multiplicative collision noncancellation follows immediately from phase alignment.

This is a real gain.

---

# 3. Divided-difference phase control

Use the Hermite–Genocchi formula:

\[
[x_1,\ldots,x_N]f
=
\int_{\Delta_{N-1}}
f^{(N-1)}
\left(
\sum_{i=1}^N t_ix_i
\right)
d\sigma(t)
\]

up to the standard normalization.

Thus if

\[
f=(1/m_{\can})
\]

satisfies

\[
\arg f^{(N-1)}(x)
\in[\theta_0-\vartheta,\theta_0+\vartheta]
\]

on the convex hull of the chosen nodes, then

\[
[x_1,\ldots,x_N]f
\]

has the same sectorial phase.

So the analytic phase condition is:

\[
\boxed{
\arg \left(\frac{d^{R+1}}{dx^{R+1}}\frac1{m_{\can}(x)}\right)
\text{ is sectorial on the selected subpacket.}
}
\]

The analytic mass condition is:

\[
\boxed{
\left|
\left(\frac{d^{R+1}}{dx^{R+1}}\frac1{m_{\can}(x)}\right)
\right|
\text{ is not too small on that subpacket.}
}
\]

If both hold, then the divided differences have controlled phase and polynomial mass after Riesz normalization.

This is substantially sharper than the earlier “\(m\) non-scalar” condition.

---

# 4. Can the ordinary interval be partitioned into good phase subpackets?

This depends on the analytic structure of \(m_{\can}\).

For a general analytic multiplier, the high derivative

\[
(1/m_{\can})^{(R+1)}
\]

may have zeros or rapid phase variation. So no unconditional sector partition is automatic.

A sufficient analytic theorem would be:

There is a partition of the ordinary \(x\)-interval into

\[
Q^{o(1)}
\]

subintervals \(I_\nu\), excluding at most \(Q^{-A}\) canonical exterior mass, such that on each \(I_\nu\),

\[
\arg (1/m_{\can})^{(R+1)}
\]

varies by \(<\pi/8\), and

\[
|(1/m_{\can})^{(R+1)}|
\]

has a lower bound compatible with \(e^{-O(R\log R)}\).

This is plausible if \(m_{\can}\) has a simple explicit symbol with no high-order degeneracy, but it must be audited. It is an analytic-symbol theorem, not a zeta-arithmetic theorem.

The subintervals must still support at least

\[
N=R+2
\]

well-separated nodes. Since \(R\sim \log Q/\log\log Q\), any subinterval of fixed or even moderately shrinking \(x\)-width contains enough primes/integers in the original \(n\)-scale.

So phase partitioning is not dimensionally impossible.

---

# 5. Prime-labelled subpacket: collision-safe skeleton

The strongest collision control comes from selecting primes in a narrow ordinary log-frequency subpacket.

Take a subpacket of primes

\[
p\in\mathcal P_I
\]

with

\[
x_p=\lambda\log p\in I.
\]

For exterior subsets \(S\subset\mathcal P_I\), unique factorization gives

\[
\prod_{p\in S}p=\prod_{p\in S'}p
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
S=S'.
\]

Moreover, if the full integer packet is restricted to a narrow log interval around \(\log p\asymp Y\), then a product of \(N\) such primes cannot be represented as a product of \(N\) other packet integers unless those integers are the same primes. Reason: every divisor of the selected product is a product of selected primes; a product of two selected primes has log-size about \(2Y\), outside the one-particle packet near \(Y\). Hence each one-particle factor must be a single selected prime.

Thus prime exterior frequencies are collision-free even inside the full determinant expansion, provided the one-particle packet is sufficiently narrow relative to \(Y\).

This is better than the generic large-prime-label scheme.

---

# 6. Mass of prime subpackets

The weighted AFE mass over primes is polynomially smaller than total integer mass, not exponentially smaller.

For smooth \(n^{-1/2}\)-normalized packets, the relevant square mass is roughly weighted by \(1/n\). On a log interval of width \(\Delta\),

\[
\sum_{n\in \text{interval}}\frac1n
\asymp \Delta,
\]

while

\[
\sum_{p\in \text{interval}}\frac1p
\asymp \frac{\Delta}{Q}.
\]

So the prime subpacket carries a factor about

\[
Q^{-1}
\]

of mass. This is acceptable in the polynomial bookkeeping.

For exterior subsets of size \(N=R+2\), prime restriction costs roughly

\[
Q^{-N}.
\]

Since

\[
N\sim \frac{\log Q}{\log\log Q},
\]

this is

\[
\exp(-N\log Q)
=
\exp\!\left(-\frac{(\log Q)^2}{\log\log Q}\right),
\]

which is **super-polynomial** if interpreted naively.

However, after Riesz/exterior normalization, the determinant mass should not be compared to all integer exterior subsets by raw counting. It should be compared to the normalized exterior mass of the chosen collision-safe subpacket. The loss is acceptable only if the local frame theorem is allowed to use the prime-labelled subpacket as the actual certificate.

This is the key compatibility issue.

If the final O2/O3 local condition requires the full augmented matrix, a prime subdeterminant alone may not be enough.

---

# 7. Does a prime subdeterminant certify the full augmented matrix?

Not automatically.

Let

\[
A=A_{\mathrm{prime}}+A_{\mathrm{rest}}.
\]

A large determinant of \(A_{\mathrm{prime}}\) does not imply a large determinant or singular value of \(A\), since \(A_{\mathrm{rest}}\) can cancel.

So the prime-labelled route requires one of the following:

## Route P1: sector alignment for the full determinant

If the full ordinary branch, not only primes, has sector-aligned \(C_S\), then prime terms are only a collision-free subset of a larger aligned positive sum. Then the full determinant coefficient mass is at least the prime exterior mass.

This is the best route.

## Route P2: projected exterior certificate

Define the O2/O3 augmented map with a prime-projected moment functional. This is collision-safe, but it may no longer certify the full scalar canonical \(q''_{\can}\) source accepted by downstream.

This risks becoming a source-family architecture, which the current stack avoids.

## Route P3: perturbative rest

Show

\[
A_{\mathrm{rest}}
\]

is small compared with

\[
A_{\mathrm{prime}}.
\]

This is false in general because primes carry only polynomial or smaller fraction of mass, not dominant mass.

Therefore, the only scalar-compatible prime route is P1:

\[
\boxed{
\text{phase alignment for the full dominant branch, with primes as a collision-safe mass witness.}
}
\]

---

# 8. Near-collisions

Exact collision control is not enough. Zero/routed-center sampling has finite resolution, so products with

\[
|\log(N_S/N_{S'})|
\lesssim H^{-1}
\]

belong to the same near-collision cluster.

If coefficients in each near-cluster are phase-aligned, then near-cluster sums also do not cancel. For a smooth positive sampling kernel, near-cluster cross terms are favorable or at least not destructive.

Thus phase alignment handles both exact and near-collision cancellation **provided** the sampling kernel is nonnegative or sector-controlled on near-cluster scales.

This creates a new analytic sampling condition:

\[
\boxed{
\text{the zero/routed-center kernel is nonnegative or sectorial on the near-collision scale.}
}
\]

For raw zero sums this is not automatic. For smoothed windows and actual zero-Gram laws, it may follow only after the explicit-formula kernel is understood.

So near-collisions are controlled by the same phase alignment only if the sampling kernel itself does not introduce adverse phase.

---

# 9. Labelled subpackets beyond primes

Large-prime labels or squarefree-kernel labels can reduce collision multiplicity, but they do not fully solve cancellation unless phases are controlled.

Bounded collision multiplicity alone is insufficient: complex coefficients in a collision class can cancel exactly.

So labelled subpackets are useful only as an auxiliary to phase alignment.

Among label choices, primes are best because:

1. collision-free by unique factorization;
2. compatible with sector phase if the prime-side branch is positive;
3. polynomially present in smooth packets at the one-particle level.

But prime exterior mass may be too small unless the determinant certificate can use it without comparing against all integer exterior mass.

---

# 10. Block-exterior decomposition

A block-exterior decomposition could reduce product length.

Split the augmented space

\[
\mathbb C\cdot1+m\mathbb C_R[x]
\]

into smaller blocks and use determinants of dimension \(L\ll R\).

This lowers product length from

\[
e^{RQ}
\]

to

\[
e^{LQ}.
\]

If \(L\) is fixed or slowly growing, sampling becomes more feasible.

But block determinants do not imply full augmented singular-value lower bounds unless we also prove cross-block angle/nonorthogonality conditions. Those conditions are essentially another form of O2/O3 frame control.

So block-exterior helps only if there is a natural orthogonal polynomial decomposition in which blocks are almost orthogonal under the zero-conditioned moment geometry.

At present, this is less compatible with the scalar canonical stack than the phase-aligned branch route.

---

# 11. Best theorem from this attack

The most compatible theorem is:

\[
\boxed{
\textbf{Phase-aligned dominant-branch exterior mass theorem.}
}
\]

A precise version:

On a branch-complete ordinary subpacket \(I\), suppose:

1. the dominant canonical branch coefficients \(a_n\) lie in a sector of width \(<\vartheta\);
2. \(m_{\can}(x)\) lies in a sector of width \(<\vartheta\);
3. \((1/m_{\can})^{(R+1)}\) lies in a sector of width \(<\vartheta\) and has \(e^{-O(R\log R)}\)-scale lower mass on \(I\);
4. the one-particle packet is narrow enough that prime exterior subsets are collision-free inside the full packet;
5. the zero/routed-center sampling kernel is sectorial/nonnegative on near-collision clusters.

Then the exterior determinant coefficient mass satisfies

\[
\sum_{\text{near clusters }\mathcal C}
\left|
\sum_{S\in\mathcal C}C_S
\right|^2
\ge
Q^{-A}
\sum_{\substack{S\text{ in dominant branch}\\ S\text{ separated}}}
|C_S|^2.
\]

This gives collision-safe determinant mass.

It still must be combined with a zero-sampling lower bound for the clustered determinant polynomial.

---

# 12. Final classification of the three routes

## A. Phase-aligned single-branch exterior mass

\[
\boxed{\text{Most compatible with scalar canonical downstream.}}
\]

It keeps the determinant inside the actual canonical object and may neutralize collision cancellation. This is the best route.

## B. Labelled collision-safe subpacket

\[
\boxed{\text{Useful as a witness, but insufficient alone.}}
\]

Prime-labelled subsets are collision-free, but unless the full determinant is phase-aligned, a projected prime determinant does not certify the full augmented map.

## C. Block-exterior decomposition

\[
\boxed{\text{Potentially useful later, but introduces new cross-block frame problems.}}
\]

It lowers product length but risks rebuilding O2/O3 in another form.

---

# Final answer

Phase alignment can plausibly eliminate product collision cancellation **only** in a dominant branch/subpacket regime where the coefficient phases, multiplier phases, and divided-difference phases are sector-controlled.

The best next mathematical target is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{prove sectorial phase and divided-difference mass for a dominant canonical branch}
}
\]

and then use prime-labelled subsets as collision-free witnesses inside the full determinant.

This does not yet prove \(\mathrm{JointAgg}_{1,23,\mathrm{forb}}\). It reduces one major obstruction—multiplicative collision cancellation—to two concrete theorems:

\[
\boxed{
\text{sectorial divided-difference theorem for }1/m_{\can},
}
\]

and

\[
\boxed{
\text{wall-weighted zero-sampling lower bound for the resulting clustered exterior determinant.}
}
\]

## Assistant (2026-04-27 20:00:35)

## Agent 4 report — phase-aligned determinant route and scalar compatibility

I incorporated the update: the determinant route is now only useful if it feeds the **weighted O2/O3 anti-trough** inside the \(\Lambda\)-dependent Stack C2 measure, and it must remain a certificate for the **full scalar canonical** augmented map, not a projected/source-family substitute. 

## Verdict

\[
\boxed{
\text{The phase-aligned determinant route is viable only as a subpacket lower-mass theorem for the full determinant.}
}
\]

A projected prime/label determinant is **not** enough unless it is shown to contribute sectorially to the full determinant. The correct target is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{dominant-branch sectorial exterior mass inside the actual full } \det A_m.
}
\]

The key analytic theorem is a sectorial divided-difference theorem for

\[
f=\frac1{m_{\can}}.
\]

The key arithmetic theorem is collision and near-collision noncancellation for the corresponding exterior coefficients.

---

# 1. Sectorial divided-difference lemma

Let

\[
N=R+2,
\qquad
f(x)=\frac1{m_{\can}(x)}.
\]

By the Hermite–Genocchi formula,

\[
[x_1,\ldots,x_N]f
=
\int_{\Delta_{N-1}}
f^{(N-1)}
\left(\sum_{i=1}^N t_ix_i\right)
\,d\sigma(t),
\]

up to the standard normalization.

Suppose that on an interval \(I\),

\[
f^{(N-1)}(x)
\]

lies in a sector

\[
\left\{
re^{i\theta}: r\ge0,\ |\theta-\theta_0|\le\vartheta
\right\},
\qquad
\vartheta<\frac{\pi}{4}.
\]

Then for every

\[
x_1,\ldots,x_N\in I,
\]

the divided difference

\[
[x_1,\ldots,x_N]f
\]

lies in the same sector. Moreover,

\[
\left|[x_1,\ldots,x_N]f\right|
\ge
\cos\vartheta
\int_{\Delta_{N-1}}
\left|
f^{(N-1)}
\left(\sum_i t_ix_i\right)
\right|
\,d\sigma(t).
\]

So sectoriality of \(f^{(R+1)}\) gives both phase control and a lower-mass mechanism for divided differences.

The remaining analytic requirement is that the integral average of

\[
|f^{(R+1)}|
\]

does not decay beyond the allowed scale. A natural target is

\[
\int_{\Delta_{N-1}}
\left|
f^{(N-1)}
\left(\sum_i t_ix_i\right)
\right|
\,d\sigma(t)
\ge e^{-O(R\log R)}
\]

after the chosen Riesz/exterior normalization. Since

\[
R\asymp \frac{\log Q}{\log\log Q},
\]

the loss

\[
e^{-O(R\log R)}
\]

is only polynomial in \(Q\).

Thus the analytic part can plausibly be made polynomial if \(m_{\can}\) has no high-order reciprocal degeneracy on the selected subpacket.

---

# 2. Sectoriality of the full exterior coefficient

For one dominant branch, the Cauchy–Binet coefficient is

\[
C_S=
\left(\prod_{n\in S}a_n\right)
(\det V_S)^2
\left(\prod_{n\in S}m(x_n)\right)
[x_n:n\in S]\left(\frac1m\right).
\]

The factor

\[
(\det V_S)^2
\]

is nonnegative real. Therefore \(C_S\) is sectorial if the following three factors are sectorial:

\[
\prod_{n\in S}a_n,
\qquad
\prod_{n\in S}m(x_n),
\qquad
[x_n:n\in S](1/m).
\]

A sufficient set of phase hypotheses on a subpacket \(I\) is:

\[
\arg a_n\in[\alpha_0-\vartheta_a,\alpha_0+\vartheta_a],
\]

\[
\arg m(x)\in[\mu_0-\vartheta_m,\mu_0+\vartheta_m],
\]

\[
\arg (1/m)^{(R+1)}(x)
\in[\delta_0-\vartheta_d,\delta_0+\vartheta_d],
\]

with

\[
N\vartheta_a+N\vartheta_m+\vartheta_d<\frac{\pi}{2}-\epsilon.
\]

Because products over \(N=R+2\) terms amplify phase variation, the one-particle phases must vary by

\[
O(1/R)
\]

unless the phase variation is removed by a normalization/gauge.

So the practical theorem should not ask raw \(a_n\) and \(m(x_n)\) to be sectorial at fixed angular width. It should first gauge out the smooth phase:

\[
a_n=e^{i\phi_a(x_n)}\tilde a_n,
\qquad
m(x_n)=e^{i\phi_m(x_n)}\tilde m(x_n),
\]

and include the smooth phase in the oscillatory frequency or in the branch definition. After gauge normalization, one needs

\[
\arg \tilde a_n=O(1/R),
\qquad
\arg \tilde m(x_n)=O(1/R)
\]

on the subpacket.

This is the first important caveat: **sector alignment is plausible only after branch/gauge normalization and subpacket refinement.**

---

# 3. Number and size of phase subpackets

To keep product phases sectorial, subpacket widths must make the normalized phase variation \(O(1/R)\).

If the relevant phase derivatives in \(x\) are \(O(1)\), then width

\[
|I|\asymp R^{-1}
\]

suffices. The ordinary interval has \(O(1)\) width in \(x=\lambda\log n\), so it can be partitioned into

\[
O(R)=Q^{o(1)}
\]

subpackets.

This is acceptable.

Each subpacket still contains many integers/primes because in \(n\)-scale the logarithmic width is

\[
\Delta\log n\asymp \frac{Q}{R}
\asymp
\frac{Q\log\log Q}{\log Q},
\]

which is large.

So the subpacket phase partition is dimensionally harmless.

---

# 4. Exact collision noncancellation

If every \(C_S\) in a collision class lies in a common sector of width \(<\pi/2\), then exact collisions cannot cancel.

Let

\[
C_S=e^{i\theta_S}|C_S|,
\qquad
|\theta_S-\theta_0|\le \vartheta<\pi/2.
\]

For each exact product class

\[
\mathcal C_N=\{S:N_S=N\},
\]

we have

\[
\left|
\sum_{S\in \mathcal C_N}C_S
\right|
\ge
\cos\vartheta
\sum_{S\in\mathcal C_N}|C_S|.
\]

Thus

\[
\sum_N
\left|
\sum_{S:N_S=N}C_S
\right|^2
\ge
\cos^2\vartheta
\sum_N
\left(\sum_{S:N_S=N}|C_S|\right)^2
\ge
\cos^2\vartheta
\sum_S|C_S|^2.
\]

So sectoriality solves exact multiplicative collision cancellation completely.

This is the strongest positive result of this route.

---

# 5. Near-collision clusters

Near-collisions are defined by

\[
|\log(N_S/N_{S'})|\le H^{-1}Q^B
\]

or the corresponding resolution of the smoothed routed-center kernel.

If the sampling kernel is nonnegative on these near-collision scales, then sectorial coefficient sums again do not cancel. More precisely, if the kernel

\[
K(\log(N_S/N_{S'}))
\]

has nonnegative real part for near-collisions and decays outside, then the cluster contribution is positive up to controlled error.

The needed theorem is therefore twofold:

1. coefficient sectoriality inside each near-cluster;
2. sampling-kernel sectoriality/nonnegativity on the near-cluster scale.

For smoothed height windows, the kernel can be arranged nonnegative if one uses a positive-definite weight, e.g. a square weight whose Fourier transform produces a nonnegative autocorrelation kernel. For zero sums, however, the explicit-formula correction can introduce signed prime terms. So this must be checked in the actual zero-Gram law.

Thus near-collision control is not purely coefficient-side; it also depends on the smoothed zero-sampling kernel.

---

# 6. Prime-labelled witness

Prime exterior subsets are collision-free:

\[
\prod_{p\in S}p=\prod_{p\in S'}p
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
S=S'.
\]

They are also near-collision safer if the prime products are well separated at the sampling scale. Exact uniqueness is automatic, but near separation may still be as small as

\[
e^{-O(RQ)}
\]

for products of \(R\) primes, which is below the \(e^{-Q}\) scale. So primes solve exact collisions, not all near-collisions.

Can prime exterior subsets witness determinant mass?

Only if the full determinant is sector-aligned. Otherwise, a prime-projected determinant

\[
\det A_m^{\mathrm{prime}}
\]

does not imply anything about

\[
\det A_m^{\mathrm{full}}.
\]

A large prime subdeterminant can be canceled by the rest of the full determinant.

Therefore the prime-labelled witness is valid only under:

\[
\boxed{
\text{full dominant-branch sectoriality.}
}
\]

Then prime terms provide a collision-free lower-mass subset, and all other same-sector terms can only add, not cancel.

Without full-sector alignment, a prime determinant becomes a projected/source-family certificate and is not compatible with the scalar canonical Stack C2.

---

# 7. Scalar compatibility

This is the main constraint from the updated architecture.

A determinant built from a projected subpacket, prime-only packet, or labelled packet certifies the full scalar canonical augmented map only if it is a **sectorial sub-sum of the full determinant**.

Otherwise it certifies a different projected map:

\[
A_m^{\mathrm{proj}},
\]

not the actual

\[
A_m
\]

entering \(\mathcal H_{23}\). That would drift into a source-family architecture, which the current stack rejects.

So scalar compatibility requires:

\[
\boxed{
\det A_m^{\mathrm{full}}
=
D_{\mathrm{dom}}(m)+D_{\mathrm{rest}}(m),
}
\]

with

\[
D_{\mathrm{dom}}
\]

sectorial and

\[
D_{\mathrm{rest}}
\]

either sector-compatible or perturbatively smaller after wall/Gate–1 weighting.

This is stronger than merely finding a collision-free subdeterminant.

---

# 8. Block-exterior decomposition

Block-exterior decomposition reduces product length by replacing one determinant of dimension

\[
R+2
\]

with several smaller determinants. But blockwise determinants do not imply

\[
\sigma_{\min}(A_m)\ge Q^{-A}
\]

unless cross-block angles are controlled.

In other words, block exterior reintroduces an O2/O3 frame problem at the block level.

It may become useful later, but it is not the cleanest scalar-compatible route now.

Classification:

\[
\boxed{
\text{block-exterior is lower priority than phase-aligned dominant branch.}
}
\]

---

# 9. Clean theorem: PhaseAlignedDominantBranchExteriorMass

The most useful theorem I can now state is:

Let \(I\) be an ordinary subpacket after branch/gauge normalization. Let \(\mathfrak S_I\) denote exterior subsets \(S\) of size \(N=R+2\) drawn from this subpacket. Suppose:

1. **Branch dominance:** the full determinant’s contribution from \(\mathfrak S_I\) is not canceled by other branches/rest terms; quantitatively, the full determinant coefficient clusters contain the \(\mathfrak S_I\)-contribution as a sectorial dominant component.

2. **Coefficient phase alignment:** for all \(S\in\mathfrak S_I\),

\[
C_S
\]

lies in a common sector of width \(<\pi/2-\epsilon\).

3. **Divided-difference mass:** for \(f=1/m_{\can}\),

\[
|[x_n:n\in S]f|
\]

has \(e^{-O(R\log R)}\)-scale lower mass on a polynomial fraction of separated \(S\).

4. **Near-cluster kernel positivity:** the smoothed zero/routed-center kernel is sectorial/nonnegative on near-collision clusters.

Then the clustered exterior coefficient mass satisfies

\[
\sum_{\mathcal C}
\left|
\sum_{S\in\mathcal C}C_S
\right|^2
\ge
Q^{-A}
\sum_{S\in\mathfrak S_I}|C_S|^2.
\]

This yields collision-safe exterior determinant mass for the full scalar canonical determinant.

---

# 10. Remaining zero-sampling theorem

Even after collision-safe coefficient mass, we still need the sampled determinant lower bound:

\[
\sum_m
\rho(m)
|T_{\can}X_{\can}(m)|^2
E_{\mathrm{wall}}^{\mathrm{forb}}(m)
|\det A_m|^2
\ge
Q^{-A}
\sum_m\rho(m)|X_{\can}(m)|^2.
\]

The phase-aligned theorem helps by preventing coefficient mass collapse. It does not by itself prove this zero-sampling lower bound.

Thus the determinant route now splits into:

\[
\boxed{
\text{sectorial coefficient/collision theorem}
}
\]

plus

\[
\boxed{
\text{wall-weighted zero-sampling lower theorem for the determinant polynomial.}
}
\]

---

# Final classification

Among the proposed routes:

## A. Phase-aligned single-branch exterior mass

\[
\boxed{\text{Best route.}}
\]

It is scalar-compatible if and only if it controls a sectorial component of the full determinant.

## B. Labelled collision-safe subpacket

\[
\boxed{\text{Useful only as a witness under full-sector dominance.}}
\]

Prime labels solve exact collisions but do not certify the full augmented map by themselves.

## C. Block-exterior decomposition

\[
\boxed{\text{Not currently preferred.}}
\]

It reduces length but introduces new cross-block frame conditions.

## Final answer

The phase-aligned route can plausibly eliminate product-collision cancellation, but only under a strong dominant-branch sector theorem:

\[
\boxed{
\text{after branch/gauge normalization, the exterior coefficients of a dominant full-determinant component are sectorial.}
}
\]

The next concrete audit is analytic:

\[
\boxed{
\text{prove sectorial divided-difference mass for }(1/m_{\can})^{(R+1)}
}
\]

on \(O(R)\) ordinary subpackets, and then arithmetic:

\[
\boxed{
\text{prove wall-weighted zero-sampling lower mass for the sectorial exterior determinant.}
}
\]
