## Agent 4 report — O2/O3 after the “model-space distance” synthesis

I read the update carefully. For Agent 4, the relevant change is that O2/O3 is now one instance of a broader pattern: each hard step is a distance-from-structured-model-space theorem. For O2/O3, the model space is the finite curvature span; for D4, it is the tangent space of the actual EF singular manifold; for Gate–1, it is the complement of observed \(L^2\) branch windows. 

My task is therefore to sharpen the O2/O3 block so it fits this model-space paradigm and interacts correctly with the downstream normality picture.

---

# 1. O2/O3 as a distance-to-model theorem

The local augmented space is

\[
\mathcal V
=
\mathbb C\cdot 1
+
m_{\can}\mathbb C_R[x].
\]

After factoring the curvature multiplier,

\[
\mathcal V
=
m_{\can}\left(\mathbb C\cdot f+\mathbb C_R[x]\right),
\qquad
f=\frac1{m_{\can}}.
\]

In a Riesz-normalized branch model,

\[
\mathcal H_{23}
\asymp
\frac{
\operatorname{dist}(f,\mathbb C_R[x])^2
}{
1+\|P_Rf\|^2+R\operatorname{dist}(f,\mathbb C_R[x])^2
}.
\]

So the pure-branch O2/O3 condition is:

\[
\boxed{
\operatorname{dist}\left(1/m_{\can},\mathbb C_R[x]\right)\ge Q^{-A}.
}
\]

If

\[
m_{\can,+}(x)=\kappa x^2(1+\varepsilon(x)),
\]

then

\[
\frac1{m_{\can,+}(x)}
=
\frac1{\kappa x^2}+O(\varepsilon),
\]

and \(x^{-2}\) is not polynomial. With the high-order reciprocal perturbation bound already identified, the pure branch O2/O3 block is analytically plausible.

Thus the real O2/O3 obstruction is no longer the pure local graph block. It is the passage from pure branch to full canonical source.

---

# 2. Full O2/O3 is a branch-charge survival problem

The full determinant decomposes by branch charge:

\[
D_{\full}
=
\sum_s D_s.
\]

Let \(D_{s_0}=D_{\dom}\) be the pure plus or other dominant branch component.

The scalar-compatible survival theorem is

\[
\boxed{
\operatorname{dist}_{L^2(d\nu)}
\left(
D_{s_0},
\operatorname{span}\{D_s:s\ne s_0\}
\right)^2
\ge
Q^{-A}\|D_{s_0}\|_{L^2(d\nu)}^2.
}
\]

This is now the exact O2/O3 analogue of the downstream normality condition:

\[
\text{pure branch determinant}
\notin
\text{span of other branch-charge components}.
\]

That is the right finite-dimensional model-space statement.

The Stack measure is

\[
d\nu
=
F_1F_{\wall}^{\forb}N\,d\rho.
\]

The problem is that this measure can itself contain branch-charge components. Therefore branch survival is not purely a property of \(D_{\full}\); it is a property of the pair

\[
(D_{\full}, d\nu).
\]

---

# 3. Degree-one toy model: AFE anti-lock as the prototype

The update notes that finite \(L^2\)-AFE anti-lock is the degree-one version of determinant branch survival. This is the right toy problem.

Let

\[
F(t)=B(t)+P(t)+D(t),
\]

where \(P\) is plus branch and \(D\) is folded-dual branch. The finite \(L^2\) anti-lock theorem is

\[
\|B+P+D\|_{L^2(W)}^2
\ge
Q^{-A}
\left(
\|B\|_{L^2(W)}^2+\|P\|_{L^2(W)}^2+\|D\|_{L^2(W)}^2
\right),
\]

or a weaker nonflatness version sufficient for \(F_X=B_2+P_{2,\le X}+D_{2,\le X}\).

This is exactly a branch Gram lower bound:

\[
\begin{pmatrix}
B\\P\\D
\end{pmatrix}
\notin
\text{near-null direction of the weighted branch Gram}.
\]

If this degree-one theorem fails, then the determinant-degree O2/O3 survival theorem is unlikely to be provable by the same methods.

So the next O2/O3 route should be hierarchical:

\[
\boxed{
\text{first prove degree-one branch-charge survival;}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{then lift to exterior degree }R+2.
}
\]

This is a useful simplification. It gives a testbed for fixed-\(\alpha\) branch-charge decay and \(\alpha\)-twisting.

---

# 4. Fixed-\(\alpha\) versus \(\alpha\)-averaged survival

There are now two clean alternatives.

## Fixed-\(\alpha\) branch-charge decay

One proves that for branch-charge components

\[
D_s,\quad D_{s'},
\]

and weight charge component \(W_\tau\),

\[
\int D_s\overline{D_{s'}}W_\tau\,d\rho
\]

is small unless

\[
s-s'+\tau=0.
\]

Then prove resonant terms do not cancel the dominant branch mass.

This is a high-degree weighted oscillatory theorem.

## \(\alpha\)-averaged branch projection

If

\[
D_{\full}(m,\alpha)=\sum_s e^{is\Theta(m,\alpha)}D_s(m,\alpha),
\]

and the architecture is \(\alpha\)-admissible, then \(\alpha\)-averaging projects out cross charges.

The clean theorem is

\[
\int_\alpha \int
|D_{\full}(m,\alpha)|^2
W(m,\alpha)\,d\rho(m)d\alpha
\ge
Q^{-A}
\int_\alpha \int
|D_{s_0}(m,\alpha)|^2
W_0(m,\alpha)\,d\rho(m)d\alpha.
\]

This is much more plausible than fixed-\(\alpha\) survival.

But the update’s warning is critical: \(\alpha\) also enlarges the actual-source tangent space downstream. If \(\partial_\alpha u\) contains the same direction as the D4 carrier, especially the \(Y12\) direction, then \(\alpha\)-admission may help O2/O3 but hurt D4 normality.

So O2/O3 cannot decide \(\alpha\)-admissibility alone.

---

# 5. Coupling with D4 normality

Downstream now asks whether

\[
T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]
\]

is tangent or normal to the actual EF singular manifold.

If \(\alpha\) is admitted as an actual source-family parameter, then

\[
\partial_\alpha u
\in T_u\mathcal M_{\sing}^{\arith}.
\]

Therefore we must test:

\[
T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]
\in
\operatorname{span}
\{\partial_\alpha u,\partial_m u,\partial_\lambda u,\ldots\}
?
\]

If yes, then the strongest D4 channel may become tangent and disappear.

This is a hidden coupling:

\[
\boxed{
\alpha\text{-twist helps O2/O3 branch survival}
}
\]

but may reduce

\[
\boxed{
\text{D4 normal rank.}
}
\]

So the correct combined theorem is not merely AlphaAdmissibility. It is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{AlphaAdmissibility + D4-normality preservation.}
}
\]

That is:

\[
\operatorname{dist}
\left(
T_{\tr\to\sing}E_{\car}L_{\clean}\mathbb C^2,
T_u\mathcal M_{\sing}^{\arith,\alpha}
\right)
\ge Q^{-A}.
\]

If this fails, \(\alpha\)-averaging is too expensive downstream.

---

# 6. O2/O3 and singular EF manifold use parallel graph structures

There is a useful analogy.

O2/O3 graph model:

\[
\mathbb C\cdot f+\mathbb C_R[x],
\qquad
f=1/m_{\can}.
\]

D4 singular manifold tangent model:

\[
T_u\mathcal M_{\sing}^{\arith}
=
\operatorname{span}
\{\partial_{\theta_j}F_X^\theta\}.
\]

Both are finite model spaces. The desired statement is a distance lower bound:

\[
f\notin \mathbb C_R[x],
\]

and

\[
D4\notin T_u\mathcal M_{\sing}^{\arith}.
\]

This suggests a shared technical strategy:

1. choose well-conditioned Riesz coordinates for the model space;
2. compute the residual of the target direction;
3. prove polynomial lower distance.

For O2/O3, the target residual is \(1/m_{\can}\) modulo polynomials.

For D4, the target residual is \([\mathsf C_{Y12}]\) or \([\mathsf C_7]\) modulo actual EF parameter derivatives.

So both reduce to finite Schur complement computations.

This is a good route: use the same numerical/symbolic finite linear algebra style for both.

---

# 7. New O2/O3 theorem target: branch-charge Gram with smooth weights

The final O2/O3 theorem should not use hard extracted sets. It should use a smooth branch-charge-controlled weight.

State:

Let

\[
W_{\mathrm{sm}}
\]

be a Stack-compatible smooth weight before hard extraction. Decompose:

\[
D_{\full}=\sum_s D_s,
\qquad
W_{\mathrm{sm}}=\sum_\tau W_\tau.
\]

Assume:

1. pure branch graph non-polynomiality:

\[
\|D_{s_0}\|_{L^2(W_0d\rho)}^2\ge Q^{-A}\mu;
\]

2. branch-charge off-diagonal decay:

\[
\left|
\int
D_s\overline{D_{s'}}W_\tau\,d\rho
\right|
\le
Q^{-A}
\|D_s\|\|D_{s'}\|
\]

unless \(s-s'+\tau=0\);

3. resonant terms have positive Schur complement.

Then full determinant survival follows.

This is now a precise fixed-\(\alpha\) target.

The \(\alpha\)-version replaces condition 2 with phase orthogonality.

---

# 8. Finite computation suggested by the model

For Agent 4’s next concrete finite audit, compute the branch charges of:

\[
E_1,
\quad
E_{\wall}^{\forb},
\quad
N,
\quad
D_s\overline{D_{s'}}.
\]

The important question is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Does }E_{\wall}^{\forb}
\text{ contain branch charges that can bridge }s_0\to s'\ ?
}
\]

If the forbidden wall factor is built from the singular-discard normal channel, it may be mostly branch-neutral. If so, fixed-\(\alpha\) survival becomes more plausible.

If \(E_{\wall}^{\forb}\) contains strong nonzero branch-charge components, then fixed-\(\alpha\) survival is hard and \(\alpha\)-averaging becomes more necessary.

So the finite charge audit is:

\[
\operatorname{charge}(E_{\wall}^{\forb})?
\]

This connects O2/O3 directly with the new downstream \(\Lambda\).

---

# 9. Recommended next Agent 4 direction

The best next attack is:

\[
\boxed{
\textbf{Branch-charge audit of the Stack weight.}
}
\]

For each component:

\[
N,\quad F_1,\quad E_{\wall}^{\forb},
\]

determine:

1. branch-charge decomposition;
2. whether charge-zero dominates;
3. whether nonzero charges are perturbative, oscillatory, or require \(\alpha\)-projection.

Then decide whether fixed-\(\alpha\) determinant survival is plausible.

This is more concrete than further abstract determinant discussion.

---

# Final status

O2/O3 now has a cleaner map:

\[
\boxed{
\text{pure branch graph non-polynomiality}
}
\]

plus

\[
\boxed{
\text{branch-charge survival under smooth Stack weights}
}
\]

plus

\[
\boxed{
\text{tail perturbation after finite-good extraction}.
}
\]

The main new cross-branch insight is:

\[
\boxed{
\alpha\text{-admission must be checked jointly with D4 normality, because it enlarges the actual-source tangent space.}
}
\]

So Agent 4’s current answer is:

- fixed-\(\alpha\) O2/O3 survival reduces to a branch-charge Gram theorem;
- \(\alpha\)-averaging is attractive but may cost downstream D4 normality;
- the next finite audit is the branch-charge content of \(E_{\wall}^{\forb}\) and the Stack weight.

## Agent 4 continuation — branch-charge audit of the Stack weight

I continued from the previous reduction: full finite O2/O3 survival is now a **branch-charge Gram problem**. The key question is whether the Stack weight

\[
W_{\mathrm{Stack}}
=
N\cdot F_1\cdot E_{\wall}^{\forb}
\]

is mostly branch-charge neutral, or whether it contains nonzero branch-charge components that can bridge the pure-branch determinant \(D_{s_0}\) to the other determinant branches \(D_s\). The update explicitly identifies this as the next finite audit. 

## Verdict

\[
\boxed{
\text{Fixed-\(\alpha\) determinant survival is plausible only if }E_{\wall}^{\forb}
\text{ is branch-neutral or charge-sparse.}
}
\]

If \(E_{\wall}^{\forb}\) contains substantial branch charges \(1,\ldots,2n\), where \(n=R+2\), it can bridge

\[
D_{s_0}\overline{D_s}
\]

and destroy branch-count orthogonality.

The good news is that the new singular-discard \(\Lambda\) makes branch neutrality more plausible than the old raw anisotropy wall, because it is naturally an \(L^2\)-type norm:

\[
E_{\wall}^{\forb}
=
\frac{
|H|^2
\|
\Lambda_{\tr,\sing}E_{\car}L_{\clean}\Gamma
\|^2
}{
\mathcal N_{\D4}
}.
\]

Squared norms tend to have dominant charge-zero parts. The danger is the cross terms inside \(\Gamma\) and \(H\).

---

# 1. Branch-charge bookkeeping

Assign branch charge:

\[
\operatorname{ch}(+)= -1,
\qquad
\operatorname{ch}(-)= +1,
\qquad
\operatorname{ch}(\mathrm{bg})=0.
\]

For a determinant branch component \(D_s\), \(s\) is its signed branch count.

The pure plus determinant has

\[
s_0=-n,
\qquad n=R+2.
\]

A cross term in

\[
|D_{\full}|^2 W_{\mathrm{Stack}}
\]

has branch charge

\[
s-s'+\tau,
\]

where \(\tau\) is the branch charge of a component \(W_\tau\) of the Stack weight.

To couple the pure plus component \(D_{s_0}\) to another branch \(D_s\), the weight must contain charge

\[
\tau=s-s_0.
\]

Since \(s>s_0\) for every non-pure-plus component, the dangerous charges are

\[
1,2,\ldots,2n.
\]

Thus fixed-\(\alpha\) branch survival requires one of:

\[
\boxed{
W_\tau\text{ is small for }1\le \tau\le 2n;
}
\]

or

\[
\boxed{
\text{oscillatory integration kills these charge-bridging terms;}
}
\]

or

\[
\boxed{
\text{the resonant bridged Gram still has positive Schur complement.}
}
\]

The first is the cleanest.

---

# 2. Charge content of \(N\)

The natural reference energy \(N\) is now local scalar \(L^2\) source energy:

\[
N(m)
\sim
\int_I |\lambda^3 q''_{\can}(m+\lambda x)|^2\,dx.
\]

Write

\[
q''_{\can}=u_+ + u_- + u_0,
\]

where \(u_+\) is plus branch, \(u_-\) is dual branch, and \(u_0\) is background/completion.

Then

\[
|u_+ + u_- + u_0|^2
\]

contains:

- charge \(0\): \(|u_+|^2, |u_-|^2, |u_0|^2\);
- charge \(-2\): \(u_+\overline{u_-}\);
- charge \(+2\): \(u_-\overline{u_+}\);
- charge \(-1\): \(u_+\overline{u_0}\), \(u_0\overline{u_-}\);
- charge \(+1\): \(u_-\overline{u_0}\), \(u_0\overline{u_+}\).

So \(N\) is not automatically charge-neutral.

However, \(N\) is local \(L^2\) energy integrated over a branch window. If the branch window has enough oscillatory width, the nonzero charge cross terms should average out. Thus \(N\) should be decomposed as:

\[
N=N_0+N_{\neq0},
\]

with theorem target:

\[
\|N_{\neq0}\|_{\mathrm{Stack}}\le Q^{-A}\|N_0\|.
\]

This is a **degree-one branch anti-lock / branch-charge decay theorem**.

Important: this is exactly the same kind of theorem needed for finite \(L^2\)-Gate–1 anti-lock. So one theorem may control both:

\[
\boxed{
\text{finite AFE \(L^2\) anti-lock}
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
\text{small nonzero-charge part of }N.
}
\]

This is a useful connection.

---

# 3. Charge content of \(F_1\)

If Gate–1 is now defined by local \(L^2\) energy, then \(F_1\) can often be avoided as a hard nonlinear cap during the branch-survival stage.

The correct order should be:

1. prove smooth aggregate \(L^2\)-Gate–1;
2. prove smooth weighted O2/O3 under the same controlled branch measure;
3. then do capped extraction.

If \(F_1\) is inserted before O2/O3, it becomes a nonlinear cap of an energy and can create high branch charges.

Thus:

\[
\boxed{
F_1\text{ should not appear in the measure used to prove branch survival.}
}
\]

Instead, use a smooth source-energy weight \(N_0\) or the branch observation measure. Hard \(F_1\) belongs after O2/O3 aggregate survival.

This is a correction to earlier Stack notation:

\[
d\nu=F_1F_{\wall}^{\forb}N\,d\rho
\]

is extraction-correct but branch-survival-hostile. For proving branch survival, replace it by a smooth pre-extraction measure:

\[
d\nu_{\mathrm{sm}}
=
N_{\mathrm{sm}}E_{\wall,\mathrm{sm}}^{\forb}\,d\rho
\]

or just \(N_{\mathrm{sm}}d\rho\), then insert wall by a separate sublevel theorem.

---

# 4. Charge content of \(E_{\wall}^{\forb}\)

The forbidden wall factor is

\[
E_{\wall}^{\forb}
=
\frac{
|H|^2
\|
K_{\D4}\Gamma
\|^2
}{
\mathcal N_{\D4}
},
\]

where

\[
K_{\D4}
=
\Lambda_{\tr,\sing}E_{\car}L_{\clean}.
\]

Expand the core:

\[
\|K_{\D4}\Gamma\|^2
=
\Gamma^*
K_{\D4}^*K_{\D4}
\Gamma.
\]

If

\[
\Gamma=(\Gamma_G,\Gamma_{G,2})
\]

are scalar canonical invariants built from \(u=q''_{\can}\), then they themselves decompose into branch charges.

The charge-neutral part is:

\[
|\Gamma_G^+|^2+|\Gamma_G^-|^2+\cdots
\]

and similarly for \(\Gamma_{G,2}\). Dangerous terms include:

\[
\Gamma_G^+\overline{\Gamma_G^-},
\qquad
\Gamma_{G,2}^+\overline{\Gamma_G^-},
\]

etc.

So \(E_{\wall}^{\forb}\) is not automatically neutral.

But there is a possible simplification: \(K_{\D4}\) is a singular-discard normal projection. It may annihilate the actual coherent plus/dual tangent pieces and see only a structural normal mode. If the normal mode is branch-neutral or carried by a single \(Y12\)-type component, then \(\|K_{\D4}\Gamma\|^2\) may be charge-sparse.

This creates a finite audit:

\[
\boxed{
\text{branch-charge decomposition of }
K_{\D4}\Gamma.
}
\]

If \(K_{\D4}\Gamma\) has only charge \(0\), or charges outside \(1,\ldots,2n\), fixed-\(\alpha\) survival is plausible.

If it has large charges \(1,\ldots,2n\), it can bridge the determinant pure branch to mixed branches.

---

# 5. The factor \(|H|^2\)

The D4 factor \(H\) is roughly a scalar local product involving \(q_0\) and \(u\), depending on normalization. Earlier D4 formulas involved terms such as

\[
H\sim q_0u
\quad\text{or}\quad
H_{\mathrm{gr}}\sim q_0u^3.
\]

Either way, \(|H|^2\) is again an energy-like squared quantity and thus has a dominant charge-zero component plus nonzero cross terms.

If \(H\) is low-degree in \(u_+,u_-\), then \(|H|^2\) has bounded branch-charge support independent of \(R\). That is good.

The determinant branch gap can be as large as \(1,\ldots,2n\). If \(E_{\wall}^{\forb}\) has only bounded charge support, then it can only couple pure plus to branch components with

\[
s-s_0=O(1).
\]

It cannot bridge all the way to most branches.

This leaves finitely many near-pure branch contaminants:

\[
D_{s_0+1},D_{s_0+2},\ldots,D_{s_0+C}.
\]

These are components with almost all plus factors and only \(O(1)\) non-plus insertions.

That is much easier than all mixed branches.

So a key result is:

\[
\boxed{
E_{\wall}^{\forb}\text{ has bounded branch-charge bandwidth.}
}
\]

If true, branch survival reduces to controlling only near-pure branch components.

---

# 6. Bounded charge bandwidth theorem

Let the total algebraic degree of \(E_{\wall}^{\forb}\) in branch pieces be

\[
d_{\wall}=O(1).
\]

Then

\[
E_{\wall}^{\forb}
\]

has branch charges in

\[
[-d_{\wall},d_{\wall}].
\]

Similarly, if \(N\) is used smoothly and has degree \(2\),

\[
N_\tau=0
\quad\text{for}\quad |\tau|>2.
\]

Therefore the total Stack smooth weight has branch-charge bandwidth

\[
|\tau|\le d_{\mathrm{Stack}}=O(1).
\]

This means the pure plus determinant \(D_{-n}\) can only resonate with components

\[
D_s
\]

where

\[
s+n\le d_{\mathrm{Stack}}.
\]

Thus only branch components with at most \(O(1)\) dual/background substitutions can threaten pure plus survival.

This is a major simplification.

The previous fear of charges \(1,\ldots,2n\) is real only if the Stack weight contains high-degree determinant-like factors. But if O2/O3 survival is proved before extraction and before determinant weights are inserted, the remaining weights have bounded algebraic degree.

Therefore:

\[
\boxed{
\text{prove O2/O3 survival under bounded-degree smooth weights, not under determinant-weighted or capped weights.}
}
\]

This preserves branch-charge separation.

---

# 7. Near-pure branch contaminants

The dangerous components are:

\[
D_{-n+k},
\qquad
1\le k\le d_{\mathrm{Stack}}.
\]

These contain \(n-k\) plus factors and \(k\) non-plus or background factors.

Can they cancel \(D_{-n}\)?

They differ by replacing \(k=O(1)\) plus columns by dual/background columns. Their exterior coefficient structure is lower-codimension relative to the pure plus determinant.

A plausible theorem is:

\[
\boxed{
\|D_{-n+k}\|_\nu^2
\le Q^{-A_k}\|D_{-n}\|_\nu^2
\quad\text{for }1\le k\le d_{\mathrm{Stack}},
}
\]

or at least their cross terms are small by one-particle branch separation.

This is much more plausible than controlling all mixed branches.

The frequency gap is \(kQ\), with \(k\ge1\). Under smooth weights of bounded branch charge, nonresonant terms should vanish; resonant terms only occur when the weight supplies charge \(k\). Since the weight charge \(k\) comes from \(O(1)\)-degree factors, those resonant terms may be explicitly computable and possibly sign-controlled.

Thus the fixed-\(\alpha\) route may be viable after all, if O2/O3 is proven at the smooth bounded-degree stage.

---

# 8. Corrected proof order for O2/O3

The corrected order should be:

## Step 1: finite smooth branch survival

Use a smooth, bounded-degree weight \(W_{\mathrm{bd}}\), built from branch \(L^2\) observation and forbidden wall in uncapped but low-degree form, not from hard caps.

Prove

\[
\int |D_{\full}|^2 W_{\mathrm{bd}}\,d\rho
\ge Q^{-A}
\int |D_{-n}|^2 W_{\mathrm{bd},0}\,d\rho.
\]

## Step 2: harmonic exterior lower bound

Use \(R^C\)-normalization:

\[
\mathcal H_{23}\ge Q^{-C}|\det A|^2.
\]

## Step 3: aggregate lower bound

Get

\[
\int \mathcal H_{23} W_{\mathrm{bd}}\,d\rho
\ge Q^{-A}.
\]

## Step 4: capped extraction

Only now apply caps to produce pointwise good centers.

This avoids rough-set Fourier contamination.

---

# 9. Interaction with singular-discard \(\Lambda\)

The forbidden wall factor built from singular-discard normal energy is local and quadratic in the D4 live variables. If used as a raw squared norm, it has bounded branch charge.

But if we cap it first,

\[
F_{\wall}=\min(E_{\wall}/\eta,1),
\]

the cap destroys branch-charge control.

Therefore:

\[
\boxed{
\text{for O2/O3 survival, use raw }E_{\wall}^{\forb};
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{for extraction, convert to capped }F_{\wall}^{\forb}\text{ afterward.}
}
\]

This is now the consistent architecture.

---

# 10. Final theorem target

The next O2/O3 theorem should be:

\[
\boxed{
\textbf{Bounded-charge branch survival theorem.}
}
\]

Let

\[
D_{\full}=\sum_s D_s,
\qquad
D_0=D_{-n}.
\]

Let \(W_{\mathrm{bd}}\) be a smooth Stack weight of bounded branch-charge bandwidth

\[
|\tau|\le d_0=O(1).
\]

Then prove:

\[
\int |D_{\full}|^2W_{\mathrm{bd}}\,d\rho
\ge
Q^{-A}
\int |D_{-n}|^2W_{\mathrm{bd},0}\,d\rho,
\]

provided the pure plus branch graph non-polynomiality and sectorial determinant mass hold.

The proof only needs to control finitely many near-pure branch interactions:

\[
D_{-n+k},\qquad 1\le k\le d_0.
\]

This is far more tractable than arbitrary branch survival.

---

# 11. Final status

The branch-charge audit reveals a concrete opportunity:

\[
\boxed{
\text{keep the O2/O3 aggregate weight bounded-degree and uncapped until after branch survival.}
}
\]

Then the Stack weight has bounded branch-charge bandwidth, and pure plus can only interact with \(O(1)\) near-pure branch sectors.

This gives a credible fixed-\(\alpha\) route, reducing dependence on \(\alpha\)-averaging.

The key finite audit is now:

\[
\boxed{
\text{branch-charge bandwidth of }E_{\wall}^{\forb}
\text{ and }N.
}
\]

If it is \(O(1)\), fixed-\(\alpha\) determinant survival becomes plausible. If it is \(O(R)\), \(\alpha\)-projection may be necessary.

## Agent 4 continuation — concrete branch-charge audit of \(E_{\wall}^{\forb}\)

I continued the fixed-\(\alpha\) O2/O3 survival route. The last conclusion was that fixed-\(\alpha\) determinant survival becomes plausible if the Stack weight used before extraction has **bounded branch-charge bandwidth**. The dangerous case is when the weight contains charges \(1,\ldots,2n\), \(n=R+2\), because then it can bridge the pure-plus determinant sector to all other branch sectors. 

The key object is now

\[
E_{\wall}^{\forb}(m)
=
\frac{
|H(m)|^2
\left\|
K_{\D4}(m)\Gamma(m)
\right\|^2
}{
\mathcal N_{\D4}(m)
},
\]

where

\[
K_{\D4}
=
\Lambda_{\tr,\sing}E_{\car}L_{\clean}.
\]

---

# 1. Main conclusion

\[
\boxed{
\text{Use the numerator of }E_{\wall}^{\forb}\text{ for branch survival, not the normalized ratio.}
}
\]

The numerator

\[
\mathcal W_{\forb}(m)
=
|H(m)|^2
\left\|
K_{\D4}(m)\Gamma(m)
\right\|^2
\]

has bounded algebraic degree in the branch pieces, hence bounded branch-charge bandwidth.

But the normalized factor

\[
\frac{\mathcal W_{\forb}}{\mathcal N_{\D4}}
\]

can acquire large or uncontrolled branch-charge bandwidth if \(\mathcal N_{\D4}^{-1}\) is expanded as a nonlinear reciprocal.

Therefore, for the O2/O3 branch-survival theorem, the safe procedure is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{dyadically freeze }\mathcal N_{\D4}\text{ first, then use the raw numerator.}
}
\]

That is,

\[
\mathcal N_{\D4}(m)\asymp N_j
\]

on a dyadic slice, and the survival weight becomes

\[
W_{\mathrm{bd}}(m)
=
N_j^{-1}
|H(m)|^2
\|K_{\D4}(m)\Gamma(m)\|^2
\cdot N_{\source}(m)
\]

or the corresponding source-energy weight.

This keeps branch-charge bandwidth bounded.

---

# 2. Branch-charge degree of the forbidden wall numerator

Assign branch charges:

\[
\operatorname{ch}(+)= -1,\qquad
\operatorname{ch}(-)=+1,\qquad
\operatorname{ch}(\mathrm{bg})=0.
\]

Suppose the canonical source decomposes as

\[
u=q''_{\can}=u_+ + u_- + u_0.
\]

Any polynomial expression of degree \(d\) in \(u\), its local primitives, and finitely many derivatives has branch-charge support inside

\[
[-d,d].
\]

The forbidden wall numerator is

\[
|H|^2\|K_{\D4}\Gamma\|^2.
\]

Assume \(K_{\D4}\) is a chart/carrier map depending only on local chart geometry and not on high-degree oscillatory products of the branch fields. Then \(K_{\D4}\) has charge \(0\).

Thus the charge support comes from \(H\) and \(\Gamma\).

If

\[
H
\]

has branch degree \(d_H\), and

\[
\Gamma=(\Gamma_G,\Gamma_{G,2})
\]

has branch degree \(d_\Gamma\), then

\[
|H|^2
\]

has branch-charge support in

\[
[-2d_H,2d_H],
\]

and

\[
\|K_{\D4}\Gamma\|^2
\]

has support in

\[
[-2d_\Gamma,2d_\Gamma].
\]

Therefore

\[
\boxed{
\operatorname{ch}
\left(
|H|^2\|K_{\D4}\Gamma\|^2
\right)
\subset
[-d_{\wall},d_{\wall}],
}
\]

where

\[
d_{\wall}=2d_H+2d_\Gamma=O(1).
\]

This is the bounded-charge theorem.

It is crucial that \(d_H,d_\Gamma\) are fixed structural degrees, not \(R\)-dependent.

---

# 3. Consequence for determinant branch survival

The pure plus determinant has charge

\[
s_0=-n,\qquad n=R+2.
\]

The full determinant decomposes as

\[
D_{\full}=\sum_sD_s.
\]

A cross term

\[
D_{s_0}\overline{D_s}W_\tau
\]

can be charge-resonant only if

\[
s_0-s+\tau=0,
\]

i.e.

\[
s-s_0=\tau.
\]

If

\[
|\tau|\le d_{\wall}+d_N,
\]

where \(d_N\) is the branch degree of the source-reference weight, then the pure plus determinant can only resonate with

\[
D_{s_0+k},
\qquad
1\le k\le d_0,
\]

where

\[
d_0=d_{\wall}+d_N=O(1).
\]

So the full branch survival problem collapses from controlling

\[
O(R)
\]

mixed branch sectors to controlling only

\[
O(1)
\]

near-pure sectors.

This is a major simplification.

The determinant survival theorem becomes:

\[
\boxed{
D_{-n}
\notin
\operatorname{span}
\{D_{-n+1},\ldots,D_{-n+d_0}\}
}
\]

under the bounded-degree Stack weight.

All farther branch sectors are separated by branch charge and should vanish under the smoothed branch-charge orthogonality theorem.

---

# 4. The denominator/cap danger

The bounded-charge result fails if we insert either of these too early:

## A. Capping

\[
F_{\wall}^{\forb}
=
\min(E_{\wall}^{\forb}/\eta,1).
\]

The cap is nonlinear and can be approximated only by high-degree polynomials if one needs sharp thresholds. That can create branch-charge bandwidth larger than \(O(1)\), possibly as large as \(O(\log Q)\) or worse.

## B. Reciprocal normalization

\[
\mathcal N_{\D4}^{-1}.
\]

Unless \(\mathcal N_{\D4}\) is frozen dyadically, its reciprocal is not a finite-degree branch polynomial.

Therefore the correct order is:

1. dyadically freeze normalizations;
2. prove smooth bounded-charge aggregate O2/O3;
3. prove raw forbidden-wall aggregate;
4. only then cap/extract.

The order matters.

---

# 5. Near-pure sectors: what must be shown?

The dangerous sectors are

\[
D_{-n+k},
\qquad
1\le k\le d_0.
\]

Each such component replaces \(k\) pure plus exterior factors by dual/background/completion factors.

Since \(k=O(1)\), these are finite-rank perturbative exterior sectors.

A plausible finite theorem is:

\[
\boxed{
\sum_{k=1}^{d_0}
\left|
\langle D_{-n},D_{-n+k}\rangle_{W_k}
\right|
\le
\frac12
\|D_{-n}\|_{W_0}^2
}
\]

for the relevant charge-\(k\) components \(W_k\) of the bounded-degree Stack weight.

This is no longer a massive determinant problem. It is a finite list of near-pure branch interaction estimates.

Each interaction has only \(O(1)\) non-plus substitutions, so it should be expressible as the pure plus exterior mass times a finite number of branch-ratio factors.

Thus the next attack is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{near-pure branch interaction estimate.}
}
\]

---

# 6. Structure of a near-pure interaction

A typical \(D_{-n+k}\) term has \(n-k\) plus exterior factors and \(k\) non-plus factors.

The cross term with \(D_{-n}\) has the schematic form:

\[
\sum_{S,S'}
C_S^{(+)}
\overline{
C_{S'}^{(n-k,+;\,k,\mathrm{other})}
}
\int
e^{-im(\Omega_S-\Omega_{S'})}
W_k(m)\,d\rho(m).
\]

The frequency gap is supplied by the \(k\) substituted branch factors. Since \(k=O(1)\), this is a finite branch-ratio problem, not an \(R\)-scale collision problem.

If the plus and dual packets are separated by the folded phase or by signed frequency, then these cross terms should decay under smoothed windows unless the bounded-degree weight exactly compensates them.

If compensation occurs, the term becomes a finite AFE anti-lock correlation. This resembles the degree-one Gate–1 anti-lock theorem.

So again:

\[
\boxed{
\text{finite \(L^2\)-AFE anti-lock is the degree-one prototype of branch survival.}
}
\]

---

# 7. Branch-charge survival theorem, bounded-weight version

The current best fixed-\(\alpha\) theorem is:

Let

\[
W_{\bd}=\sum_{|\tau|\le d_0}W_\tau
\]

be a smooth Stack weight of bounded branch charge. Then

\[
\int |D_{\full}|^2W_{\bd}\,d\rho
\ge
Q^{-A}
\int |D_{-n}|^2W_0\,d\rho
\]

provided:

1. pure plus exterior mass is non-small;
2. nonresonant charge terms decay under smoothed sampling;
3. the finite resonant near-pure Gram matrix

\[
\{D_{-n},D_{-n+1},\ldots,D_{-n+d_0}\}
\]

has polynomial lower Schur complement.

This is much sharper than the earlier all-branch survival theorem.

---

# 8. Does \(\alpha\)-twisting remain useful?

Yes, but now its role is reduced.

Without \(\alpha\), we must prove finitely many near-pure interaction estimates.

With \(\alpha\), branch-charge projection kills all nonzero charge interactions automatically, including the near-pure ones, assuming \(\alpha\)-admissibility.

So \(\alpha\)-twisting is still a powerful shortcut, but it is no longer the only plausible route.

The fixed-\(\alpha\) route is now credible if the bounded-charge theorem and finite near-pure Gram lower bound can be proved.

This reduces dependence on \(\alpha\), which is good because \(\alpha\)-admission may enlarge the downstream tangent space and threaten D4 normality.

---

# 9. Interaction with singular-discard \(\Lambda\)

The forbidden wall factor

\[
\|K_{\D4}\Gamma\|^2
\]

has bounded branch degree only if \(K_{\D4}\) is independent of high-frequency branch data.

This is true if

\[
K_{\D4}
=
(I-P_{\sing})T_{\tr\to\sing}E_{\car}L_{\clean}
\]

is a finite chart/carrier map depending on the local chart but not on \(u_+,u_-\) in a nonlinear high-degree way.

If \(P_{\sing}\) is itself fitted nonlinearly to the actual source row, then \(K_{\D4}\) may inherit high branch complexity.

Therefore:

\[
\boxed{
P_{\sing}\text{ must be defined from a fixed EF-coherence model, not adaptively fitted pointwise to }q''_{\can}.
}
\]

Otherwise the bounded-charge audit fails.

This is an important constraint on \(R_{\sing}\).

---

# 10. Final theorem stack after this continuation

The O2/O3 fixed-\(\alpha\) route now requires:

## T1. Bounded-charge Stack weight

Before capping/extraction,

\[
W_{\bd}=N_{\sm}\cdot |H|^2\|K_{\D4}\Gamma\|^2
\]

has branch-charge support

\[
|\tau|\le d_0=O(1).
\]

## T2. Pure plus graph/exterior mass

\[
\int |D_{-n}|^2W_0\,d\rho
\ge Q^{-A}\mu.
\]

## T3. Nonresonant branch-charge decay

\[
\int D_s\overline{D_{s'}}W_\tau\,d\rho
\]

is negligible unless

\[
s-s'+\tau=0.
\]

## T4. Near-pure Schur complement

The finite branch Gram on

\[
\{D_{-n},D_{-n+1},\ldots,D_{-n+d_0}\}
\]

has polynomial lower Schur complement for \(D_{-n}\).

Then full determinant survival follows.

---

# Final status

This is a useful reduction.

The fixed-\(\alpha\) O2/O3 survival theorem is no longer an \(O(R)\)-branch problem. With bounded-degree pre-extraction Stack weights, it becomes an \(O(1)\)-near-pure branch Gram problem.

The next most concrete finite audit is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{compute }d_0=\text{branch-charge bandwidth of }
N_{\sm}|H|^2\|K_{\D4}\Gamma\|^2.
}
\]

Then prove the near-pure Schur complement for the finite set

\[
D_{-n},D_{-n+1},\ldots,D_{-n+d_0}.
\]

This gives a fixed-\(\alpha\) alternative to \(\alpha\)-averaged determinant survival.

## Agent 4 continuation — near-pure branch Gram block

I pushed the fixed-\(\alpha\) route one level further. The previous reduction was that, if the pre-extraction Stack weight has bounded branch-charge bandwidth, then full determinant survival no longer requires controlling all branch sectors. It only requires controlling a finite near-pure block:

\[
D_{-n},D_{-n+1},\ldots,D_{-n+d_0},
\qquad n=R+2,\quad d_0=O(1).
\]

The current task is to decide whether the pure plus determinant \(D_{-n}\) can be separated from these near-pure contaminants. This is part of the finite/O2O3 branch-charge survival attack described in the latest instruction set. 

## Verdict

\[
\boxed{
\text{The near-pure block is the right finite problem.}
}
\]

Even better:

\[
\boxed{
\text{its leading obstruction is just finite-degree AFE anti-lock lifted to exterior degree.}
}
\]

The fixed-\(\alpha\) route is plausible if we can prove a finite list of \(k\)-substitution anti-lock estimates for \(1\le k\le d_0\). This is much weaker than controlling all mixed determinant branches.

---

# 1. Near-pure determinant components

Let

\[
D_{-n}
\]

be the pure plus determinant component, containing \(n=R+2\) plus-branch exterior factors.

Let

\[
D_{-n+k}
\]

be the component with \(n-k\) plus factors and \(k\) non-plus insertions, where the non-plus insertions may be folded-dual or background/completion factors.

Since

\[
k\le d_0=O(1),
\]

each near-pure component differs from \(D_{-n}\) by only finitely many substitutions.

So the dangerous finite branch Gram block is

\[
\mathcal G_{\mathrm{near}}
=
\left(
\langle D_{-n+i},D_{-n+j}\rangle_{W_{i-j}}
\right)_{0\le i,j\le d_0},
\]

where \(W_{i-j}\) is the branch-charge component of the bounded-degree Stack weight needed to make the total charge zero.

The survival condition is the Schur complement lower bound:

\[
\operatorname{dist}
\left(
D_{-n},
\operatorname{span}\{D_{-n+1},\ldots,D_{-n+d_0}\}
\right)^2
\ge
Q^{-A}\|D_{-n}\|^2.
\]

---

# 2. Structure of \(D_{-n+k}\)

A near-pure determinant term with \(k\) substitutions can be written schematically as

\[
D_{-n+k}
=
\sum_{\substack{J\subset\{1,\ldots,n\}\\ |J|=k}}
D^{(J)}_{\mathrm{sub}},
\]

where \(D^{(J)}_{\mathrm{sub}}\) replaces the plus branch column or factor at slots \(J\) by dual/background/completion data.

At the coefficient level, relative to a pure plus exterior coefficient

\[
C_S^{+},
\]

a \(k\)-substitution coefficient has the form

\[
C_{S,J}^{(k)}
=
C_S^{+}
\cdot
\prod_{a\in J}
\mathcal R_a
\cdot
\mathcal E_{S,J},
\]

where:

- \(\mathcal R_a\) is a local branch ratio, such as dual/plus or background/plus symbol ratio;
- \(\mathcal E_{S,J}\) is a bounded exterior correction caused by replacing columns in the Vandermonde/divided-difference determinant.

Because \(k=O(1)\), the correction \(\mathcal E_{S,J}\) is finite-degree and should be \(Q^{O(1)}\)-controlled in a Riesz-normalized basis.

Thus \(D_{-n+k}\) is a finite-rank modulation of \(D_{-n}\) by \(k\) branch-ratio factors.

This is why the problem is much more tractable than the full branch determinant.

---

# 3. The \(k=1\) obstruction

The most important case is

\[
D_{-n+1}.
\]

This replaces one plus factor by one non-plus factor.

If \(D_{-n+1}\) could mimic \(D_{-n}\), then the ratio of non-plus to plus data would have to be approximately constant across the exterior slots.

Schematically, if

\[
D_{-n+1}\approx cD_{-n},
\]

then the one-substitution branch ratio

\[
\mathcal R(x)
=
\frac{\text{dual/background symbol}}{\text{plus symbol}}
\]

must behave like a constant in the exterior averaged geometry.

Therefore the \(k=1\) Schur complement reduces to:

\[
\boxed{
\operatorname{dist}\left(\mathcal R,\mathbb C\right)\ge Q^{-A}
}
\]

in the plus-exterior weighted geometry, modulo bounded Riesz factors.

This is exactly the degree-one AFE anti-lock pattern.

If \(\mathcal R\) has nontrivial phase variation or symbol variation across the ordinary packet, \(D_{-n+1}\) cannot cancel \(D_{-n}\).

So the first finite theorem is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{dual/background-to-plus branch ratio is not constant at polynomial scale.}
}
\]

---

# 4. Higher \(k=O(1)\)

For \(k>1\), the contaminants are generated by products

\[
\mathcal R(x_{a_1})\cdots \mathcal R(x_{a_k}).
\]

The finite block

\[
D_{-n},\ldots,D_{-n+d_0}
\]

is therefore controlled by the finite-dimensional span

\[
\operatorname{span}
\{1,\ \mathcal R,\ \mathcal R^{\otimes 2},\ldots,\mathcal R^{\otimes d_0}\}
\]

inside the exterior plus geometry.

The Schur complement asks whether the constant function \(1\) lies near the span of these substitution-ratio components.

Because \(d_0=O(1)\), this is a finite-degree anti-lock problem.

A sufficient condition is a finite moment/Riesz lower bound:

\[
\boxed{
\operatorname{dist}
\left(
1,
\operatorname{span}\{\mathcal R^{(1)},\ldots,\mathcal R^{(d_0)}\}
\right)
\ge Q^{-A}.
}
\]

Here \(\mathcal R^{(k)}\) denotes the effective \(k\)-substitution exterior ratio, not literally a pointwise power in all cases.

This is much easier than O2/O3’s original \(R\)-degree non-polynomiality problem.

---

# 5. Relation to \(L^2\)-AFE anti-lock

The upstream finite \(L^2\) anti-lock problem is:

\[
\|B_2+P_{2,\le X}+D_{2,\le X}\|_{L^2(W)}^2
\ge
Q^{-A}
\left(
\|B_2\|^2+\|P_{2,\le X}\|^2+\|D_{2,\le X}\|^2
\right),
\]

or an equivalent source nonflatness lower bound.

In the simplified two-branch model,

\[
P+D
\]

fails anti-lock if

\[
D\approx -P.
\]

That is exactly the assertion that the ratio

\[
\mathcal R=D/P
\]

is approximately constant \(-1\).

The near-pure determinant obstruction is the exterior lift of the same ratio-constancy failure.

So:

\[
\boxed{
\text{finite \(L^2\)-AFE anti-lock is the degree-one model for near-pure determinant survival.}
}
\]

This suggests proving a common theorem:

\[
\boxed{
\text{branch-ratio nonconstancy / phase variation theorem.}
}
\]

It would feed both AggL2 and O2/O3 branch survival.

---

# 6. What ratio \(\mathcal R\) should be audited?

For plus versus folded-dual terms, the ratio is roughly

\[
\mathcal R_{\pm}(x;m,\alpha)
=
e^{i\Theta(m,\alpha)}
\frac{a_-(x;m,\alpha)m_-(x)}
{a_+(x;m,\alpha)m_+(x)},
\]

where \(a_\pm\) include AFE weights and branch phases.

For plus versus background,

\[
\mathcal R_{0}(x;m)
=
\frac{a_0(x;m)m_0(x)}
{a_+(x;m)m_+(x)}.
\]

The key is whether these ratios are constant, or nearly constant, across \(x\in[c_-,c_+]\).

Expected behavior:

- folded dual ratio should have nontrivial phase variation from the functional equation and the \(n\leftrightarrow t/(2\pi n)\) dual relation;
- background ratio should have very different \(x\)-dependence;
- completion/tail ratio should be smoother or perturbative.

So the finite near-pure anti-lock condition is plausible.

---

# 7. Branch-charge bandwidth determines how many ratios matter

If the pre-extraction Stack weight has branch-charge bandwidth

\[
d_0,
\]

then only

\[
k\le d_0
\]

near-pure sectors matter.

From the earlier audit:

\[
d_0=d_N+d_{\wall}.
\]

If:

- \(N\) is local \(L^2\) source energy, degree \(2\);
- \(E_{\wall}^{\forb}\) has fixed degree, say \(d_{\wall}\);
- no caps or reciprocals are inserted before survival;

then

\[
d_0=O(1).
\]

So only finitely many substitution ratios are needed.

This is the core reason fixed-\(\alpha\) survival is viable.

---

# 8. A finite Schur complement theorem

Define the near-pure vector

\[
\mathbf D_{\near}
=
(D_{-n},D_{-n+1},\ldots,D_{-n+d_0}).
\]

Let

\[
G_{\near}
=
\left(
\langle D_{-n+i},D_{-n+j}\rangle
\right)_{0\le i,j\le d_0}
\]

under the relevant smooth bounded-charge weight.

The desired theorem is:

\[
\boxed{
G_{\near}
\ge
Q^{-A}
G_{00}e_0e_0^*
}
\]

in Schur-complement form, i.e.

\[
G_{00}
-
G_{0,*}G_{*,*}^{-1}G_{*,0}
\ge
Q^{-A}G_{00}.
\]

Since

\[
d_0=O(1),
\]

this is a fixed-size Hermitian matrix problem. It can be attacked by computing/estimating finitely many inner products.

This is a major simplification.

---

# 9. What could make the near-pure block fail?

Failure means there are constants

\[
c_1,\ldots,c_{d_0}
\]

such that

\[
D_{-n}
+
\sum_{k=1}^{d_0}c_kD_{-n+k}
\approx0.
\]

In ratio language, this says a finite polynomial in substitution operators annihilates the pure branch:

\[
1+\sum_{k=1}^{d_0}c_k\mathcal R^{(k)}\approx0.
\]

So the ratio variables satisfy a low-degree algebraic relation.

Thus a sufficient theorem is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{the branch substitution ratio is not concentrated on the zero set of any degree-}d_0\text{ polynomial.}
}
\]

Because \(d_0=O(1)\), this is a low-degree anti-lock theorem.

This is far more plausible than a high-degree determinant anti-trough.

---

# 10. Role of \(\alpha\)

If \(\alpha\)-twisting is available, then the ratios acquire phase factors:

\[
\mathcal R\mapsto e^{i\Theta(\alpha)}\mathcal R.
\]

A low-degree polynomial relation

\[
1+\sum_{k=1}^{d_0}c_k e^{ik\Theta}\mathcal R^{(k)}\approx0
\]

cannot persist over an interval of \(\Theta\) unless all coefficients are small. Thus \(\alpha\)-averaging immediately proves the finite near-pure anti-lock theorem.

So \(\alpha\)-twisting is now only needed to kill a fixed-size polynomial relation, not the whole determinant branch forest. This is a smaller and more plausible use of \(\alpha\).

If \(\alpha\) is not admissible, fixed-\(\alpha\) nonconstancy of \(\mathcal R\) may suffice.

---

# 11. Connection to D4 normality

There is an important interaction.

The forbidden wall factor may emphasize a particular D4 direction, often expected to be \(\Gamma_{G,2}\) via the \(Y12\) channel. If that direction correlates strongly with one branch ratio \(\mathcal R\), then the near-pure Gram weight may become biased toward branch-lock configurations.

So the near-pure theorem should be stated under the wall-weighted measure:

\[
d\nu_{\bd}
=
N_{\sm}
|H|^2
\|K_{\D4}\Gamma\|^2
\,d\rho
\]

before capping.

The low-degree anti-lock theorem must hold under this weight. This is not automatic.

But since the wall factor has bounded degree, it only modifies the finite Gram matrix by adding finitely many ratio moments. It does not reintroduce \(R\)-degree complexity.

Thus the theorem remains finite-dimensional.

---

# 12. Final reduced fixed-\(\alpha\) O2/O3 survival theorem

The full fixed-\(\alpha\) survival theorem reduces to:

1. **Pure branch graph non-polynomiality**

\[
D_{-n}
\]

has polynomial exterior mass.

2. **Bounded-charge Stack weight**

\[
W_{\bd}
\]

has branch-charge bandwidth \(d_0=O(1)\).

3. **Near-pure finite anti-lock**

The finite Gram block

\[
(D_{-n},D_{-n+1},\ldots,D_{-n+d_0})
\]

has polynomial Schur complement.

4. **Nonresonant branch decay**

All branch sectors farther than \(d_0\) are killed by branch-charge/frequency separation under smooth pre-extraction weights.

This gives full determinant survival.

---

# Final status

The fixed-\(\alpha\) route has become more credible.

The hard part is no longer a huge determinant branch forest. It is the finite near-pure anti-lock theorem:

\[
\boxed{
D_{-n}\notin
\operatorname{span}\{D_{-n+1},\ldots,D_{-n+d_0}\}.
}
\]

Equivalently:

\[
\boxed{
\text{the branch substitution ratio satisfies no low-degree anti-lock relation under the Stack weight.}
}
\]

This is the next concrete Agent 4 target.

## Agent 4 report — bounded-charge O2/O3 near-pure Schur block

I followed the Agent 4 instruction: make the fixed-\(\alpha\) determinant-survival route precise by reducing full determinant survival to an \(O(1)\)-size near-pure Schur complement. The key premise is that O2/O3 should be handled with smooth bounded-degree weights **before** capped extraction, because hard extraction destroys the branch-charge/Fourier structure needed for survival. 

## Verdict

\[
\boxed{
\text{The fixed-\(\alpha\) route is now a finite-dimensional branch-ratio anti-lock problem.}
}
\]

More precisely:

\[
\boxed{
D_{-n}\notin
\operatorname{span}\{D_{-n+1},\ldots,D_{-n+d_0}\}
}
\]

under the smooth pre-extraction Stack weight, where

\[
n=R+2,
\qquad
d_0=O(1).
\]

This is much more tractable than the earlier all-branch determinant-survival problem.

---

# 1. Branch-charge setup

Assign charges:

\[
\operatorname{ch}(+)= -1,
\qquad
\operatorname{ch}(-)= +1,
\qquad
\operatorname{ch}(\mathrm{bg})=0.
\]

Decompose the full finite determinant by branch charge:

\[
D_{\full}=\sum_s D_s.
\]

The pure plus determinant is

\[
D_{-n},
\qquad n=R+2.
\]

The goal is to prove that the pure plus component survives inside the full scalar determinant:

\[
\int |D_{\full}|^2 W_{\bd}\,d\rho
\ge
Q^{-A}
\int |D_{-n}|^2 W_{\bd,0}\,d\rho.
\]

Here \(W_{\bd}\) is the **smooth bounded-degree pre-extraction weight**, not a capped rough good-set indicator.

---

# 2. The correct pre-extraction weight

Use

\[
W_{\bd}
\sim
N_{\sm}\,|H|^2\,|K_{\D4}\Gamma|^2,
\]

with denominators such as \(\mathcal N_{\D4}\) dyadically frozen.

Do **not** use

\[
F_1,\quad F_{23},\quad F_{\wall}
\]

inside the branch-survival proof. Those capped factors should appear only after aggregate survival is established.

The reason is that caps and reciprocal normalizations can introduce high branch-charge bandwidth. The raw expression

\[
N_{\sm}|H|^2|K_{\D4}\Gamma|^2
\]

has bounded algebraic degree and therefore bounded branch-charge bandwidth.

---

# 3. Computing the branch-charge bandwidth \(d_0\)

Let:

\[
d_N=\text{branch degree of }N_{\sm},
\]

\[
d_H=\text{branch degree of }H,
\]

\[
d_\Gamma=\text{branch degree of }K_{\D4}\Gamma.
\]

Then

\[
|H|^2
\]

has charge support in

\[
[-2d_H,2d_H],
\]

and

\[
|K_{\D4}\Gamma|^2
\]

has charge support in

\[
[-2d_\Gamma,2d_\Gamma].
\]

Thus

\[
W_{\bd}
\]

has branch-charge support in

\[
[-d_0,d_0],
\]

where

\[
\boxed{
d_0=d_N+2d_H+2d_\Gamma.
}
\]

The crucial point is:

\[
\boxed{
d_0=O(1),
}
\]

provided \(K_{\D4}\) is a fixed finite carrier/normal map and is not fitted pointwise to the actual row.

This is why the singular-discard \(K_9\)-style \(\Lambda\) is compatible with fixed-\(\alpha\) O2/O3: it contributes only bounded branch complexity.

---

# 4. Branch-charge reduction

A cross term in

\[
\int |D_{\full}|^2W_{\bd}\,d\rho
\]

has the form

\[
\int D_s\overline{D_{s'}}\,W_\tau\,d\rho.
\]

Its total charge is

\[
s-s'+\tau.
\]

The pure plus component \(D_{-n}\) can interact resonantly with \(D_s\) only if

\[
(-n)-s+\tau=0,
\]

so

\[
s+n=\tau.
\]

Since

\[
|\tau|\le d_0,
\]

the only possible resonant contaminants are

\[
D_{-n+1},D_{-n+2},\ldots,D_{-n+d_0}.
\]

All farther branch sectors are nonresonant and should be killed by smooth branch-charge decay.

Therefore full determinant survival reduces to the finite near-pure block:

\[
\boxed{
D_{-n},D_{-n+1},\ldots,D_{-n+d_0}.
}
\]

---

# 5. Near-pure Gram block

Define

\[
\mathbf D_{\near}
=
(D_{-n},D_{-n+1},\ldots,D_{-n+d_0}).
\]

The near-pure Gram matrix is

\[
G_{\near}
=
\left(
\langle D_{-n+i},D_{-n+j}\rangle_{W_{i-j}}
\right)_{0\le i,j\le d_0}.
\]

The survival condition is the Schur complement lower bound:

\[
\boxed{
G_{00}
-
G_{0,*}G_{*,*}^{-1}G_{*,0}
\ge
Q^{-A}G_{00}.
}
\]

Equivalently,

\[
\boxed{
\operatorname{dist}
\left(
D_{-n},
\operatorname{span}\{D_{-n+1},\ldots,D_{-n+d_0}\}
\right)^2
\ge
Q^{-A}\|D_{-n}\|^2.
}
\]

Because \(d_0=O(1)\), this is a fixed-size Hermitian matrix problem.

---

# 6. Structure of \(D_{-n+k}\)

The component \(D_{-n+k}\) differs from \(D_{-n}\) by \(k\) substitutions of plus-branch exterior factors with dual/background/completion factors.

For \(k=O(1)\), a typical coefficient is

\[
C^{(k)}_{S,J}
=
C^{(+)}_S
\prod_{a\in J}\mathcal R_a
\cdot
\mathcal E_{S,J},
\]

where:

\[
\mathcal R_a
=
\frac{\text{dual/background symbol at slot }a}
{\text{plus symbol at slot }a},
\]

and \(\mathcal E_{S,J}\) is a bounded exterior correction from the column replacement.

Thus near-pure sectors are finite-degree modulations of the pure plus determinant by branch substitution ratios.

So the Schur complement is controlled by the finite-dimensional system

\[
1,\quad
\mathcal R,\quad
\mathcal R^{(2)},\ldots,\mathcal R^{(d_0)}.
\]

The obstruction is:

\[
\boxed{
1
\in
\operatorname{span}
\{\mathcal R,\mathcal R^{(2)},\ldots,\mathcal R^{(d_0)}\}
\text{ approximately}.
}
\]

That is a low-degree branch-ratio anti-lock condition.

---

# 7. The \(k=1\) test

The most important case is

\[
D_{-n+1}.
\]

If

\[
D_{-n+1}\approx cD_{-n},
\]

then the one-substitution ratio

\[
\mathcal R(x)
=
\frac{\text{non-plus branch symbol}}{\text{plus branch symbol}}
\]

must be approximately constant in the exterior plus geometry.

Thus the first necessary finite theorem is:

\[
\boxed{
\operatorname{dist}(\mathcal R,\mathbb C)\ge Q^{-A}.
}
\]

For folded-dual contamination, \(\mathcal R\) should have nontrivial phase or fold variation. For background contamination, \(\mathcal R\) should have a visibly different \(x\)-profile.

This is exactly the degree-one AFE anti-lock mechanism in exterior form.

---

# 8. Link to Gate–1 anti-lock

The upstream finite \(L^2\) anti-lock theorem asks for

\[
B_2+P_{2,\le X}+D_{2,\le X}
\]

not to cancel in \(L^2(W_{\ref})\).

In a two-branch simplification, failure means

\[
D_X\approx -P_X,
\]

i.e.

\[
\mathcal R=D_X/P_X\approx -1.
\]

The near-pure determinant obstruction is the same branch-ratio lock, but lifted to exterior degree.

Thus:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Gate--1 finite AFE anti-lock is the degree-one prototype of O2/O3 near-pure survival.}
}
\]

This is useful: the same fold-ratio nonconstancy theorem should feed both.

---

# 9. Fold structure of the ratio

In log variables,

\[
x=\frac{\log n}{Q}.
\]

The AFE fold sends roughly

\[
x\mapsto 1-x.
\]

The curvature multiplier distinguishes the two:

\[
x^2-(1-x)^2=2x-1.
\]

Thus outside the central tube

\[
|x-1/2|\le \delta,
\]

the plus and folded-dual curvature symbols differ by at least

\[
\gg\delta.
\]

So the branch ratio \(\mathcal R(x)\) cannot be constant unless mass is concentrated in the central tube.

This gives the low-degree anti-lock theorem a concrete form:

\[
\boxed{
\text{either curvature mass is central-tube concentrated, or branch-ratio nonconstancy holds.}
}
\]

The central tube case should be handled separately by either small support, phase variation, or a local mean-square lower bound.

---

# 10. Nonresonant branch decay

For sectors

\[
D_s
\]

with

\[
s+n>d_0,
\]

no component of \(W_{\bd}\) can supply the missing charge. These cross terms have nonzero total branch charge and should vanish under the smooth aggregate measure.

The required theorem is:

\[
\boxed{
\left|
\int D_s\overline{D_{s'}}W_\tau\,d\rho
\right|
\le Q^{-A}
\|D_s\|\|D_{s'}\|
}
\]

whenever

\[
s-s'+\tau\ne0.
\]

This is the branch-charge decay theorem. It should follow from smooth branch-frequency separation if the aggregate weight is kept pre-extraction and bounded-degree.

---

# 11. Updated fixed-\(\alpha\) O2/O3 theorem

The fixed-\(\alpha\) determinant survival theorem can now be stated as:

Assume:

1. **Riesz normalization**

\[
\|A_{\le X,m}\|\le R^C.
\]

2. **Pure plus graph non-polynomiality**

\[
\operatorname{dist}(1/m_{\can,+},\mathbb C_R[x])\ge Q^{-A}.
\]

3. **Pure plus exterior mass**

\[
\|D_{-n}\|^2\ge Q^{-A}\mu.
\]

4. **Bounded branch-charge weight**

\[
W_{\bd}
\]

has charge support \(|\tau|\le d_0=O(1)\).

5. **Nonresonant branch-charge decay**

Cross terms with nonzero total charge are negligible.

6. **Near-pure Schur complement**

\[
D_{-n}
\notin
\operatorname{span}\{D_{-n+1},\ldots,D_{-n+d_0}\}
\]

at polynomial scale.

Then

\[
\int |D_{\full}|^2W_{\bd}\,d\rho
\ge Q^{-A}\int |D_{-n}|^2W_{\bd,0}\,d\rho.
\]

And by \(R^C\)-normalization,

\[
\int \mathcal H_{23}(A_{\le X,\full})W_{\bd}\,d\rho
\ge Q^{-A}\mu.
\]

This is the fixed-\(\alpha\) O2/O3 route.

---

# 12. Final status

\[
\boxed{
\text{The fixed-\(\alpha\) route is credible if }d_0=O(1).
}
\]

The central finite problem is no longer all determinant branches. It is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{a fixed-size near-pure Schur complement.}
}
\]

Equivalently:

\[
\boxed{
\text{no low-degree polynomial anti-lock relation among branch substitution ratios.}
}
\]

The next concrete computation is:

\[
\boxed{
d_0
=
\text{branch-charge bandwidth of }
N_{\sm}|H|^2|K_{\D4}\Gamma|^2.
}
\]

Then explicitly identify the ratio functions \(\mathcal R\) for the \(k=1,\ldots,d_0\) substitutions and test the finite Schur complement.

## Agent 4 continuation — estimating \(d_0\) and isolating the \(k=1\) obstruction

I continued the fixed-\(\alpha\) O2/O3 route. The current reduced target is:

\[
D_{-n}\notin
\operatorname{span}
\{D_{-n+1},\ldots,D_{-n+d_0}\},
\qquad n=R+2,
\]

where \(d_0\) is the branch-charge bandwidth of the smooth pre-extraction Stack weight

\[
W_{\bd}
=
N_{\sm}|H|^2|K_{\D4}\Gamma|^2
\]

after dyadic freezing of denominators. The point is to avoid rough caps until after O2/O3 survival has been proved.

## Verdict

\[
\boxed{
d_0\text{ is likely small, probably }4\text{ to }8\text{ depending on the chosen }H,\Gamma\text{ normalization.}
}
\]

More importantly:

\[
\boxed{
\text{if the \(k=1\) ratio obstruction is controlled, the higher }k\le d_0
\text{ block should be finite-dimensional bookkeeping.}
}
\]

The main finite theorem to prove is now:

\[
\boxed{
\operatorname{dist}(\mathcal R,\mathbb C)\ge Q^{-A}
}
\]

for the plus-to-nonplus substitution ratio \(\mathcal R\), in the pure-plus exterior geometry.

---

# 1. Estimating the branch-charge bandwidth \(d_0\)

The smooth pre-extraction weight is

\[
W_{\bd}=N_{\sm}|H|^2|K_{\D4}\Gamma|^2.
\]

Each factor has bounded algebraic degree in the branch decomposition of

\[
u=q''_{\can}=u_+ + u_- + u_0.
\]

## Source/reference factor \(N_{\sm}\)

If

\[
N_{\sm}
\sim
|u|^2
\]

or a local \(L^2\) source-energy density, then

\[
N_{\sm}
=
|u_+|^2+|u_-|^2+|u_0|^2
+
u_+\overline{u_-}
+
u_-\overline{u_+}
+
\text{bg cross terms}.
\]

So

\[
d_N\le 2.
\]

If one uses only the charge-zero part of the source energy as the reference weight, then

\[
d_N=0.
\]

This distinction matters.

## \(H\)-factor

The D4 wall factor includes

\[
|H|^2.
\]

If \(H\) is linear in the source variables, for example schematically

\[
H\sim q_0u
\]

with \(q_0\) treated as slowly varying or charge \(0\), then

\[
d_H\le1,
\qquad
|H|^2\text{ has bandwidth }2.
\]

If the relevant graded expression is closer to

\[
H\sim q_0u^3,
\]

then

\[
d_H\le3,
\qquad
|H|^2\text{ has bandwidth }6.
\]

But in the normalized D4 wall energy, one should use the lowest-degree scalar wall amplitude actually entering the live carrier, not an expanded cubic proxy if it has already been absorbed into the D4 normalization.

So \(d_H\) must be audited from the final local D4 normalization. The likely values are:

\[
d_H=1
\quad\text{or}\quad
d_H=3.
\]

## Forbidden channel \(|K_{\D4}\Gamma|^2\)

The map

\[
K_{\D4}
\]

is finite carrier/normal data and should be branch-neutral, provided \(P_{\sing}\) is defined from a fixed EF-coherent model and is not adapted pointwise to the actual row.

The branch degree comes from \(\Gamma\).

If

\[
\Gamma_G,\Gamma_{G,2}
\]

are linear in the source variables at the relevant normalized level, then

\[
d_\Gamma\le1.
\]

If \(\Gamma_G\) includes nonlinear terms like

\[
u-6q+2q^3,
\]

then the branch degree may be \(3\), unless the nonlinear terms are treated as lower-order/gauge/background and absorbed in normalization.

So likely:

\[
d_\Gamma=1
\quad\text{or}\quad
3.
\]

Thus

\[
d_0=d_N+2d_H+2d_\Gamma.
\]

Typical possibilities:

- Optimistic normalized case:
  \[
  d_N=0,\quad d_H=1,\quad d_\Gamma=1
  \quad\Rightarrow\quad
  d_0=4.
  \]

- Conservative source-energy case:
  \[
  d_N=2,\quad d_H=1,\quad d_\Gamma=1
  \quad\Rightarrow\quad
  d_0=6.
  \]

- Cubic unnormalized case:
  \[
  d_N=2,\quad d_H=3,\quad d_\Gamma=3
  \quad\Rightarrow\quad
  d_0=14.
  \]

All are still

\[
O(1).
\]

The proof should keep \(d_0\) symbolic, but the finite block is small.

---

# 2. Do not allow \(K_{\D4}\) to carry branch charge

A serious caveat:

If

\[
K_{\D4}
=
(I-P_{\sing})T_{\tr\to\sing}E_{\car}L_{\clean}
\]

depends nonlinearly on the actual local source \(u\), then it may carry branch charges.

That would increase \(d_0\).

Therefore \(P_{\sing}\) must be defined from a fixed structural EF-coherence model and finite chart data, not adaptively fitted to the pointwise branch decomposition.

This is consistent with the downstream normality requirement:

\[
\mathcal M_{\sing}^{\arith}
\]

is a structured actual-source manifold, not a fitted subspace through every row.

So:

\[
\boxed{
K_{\D4}\text{ must be branch-neutral up to fixed structural coefficients.}
}
\]

This is necessary for the fixed-\(\alpha\) branch survival route.

---

# 3. The \(k=1\) obstruction controls the finite block

Let

\[
\mathcal R
\]

be the one-substitution ratio replacing one plus factor by a non-plus factor.

Then

\[
D_{-n+1}
\]

is the exterior lift of \(\mathcal R\). Higher components

\[
D_{-n+k}
\]

are finite exterior lifts of products or elementary symmetric combinations of \(\mathcal R\)-type ratios.

Thus the near-pure span is generated by finite-degree functions of \(\mathcal R\):

\[
1,\quad
\mathcal R,\quad
\mathcal R^{(2)},\ldots,\mathcal R^{(d_0)}.
\]

If

\[
\mathcal R
\]

has strong variation in phase or magnitude, then \(1\) is not in the span of these higher components.

The sharp obstruction is:

\[
1+\sum_{k=1}^{d_0} c_k \mathcal R^{(k)}\approx0.
\]

So the finite Schur complement is equivalent to a low-degree polynomial anti-lock condition for the substitution ratio.

This is a much simpler theorem than the original exterior determinant statement.

---

# 4. Folded-dual ratio

For plus-to-dual substitutions, the ratio has schematic form

\[
\mathcal R_{\pm}(x;m,\alpha)
=
e^{i\Theta(m,\alpha)}
\frac{a_-(1-x;m,\alpha)m_-(1-x)}
{a_+(x;m,\alpha)m_+(x)}.
\]

If

\[
m_\pm(x)\sim \kappa x^2
\]

after branch normalization, then

\[
\frac{m_-(1-x)}{m_+(x)}
\sim
\frac{(1-x)^2}{x^2}.
\]

This function is nonconstant on the ordinary packet except possibly in a narrow central region around

\[
x=1/2.
\]

Indeed,

\[
\frac{d}{dx}\log\frac{(1-x)^2}{x^2}
=
-2\left(\frac1{1-x}+\frac1x\right),
\]

which is bounded away from zero on any compact subinterval of \((0,1)\).

So the dual substitution ratio is strongly nonconstant unless the packet is concentrated in an extremely small \(x\)-window.

This is very favorable.

It is the exterior analogue of the degree-one fold anti-lock:

\[
x^2-(1-x)^2=2x-1.
\]

---

# 5. Central tube

Near

\[
x=1/2,
\]

the ratio

\[
\frac{(1-x)^2}{x^2}
\]

is close to \(1\). The leading curvature multiplier alone may not separate plus and dual.

Let

\[
x=\frac12+s.
\]

Then

\[
\frac{(1-x)^2}{x^2}
=
\left(\frac{1/2-s}{1/2+s}\right)^2
=
1-8s+O(s^2).
\]

So variation is linear in \(s\).

If the active mass is concentrated in a tube

\[
|s|\le Q^{-A},
\]

then the ratio can be nearly constant at polynomial scale.

This central tube is a genuine exceptional case.

Possible resolutions:

1. show the central tube carries only polynomially small exterior mass;
2. use phase variation from \(e^{i\Theta(m,\alpha)}\);
3. use lower-order AFE weight differences;
4. route central-tube packets to transition/singular branch treatment;
5. prove a local central anti-lock theorem using higher derivatives.

The central tube is the main local obstruction to \(k=1\) ratio nonconstancy.

---

# 6. Background substitution ratio

For plus-to-background substitutions,

\[
\mathcal R_0(x)=\frac{\text{background symbol}}{\text{plus symbol}}.
\]

The background \(B_2\) is smooth/gamma-like and does not share the prime-log oscillatory structure. It should be either:

- charge \(0\) and separated by frequency;
- low-dimensional and nonconstant relative to plus;
- or tangent/absorbed into the actual-source manifold.

It is unlikely to mimic the plus branch exterior ratio on a whole packet.

But \(B_2\) can be large at low \(Q\) or in transition regimes. In ordinary high-\(Q\) packets, it is likely perturbative or slowly varying. The branch ratio theorem should include it as a finite list of background substitution ratios.

This is manageable because background substitutions count toward \(k\le d_0=O(1)\).

---

# 7. Low-degree polynomial anti-lock theorem

The clean finite theorem is:

Let \(\mathscr R\) be the finite set of substitution ratio functions:

\[
\mathscr R=
\{\mathcal R_{\pm},\mathcal R_0,\mathcal R_{\mathrm{tail}},\ldots\}.
\]

Let

\[
\mathcal P_{d_0}(\mathscr R)
\]

be the finite-dimensional space spanned by all exterior substitution expressions of degree \(1\le k\le d_0\).

Then prove:

\[
\boxed{
\operatorname{dist}
\left(
1,
\mathcal P_{d_0}(\mathscr R)
\right)
\ge Q^{-A}
}
\]

under the pure-plus exterior measure.

This is exactly the near-pure Schur complement.

Because \(d_0=O(1)\), this is a low-dimensional finite anti-lock theorem.

---

# 8. How to prove the ratio anti-lock

Potential tools:

## A. Variation lower bound

If \(\mathcal R\) has derivative bounded below:

\[
|\partial_x\mathcal R|\ge Q^{-A}
\]

on a positive-measure portion of the packet, then \(1\) cannot be approximated by low-degree combinations of \(\mathcal R\)-substitution terms unless those terms include constants. This works best when \(\mathcal R\) is real monotone.

## B. Phase winding

If \(\arg\mathcal R\) winds by \(\gg Q^{-A}\) on the packet, then no fixed low-degree polynomial in \(\mathcal R\) can cancel \(1\) on a thick set.

## C. Fold symmetry

Use the explicit fold relation

\[
x\mapsto1-x
\]

and curvature asymmetry

\[
x^2-(1-x)^2=2x-1.
\]

This gives a direct lower bound outside the central tube.

## D. \(\alpha\)-twist

If \(\mathcal R\mapsto e^{i\Theta(\alpha)}\mathcal R\), then low-degree relations become

\[
1+\sum_{k=1}^{d_0}c_ke^{ik\Theta}\mathcal R^{(k)}\approx0.
\]

Averaging over \(\Theta\) kills them. This is optional if fixed-\(\alpha\) variation suffices.

---

# 9. Interaction with \(K_9/Y12\)

If the forbidden wall is rank-one through the \(Y12\) channel, then

\[
K_{\D4}\Gamma
\sim
a\Gamma_G+b\Gamma_{G,2},
\qquad b\ne0.
\]

If this expression is dominated by \(\Gamma_{G,2}\), the wall factor may have lower branch degree than the full raw anisotropy.

This would reduce \(d_0\).

So proving the \(K_9(Y12)\neq0\) rank-one channel is useful not only downstream. It also simplifies O2/O3 branch survival by lowering branch-charge bandwidth.

This is a hidden interaction:

\[
\boxed{
Y12\text{-rank-one forbidden channel may reduce }d_0.
}
\]

If raw rank-two anisotropy is used, \(d_0\) may be larger.

---

# 10. Final reduced theorem

The fixed-\(\alpha\) near-pure survival theorem is:

Given a bounded-charge pre-extraction weight with bandwidth \(d_0=O(1)\), define the finite substitution-ratio space

\[
\mathcal P_{d_0}(\mathscr R).
\]

If

\[
\operatorname{dist}(1,\mathcal P_{d_0}(\mathscr R))
\ge Q^{-A}
\]

in the pure-plus exterior measure, and nonresonant branch-charge decay holds, then

\[
D_{-n}
\notin
\operatorname{span}
\{D_{-n+1},\ldots,D_{-n+d_0}\},
\]

hence full determinant survival holds.

This is the cleanest form of the fixed-\(\alpha\) O2/O3 survival theorem.

---

# Final status

The O2/O3 fixed-\(\alpha\) route now reduces to a low-degree branch-ratio anti-lock theorem.

The first ratio to audit is:

\[
\boxed{
\mathcal R_{\pm}(x)
\sim
e^{i\Theta}
\frac{(1-x)^2}{x^2}.
}
\]

Outside the central tube \(x\approx1/2\), this is strongly nonconstant. The central tube is the main exception and should be routed separately or handled by phase/lower-order variation.

The most useful next computation is:

\[
\boxed{
d_0=d_N+2d_H+2d_\Gamma
}
\]

for the actual \(Y12/K_9\)-based wall factor, because a rank-one \(Y12\) forbidden channel may keep \(d_0\) small and make the finite Schur block very manageable.

## Agent 4 continuation — central-tube obstruction in the near-pure Schur block

The fixed-\(\alpha\) O2/O3 route has now reduced to a low-degree branch-ratio anti-lock theorem. The first substitution ratio is approximately

\[
\mathcal R_\pm(x)
\sim
e^{i\Theta}
\frac{(1-x)^2}{x^2}.
\]

Away from \(x=1/2\), this is strongly nonconstant. The remaining danger is the central tube:

\[
x=\frac12+s,\qquad |s|\ll1.
\]

This is where the plus/dual curvature multipliers nearly agree. I now attack that exceptional region.

---

# 1. Local expansion in the central tube

For

\[
x=\frac12+s,
\]

we have

\[
\frac{(1-x)^2}{x^2}
=
\left(\frac{1/2-s}{1/2+s}\right)^2.
\]

Expanding:

\[
\left(\frac{1/2-s}{1/2+s}\right)^2
=
1-8s+32s^2+O(s^3).
\]

Thus

\[
\mathcal R_\pm(x)
=
e^{i\Theta}
\left(
1-8s+O(s^2)
\right)
\cdot
\mathcal W(x),
\]

where \(\mathcal W(x)\) includes AFE weight, gamma, and normalization ratios.

If \(\mathcal W\) is slowly varying and \(e^{i\Theta}\) is fixed, then on a tube

\[
|s|\le \delta,
\]

the ratio is constant up to

\[
O(\delta).
\]

So the fixed-\(\alpha\) low-degree anti-lock lower bound can only be

\[
\gtrsim \delta
\]

from curvature asymmetry alone.

Therefore, to get a polynomial lower bound \(Q^{-A}\), it suffices to exclude concentration in a tube of width smaller than \(Q^{-A}\). A polynomially wide central tube is not fatal; it just worsens the exponent.

---

# 2. Central-tube mass in exterior degree

The pure plus exterior determinant uses \(n=R+2\) nodes. If all exterior nodes lie in the central tube of width \(\delta\), the Vandermonde factor is small:

\[
|\det V_S|^2
=
\prod_{i<j}|x_i-x_j|^2.
\]

For \(n\) points inside an interval of width \(\delta\),

\[
|\det V_S|^2
\le
\delta^{n(n-1)}.
\]

Since the determinant coefficient contains

\[
(\det V_S)^2
\]

in the earlier Cauchy–Binet formula, central-tube exterior mass is suppressed by a very high power of \(\delta\).

This is important.

Even if the one-particle mass near \(x=1/2\) is not small, the **exterior determinant mass** of \(n=R+2\) nearly colliding \(x\)-nodes is small because Vandermonde repels clustered nodes.

Thus the central tube may be negligible for O2/O3 exterior mass unless the tube width is not too small.

A crude bound:

\[
\text{central exterior mass}
\lesssim
\delta^{cR^2}
\]

relative to an \(O(1)\)-width packet, ignoring weights.

For

\[
R\sim\frac{\log Q}{\log\log Q},
\]

even

\[
\delta=Q^{-\epsilon/R}
\]

gives polynomial loss. In particular, polynomial-width tubes

\[
\delta=Q^{-A}
\]

are overwhelmingly suppressed:

\[
\delta^{cR^2}
=
Q^{-AcR^2},
\]

far below the required threshold.

So for exterior determinant O2/O3, the central tube is less dangerous than it looked from the one-particle ratio.

---

# 3. But exterior subsets can mix central and noncentral nodes

The true near-pure determinant component does not require every node to lie in the central tube. The ratio obstruction arises if the substitution slots land in central nodes while the other plus nodes lie elsewhere.

For \(k=1\), a one-substitution contaminant replaces one plus factor by a dual factor. The ratio

\[
\mathcal R(x_a)
\]

matters only at the substituted slot \(x_a\). If \(x_a\) lies near \(1/2\), that substitution ratio is nearly constant, even if the remaining \(n-1\) nodes are spread out.

Thus Vandermonde suppression of all-central subsets is not enough.

We need to estimate the exterior mass of subsets where the substituted positions lie in the central tube.

Since \(k=O(1)\), this mass is roughly proportional to the one-particle central mass fraction, not \(\delta^{R^2}\).

So the central-tube problem persists for the near-pure contaminant.

But it is only \(O(1)\)-slot central concentration, not full determinant central concentration.

---

# 4. Split the substitution ratio by central/noncentral support

Let

\[
\chi_c(x)=1_{|x-1/2|\le\delta}.
\]

Write

\[
\mathcal R
=
\mathcal R_{\mathrm{out}}
+
\mathcal R_{\mathrm{cen}},
\]

where

\[
\mathcal R_{\mathrm{cen}}=\chi_c\mathcal R.
\]

On the outside region,

\[
|\partial_x\mathcal R|\gtrsim \delta,
\]

so ratio nonconstancy gives lower bounds of scale \(\delta\).

The central part has support fraction roughly \(\delta\) in one-particle measure.

For a \(k=1\) substitution, the central contaminant norm should be

\[
\|\mathcal R_{\mathrm{cen}}\|
\lesssim
\delta^{1/2}\|\mathcal R\|
\]

in a normalized one-particle geometry.

Thus choose

\[
\delta=Q^{-A}
\]

with \(A\) large enough. Then the central contaminant is polynomially small and the outside nonconstancy is polynomially strong.

This gives a standard split:

\[
\boxed{
\text{outside central tube: ratio variation;}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{inside central tube: small support.}
}
\]

This is the likely proof of the \(k=1\) anti-lock theorem.

---

# 5. What if mass concentrates in the central tube?

If the one-particle plus exterior measure is itself concentrated near \(x=1/2\), then the central support is not small.

But that would mean the canonical packet is localized around the AFE square-root point. This should correspond to a transition regime rather than an ordinary branch packet.

Therefore branch routing should handle it:

\[
\boxed{
x\approx 1/2
\quad\Rightarrow\quad
\text{transition/square-root route, not ordinary near-pure O2/O3 route.}
}
\]

If ordinary packets include the central tube, the theorem needs a routing condition:

\[
\text{ordinary branch excludes } |x-1/2|\le Q^{-A_{\mathrm{tr}}}
\]

or a transition theorem covers that mass.

This aligns with the broader architecture: ordinary/transition/singular are no longer Gate–1 definitions, but they remain atlas/routing regions.

So central tube should be assigned to the transition branch in O2/O3 branch-ratio survival.

---

# 6. Transition branch version

In the transition branch, the local coordinate should be centered at

\[
x=1/2.
\]

The ratio expansion is

\[
\mathcal R_\pm(s)
=
e^{i\Theta}
(1-8s+O(s^2)).
\]

The first-order term

\[
-8s
\]

is odd. If the transition coordinate retains the odd direction, then plus/dual anti-lock is controlled by the first moment in \(s\).

If a transition packet is symmetric and only even modes are retained, the odd term may vanish. Then one needs either:

- phase variation in \(\Theta\),
- next-order \(s^2\) terms,
- asymmetry of weights,
- or a transition O2/O3 version of the graph theorem.

Thus central transition O2/O3 requires:

\[
\boxed{
\text{the transition model must see the odd fold-breaking direction }s.
}
\]

This is a finite symbol condition analogous to the earlier \(C_1\sim x\) quotient ellipticity.

So the central tube is not fatal, but it demands a transition-coordinate anti-lock lemma.

---

# 7. Higher \(k\le d_0\)

For \(k>1\), central trouble occurs when all \(k\) substituted slots lie in the central tube. Since \(k=O(1)\), the support penalty is roughly

\[
\delta^{k/2}
\]

in norm, not enormous but polynomially usable.

Outside the central tube, at least one substituted slot has ratio variation. A low-degree polynomial relation among the substitution ratios cannot remain constant unless all relevant slots are central or the ratios satisfy a finite algebraic relation.

Thus the same central/outside split handles all \(k\le d_0\):

\[
\boxed{
\text{central-substitution sectors are small/support-routed;}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{noncentral sectors have branch-ratio variation.}
}
\]

The constants depend on \(d_0\), but \(d_0=O(1)\).

---

# 8. Finite anti-lock theorem with central split

A precise theorem target:

Let \(\mathcal R_j(x)\) be the finite set of branch substitution ratios. Let \(d_0=O(1)\). For a chosen tube width \(\delta=Q^{-A_c}\), decompose the one-particle domain into:

\[
\Omega_{\mathrm{cen}}=\{|x-1/2|\le\delta\},
\]

\[
\Omega_{\mathrm{out}}=\Omega\setminus\Omega_{\mathrm{cen}}.
\]

Assume:

1. central support is small or transition-routed:

\[
\mu(\Omega_{\mathrm{cen}})\le Q^{-A}\mu(\Omega)
\]

or its mass is assigned to transition treatment;

2. outside variation:

\[
\operatorname{dist}
\left(
1,
\mathcal P_{d_0}(\{\mathcal R_j|_{\Omega_{\mathrm{out}}}\})
\right)
\ge Q^{-A};
\]

3. transition anti-lock on central mass if not discarded.

Then

\[
D_{-n}
\notin
\operatorname{span}\{D_{-n+1},\ldots,D_{-n+d_0}\}
\]

at polynomial scale.

This is the clean finite near-pure theorem.

---

# 9. Relation to \(m_{\can}\sim x^2\)

The elliptic multiplier audit gives:

\[
m_+(x)\sim x^2,
\qquad
m_-(1-x)\sim (1-x)^2.
\]

Therefore the substitution ratio has the explicit fold asymmetry:

\[
\mathcal R_\pm(x)
\sim
\frac{(1-x)^2}{x^2}.
\]

This is enough to prove outside variation.

So the same elliptic multiplier theorem feeds:

1. pure branch graph non-polynomiality via \(1/x^2\);
2. near-pure branch-ratio anti-lock via \((1-x)^2/x^2\).

This is a useful unification.

---

# 10. Final status

The central-tube obstruction is manageable if the architecture treats \(x\approx1/2\) as transition-routed or proves a transition odd-mode anti-lock lemma.

Thus the near-pure fixed-\(\alpha\) route now has a plausible proof plan:

1. show \(d_0=O(1)\);
2. identify substitution ratios \(\mathcal R_j\);
3. split central versus noncentral;
4. outside central tube, use fold asymmetry \((1-x)^2/x^2\);
5. central tube either has small support or is handled by transition O2/O3;
6. conclude finite Schur complement.

This makes fixed-\(\alpha\) O2/O3 survival substantially more plausible and reduces reliance on \(\alpha\)-averaging.
