## Agent 4 response — hidden connections after the \(Y12\)-normality refinement

The update changes Agent 4’s work in one important way: O2/O3 should no longer be treated as isolated from the downstream forbidden channel. The \(Y12/K_9\) channel affects the branch-charge bandwidth of the pre-extraction Stack weight, so proving \(Y12\)-normality first can simplify the O2/O3 near-pure Schur block. 

## 1. Main hidden connection: \(Y12\) controls both D4 and O2/O3 complexity

The downstream target is now

\[
[\mathsf C_{Y12}]\neq0
\quad\text{in}\quad
\mathcal Q_{9,2}^{\forb}
=
\mathcal Y_9/
(\mathcal T_{\par}+\mathcal T_7+\mathcal T_{<7}+\mathcal{II}_{\arith,9}).
\]

If this holds, the D4 wall can be rank one, roughly

\[
E_{\wall}^{K9}
=
\frac{
|H|^2
|\beta_9\Gamma_G+\alpha_9\Gamma_{G,2}|^2
}{
\mathcal N_{\D4}
},
\qquad \alpha_9\ne0.
\]

If the covariant residual absorbs or cancels the \(\beta_9\Gamma_G\) part, then the wall is essentially

\[
\frac{|H|^2|\Gamma_{G,2}|^2}{\mathcal N_{\D4}}.
\]

That matters upstream because the pre-extraction O2/O3 weight is

\[
W_{\bd}
=
N_{\sm}|H|^2|K_{\D4}\Gamma|^2.
\]

A cleaner rank-one \(Y12\) wall likely lowers the branch-charge degree of \(|K_{\D4}\Gamma|^2\), hence lowers

\[
d_0=d_N+2d_H+2d_\Gamma.
\]

Lower \(d_0\) means the determinant survival problem involves fewer near-pure sectors:

\[
D_{-n},D_{-n+1},\ldots,D_{-n+d_0}.
\]

So:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Prove \(Y12\)-normality before spending more effort on the O2/O3 near-pure block.}
}
\]

---

## 2. \(E_2\) is now part of the \(Y12\)-normality audit

The forbidden quotient must be defined relative to the **full actual-source manifold**, not just the truncated finite packet. So the audit is not only

\[
[\mathsf C_{Y12}]
\notin
\mathcal T_{\par}+\mathcal T_7+\mathcal T_{<7}+\mathcal{II}_{\arith,9}.
\]

It must include coherent completion directions:

\[
[\mathsf C_{Y12}]
\notin
\mathcal T_{\par}
+\mathcal T_7
+\mathcal T_{<7}
+\mathcal{II}_{\arith,9}
+\mathcal T_{E_2}^{Y12}.
\]

This is a genuine hidden dependency. If \(E_2\) carries a free \(Y12\)-signature direction, then \(Y12\) may be actual-source tangent/coherent rather than forbidden. If \(E_2\) has no such signature, or contributes only a fixed nonfree correction, then the \(Y12\) channel remains viable.

The audit should classify

\[
\Pi_{\Sigma(Y12)}E_2
\]

as one of:

\[
\boxed{
\text{absent, fixed, tangent, nuisance, or free.}
}
\]

Only “free tangent” threatens the downstream channel.

---

## 3. The master invariant is source-polarization degree

The cleanest way to prove \(Y12\)-normality is not coefficient expansion. It is a graded-signature argument.

The \(Y12\) carrier has signature

\[
\Sigma(Y12)
=
(
\text{source-Hessian degree }2,
\text{mixed branch }12,
\text{same-point},
\text{one whitening slot},
\text{reflection/source-grade parity},
\text{order }9
).
\]

Ordinary EF parameter tangents are source-linear:

\[
\partial_m,\quad \partial_\lambda,\quad \partial_\alpha,
\quad\text{normalization/chart variations}
\]

change phases, weights, cutoffs, scales, and gamma factors. Even their second parameter derivatives remain curvatures of the EF representation unless the parameter changes the nonlinear source map \(N_0\) or whitening geometry.

So the desired structural theorem is:

\[
\Pi_{\Sigma(Y12)}
(
\mathcal T_{\par}
+
\mathcal T_7
+
\mathcal T_{<7}
+
\mathcal{II}_{\arith,9}
+
\mathcal T_{E_2}^{Y12}
)
=0,
\]

while

\[
\Pi_{\Sigma(Y12)}
T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]
\ne0.
\]

This would prove the \(Y12\) channel without needing a full coefficient audit.

---

## 4. \(\alpha\)-twisting remains compatible only if it is source-linear

The \(\alpha\)-tradeoff is now sharper. \(\alpha\) helps O2/O3 determinant survival by branch-charge projection, but if admitted as an actual-source parameter it enlarges

\[
T_u\mathcal M_{\sing}^{\arith}.
\]

The compatibility condition is:

\[
[\mathsf C_{Y12}]
\notin
\mathcal T_{\par}^{(\alpha)}
+\mathcal T_7
+\mathcal T_{<7}
+\mathcal{II}_{\arith,9}.
\]

A sufficient structural condition is:

\[
\Pi_{\Sigma(Y12)}\partial_\alpha u=0,
\qquad
\Pi_{\Sigma(Y12)}\partial_\alpha^2 u=0.
\]

This should hold if \(\alpha\) is merely a source-linear AFE tilt/phase/weight parameter. It can fail if \(\alpha\) changes \(N_0\), the whitening geometry, or a nonlinear source map.

So the \(\alpha\)-audit should not just ask whether \(\Theta_\alpha\) moves. It must ask whether \(\alpha\) has \(Y12\)-signature.

---

## 5. \(K_9\) must be well-conditioned, not merely nonzero

Even if

\[
K_9(T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}])\neq0,
\]

the residual can be quantitatively useless if the connection residual is ill-conditioned.

Let the actual-source tangent matrix split into lower and order-nine pieces:

\[
T=
\begin{pmatrix}
T_L\\
T_9
\end{pmatrix}.
\]

Constructing a covariant residual

\[
K_9(s)=s_9-Rs_L
\]

requires a controlled right inverse or Schur complement:

\[
R=T_9T_L^\dagger.
\]

The needed conditioning is:

\[
|T_La|\ge Q^{-A}|T_9a|
\quad
\text{for every actual-source tangent }a.
\]

Otherwise a tangent can be almost purely order nine, and \(K_9\) becomes unstable.

So the \(Y12\) audit has two parts:

\[
\boxed{
\text{signature nonvanishing}
}
\]

and

\[
\boxed{
\text{lower-order tangent observability / \(K_9\) conditioning.}
}
\]

---

## 6. Use a targeted \(Y12\)-completion estimate, not full \(K_9\)-completion if possible

The downstream upper could require

\[
\sum_p
\left|
K_{9,p}
T_{\tr\to\sing,p}J_{\tr,p}(R_X)
\right|^2
\le Q^{-B}\mu(\mathcal C).
\]

But if the proof uses only the \(Y12\)-signature projection, a weaker and more targeted estimate may suffice:

\[
\sum_p
\left|
\Pi_{\Sigma(Y12)}
T_{\tr\to\sing,p}J_{\tr,p}(R_X)
\right|^2
\le Q^{-B}\mu(\mathcal C).
\]

This could be much easier than full \(\mathrm{EFCompletion}_{K9}\).

So the residual hierarchy should be:

\[
\mathrm{EFCompletion}_{L^2}
\quad\text{for Gate–1,}
\]

\[
\mathrm{EFCompletion}_{Y12}
\quad\text{for the rank-one downstream channel,}
\]

and only if necessary:

\[
\mathrm{EFCompletion}_{K9}.
\]

This is a useful narrowing.

---

## 7. Central-tube routing connects Gate–1 and O2/O3

Both Gate–1 finite anti-lock and O2/O3 near-pure survival reduce to the AFE fold:

\[
x\mapsto 1-x.
\]

The curvature multiplier distinguishes the two sides by

\[
x^2-(1-x)^2=2x-1.
\]

For O2/O3, the first substitution ratio is roughly

\[
\mathcal R_\pm(x)\sim e^{i\Theta}\frac{(1-x)^2}{x^2}.
\]

Both can fail only near the central tube

\[
x\approx\frac12.
\]

So a single central-tube routing theorem should feed both:

\[
\boxed{
\text{ordinary branch excludes/controls }x\approx1/2,
\quad
\text{transition branch handles it.}
}
\]

This avoids proving separate central exceptional estimates for Gate–1 and O2/O3.

---

## 8. Immediate next finite checks

From Agent 4’s perspective, the next checks should be:

### A. \(Y12\)-signature survival including second-order and \(E_2\)

\[
[\mathsf C_{Y12}]\neq0
\quad\text{in}\quad
\mathcal Q_{9,2}^{\forb}.
\]

Specifically audit:

\[
\Pi_{\Sigma(Y12)}\mathcal{II}_{\arith,9},
\qquad
\Pi_{\Sigma(Y12)}E_2,
\qquad
\Pi_{\Sigma(Y12)}\mathcal T_7,
\qquad
\Pi_{\Sigma(Y12)}\mathcal T_{<7}.
\]

### B. \(\alpha\)-D4 compatibility

\[
\Pi_{\Sigma(Y12)}\partial_\alpha u=0,
\qquad
\Pi_{\Sigma(Y12)}\partial_\alpha^2u=0.
\]

### C. \(K_9\) conditioning

\[
|T_La|\ge Q^{-A}|T_9a|.
\]

### D. Compute \(d_0\) after the \(Y12\)-wall is fixed

If the wall is essentially \(|H|^2|\Gamma_{G,2}|^2\), \(d_0\) may be small. This directly reduces O2/O3 near-pure workload.

### E. Central-tube routing

Prove one fold-central theorem usable by both Gate–1 and O2/O3.

---

## Final status

The biggest hidden connection is:

\[
\boxed{
Y12\text{-normality simplifies both downstream D4 and O2/O3 branch survival.}
}
\]

The key downstream yes/no is now:

\[

\text{ in }

# 1. Define the right finite grading

=

d_{\src}
=
\text{source-polarization degree},
\]

\[
b
=
\text{branch bidegree / mixed-branch type},
\]

\[
\pi_{\mathrm{pt}}
=
\text{same-point versus transported-slot type},
\]

\[
w
=
\text{whitening-slot count},
\]

\[
\rho
=
\text{reflection/source-grade parity},
\]

\[
o
=
\text{singular order}.
\]

Then the \(Y12\) carrier has signature

\[
\Sigma(Y12)
=
(2,\ 12,\ \mathrm{same\text{-}point},\ 1,\ \rho_{Y12},\ 9).
\]

The key invariant is the first component:

\[
d_{\src}(Y12)=2.
\]

It comes from

\[
Y_{12}^{(2)}=D^2N_0[\xi_1,\xi_2].
\]

Actual EF parameter tangents should have

\[
d_{\src}\le1
\]

unless the parameter itself deforms the nonlinear source map \(N_0\) or the whitening geometry in a second-source direction.

Thus the first candidate separation theorem is:

\[
\boxed{
\Pi_{d_{\src}=2,\ b=12,\ \pi_{\mathrm{pt}}=\mathrm{same},\ w=1,\ o=9}
(\mathcal T_{\par}+\mathcal T_7+\mathcal T_{<7}+\mathcal{II}_{\arith,9})
=0.
}
\]

If true, \(Y12\) survives except for possible \(E_2\)-completion leakage.

---

# 2. First-order parameter tangents cannot create \(Y12\)

The first-order tangent space is generated by variations such as

\[
\partial_m u,\quad
\partial_\lambda u,\quad
\partial_\alpha u,\quad
\partial_{\mathrm{norm}}u,\quad
\partial_{\mathrm{chart}}u,
\]

plus allowed fixed structural parameters.

Each one differentiates the EF representation:

\[
u = B_2+P_2+D_2+E_2.
\]

These operations change:

\[
\text{phase},\quad
\text{log-weight},\quad
\text{scale},\quad
\text{cutoff},\quad
\text{gamma/background factor}.
\]

They do **not** insert a primitive source-Hessian

\[
D^2N_0[\xi_1,\xi_2].
\]

Therefore, under the assumption that \(\alpha\) is a source-linear AFE tilt and not a deformation of \(N_0\),

\[
\Pi_{\Sigma(Y12)}\mathcal T_{\par}=0.
\]

This is the main reason \(\alpha\)-twisting may remain compatible with D4 normality.

The condition to audit is:

\[
\partial_\alpha N_0=0,
\qquad
\partial_\alpha(\text{whitening geometry})\text{ has no }(d_{\src}=2,b=12,w=1)\text{ component}.
\]

If both hold, then

\[
\Pi_{\Sigma(Y12)}\partial_\alpha u
=
\Pi_{\Sigma(Y12)}\partial_\alpha^2u
=
0.
\]

---

# 3. Second fundamental form of the actual EF manifold

The delicate term is

\[
\mathcal{II}_{\arith,9}.
\]

Because \(Y12\) is quadratic, one must quotient legitimate second-order actual-source curvature. But not every quadratic-looking term is legitimate. The actual-source second fundamental form consists of second derivatives of the EF source family:

\[
\partial_{\theta_i}\partial_{\theta_j}
T_{\tr\to\sing}J_{\tr}(F_X^\theta),
\]

where \(\theta\) ranges only over actual arithmetic parameters:

\[
m,\lambda,\alpha,\text{ normalization/chart/cutoff parameters, fixed gamma/background structure}.
\]

If \(F_X^\theta\) depends linearly on EF atoms and the parameters act by phase/scale/weight deformation, then

\[
\partial_{\theta_i}\partial_{\theta_j}F_X^\theta
\]

still has source-linear polarization degree:

\[
d_{\src}=1.
\]

It may be second order in parameters, but not second order in independent source slots.

By contrast,

\[
Y12
\]

has source-polarization degree \(2\), coming from two independent source perturbations entering \(D^2N_0\).

So the strongest possible theorem is:

\[
\boxed{
\Pi_{d_{\src}=2}\mathcal{II}_{\arith,9}=0.
}
\]

If this fails, it means some actual parameter curvature genuinely changes the nonlinear source map or whitening in a way that creates a second-source-polarization term. That would be a serious obstruction to the \(Y12\)-normal channel.

---

# 4. Lower connection terms should not generate \(Y12\)

The quotient also removes

\[
\mathcal T_7+\mathcal T_{<7}.
\]

The key concern is whether the connection map

\[
\mathcal R_{7\to9}
\]

can send an order-seven carrier into a \(Y12\)-signature order-nine carrier.

The relevant test is:

\[
\Pi_{\Sigma(Y12)}
\mathcal R_{7\to9}(\mathcal Y_7)
=
0.
\]

This should be plausible if \(\mathcal R_{7\to9}\) is a linear connection operator preserving source-polarization degree and whitening-slot type. Since \(Y12\) has source-Hessian degree \(2\), it cannot be generated from lower source-linear/tangent data unless the order-seven input already has the same \(Y12\)-signature.

Thus audit:

\[
\Pi_{\Sigma(Y12)}\mathcal Y_7=0
\quad\Rightarrow\quad
\Pi_{\Sigma(Y12)}\mathcal R_{7\to9}\mathcal Y_7=0.
\]

Similarly:

\[
\Pi_{\Sigma(Y12)}
\mathcal R_{<7\to9}(\mathcal Y_{<7})
=
0.
\]

If either lower connection can produce source-Hessian/same-point/one-whitening mixed terms, then \(Y12\) may be connection-generated and not forbidden.

---

# 5. \(E_2\) is the remaining wild card

The completion/tail term \(E_2\) must be classified by the same signature.

There are four cases.

## Case A: absent

\[
\Pi_{\Sigma(Y12)}E_2=0.
\]

Then \(E_2\) does not affect the \(Y12\)-normal channel.

## Case B: fixed correction

\[
\Pi_{\Sigma(Y12)}E_2=e_{\mathrm{fixed}},
\]

with no free parameter. Then the actual-source manifold is shifted, but its tangent space does not gain the \(Y12\) direction. D4 normality survives after recentering the residual.

## Case C: nuisance killed by quotient

If \(E_2^{Y12}\) is routed into an already quotiented nuisance carrier, then it does not affect \(\mathcal Q_{9,2}^{\forb}\).

## Case D: free tangent

If \(E_2\) supplies an actual free \(Y12\)-signature direction, then

\[
[\mathsf C_{Y12}]
\]

may lie in \(\mathcal T_{E_2}^{Y12}\), killing the channel.

The route survives in cases A–C and likely fails in case D.

Thus the next exact finite question is:

\[
\boxed{
\Pi_{\Sigma(Y12)}E_2
\in
\{\text{absent, fixed, nuisance, free}\}?
}
\]

---

# 6. Consequence for the D4 wall

If \(Y12\) survives, then D4 contributes at order nine:

\[
s_9^{\D4}
=
H\left(
\beta_9[\mathsf C_{Y12}]\Gamma_G
+
\alpha_9[\mathsf C_{Y12}]\Gamma_{G,2}
\right)
+\text{connection-compatible pieces}.
\]

Applying the covariant residual \(K_9\) gives

\[
K_9(s^{\D4})
=
H\,K_9([\mathsf C_{Y12}])
\left(
\beta_9\Gamma_G+\alpha_9\Gamma_{G,2}
\right)
\]

unless the \(c_7[\mathsf C_7]\Gamma_G\) order-seven component also feeds into the same residual through \(\mathcal R_{7\to9}\).

The important point:

\[
\alpha_9\neq0.
\]

Therefore, if

\[
K_9([\mathsf C_{Y12}])\neq0,
\]

then there is at least a rank-one forbidden wall involving

\[
\ell_m(\Gamma)=\beta_9\Gamma_G+\alpha_9\Gamma_{G,2}.
\]

If the covariant residual is chosen so that the \(R_{7\to9}\)-compatible \(\Gamma_G\) part cancels, then the wall is cleaner:

\[
\ell_m(\Gamma)=\alpha_9\Gamma_{G,2}.
\]

This is preferable, because it lowers the branch-degree of the wall factor and simplifies O2/O3.

So the refined target is:

\[
\boxed{
K_9([\mathsf C_{Y12}])\neq0
\quad\text{and, if possible,}\quad
K_9(c_7[\mathsf C_7])=0.
}
\]

Then D4 forbidden energy is essentially the \(\Gamma_{G,2}\) channel.

---

# 7. Impact on branch-charge bandwidth \(d_0\)

If the wall is

\[
|H|^2|\Gamma_{G,2}|^2,
\]

then

\[
d_\Gamma
\]

may be smaller than for the full raw anisotropy

\[
|\Gamma_G|^2+|\Gamma_{G,2}|^2
\]

or a general moving linear combination.

Recall:

\[
d_0=d_N+2d_H+2d_\Gamma.
\]

Thus proving the clean \(Y12\)-rank-one wall may reduce the O2/O3 near-pure Schur block dimension.

This is the direct O2/O3 payoff of downstream normality.

The next O2/O3 computation should wait until \(d_\Gamma\) is known from the \(K_9/Y12\) wall.

---

# 8. Finite theorem: \(Y12\)-normality package

The complete finite package is:

## Y12-signature survival

\[
\Pi_{\Sigma(Y12)}
T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]
\neq0.
\]

## Tangent annihilation

\[
\Pi_{\Sigma(Y12)}
(\mathcal T_{\par}+\mathcal{II}_{\arith,9})
=0.
\]

## Lower connection annihilation

\[
\Pi_{\Sigma(Y12)}
(\mathcal T_7+\mathcal T_{<7})
=0.
\]

## Completion classification

\[
\Pi_{\Sigma(Y12)}E_2
\]

is absent, fixed, or nuisance, not free tangent.

## Conditioning

The residual \(K_9\) has polynomial norm:

\[
\|K_9\|\le Q^A,
\]

and the tangent lower-order observability condition holds:

\[
|T_La|\ge Q^{-A}|T_9a|.
\]

Then:

\[
[\mathsf C_{Y12}]
\neq0
\quad\text{in}\quad
\mathcal Q_{9,2}^{\forb}.
\]

---

# 9. Current best route

Do not attack the whole D4 plane first. Attack only

\[
[\mathsf C_{Y12}].
\]

The smallest viable downstream theorem is rank one:

\[
K_9(T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}])\neq0.
\]

That is enough because

\[
\alpha_9\Gamma_{G,2}
\]

is present in \(L_{\clean}\).

Rank two can wait.

---

# 10. Final status

The hidden connection is now explicit:

\[
\boxed{
Y12\text{-normality is a downstream contradiction channel and an O2/O3 simplifier.}
}
\]

The next most valuable audit is finite and structural:

\[
\boxed{
\Pi_{\Sigma(Y12)}
(
\mathcal T_{\par}
+\mathcal T_7
+\mathcal T_{<7}
+\mathcal{II}_{\arith,9}
+\mathcal T_{E_2}^{Y12}
)

and

## Agent 4 continuation — stress test of the \(Y12\)-signature filtration

The current proposed downstream/O2-O3 simplifier is:

\[
[\mathsf C_{Y12}]\neq0
\quad\text{in}\quad
\mathcal Q_{9,2}^{\forb}
=
\mathcal Y_9/
(\mathcal T_{\par}+\mathcal T_7+\mathcal T_{<7}+\mathcal{II}_{\arith,9}+\mathcal T_{E_2}^{Y12}).
\]

The proof route is a signature projection:

\[
\Pi_{\Sigma(Y12)}
(
\mathcal T_{\par}
+\mathcal T_7
+\mathcal T_{<7}
+\mathcal{II}_{\arith,9}
+\mathcal T_{E_2}^{Y12}
)
=0,
\]

while

\[
\Pi_{\Sigma(Y12)}T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]\ne0.
\]

I now stress-test that filtration.

---

# 1. What can imitate \(Y12\)?

The \(Y12\) carrier has signature

\[
\Sigma(Y12)
=
(d_{\src}=2,\ b=12,\ \mathrm{same\text{-}point},\ w=1,\ \rho,\ o=9),
\]

coming from

\[
Y_{12}^{(2)}=D^2N_0[\xi_1,\xi_2]
\]

with one same-point whitening slot.

A legitimate actual-source term could imitate it only if one of the following mechanisms exists:

1. an actual EF parameter has **second source-polarization degree**;
2. a first-order tangent, after connection transport \(7\to9\), creates source-Hessian degree \(2\);
3. a lower-order coefficient already contains hidden \(Y12\)-signature and is lifted to order \(9\);
4. \(E_2\) contains a free \(Y12\)-signature completion direction;
5. the transition map \(T_{\tr\to\sing}\) changes signature grading and maps an allowed class into \(Y12\);
6. whitening variation contributes a second-source same-point term.

So the signature proof must rule out all six.

---

# 2. Actual EF parameter curvature

Let actual finite EF packets be parametrized as

\[
F_X^\theta
=
B_2^\theta+P_{2,\le X}^\theta+D_{2,\le X}^\theta.
\]

For ordinary parameters

\[
\theta\in\{m,\lambda,\alpha,\text{cutoff},\text{normalization},\text{chart}\},
\]

the dependence is through:

\[
e^{\pm it\log n},\quad
\text{weights},\quad
\chi\text{-factors},\quad
\text{cutoffs},\quad
\text{gamma factors}.
\]

These are all source-linear deformations of the EF atoms.

Thus:

\[
\partial_{\theta_i}F_X^\theta
\]

is source-linear, and

\[
\partial_{\theta_i}\partial_{\theta_j}F_X^\theta
\]

is still source-linear: it differentiates the same EF atom twice, but does not introduce a bilinear Hessian

\[
D^2N_0[\xi_1,\xi_2].
\]

So, provided \(\theta\) does not deform \(N_0\) itself,

\[
\Pi_{d_{\src}=2}\mathcal{II}_{\arith,9}=0.
\]

This is the core argument.

The exact condition to record is:

\[
\boxed{
\partial_\theta N_0=0
\quad\text{and}\quad
\partial_\theta^2 N_0=0
\quad\text{for all admitted actual EF parameters.}
}
\]

If any admitted parameter changes \(N_0\), the \(Y12\)-signature proof is no longer automatic.

---

# 3. Whitening variation is the subtle exception

Even if \(N_0\) is fixed, the whitening map can vary with parameters.

The \(Y12\) carrier includes one same-point whitening slot. So variations of whitening might create terms with

\[
w=1.
\]

The question is whether whitening variation also creates

\[
d_{\src}=2,\quad b=12,\quad \text{same-point}
\]

in the same way as \(Y12\).

If whitening depends linearly on the source through local Gram data, then its first variation can be source-linear. Coupling that with a source-linear EF tangent could produce a quadratic-looking term.

This is the most dangerous imitation channel.

We need a refined filtration:

\[
d_{\src}
=
d_{\mathrm{external\ EF}}
+
d_{\mathrm{whitening}}.
\]

Then \(Y12\) has the special form:

\[
D^2N_0[\xi_1,\xi_2]
\otimes
D(G^{-1/2})[\cdot]
\]

with a **primitive nonlinear source-Hessian** plus one whitening slot.

A whitening-induced tangent has the form:

\[
DN_0[\xi]\otimes D(G^{-1/2})[\eta]
\]

or possibly

\[
N_0\otimes D^2(G^{-1/2})[\xi,\eta].
\]

These may have total degree \(2\), but they do **not** have the primitive \(D^2N_0[\xi_1,\xi_2]\) polarization.

So the signature must distinguish:

\[
\boxed{
\text{primitive source-Hessian degree}
}
\]

from

\[
\boxed{
\text{product of source-linear variation and whitening variation.}
}
\]

Define a refined invariant:

\[
h_N=\text{order of derivative hitting }N_0.
\]

Then:

\[
h_N(Y12)=2.
\]

Whitening-induced quadratic tangents have:

\[
h_N\le1.
\]

So the projection should be to

\[
\Sigma(Y12)
=
(h_N=2,\ b=12,\ \mathrm{same\text{-}point},\ w=1,\rho,o=9).
\]

This resolves the whitening imitation risk.

---

# 4. Lower connection \(7\to9\)

The connection terms

\[
\mathcal T_7,\quad \mathcal T_{<7}
\]

could only create \(Y12\) if the connection operator changes the primitive \(N_0\)-derivative order:

\[
h_N\le1\quad\mapsto\quad h_N=2.
\]

But connection transport should be linear in the existing carrier data and should not differentiate \(N_0\) a second time. It may multiply by curvature/gamma/scalar factors, change singular order, or mix lower coefficients, but it should preserve \(h_N\).

Thus the theorem needed is:

\[
\boxed{
\mathcal R_{7\to9},\ \mathcal R_{<7\to9}
\text{ preserve }h_N.
}
\]

Then, if no lower carrier has \(h_N=2,b=12,w=1\), lower connection cannot generate \(Y12\).

This is a finite structural audit.

---

# 5. Transition map \(T_{\tr\to\sing}\)

The transition-to-singular map changes coordinates and basis. It might mix orders and whitening slots, but it should not change the primitive source-Hessian order \(h_N\). It is a chart transition acting on already-formed source carriers.

So require:

\[
\boxed{
T_{\tr\to\sing}\text{ preserves }h_N\text{ and branch polarization }b
\text{ modulo known parity signs.}
}
\]

Then

\[
T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]
\]

still has

\[
h_N=2,\quad b=12.
\]

The nonvanishing part is:

\[
\Pi_{\Sigma(Y12)}
T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]\neq0.
\]

This can fail if singular projection kills same-point/one-whitening \(Y12\) entirely, but not because of tangent contamination.

Thus the transition audit splits:

1. preservation of signature;
2. nonzero image in \(\mathcal Y_9\).

---

# 6. \(E_2\) classification under refined signature

The completion term \(E_2\) can be dangerous only if it contains a free term with

\[
h_N=2,\quad b=12,\quad \mathrm{same\text{-}point},\quad w=1,\quad o=9.
\]

Most analytic tail/completion terms are source-linear corrections to the EF expansion. They should have

\[
h_N\le1.
\]

If \(E_2\) is built from regularized tail completion of the same AFE source, it likely does not contain primitive \(D^2N_0\). Then

\[
\Pi_{\Sigma(Y12)}E_2=0.
\]

But if \(E_2\) includes nonlinear regularization of the local source model or whitening correction terms, it may contain \(h_N=2\)-type pieces.

So classify \(E_2\) by \(h_N\):

- If \(h_N(E_2)\le1\), safe.
- If \(h_N(E_2)=2\) but branch/signature differs, safe.
- If \(h_N=2,b=12,w=1,o=9\) but fixed, recenter.
- If that component is freely variable, \(Y12\) channel dies.

This is now a precise finite check.

---

# 7. Effect of \(\alpha\)

If \(\alpha\) is admitted and acts only as a phase/tilt/cutoff parameter of EF atoms, then:

\[
h_N(\partial_\alpha u)\le1,
\qquad
h_N(\partial_\alpha^2u)\le1.
\]

So \(\alpha\) is safe.

If \(\alpha\) changes the whitening geometry, then \(\partial_\alpha^2u\) can include whitening-quadratic terms but still should have

\[
h_N\le1
\]

unless it changes \(N_0\).

So the sufficient \(\alpha\)-D4 compatibility condition is:

\[
\boxed{
\partial_\alpha^j N_0=0\quad(j=1,2),
}
\]

and

\[
\boxed{
\partial_\alpha^j T_{\tr\to\sing}
\text{ preserves }h_N\text{ for }j\le2.
}
\]

Then \(\alpha\)-twisting does not kill \(Y12\)-normality.

---

# 8. The refined forbidden quotient

Use the refined signature to define

\[
\mathcal Q_{9,2}^{\forb}
\]

more sharply:

\[
\mathcal Q_{9,2}^{\forb}
=
\mathcal Y_{9,h_N=2,b=12,w=1,\mathrm{same},\rho}
/
\Pi_{\Sigma(Y12)}
(
\mathcal T_7+\mathcal T_{<7}+\mathcal{II}_{\arith,9}+\mathcal T_{E_2}
).
\]

But if the preservation theorems hold, the denominator projection is zero. Then

\[
\mathcal Q_{9,2}^{\forb}
=
\mathcal Y_{\Sigma(Y12)}
\]

on that component, and the question is only whether

\[
T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]
\]

has nonzero component there.

This is much simpler.

---

# 9. Consequence for \(K_9\)

The covariant residual \(K_9\) should be chosen as a normal covector to the actual-source tangent plus second fundamental form.

On the \(Y12\)-signature component, if all actual-source tangent/curvature projections vanish, then \(K_9\) can simply be the projection

\[
\Pi_{\Sigma(Y12)}.
\]

This avoids conditioning problems from general Schur complements.

Thus, under signature separation:

\[
K_9^{Y12}:=\Pi_{\Sigma(Y12)}
\]

is a stable residual with norm \(1\), not a potentially ill-conditioned moving covector.

This is a major simplification.

The \(K_9\)-conditioning issue remains only if we cannot isolate \(Y12\) by signature and must use a general Schur complement.

So the signature theorem also solves the \(K_9\)-conditioning problem.

---

# 10. Downstream wall under signature projection

If

\[
K_9=\Pi_{\Sigma(Y12)},
\]

then

\[
K_9(T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_7])=0
\]

unless \([\mathsf C_7]\) also has \(Y12\)-signature.

Likely it does not.

And

\[
K_9(T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}])\ne0.
\]

Then the D4 residual is clean:

\[
K_9(\delta s_{\D4})
=
H\,\alpha_9\,K_9([\mathsf C_{Y12}])\,\Gamma_{G,2}.
\]

So the forbidden wall factor becomes:

\[
E_{\wall}^{Y12}
=
\frac{
|H|^2|\Gamma_{G,2}|^2
}{
\mathcal N_{\D4}
}
\cdot
\|K_9([\mathsf C_{Y12}])\|^2|\alpha_9|^2.
\]

This is the best possible case:

- rank one;
- no moving linear form;
- lower branch-charge bandwidth;
- no \(K_9\)-conditioning issue.

So the next finite target should be exactly this:

\[
\boxed{
\Pi_{\Sigma(Y12)}[\mathsf C_7]=0,
\qquad
\Pi_{\Sigma(Y12)}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]\ne0.
}
\]

---

# 11. Failure scenarios

The signature route fails if:

1. \(E_2\) has a free \(Y12\)-signature tangent;
2. \(\partial_\alpha^2 N_0\) or whitening variation creates primitive \(h_N=2,b=12,w=1\);
3. lower connection \(7\to9\) creates \(h_N=2,b=12,w=1\);
4. transition-to-singular projection kills \(Y12\);
5. \([\mathsf C_7]\) also has \(Y12\)-signature, causing the wall to be a moving \(\beta_9\Gamma_G+\alpha_9\Gamma_{G,2}\) form rather than pure \(\Gamma_{G,2}\).

Among these, 1 and 4 are the most serious.

---

# 12. Final refined theorem

The clean \(Y12\)-normality theorem should be:

## Signature preservation

The operators

\[
T_{\tr\to\sing},
\quad
\mathcal R_{7\to9},
\quad
\mathcal R_{<7\to9},
\quad
\partial_\theta,\quad
\partial_{\theta_i}\partial_{\theta_j}
\]

for all admitted actual-source parameters preserve the primitive nonlinear-source derivative degree \(h_N\), except for explicitly classified fixed corrections.

## Signature exclusion

Actual EF tangents, their second fundamental form, lower connection terms, and \(E_2\)-free tangents have no component in

\[
\Sigma(Y12)=(h_N=2,b=12,\mathrm{same},w=1,o=9,\rho).
\]

## Carrier nonvanishing

\[
\Pi_{\Sigma(Y12)}
T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]
\ne0,
\]

and preferably

\[
\Pi_{\Sigma(Y12)}
T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_7]
=0.
\]

Then finite D4 has a stable rank-one forbidden channel through \(\Gamma_{G,2}\).

---

## Final status

This continuation strengthens the route.

The \(Y12\) normality problem is no longer a general Schur-complement/tangent computation if we can use the refined primitive-source-Hessian signature \(h_N=2\). It becomes a finite grading theorem.

Best case:

\[
K_9=\Pi_{\Sigma(Y12)},
\]

and the downstream wall is simply

\[
|H|^2|\Gamma_{G,2}|^2.
\]

That would materially simplify both the D4 contradiction and the O2/O3 bounded-charge survival theorem.

\[

}

D_{-n+1},\ldots,D_{-n+d_0}.

---

# 1. Branch-charge convention

Use

\[
\operatorname{ch}(+)= -1,
\qquad
\operatorname{ch}(-)= +1,
\qquad
\operatorname{ch}(0)=0
\]

for plus, folded-dual, and background/completion components.

The full finite determinant decomposes as

\[
D_{\full}=\sum_s D_s.
\]

The pure plus determinant has

\[
s=-n,\qquad n=R+2.
\]

A pre-extraction Stack weight decomposes as

\[
W_{\bd}=\sum_\tau W_\tau.
\]

A cross term

\[
D_s\overline{D_{s'}}W_\tau
\]

is branch-charge resonant only if

\[
s-s'+\tau=0.
\]

So \(D_{-n}\) can interact with \(D_s\) only if

\[
s+n=\tau.
\]

If \(|\tau|\le d_0\), only

\[
D_{-n+1},\ldots,D_{-n+d_0}
\]

can resonate with \(D_{-n}\).

---

# 2. The correct pre-extraction weight

Use a raw smooth bounded-degree weight:

\[
W_{\bd}
=
N_{\sm}|H|^2|\ell_m(\Gamma)|^2,
\]

with denominators such as \(\mathcal N_{\D4}\) frozen on dyadic slices.

Do **not** use capped factors before determinant survival, such as

\[
F_1,\quad F_{23},\quad F_{\wall}.
\]

Do **not** use unfrozen reciprocals such as

\[
\mathcal N_{\D4}^{-1}
\]

inside the branch-survival proof.

Caps and reciprocals can introduce high branch-charge complexity and destroy the finite near-pure reduction.

---

# 3. Computing \(d_0\)

Write

\[
d_0=d_N+2d_H+2d_\Gamma.
\]

Here:

- \(d_N\) is the branch degree of the smooth reference/source weight \(N_{\sm}\);
- \(d_H\) is the branch degree of the scalar D4 amplitude \(H\);
- \(d_\Gamma\) is the branch degree of the forbidden wall linear form \(\ell_m(\Gamma)\).

## \(N_{\sm}\)

If \(N_{\sm}\) is a full local source energy,

\[
N_{\sm}\sim |u_+ + u_- + u_0|^2,
\]

then

\[
d_N\le 2.
\]

If one uses the branch-neutral part only,

\[
N_{\sm,0}
=
|u_+|^2+|u_-|^2+|u_0|^2,
\]

then

\[
d_N=0.
\]

For determinant survival, the branch-neutral version is preferable if it is sufficient for the aggregate theorem.

## \(H\)

If the normalized D4 scalar amplitude is effectively linear in the source variable,

\[
H\sim q_0u
\]

with \(q_0\) branch-neutral, then

\[
d_H=1.
\]

If the unnormalized graded expression behaves like

\[
H\sim q_0u^3,
\]

then

\[
d_H=3.
\]

The architecture should use the normalized live D4 amplitude, not an unnecessarily expanded cubic proxy, if the normalization is legitimate. This can reduce \(d_0\) substantially.

## \(\ell_m(\Gamma)\)

If \(Y12\)-normality gives a rank-one wall

\[
\ell_m(\Gamma)
=
\beta_9(m)\Gamma_G+\alpha_9(m)\Gamma_{G,2},
\qquad \alpha_9\ne0,
\]

then \(d_\Gamma\) depends on the branch degree of the active \(\Gamma\)-combination.

If the covariant \(K_9\) residual removes the \(\beta_9\Gamma_G\) component and leaves

\[
\ell_m(\Gamma)=\Gamma_{G,2},
\]

then \(d_\Gamma\) may be smaller.

This is one of the main payoffs of a clean \(Y12\) quotient.

---

# 4. Two wall cases

## Case A: moving linear wall

\[
\ell_m(\Gamma)
=
\beta_9\Gamma_G+\alpha_9\Gamma_{G,2}.
\]

Then

\[
d_\Gamma
=
\max(d_{\Gamma_G},d_{\Gamma_{G,2}}).
\]

If both are effectively linear at the normalized wall level,

\[
d_\Gamma=1.
\]

If \(\Gamma_G\) contributes cubic terms such as

\[
u-6q+2q^3
\]

without normalization/linearization, then

\[
d_\Gamma=3.
\]

Possible values:

\[
d_0=
d_N+2d_H+2d_\Gamma.
\]

A realistic normalized estimate is:

\[
d_N=0\text{ or }2,\quad d_H=1,\quad d_\Gamma=1,
\]

so

\[
d_0=4\text{ or }6.
\]

A conservative unnormalized estimate could be:

\[
d_N=2,\quad d_H=3,\quad d_\Gamma=3,
\]

so

\[
d_0=14.
\]

Still \(O(1)\), but less pleasant.

## Case B: clean \(Y12\) wall

If the covariant residual gives

\[
\ell_m(\Gamma)=\Gamma_{G,2},
\]

and \(\Gamma_{G,2}\) is normalized as a linear wall quantity, then

\[
d_\Gamma=1.
\]

If also \(N_{\sm}\) is taken branch-neutral and \(H\) is linear, then

\[
\boxed{
d_0=4.
}
\]

That is the best current scenario.

So proving that \(K_9\) isolates the \(Y12/\Gamma_{G,2}\) channel is not merely a downstream convenience; it makes the O2/O3 Schur block very small.

---

# 5. Branch-charge reduction theorem

Assume

\[
W_{\bd}=\sum_{|\tau|\le d_0}W_\tau.
\]

Then

\[
\int |D_{\full}|^2W_{\bd}\,d\rho
\]

splits into branch-charge terms. The pure plus component \(D_{-n}\) can have resonant cross terms only with

\[
D_{-n+k},
\qquad
1\le k\le d_0.
\]

All sectors

\[
D_s,\qquad s>-n+d_0,
\]

are nonresonant relative to \(D_{-n}\) and should be killed by smooth branch-charge decay.

Thus determinant survival reduces to

\[
\boxed{
D_{-n}\notin
\operatorname{span}\{D_{-n+1},\ldots,D_{-n+d_0}\}
}
\]

in the smooth weighted geometry.

---

# 6. Near-pure Gram block

Define

\[
\mathbf D_{\near}
=
(D_{-n},D_{-n+1},\ldots,D_{-n+d_0}).
\]

The near-pure Gram matrix is

\[
G_{\near}
=
\left(
\langle D_{-n+i},D_{-n+j}\rangle_{W_{i-j}}
\right)_{0\le i,j\le d_0}.
\]

The needed Schur complement is

\[
\boxed{
G_{00}-G_{0,*}G_{*,*}^{-1}G_{*,0}
\ge
Q^{-A}G_{00}.
}
\]

Equivalently,

\[
\boxed{
\operatorname{dist}
\left(
D_{-n},
\operatorname{span}\{D_{-n+1},\ldots,D_{-n+d_0}\}
\right)^2
\ge
Q^{-A}\|D_{-n}\|^2.
}
\]

Since \(d_0=O(1)\), this is a fixed-size Hermitian problem.

---

# 7. Reduction to substitution-ratio anti-lock

Each \(D_{-n+k}\) replaces \(k=O(1)\) plus exterior factors by non-plus factors.

A coefficient in \(D_{-n+k}\) has the form

\[
C_{S,J}^{(k)}
=
C_S^{(+)}
\prod_{a\in J}\mathcal R_a
\cdot
\mathcal E_{S,J},
\]

where \(\mathcal R_a\) is a branch substitution ratio and \(\mathcal E_{S,J}\) is a bounded exterior correction.

Thus the near-pure span is generated by finite-degree expressions in a finite set of substitution ratios:

\[
\mathscr R
=
\{\mathcal R_{\pm},\mathcal R_0,\mathcal R_{\tail},\ldots\}.
\]

The Schur complement fails only if

\[
1
\]

is close to a low-degree expression in these ratios:

\[
1+\sum_{k=1}^{d_0}P_k(\mathscr R)\approx0.
\]

So the O2/O3 survival theorem becomes:

\[
\boxed{
\operatorname{dist}
\left(
1,
\mathcal P_{d_0}(\mathscr R)
\right)
\ge Q^{-A}.
}
\]

This is the finite low-degree branch-ratio anti-lock theorem.

---

# 8. The first ratio: plus-to-dual

The most important ratio is

\[
\mathcal R_{\pm}(x)
\sim
e^{i\Theta}
\frac{(1-x)^2}{x^2}
\]

after elliptic normalization

\[
m_{\can,+}(x)\sim \kappa x^2.
\]

Outside the central tube \(x=1/2\), this ratio is strongly nonconstant:

\[
\frac{d}{dx}\log \frac{(1-x)^2}{x^2}
=
-2\left(\frac1{1-x}+\frac1x\right).
\]

So outside the central tube the \(k=1\) ratio obstruction is controlled by fold asymmetry.

Near

\[
x=\frac12+s,
\]

\[
\frac{(1-x)^2}{x^2}
=
1-8s+O(s^2),
\]

so the ratio becomes nearly constant. That is the central-tube exception.

---

# 9. Central-tube connection

The same central tube appears in finite \(L^2\)-Gate–1 anti-lock, because

\[
x^2-(1-x)^2=2x-1.
\]

So the same theorem should handle both:

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{CentralTubeRouting}
}
\]

with two conclusions:

1. outside the tube, fold asymmetry gives anti-lock;
2. inside the tube, either mass is small, phase varies, or transition routing handles the packet.

Thus the O2/O3 near-pure block and Gate–1 finite AFE anti-lock should share one central-tube lemma.

---

# 10. Final bounded-charge O2/O3 theorem

Assume:

1. **Riesz normalization**

\[
\|A_{\le X,m}\|\le R^C.
\]

2. **Pure branch graph non-polynomiality**

\[
\operatorname{dist}(1/m_{\can,+},\mathbb C_R[x])\ge Q^{-A}.
\]

3. **Bounded-degree pre-extraction weight**

\[
W_{\bd}=N_{\sm}|H|^2|\ell_m(\Gamma)|^2
\]

with denominators frozen and branch-charge bandwidth \(d_0=O(1)\).

4. **Nonresonant charge decay**

Nonzero total branch-charge terms are negligible under the smooth aggregate measure.

5. **Near-pure Schur complement**

\[
D_{-n}
\notin
\operatorname{span}\{D_{-n+1},\ldots,D_{-n+d_0}\}
\]

at polynomial scale, equivalently low-degree branch-ratio anti-lock.

Then

\[
\int |D_{\full}|^2W_{\bd}\,d\rho
\ge
Q^{-A}
\int |D_{-n}|^2W_{\bd,0}\,d\rho.
\]

By \(R^C\)-normalization,

\[
\int \mathcal H_{23}(A_{\le X,\full})W_{\bd}\,d\rho
\ge Q^{-A}\mu.
\]

Then capped extraction can be applied.

---

## Final recommendation

Do not attack the full \(O(R)\)-branch determinant forest.

First prove the \(Y12\) quotient result and determine the actual wall:

- if \(\ell=\Gamma_{G,2}\), likely \(d_0\approx4\);
- if \(\ell=\beta_9\Gamma_G+\alpha_9\Gamma_{G,2}\), likely \(d_0\approx4\) to \(6\) under normalized degrees, larger only if unnormalized cubic terms remain.

Then solve the fixed-size near-pure Schur complement via the branch-ratio anti-lock theorem, with the central tube routed jointly with Gate–1.

## Agent 4 continuation — near-pure Schur complement as a fixed-degree moment problem

The previous reduction left us with the finite Schur block

\[
D_{-n},D_{-n+1},\ldots,D_{-n+d_0},
\qquad n=R+2,\quad d_0=O(1),
\]

where \(d_0\) is the bounded branch-charge bandwidth of the smooth pre-extraction Stack weight. The next useful step is to replace this determinant language by a fixed-degree moment problem for the branch substitution ratio. This is the cleanest fixed-\(\alpha\) O2/O3 survival formulation so far. The current architecture and \(Y12\)-wall context are as in the latest coordinator update. 

---

# 1. Effective substitution-ratio measure

The near-pure component \(D_{-n+k}\) is obtained from the pure plus component \(D_{-n}\) by replacing \(k\) plus slots with non-plus slots.

For \(k=1\), the replacement is controlled by a branch substitution ratio

\[
\mathcal R(x)
=
\frac{\text{non-plus symbol at }x}{\text{plus symbol at }x}.
\]

For folded-dual substitution,

\[
\mathcal R_\pm(x)
\sim
e^{i\Theta}
\frac{(1-x)^2}{x^2}
\]

after the elliptic normalization \(m_{\can,+}(x)\sim \kappa x^2\).

The pure plus exterior determinant induces an effective one-slot substitution measure. Informally,

\[
d\mu_{\mathrm{sub}}(x)
\]

is the distribution of one randomly selected exterior slot under the pure plus exterior mass

\[
|D_{-n}|^2W_{\bd,0}\,d\rho.
\]

Then the \(k=1\) contaminant is controlled by the image of \(\mathcal R\) in

\[
L^2(d\mu_{\mathrm{sub}}).
\]

The finite near-pure Schur complement is equivalent to

\[
\boxed{
1\notin
\operatorname{span}
\{\mathcal R,\mathcal R^{(2)},\ldots,\mathcal R^{(d_0)}\}
}
\]

at polynomial scale, in the effective exterior substitution geometry.

For a single dominant ratio, this becomes

\[
\boxed{
\operatorname{dist}_{L^2(\mu_{\mathrm{sub}})}
\left(
1,
\operatorname{span}\{\mathcal R,\mathcal R^2,\ldots,\mathcal R^{d_0}\}
\right)
\ge Q^{-A}.
}
\]

This is now a fixed-degree moment problem.

---

# 2. Moment determinant criterion

Let

\[
z=\mathcal R(x),
\]

and let \(\nu=\mathcal R_\#\mu_{\mathrm{sub}}\) be the pushforward measure.

Define the Gram matrix

\[
M_{ij}
=
\int z^i\overline{z^j}\,d\nu(z),
\qquad
1\le i,j\le d_0,
\]

and the vector

\[
b_i=\int \overline{z^i}\,d\nu(z).
\]

Then

\[
\operatorname{dist}^2(1,\operatorname{span}\{z,\ldots,z^{d_0}\})
=
\|1\|^2-b^*M^{-1}b.
\]

Equivalently, it is the Schur complement of the block moment matrix

\[
\begin{pmatrix}
\int 1\,d\nu & \int \overline{z^j}\,d\nu\\
\int z^i\,d\nu & \int z^i\overline{z^j}\,d\nu
\end{pmatrix}_{1\le i,j\le d_0}.
\]

Thus the near-pure Schur condition is exactly:

\[
\boxed{
\text{the fixed moment system }1,z,\ldots,z^{d_0}
\text{ has polynomial Riesz lower bound.}
}
\]

Since \(d_0=O(1)\), no \(R\)-dependent determinant catastrophe remains.

---

# 3. A simple sufficient condition

A robust sufficient condition is that the ratio \(\mathcal R\) assumes at least \(d_0+1\) polynomially separated values on sets of polynomial \(\mu_{\mathrm{sub}}\)-mass.

Concretely, suppose there exist disjoint sets

\[
E_0,\ldots,E_{d_0}
\]

such that

\[
\mu_{\mathrm{sub}}(E_j)\ge Q^{-A}\mu_{\mathrm{sub}}(\Omega)
\]

and

\[
|\mathcal R(x)-z_j|\le Q^{-A}\quad \text{on }E_j,
\]

with the points \(z_j\) satisfying a Vandermonde lower bound

\[
\prod_{i<j}|z_i-z_j|\ge Q^{-A}.
\]

Then

\[
1,z,\ldots,z^{d_0}
\]

are \(Q^{-A}\)-Riesz on the pushforward measure, and hence

\[
\operatorname{dist}
\left(
1,\operatorname{span}\{z,\ldots,z^{d_0}\}
\right)
\ge Q^{-A}.
\]

So the finite O2/O3 anti-lock theorem reduces to finding \(d_0+1\) separated ratio values with polynomial mass.

---

# 4. Fold ratio supplies separated values outside the central tube

For the folded-dual ratio,

\[
\mathcal R_\pm(x)
\sim
e^{i\Theta}\frac{(1-x)^2}{x^2}.
\]

Ignoring the harmless global phase \(e^{i\Theta}\), define

\[
r(x)=\frac{(1-x)^2}{x^2}.
\]

Then

\[
\frac{d}{dx}\log r(x)
=
-2\left(\frac1{1-x}+\frac1x\right).
\]

On any interval

\[
x\in[c,1-c]\subset(0,1),
\]

this derivative is bounded away from zero. Hence \(r(x)\) is strongly monotone.

Therefore, if the effective substitution measure has polynomial mass in \(d_0+1\) separated \(x\)-subintervals away from \(x=1/2\), the moment determinant criterion follows.

This gives the clean theorem:

\[
\boxed{
\text{outside the central tube, fold curvature ratio variation proves near-pure anti-lock.}
}
\]

---

# 5. Central tube again

The only problematic region is

\[
x=\frac12+s,
\qquad |s|\le\delta.
\]

There,

\[
r(x)=1-8s+O(s^2).
\]

If the entire effective substitution measure is concentrated in

\[
|s|\le\delta,
\]

then the pushforward ratio measure is concentrated in an interval of diameter \(O(\delta)\), and the distance of \(1\) from the span of \(r,\ldots,r^{d_0}\) may be as small as \(O(\delta)\).

But if \(\delta\ge Q^{-A}\), this is still polynomial. If \(\delta\ll Q^{-A}\), then the packet is genuinely central and should be routed to transition analysis.

Thus the central tube is not fatal. It creates a scale split:

\[
\boxed{
\delta\ge Q^{-A}: \text{ratio variation is polynomial;}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\delta<Q^{-A}: \text{route to transition / central anti-lock theorem.}
}
\]

The transition branch should retain the odd coordinate \(s=x-1/2\), because the leading fold-breaking term is

\[
-8s.
\]

---

# 6. Multiple substitution ratios

In reality there are several substitution ratios:

\[
\mathscr R=
\{\mathcal R_\pm,\mathcal R_0,\mathcal R_{\tail},\ldots\}.
\]

The near-pure span is generated by degree-\(\le d_0\) expressions in these ratios.

A sufficient condition is still finite moment nondegeneracy: the map

\[
x\mapsto
(\mathcal R_1(x),\ldots,\mathcal R_M(x))
\]

must not be supported near the zero set of a nonzero polynomial of degree \(\le d_0\) that also approximates \(-1\).

Since \(\mathcal R_\pm\) alone is already strongly varying outside the central tube, the other ratios can only help unless they are arranged to cancel it exactly.

Thus the theorem can be stated using only \(\mathcal R_\pm\) as a separating coordinate, treating background/tail ratios as additional bounded perturbations.

---

# 7. Near-pure Schur theorem in final form

Let \(d_0=O(1)\). Let \(\mu_{\mathrm{sub}}\) be the effective one-slot pure plus exterior substitution measure. Suppose:

1. **Noncentral mass or transition routing**

   Either \(\mu_{\mathrm{sub}}\) has polynomial mass outside the central tube, or the central tube is assigned to transition treatment.

2. **Separated fold-ratio values**

   On the noncentral region, the ratio

   \[
   r(x)=\frac{(1-x)^2}{x^2}
   \]

   assumes \(d_0+1\) polynomially separated values on polynomial-mass subsets.

3. **Bounded perturbations**

   AFE weights, gamma factors, background ratios, and exterior correction terms perturb the ratio system by at most polynomial factors and do not collapse the Vandermonde determinant.

Then

\[
\operatorname{dist}
\left(
D_{-n},
\operatorname{span}\{D_{-n+1},\ldots,D_{-n+d_0}\}
\right)^2
\ge
Q^{-A}\|D_{-n}\|^2.
\]

This proves the near-pure branch survival theorem.

---

# 8. What remains genuinely nontrivial

The two nontrivial inputs are:

## Effective substitution measure lower spread

We need to know that \(\mu_{\mathrm{sub}}\) is not concentrated in too few \(x\)-locations.

This should follow from the pure plus exterior mass/Riesz basis. But it must be stated:

\[
\boxed{
\mu_{\mathrm{sub}}\text{ has polynomial spread across the active ordinary packet.}
}
\]

If the pure plus exterior mass itself concentrates on a tiny \(x\)-subinterval, then ratio variation fails. But such concentration should contradict Riesz normalization or be routed to a smaller packet.

## Perturbation control

The actual ratio is not exactly

\[
(1-x)^2/x^2.
\]

It includes AFE weights, gamma factors, and normalization terms. Need:

\[
\mathcal R_\pm(x)
=
e^{i\Theta}
\frac{(1-x)^2}{x^2}
(1+\epsilon(x)),
\]

with \(\epsilon\) not destroying the finite Vandermonde lower bound.

Since \(d_0=O(1)\), only \(C^{d_0}\)-level control is needed here, not \(R\)-level high derivatives. This is much easier than the divided-difference audit.

---

# 9. Interaction with \(Y12\)

If the \(Y12/K_9\) wall gives

\[
d_0=4
\]

instead of a larger value, then the ratio-moment theorem only needs five separated ratio values.

This is very manageable.

If the wall is more complicated and \(d_0=10\) or \(14\), the theorem still remains finite, but the central spread and Vandermonde conditions require more separated sets.

Thus \(Y12\)-normality directly lowers the complexity of O2/O3.

---

# Final status

The fixed-\(\alpha\) O2/O3 survival route has now reduced to a concrete finite moment theorem:

\[
\boxed{
1\notin
\operatorname{span}\{r,r^2,\ldots,r^{d_0}\},
\qquad
r(x)=\frac{(1-x)^2}{x^2},
}
\]

under the effective pure plus exterior substitution measure, modulo bounded perturbations.

Outside the central tube this is elementary monotone-ratio geometry. Inside the central tube, route to transition analysis or use the odd coordinate \(s=x-1/2\).

This is the most tractable fixed-\(\alpha\) O2/O3 formulation so far.

I’ll push the effective substitution-measure point: if the pure plus exterior determinant is Riesz-normalized, its one-slot marginal should be a Christoffel-type measure, so central concentration can be bounded rather than assumed.

## Agent 4 continuation — effective substitution measure as a Christoffel marginal

The previous reduction turned fixed-\(\alpha\) O2/O3 survival into a finite moment problem for the branch substitution ratio

\[
r(x)=\frac{(1-x)^2}{x^2}.
\]

The remaining nontrivial input was:

\[
\boxed{
\mu_{\mathrm{sub}}\text{ must have polynomial spread in }x.
}
\]

I now attack that spread condition. The key observation is that \(\mu_{\mathrm{sub}\) is not arbitrary. It is the one-point marginal of an exterior determinant/Vandermonde ensemble. That gives strong anti-concentration. This is exactly the kind of structural information needed after the bounded-charge reduction in the current stack. 

---

# 1. Pure plus exterior mass defines a polynomial ensemble

In the pure plus branch, the exterior determinant coefficient contains a Vandermonde factor. Schematically,

\[
D_{-n}
\quad\leadsto\quad
\sum_{S=\{x_1,\ldots,x_n\}}
C_S^+,
\qquad n=R+2,
\]

with

\[
C_S^+
\sim
\left(\prod_{a=1}^n a(x_a)\right)
(\det V_S)^2
\cdot
\text{elliptic divided-difference factor}.
\]

After Riesz normalization and assuming the elliptic multiplier factor is bounded above and below polynomially on the branch packet, the mass has the form

\[
d\mathbb P(S)
\propto
\prod_{a=1}^n w(x_a)
\,
|\Delta(x_1,\ldots,x_n)|^2
\,dx_1\cdots dx_n.
\]

This is a standard polynomial/Vandermonde ensemble.

The effective one-slot substitution measure is the one-point marginal:

\[
d\mu_{\mathrm{sub}}(x)
=
\frac1n
\mathbb E\left[\sum_{a=1}^n\delta_{x_a}\right].
\]

For orthonormal polynomials \(p_0,\ldots,p_{n-1}\) with respect to the base weight \(w(x)dx\), this marginal has density

\[
K_n(x,x)w(x),
\]

where

\[
K_n(x,x)=\sum_{j=0}^{n-1}|p_j(x)|^2
\]

is the Christoffel-Darboux kernel.

Thus the spread of \(\mu_{\mathrm{sub}}\) is not a new arithmetic mystery. It is a finite orthogonal-polynomial estimate.

---

# 2. Central tube cannot carry all exterior mass

Let

\[
I_{\mathrm{cen}}(\delta)
=
\{|x-1/2|\le\delta\}.
\]

The one-point mass is

\[
\mu_{\mathrm{sub}}(I_{\mathrm{cen}})
=
\int_{I_{\mathrm{cen}}} K_n(x,x)w(x)\,dx.
\]

For a Riesz-normalized polynomial basis on a fixed interval, the Christoffel kernel satisfies a polynomial bound

\[
K_n(x,x)\le R^C
\]

away from endpoint singularities, and here \(x=1/2\) is an interior point. Since

\[
n=R+2,
\]

one expects

\[
K_n(x,x)\ll R^C.
\]

Therefore

\[
\mu_{\mathrm{sub}}(I_{\mathrm{cen}}(\delta))
\le
R^C\delta.
\]

Take

\[
\delta=Q^{-A_c}.
\]

Since \(R=Q^{o(1)}\), this gives

\[
\mu_{\mathrm{sub}}(I_{\mathrm{cen}})
\le
Q^{-A_c+o(1)}.
\]

So central-tube mass is polynomially small as long as the tube is polynomially thin.

This directly handles the main exception in the fixed-\(\alpha\) ratio theorem.

---

# 3. Spread outside the central tube

Outside

\[
I_{\mathrm{cen}}(\delta),
\]

the fold ratio

\[
r(x)=\frac{(1-x)^2}{x^2}
\]

is monotone with derivative bounded below in logarithmic scale:

\[
\left|\frac{d}{dx}\log r(x)\right|
=
2\left(\frac1{x}+\frac1{1-x}\right)
\gg 1
\]

on compact subintervals of \((0,1)\).

Thus intervals in \(x\) of length \(\gg Q^{-A}\) map to \(r\)-values separated by \(\gg Q^{-A}\).

If \(\mu_{\mathrm{sub}}\) has no large gaps in \(x\), we can find \(d_0+1\) subintervals

\[
J_0,\ldots,J_{d_0}
\subset \Omega_{\mathrm{out}}
\]

with:

\[
\mu_{\mathrm{sub}}(J_j)\ge Q^{-A},
\]

and

\[
\operatorname{dist}(r(J_i),r(J_j))\ge Q^{-A}.
\]

The Christoffel marginal estimate supplies this if the base branch packet has thick support and the weight \(w\) is not itself concentrated on \(O(1)\) tiny intervals.

So the remaining spread condition is:

\[
\boxed{
w(x)dx\text{ is thick on the ordinary branch packet.}
}
\]

This is the same branch-thickness condition already needed for \(L^2\)-Gate–1. Good: no new geometric hypothesis is needed.

---

# 4. Fixed-degree Riesz lower bound

Once we have \(d_0+1\) separated \(r\)-values with polynomial mass, the fixed moment system

\[
1,r,r^2,\ldots,r^{d_0}
\]

has a polynomial Riesz lower bound.

Precisely, suppose there are sets \(J_j\) with

\[
\mu_{\mathrm{sub}}(J_j)\ge Q^{-A}
\]

and representative values \(z_j\) satisfying

\[
|z_i-z_j|\ge Q^{-A},\qquad i\ne j,
\]

while

\[
|r(x)-z_j|\le Q^{-A'}
\quad \text{on }J_j.
\]

Then the Vandermonde matrix

\[
(z_j^k)_{0\le j,k\le d_0}
\]

has determinant

\[
\prod_{i<j}(z_j-z_i),
\]

hence lower bound

\[
Q^{-A}
\]

because \(d_0=O(1)\).

Therefore,

\[
\operatorname{dist}
\left(
1,\operatorname{span}\{r,\ldots,r^{d_0}\}
\right)
\ge Q^{-A}
\]

in

\[
L^2(d\mu_{\mathrm{sub}}).
\]

This proves the near-pure Schur complement in the one-ratio model.

---

# 5. Perturbed ratio

The actual ratio is

\[
\mathcal R_\pm(x)
=
e^{i\Theta}
\frac{(1-x)^2}{x^2}
(1+\epsilon(x)).
\]

For fixed-degree \(d_0\), perturbation control only requires

\[
\|\epsilon\|_{C^{d_0}}\le Q^{-A}
\]

or more generally that \(\epsilon\) does not collapse the separated ratio values.

This is far weaker than the earlier high-order \(R+1\) derivative requirement needed for divided differences. Here \(d_0=O(1)\), so ordinary symbol smoothness suffices.

Thus, after the pure branch graph theorem has already handled high-order non-polynomiality, the near-pure branch survival only needs low-order control of AFE/gamma symbol ratios.

This is a major simplification.

---

# 6. Multiple ratios

Let

\[
\mathscr R=\{\mathcal R_1,\ldots,\mathcal R_M\}
\]

include plus-to-dual, plus-to-background, and plus-to-tail substitution ratios.

The near-pure span is generated by finite-degree expressions

\[
\mathcal P_{d_0}(\mathscr R).
\]

If one ratio, namely \(\mathcal R_\pm\), already yields a polynomial Riesz system

\[
1,\mathcal R_\pm,\ldots,\mathcal R_\pm^{d_0},
\]

then adding more ratios cannot make \(1\) closer to the span **unless the additional ratios provide cancellation directions**.

This sounds dangerous, but because the full near-pure space includes those directions, the correct finite theorem is:

\[
1\notin \mathcal P_{d_0}(\mathscr R).
\]

A sufficient condition is that all other ratios are functions of \(\mathcal R_\pm\) of degree \(\le d_0\) that do not include the constant relation, or that their joint image has \(d_0+1\) separated points in \(\mathbb C^M\).

The multivariate version is similar: choose \(d_0+1\) points \(x_j\) such that the evaluation matrix of a basis of \(\mathcal P_{d_0}(\mathscr R)\) has polynomially large smallest singular value.

Since \(\mathcal R_\pm\) alone is monotone, this is plausible unless the other ratios are adversarially tuned to force a low-degree identity.

Thus the exact finite theorem is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{the substitution-ratio map }
x\mapsto \mathscr R(x)
\text{ is not contained in a degree-}d_0\text{ algebraic hypersurface through }-1.
}
\]

But in practice, \(\mathcal R_\pm\) monotonicity should suffice.

---

# 7. Endpoint versus central issues

The fold ratio

\[
r(x)=\frac{(1-x)^2}{x^2}
\]

has singular behavior near \(x=0\) or \(x=1\). Ordinary packets should stay away from endpoints, or else branch routing changes.

If endpoints are included, variation is even stronger, but weights may be small or singular. The Christoffel kernel may also grow near endpoints. This is manageable with a Jacobi-type Riesz basis, but the clean theorem should assume the active ordinary packet is in

\[
x\in[c_-,c_+]\subset(0,1)
\]

or use endpoint-adapted weights.

Thus the only real interior exceptional point is \(x=1/2\).

---

# 8. Final near-pure theorem

A clean statement:

Let \(d_0=O(1)\), and let \(\mu_{\mathrm{sub}}\) be the effective one-slot marginal of the pure plus exterior determinant in an \(R^C\)-Riesz-normalized branch packet. Assume:

1. the base branch weight is thick on an interval \(I\subset(0,1)\);
2. \(\mu_{\mathrm{sub}}\) satisfies the Christoffel bound

\[
\mu_{\mathrm{sub}}(J)\le R^C |J|
\]

for intervals \(J\);

3. the central tube \(I_{\mathrm{cen}}(Q^{-A_c})\) is either removed/routed or has small \(\mu_{\mathrm{sub}}\)-mass;

4. the dual/plus substitution ratio satisfies

\[
\mathcal R_\pm(x)
=
e^{i\Theta}\frac{(1-x)^2}{x^2}(1+\epsilon(x)),
\qquad
\|\epsilon\|_{C^{d_0}}\le Q^{-A}.
\]

Then

\[
\operatorname{dist}
\left(
1,
\mathcal P_{d_0}(\mathscr R)
\right)
\ge Q^{-A}.
\]

Consequently,

\[
D_{-n}
\notin
\operatorname{span}
\{D_{-n+1},\ldots,D_{-n+d_0}\}
\]

at polynomial scale.

---

# 9. What remains

The remaining nontrivial items are now finite and clear:

1. prove the Christoffel/Riesz marginal bound for the pure plus exterior measure;
2. prove branch thickness/spread for the ordinary packet;
3. route or handle the central tube;
4. verify the actual substitution ratio has the folded curvature form with \(C^{d_0}\)-controlled perturbation;
5. compute \(d_0\) after the \(Y12\)-wall is fixed.

This is much more manageable than the original O2/O3 determinant anti-trough.

---

# Final status

The fixed-\(\alpha\) O2/O3 route now has a concrete proof skeleton:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Riesz exterior ensemble}
\Rightarrow
\text{Christoffel spread of substitution slots}
\Rightarrow
\text{fold-ratio monotonicity}
\Rightarrow
\text{fixed-degree moment lower bound}
\Rightarrow
\text{near-pure Schur complement}.
}
\]

The central tube is the only serious local exception, and it is shared with Gate–1. A single central-routing theorem should handle it for both modules.

## Agent 4 report — multiscale fold routing for Gate–1 and O2/O3

I followed the Agent 4 instruction: build one central-tube theorem that feeds both \(L^2\)-Gate–1 and bounded-charge O2/O3. The current architecture asks us to treat the \(x=1/2\) fold degeneracy as a **scale-routing problem**, not as a bad exceptional set. 

## Verdict

\[
\boxed{
\text{The central tube can be handled by multiscale routing with only polynomial loss.}
}
\]

The correct scale is \(R\)-polynomial, not \(Q\)-polynomial:

\[
\delta=R^{-C},
\qquad
R\asymp \frac{\log Q}{\log\log Q}.
\]

Then exterior-degree losses satisfy

\[
\delta^R=R^{-CR}=Q^{-O(1)}.
\]

So concentration near \(x=1/2\) is not a fatal degeneracy. It should be routed to transition-scale analysis.

---

# 1. Fold geometry

Use

\[
x=\frac{\log n}{Q}.
\]

The AFE fold is

\[
x\longmapsto 1-x.
\]

The central fold point is

\[
x=\frac12.
\]

For Gate–1, the curvature multiplier separates the two sides by

\[
x^2-(1-x)^2=2x-1.
\]

For O2/O3, the first near-pure substitution ratio is

\[
r(x)=\frac{(1-x)^2}{x^2}.
\]

Its logarithmic derivative is

\[
\frac{d}{dx}\log r(x)
=
-2\left(\frac1{1-x}+\frac1x\right).
\]

On any compact subinterval of \((0,1)\), this is bounded away from zero. Thus outside the central tube, fold separation is strong.

---

# 2. Multiscale annuli

Define scales

\[
\delta_j=R^{-C_j},
\]

and annuli

\[
\mathcal A_j
=
\left\{
\delta_{j+1}
\le
\left|x-\frac12\right|
<
\delta_j
\right\}.
\]

On \(\mathcal A_j\),

\[
|x^2-(1-x)^2|
=
|2x-1|
\asymp \delta_j.
\]

Also,

\[
r(x)
=
\left(\frac{1/2-s}{1/2+s}\right)^2,
\qquad
s=x-\frac12,
\]

so

\[
r(x)=1-8s+O(s^2).
\]

Thus on \(\mathcal A_j\),

\[
|r(x)-1|\asymp \delta_j.
\]

This gives polynomial fold separation on every annulus with

\[
\delta_j\ge R^{-C}.
\]

---

# 3. Why \(R^{-C}\), not \(Q^{-C}\)

O2/O3 exterior degree is

\[
n=R+2.
\]

If a central scale produces a factor \(\delta\) in the branch-ratio or graph residual, the exterior lift may lose roughly

\[
\delta^R.
\]

If

\[
\delta=R^{-C},
\]

then

\[
\delta^R
=
R^{-CR}
=
\exp(-CR\log R).
\]

Since

\[
R\asymp\frac{\log Q}{\log\log Q},
\]

we get

\[
R^{-CR}=Q^{-O(1)}.
\]

So \(R^{-C}\)-scale central routing preserves polynomial bookkeeping.

By contrast, if one uses a much smaller scale without transition routing, losses can become unnecessary or harder to track.

---

# 4. Gate–1 consequence

The finite Gate–1 anti-lock model is

\[
F_X=B_2+P_X+D_X,
\]

with plus and folded-dual pieces related by the fold.

Away from the central tube, the curvature symbols differ by

\[
x^2-(1-x)^2=2x-1.
\]

On \(\mathcal A_j\), this is

\[
\asymp \delta_j.
\]

Thus plus/dual anti-lock has a lower scale of at worst

\[
\delta_j^2
\]

in \(L^2\)-energy, up to symbol and weight factors.

If mass lies on annuli with \(\delta_j\ge R^{-C}\), Gate–1 anti-lock loses only

\[
R^{-O(1)}
\]

or, after exterior/iteration bookkeeping,

\[
Q^{-O(1)}.
\]

If mass is concentrated inside

\[
|x-1/2|<R^{-C},
\]

it should be routed to the transition branch, where the local coordinate is

\[
y=\frac{x-1/2}{\delta}.
\]

Then the rescaled source has bandwidth

\[
B_{\mathrm{cen}}\asymp \delta Q,
\]

and the correct physical scale is

\[
\lambda_{\mathrm{cen}}\asymp (\delta Q)^{-1}.
\]

Thus central concentration is exactly transition-scale concentration.

---

# 5. O2/O3 consequence

For O2/O3, the dangerous near-pure substitution ratio is

\[
r(x)=\frac{(1-x)^2}{x^2}.
\]

Outside the central tube, \(r\) is monotone and separated. Hence the fixed-degree moment system

\[
1,r,r^2,\ldots,r^{d_0}
\]

is Riesz on any collection of \(d_0+1\) subannuli with polynomial mass and polynomially separated \(r\)-values.

Inside a central scale

\[
x=\frac12+\delta y,
\]

we have

\[
r(x)=1-8\delta y+O(\delta^2 y^2).
\]

The ratio variation is now \(\delta\)-small. But if

\[
\delta=R^{-C},
\]

then lifting this through exterior degree \(R\) loses only

\[
\delta^R=Q^{-O(1)}.
\]

So the rescaled central O2/O3 graph remains polynomially nondegenerate, provided the transition coordinate \(y\) retains the odd direction.

The finite transition condition is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{the transition model must see the odd fold-breaking coordinate }y.
}
\]

If the transition basis kills the odd direction, then plus/dual anti-lock may fail at the central scale.

---

# 6. Central tube as transition branch, not bad set

The right theorem is not:

\[
\text{central tube has small mass}.
\]

The better theorem is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{central tube mass is routed to transition scale, where rescaled fold separation is polynomial.}
}
\]

This avoids needing to prove that canonical mass avoids \(x=1/2\). It may concentrate there; if it does, the transition chart must handle it.

So branch routing should include:

\[
|x-1/2|\asymp \delta
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
\lambda_{\mathrm{tr}}\asymp (\delta Q)^{-1}.
\]

Then both Gate–1 and O2/O3 use the same central rescaling.

---

# 7. Unified theorem

A clean statement is:

## MultiscaleFoldRouting

Let \(R\asymp \log Q/\log\log Q\). Decompose the active fold region into annuli

\[
\mathcal A_j
=
\{\delta_{j+1}\le |x-1/2|<\delta_j\},
\qquad
\delta_j=R^{-C_j}.
\]

On every annulus \(\mathcal A_j\):

1. Gate–1 fold separation satisfies

\[
|x^2-(1-x)^2|\asymp \delta_j.
\]

2. O2/O3 ratio separation satisfies

\[
r(x)=1+O(\delta_j),
\qquad
\frac{d}{dy}r(1/2+\delta_j y)\asymp \delta_j
\]

in the rescaled coordinate \(y\).

3. Exterior-degree loss is bounded by

\[
\delta_j^R\ge Q^{-A}.
\]

Therefore either:

- mass lies on an outer annulus and fold anti-lock works directly; or
- mass lies in a central annulus and is handled by transition-scale observability with scale

\[
\lambda_j\asymp(\delta_j Q)^{-1}.
\]

This yields polynomial-loss anti-lock for both \(L^2\)-Gate–1 and O2/O3 near-pure survival.

---

# 8. Required inputs

The theorem needs three supporting inputs.

## A. Branch coverage at multiscale transition scales

For each central annulus, transition windows must cover the physical scale

\[
\lambda_j\asymp(\delta_jQ)^{-1}.
\]

If the transition atlas only covers one fixed central scale, the theorem may have gaps.

## B. Odd-mode visibility

The transition chart must retain the fold-breaking odd coordinate

\[
y=x-\frac12.
\]

Equivalently, the transition finite model must not project away the first odd direction.

## C. Riesz normalization under rescaling

The O2/O3 Riesz basis must remain \(R^C\)-conditioned after the rescaling

\[
x=\frac12+\delta y.
\]

The polynomial approximation loss

\[
\delta^R
\]

is acceptable only if no additional super-polynomial conditioning appears.

---

# 9. Failure modes

The multiscale routing fails only if:

1. transition windows do not cover the relevant central scales;
2. transition coordinates kill the odd fold-breaking direction;
3. the rescaled Riesz basis has super-polynomial condition number;
4. central mass concentrates below all routed \(R^{-C}\)-scales;
5. AFE/gamma perturbations cancel the \(1-8\delta y\) fold-breaking term.

These are now precise, finite audit points.

---

# Final status

The central tube should be treated as a multiscale transition branch, not as an exceptional bad set.

This gives one shared theorem feeding:

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{FiniteAFEAntiLock}
}
\]

and

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{NearPureSchurO2/O3}.
}
\]

The main actionable condition is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{transition routing must preserve the odd fold-breaking coordinate.}
}
\]

If that holds, the central fold degeneracy costs only \(Q^{-O(1)}\), which is acceptable.

## Agent 4 continuation — odd-mode visibility in the transition chart

The multiscale fold-routing theorem reduced the central-tube problem to one finite condition:

\[
\boxed{
\text{the transition chart must see the odd fold-breaking coordinate.}
}
\]

I now attack that condition. The issue is shared by both \(L^2\)-Gate–1 and O2/O3: near \(x=1/2\), plus and dual curvature symbols agree to zeroth order, and the first separating term is odd in the centered coordinate. 

---

# 1. Central coordinate and fold action

Set

\[
x=\frac12+\delta y,
\qquad
0<\delta\le R^{-C}.
\]

The AFE fold is

\[
x\mapsto 1-x,
\]

which becomes

\[
y\mapsto -y.
\]

So transition analysis is naturally a parity problem in \(y\).

The curvature multiplier satisfies

\[
x^2-(1-x)^2=2x-1=2\delta y.
\]

The near-pure O2/O3 substitution ratio satisfies

\[
r(x)=\frac{(1-x)^2}{x^2}
=
1-8\delta y+O(\delta^2y^2).
\]

Thus, in both Gate–1 and O2/O3, the first nontrivial plus/dual separator is the odd mode

\[
y.
\]

If the transition model quotients out or fails to sample this odd mode, central fold anti-lock fails at leading order.

---

# 2. Transition finite model condition

Let \(V_{\tr}\) be the transition finite model space used for local source representation. It should include enough functions of \(y\) to detect both:

\[
1
\quad\text{and}\quad
y.
\]

The minimal condition is:

\[
\boxed{
\operatorname{dist}_{L^2(w_{\tr})}
\left(
y,\ V_{\tr}^{\mathrm{even}}
\right)
\ge Q^{-A}\|y\|_{L^2(w_{\tr})},
}
\]

where \(V_{\tr}^{\mathrm{even}}\) is the even/symmetric retained subspace.

Equivalently, the transition projection \(P_{\tr}\) must satisfy

\[
\boxed{
\|P_{\tr}y\|_{L^2(w_{\tr})}
\ge Q^{-A}\|y\|_{L^2(w_{\tr})}.
}
\]

If the retained transition basis contains odd functions, this is automatic. If the transition basis only retains even or folded-symmetric combinations, it fails.

So the transition audit is:

\[
\boxed{
y\in V_{\tr}\text{ with polynomial conditioning?}
}
\]

---

# 3. Gate–1 consequence

In the central scale, the plus/dual curvature contribution has schematic form

\[
P_X+D_X
\approx
A(y)+A(-y)
+
\delta y\,B(y)
+
\text{higher terms}.
\]

The symmetric part

\[
A(y)+A(-y)
\]

can cancel or reinforce depending on phase. The fold-breaking curvature term is

\[
\delta y\,B(y).
\]

If \(P_{\tr}\) sees \(y\), then

\[
\|P_{\tr}(P_X+D_X)\|_{L^2}
\]

has a lower component of size roughly

\[
\delta^2\|yB(y)\|^2.
\]

For \(\delta=R^{-C}\), this is

\[
R^{-O(1)}
\]

or after exterior bookkeeping

\[
Q^{-O(1)}.
\]

Thus central Gate–1 anti-lock is recovered at polynomial cost.

If \(P_{\tr}y=0\), the first visible fold-breaking term may be \(y^2\) or higher, and the loss can worsen or become symmetry-dependent.

---

# 4. O2/O3 consequence

For near-pure O2/O3, the ratio expansion is

\[
r_\delta(y)=1-8\delta y+O(\delta^2y^2).
\]

Consider the finite system

\[
1,r_\delta,r_\delta^2,\ldots,r_\delta^{d_0}.
\]

Expanding,

\[
r_\delta^k
=
1-8k\delta y+O(\delta^2y^2).
\]

The span of

\[
r_\delta,\ldots,r_\delta^{d_0}
\]

contains a constant-like direction and an odd \(y\)-direction at scale \(\delta\).

The distance

\[
\operatorname{dist}
\left(
1,\operatorname{span}\{r_\delta,\ldots,r_\delta^{d_0}\}
\right)
\]

is controlled by the ability to distinguish the constant from these perturbed copies. If all \(r_\delta^k\) are too close to \(1\), the raw distance can be small. But after rescaling by the transition coordinate, the meaningful separator is again the odd \(y\)-component.

A stable transition O2/O3 theorem should therefore be formulated in the rescaled basis

\[
\frac{r_\delta-1}{\delta}
=
-8y+O(\delta y^2).
\]

Then the finite moment system becomes

\[
1,\ y,\ y^2,\ldots
\]

with polynomial conditioning. This shows why transition rescaling is essential.

So the central O2/O3 theorem is:

\[
\boxed{
\operatorname{dist}
\left(
1,\operatorname{span}\left\{
\frac{r_\delta-1}{\delta},
\ldots,
\text{finite powers}
\right\}
\right)
\ge Q^{-A}
}
\]

in the transition-scaled Riesz basis.

---

# 5. Avoiding a false degeneracy

If one studies

\[
1,r_\delta,\ldots,r_\delta^{d_0}
\]

in the unscaled coordinate as \(\delta\to0\), the system becomes ill-conditioned because all \(r_\delta^k\to1\).

That is a coordinate artifact. The correct transition basis divides by the small scale \(\delta\) and retains the first odd variation.

Thus:

\[
\boxed{
\text{central O2/O3 fails in raw ordinary coordinates but is stable in transition coordinates.}
}
\]

This is exactly the role of transition routing.

---

# 6. Required transition Riesz normalization

Let

\[
\mathcal B_{\tr}
=
\{1,y,y^2,\ldots,y^R\}
\]

or an orthonormal/Chebyshev version on the transition interval \(y\in[-Y,Y]\).

The transition Riesz condition is:

\[
\boxed{
\|\mathcal B_{\tr}\|_{\mathrm{cond}}\le R^C.
}
\]

Under the rescaling

\[
x=\frac12+\delta y,
\]

ordinary monomials in \(x\) become combinations of powers of \(y\) with factors \(\delta^k\). Raw monomials are ill-conditioned, but the rescaled \(y\)-basis is not.

Therefore the transition O2/O3 matrix must be built in \(y\)-coordinates, not inherited directly from ordinary \(x\)-monomials.

This is a basis-design requirement, not a new arithmetic theorem.

---

# 7. Compatibility with the \(Y12\) singular channel

The transition chart is also the source of the downstream singular-discard map

\[
T_{\tr\to\sing}.
\]

The odd fold mode \(y\) should not be confused with the \(Y12\) carrier. They live in different filtrations:

- \(y\) is a source-linear fold-coordinate mode;
- \(Y12\) is primitive \(D^2N_0\)-origin, mixed-12, same-point, one-whitening-slot.

Thus retaining odd \(y\) for transition O2/O3 should not automatically kill the \(Y12\)-normal D4 channel.

This is good: transition O2/O3 needs odd source-linear modes, while D4 normality detects a primitive nonlinear signature.

So there is no inherent conflict between:

\[
\boxed{
P_{\tr}y\ne0
}
\]

and

\[
\boxed{
\Pi_YT_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]\ne0.
}
\]

---

# 8. Transition branch theorem

The central transition theorem should be:

Let

\[
x=\frac12+\delta y,
\qquad
\delta=R^{-C}.
\]

Assume:

1. transition windows cover the physical scale

\[
\lambda_{\tr}\asymp (\delta Q)^{-1};
\]

2. the transition source basis is \(R^C\)-Riesz in \(y\);

3. the retained model includes the odd coordinate \(y\);

4. AFE/gamma perturbations are \(C^{d_0}\)-controlled in \(y\);

5. the \(Y12\)-signature quotient remains separated from source-linear \(y\)-modes.

Then:

- Gate–1 fold anti-lock holds on the central annulus with \(Q^{-A}\) loss;
- O2/O3 near-pure Schur complement holds on the central annulus with \(Q^{-A}\) loss;
- downstream \(Y12\)-normality is not affected by retaining the odd transition mode.

---

# 9. Failure modes

The central transition route fails if:

1. transition routing does not include scales \(\lambda\asymp(\delta Q)^{-1}\);
2. the transition basis is even-only or folded-symmetric;
3. the odd mode \(y\) is quotiented as nuisance;
4. rescaled basis conditioning is worse than \(R^C\);
5. \(\delta\) is taken too small, so \(\delta^R\) becomes super-polynomial;
6. source-linear odd mode and \(Y12\) signature are accidentally identified in the singular quotient.

These are all finite/basis audits.

---

# Final status

The central tube is controllable if transition routing preserves the odd fold-breaking coordinate. The required theorem is:

\[
\boxed{
\|P_{\tr}y\|\ge Q^{-A}\|y\|.
}
\]

Once that holds, the fold degeneracy at \(x=1/2\) costs only polynomial factors and feeds both:

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{FiniteAFEAntiLock}
}
\]

and

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{NearPureSchurO2/O3}.
}
\]

The key basis insight is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{use raw ordinary \(x\)-coordinates outside the tube, but rescaled \(y=(x-1/2)/\delta\) coordinates inside.}
}
\]

This keeps the central model Riesz-conditioned and preserves the odd separator needed for both upstream and O2/O3.

## Agent 4 continuation — central transition requires degree-\(d_0\) visibility, not just odd-mode visibility

The previous step identified the central fold coordinate

\[
x=\frac12+\delta y
\]

and noted that the first fold-breaking term is odd in \(y\). That is enough for the **degree-one** Gate–1 anti-lock model. But for O2/O3, the near-pure block has size

\[
d_0=O(1),
\]

so the central transition theorem must be slightly stronger.

## Verdict

\[
\boxed{
\text{For O2/O3, the central transition chart must retain polynomial modes through degree }d_0.
}
\]

Odd-mode visibility is the \(d_0=1\) case. In general, powers of the near-central branch ratio can cancel the constant through order \(d_0-1\), leaving a residual only at order \(\delta^{d_0}\). This is still polynomially acceptable because \(d_0=O(1)\), but it must be tracked explicitly.

---

# 1. Central expansion of the ratio

Set

\[
x=\frac12+\delta y.
\]

The folded ratio is

\[
r_\delta(y)
=
\frac{(1-x)^2}{x^2}
=
\left(\frac{1/2-\delta y}{1/2+\delta y}\right)^2.
\]

Expanding,

\[
r_\delta(y)
=
1-8\delta y+32\delta^2y^2+O(\delta^3y^3).
\]

Thus

\[
r_\delta(y)-1
=
-8\delta y+O(\delta^2y^2).
\]

For \(d_0=1\), seeing \(y\) is enough.

But for \(d_0>1\), the span

\[
\operatorname{span}\{r_\delta,r_\delta^2,\ldots,r_\delta^{d_0}\}
\]

can approximate the constant \(1\) to higher order in \(\delta\).

Indeed, for \(z=r_\delta\),

\[
1-(1-z)^{d_0}
=
\sum_{k=1}^{d_0}(-1)^{k+1}\binom{d_0}{k}z^k
\]

lies in

\[
\operatorname{span}\{z,z^2,\ldots,z^{d_0}\},
\]

and approximates \(1\) with error

\[
(1-z)^{d_0}.
\]

Since

\[
1-z\asymp \delta y,
\]

the natural residual scale is

\[
\delta^{d_0}.
\]

So the central O2/O3 lower bound cannot be better than

\[
\delta^{d_0}
\]

in norm, or

\[
\delta^{2d_0}
\]

in squared energy.

This is acceptable if

\[
\delta=R^{-C},
\]

because

\[
\delta^{2d_0}=R^{-O(1)}=Q^{-o(1)}
\]

for fixed \(d_0\).

---

# 2. Correct central finite theorem

The central finite theorem should be:

Let

\[
z=r_\delta(y).
\]

In the transition-scaled measure \(d\mu_{\tr}(y)\), prove

\[
\operatorname{dist}
\left(
1,
\operatorname{span}\{z,z^2,\ldots,z^{d_0}\}
\right)
\ge
Q^{-A}.
\]

After rescaling, this is equivalent to saying that the transition measure sees the degree-\(d_0\) polynomial mode generated by

\[
(1-z)^{d_0}
\sim
(8\delta y)^{d_0}.
\]

Thus the transition chart must satisfy

\[
\boxed{
\operatorname{dist}
\left(
y^{d_0},
\mathbb C_{d_0-1}[y]
\right)
\ge Q^{-A}\|y^{d_0}\|
}
\]

in its retained/Riesz transition geometry.

Equivalently, the transition model must retain a Riesz-stable polynomial basis at least through degree \(d_0\):

\[
1,y,y^2,\ldots,y^{d_0}.
\]

This is stronger than merely retaining \(y\), but still finite and mild.

---

# 3. Chebyshev view

The best approximation problem has a classical Chebyshev form.

We are approximating the constant \(1\) on a small \(z\)-interval near \(1\) by polynomials in \(z\) with no constant term:

\[
P(z)=\sum_{k=1}^{d_0}c_kz^k.
\]

Equivalently, the residual

\[
Q(z)=1-P(z)
\]

has

\[
Q(0)=1
\]

and degree at most \(d_0\). On a \(z\)-interval of width \(\asymp \delta\) around \(1\), the minimal possible residual is of order

\[
\delta^{d_0}.
\]

So the \(\delta^{d_0}\) loss is not an artifact. It is the exact scale.

Since \(d_0\) is fixed, this is harmless. If \(d_0\) were \(O(R)\), this would become dangerous. This is another reason why the \(Y12\)-rank-one wall, which keeps \(d_0\) small, is valuable.

---

# 4. Updated transition visibility condition

The transition chart condition should be upgraded from

\[
P_{\tr}y\ne0
\]

to

\[
\boxed{
P_{\tr}\mathbb C_{d_0}[y]
\text{ is }R^C\text{-Riesz}.
}
\]

More concretely:

\[
\|P_{\tr}p(y)\|_{L^2(w_{\tr})}
\ge
R^{-C}\|p(y)\|_{L^2(w_{\tr})}
\]

for every polynomial

\[
p\in\mathbb C_{d_0}[y].
\]

Since \(d_0=O(1)\), this is a finite basis audit.

If the transition chart already retains a polynomial or Chebyshev basis through order \(R\), then this is automatic.

If the transition chart keeps only even/folded-symmetric modes, it fails.

---

# 5. Gate–1 versus O2/O3 central requirements

The two modules now differ slightly.

## Gate–1

The degree-one AFE anti-lock only needs the first fold-breaking mode:

\[
y.
\]

So Gate–1 requires

\[
P_{\tr}y\ne0
\]

with polynomial conditioning.

## O2/O3

The near-pure Schur block of size \(d_0\) requires modes through degree \(d_0\):

\[
1,y,\ldots,y^{d_0}.
\]

Thus O2/O3 imposes the stronger transition-basis condition.

This is still easy if \(d_0\) is small.

---

# 6. Interaction with \(Y12\) wall

If the \(Y12/K_9\) wall gives a clean rank-one form, then likely

\[
d_0=4
\]

or at worst around

\[
d_0=6
\]

under normalized degrees.

Then transition O2/O3 only needs modes through quartic or sextic order in \(y\).

That is completely plausible.

If the wall remains a complicated moving linear form with unnormalized cubic pieces, \(d_0\) could be larger, e.g.

\[
d_0\approx 14.
\]

Still finite, but the transition central loss becomes

\[
\delta^{14}.
\]

For \(\delta=R^{-C}\), this remains

\[
R^{-O(1)}=Q^{-o(1)},
\]

so it is acceptable. The basis audit is still finite.

---

# 7. Central O2/O3 theorem

The central branch theorem should now be:

Let

\[
x=\frac12+\delta y,
\qquad
\delta=R^{-C}.
\]

Let

\[
r_\delta(y)
=
\frac{(1/2-\delta y)^2}{(1/2+\delta y)^2}.
\]

Assume:

1. the transition measure in \(y\) is thick on a fixed interval;
2. the transition projection is \(R^C\)-Riesz on \(\mathbb C_{d_0}[y]\);
3. perturbations to \(r_\delta\) from AFE weights/gamma factors are \(C^{d_0}\)-controlled;
4. the central scale \(\delta\) is no smaller than \(R^{-C}\).

Then

\[
\operatorname{dist}
\left(
1,
\operatorname{span}
\{r_\delta,\ldots,r_\delta^{d_0}\}
\right)
\ge
R^{-C'}
\]

and hence

\[
\ge Q^{-A}.
\]

This gives central near-pure O2/O3 survival.

---

# 8. No conflict with \(Y12\)-normality

The transition polynomial modes

\[
1,y,\ldots,y^{d_0}
\]

are source-linear fold-coordinate modes.

The \(Y12\) channel has primitive origin

\[
D^2N_0[\xi_1,\xi_2].
\]

These are different filtration sectors. Retaining polynomial transition modes through degree \(d_0\) should not make \(Y12\) tangent to the actual EF manifold.

Thus the central transition requirement does not threaten downstream \(Y12\)-normality.

This is important: the transition chart can be rich enough for O2/O3 without killing the forbidden \(Y12\) D4 channel.

---

# 9. Remaining audit

The finite audit is now:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Does the transition chart retain a Riesz-stable }\mathbb C_{d_0}[y]\text{ block?}
}
\]

If yes, the central tube is solved for both Gate–1 and O2/O3.

If no, then central mass remains an obstruction, and one must either:

1. enrich the transition chart;
2. use \(\alpha\)-phase variation;
3. prove central mass is small;
4. route central mass to singular analysis.

---

# Final status

The central fold degeneracy is now fully quantified.

The loss in central O2/O3 is

\[
\delta^{d_0}
\]

in norm, not just \(\delta\). But because

\[
d_0=O(1)
\]

and

\[
\delta=R^{-C},
\]

this is still polynomially acceptable.

Therefore the multiscale fold-routing theorem should require:

\[
\boxed{
\text{transition Riesz visibility of }\mathbb C_{d_0}[y].
}
\]

Odd-mode visibility is enough only for Gate–1 or for the special case \(d_0=1\).
