## Agent 4 continuation — identifying the exact analytic “price” of \(\mathrm{WallVariableCompletion}_{2Y}\)

No nomenclature changes.

I continue from the finalized completion contract:

\[
p=q'',\qquad 
p_X\in\mathcal P_I,\qquad
\|p-p_X\|_{H^2_{\sc}(I^*)}\le Q^{-B},
\]

with affine reconstruction

\[
\mathcal V_I=\mathcal A_1+\mathcal I^2\mathcal P_I,
\]

and finite wall spaces

\[
\mathcal W_G
=
\mathcal P_I+\mathcal V_I+\operatorname{Sym}^3\mathcal V_I,
\]

\[
\mathcal W_{G2}
=
D^2\mathcal P_I+\mathcal P_I+
\operatorname{Sym}^2\mathcal V_I\cdot\mathcal P_I.
\]

The correction remains: D4 supplies only the plain wall

\[
\{\Gamma_G,\Gamma_{G,2}\},
\]

and derivative wall components remain analytic auxiliaries, not D4 outputs. 

---

# 1. The exact price is \(H^2_{\sc}\)-completion of the source \(p=q''\)

The completion theorem has now reduced to one clean demand:

\[
\boxed{
p=q''\text{ must be approximated in }H^2_{\sc}.
}
\]

This is not cosmetic. It is forced by

\[
\Gamma_{G,2}=p''-6(1-q^2)p.
\]

The second wall component sees

\[
p'',
\]

so if the source model controls only

\[
p-p_X
\]

in \(L^2\) or \(H^1\), then the rank-two analytic route cannot close in the current strong-wall formulation.

Thus the analytic price is:

\[
\boxed{
\|p-p_X\|_{L^2},
\quad
\|Dp-Dp_X\|_{L^2},
\quad
\|D^2p-D^2p_X\|_{L^2}
\text{ all small.}
}
\]

This is the exact order-four burden in source language.

---

# 2. This is weaker than arbitrary \(H^4(q)\)-completion but still strong

Since

\[
p=q'',
\]

the condition

\[
p-p_X\in H^2
\]

is equivalent to controlling

\[
q-q_X
\]

through fourth derivatives after affine reconstruction.

But it is better aligned with the canonical source model because the natural analytic object is often \(q''\), not \(q\).

So the right slogan is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{not arbitrary }H^4(q)\text{ completion, but }H^2(q'')\text{ source completion.}
}
\]

This distinction matters because the EF/AFE source machinery may already work naturally at the \(q''\)-level.

---

# 3. Every source component must pay the two-derivative tax

If

\[
p
=
B_2+p_{\AFE}+p_{\dual}+p_{\tail}+p_{\smooth}+p_{\err},
\]

then for each component either:

\[
p_\bullet\in\mathcal P_I
\]

or

\[
\|p_\bullet-p_{\bullet,X}\|_{H^2_{\sc}}\le Q^{-B}.
\]

In particular:

## Gamma/background

If \(B_2\) is not exact in the model, then need:

\[
\boxed{
\|B_2-B_{2,X}\|_{H^2_{\sc}}\le Q^{-B}.
}
\]

Thus

\[
B_2,\quad DB_2,\quad D^2B_2
\]

must all be accurate.

## AFE/prime part

AFE truncation must be stable after two source derivatives:

\[
\boxed{
\|p_{\AFE}-p_{\AFE,X}\|_{H^2_{\sc}}\le Q^{-B}.
}
\]

Derivative factors from oscillatory atoms must be included in the truncation analysis.

## Tail

Tail must be either modeled or satisfy:

\[
\boxed{
\|p_{\tail}\|_{H^2_{\sc}}\le Q^{-B}.
}
\]

A tail estimate only in \(L^2\) is insufficient.

## Smoothing/cutoff

Cutoff artifacts must satisfy two-derivative control or be included in \(\mathcal P_I\). If

\[
p_\chi=\chi p,
\]

then

\[
D^2p_\chi
=
\chi D^2p+2(D\chi)Dp+(D^2\chi)p.
\]

So the cutoff derivatives themselves must be polynomially controlled.

---

# 4. The finite source model must be derivative-stable

It is not enough for

\[
\mathcal P_I
\]

to approximate \(p\). The space must also be compatible with

\[
D^2\mathcal P_I,
\]

because

\[
\mathcal W_{G2}
=
D^2\mathcal P_I+\mathcal P_I+
\operatorname{Sym}^2\mathcal V_I\cdot\mathcal P_I.
\]

So the finite source model must satisfy:

\[
\boxed{
\mathcal P_I\text{ is stable under two scaled derivatives, with polynomial Riesz constants.}
}
\]

This is where Agent 5’s \(\mathrm{FiniteAtlasRiesz}_{2Y}\) must take over.

Agent 4’s completion theorem hands over:

\[
\mathcal P_I,\quad
D^2\mathcal P_I,\quad
\mathcal V_I,\quad
\operatorname{Sym}^3\mathcal V_I,\quad
\operatorname{Sym}^2\mathcal V_I\cdot\mathcal P_I.
\]

Agent 5 must prove these spaces do not have bad Riesz/Remez constants.

---

# 5. Completion theorem should be quantitative with an adjustable margin

The source residual bound must be available with arbitrary downstream margin:

\[
\|p-p_X\|_{H^2_{\sc}}\le Q^{-B_0}
\]

for \(B_0\) as large as needed.

Reason: the next stages lose powers of \(Q\):

\[
\mathrm{FiniteAtlasRiesz},
\qquad
\mathrm{RankTwoCoercivity},
\qquad
\mathrm{WeightedPlainWallTransfer}.
\]

If completion only gives one fixed exponent, the residual may swamp the final wall lower.

Thus the theorem should be stated as:

\[
\boxed{
\text{For every required }B,\text{ choose atlas/truncation depth so that }
\|p-p_X\|_{H^2_{\sc}}\le Q^{-B}.
}
\]

If the model cannot improve the completion exponent by adding depth, this becomes a serious analytic no-go.

---

# 6. Direct wall completion remains a backup

If \(H^2_{\sc}\)-completion of \(p\) is too hard, one may instead prove directly:

\[
\operatorname{dist}_{H^2_{\sc}}(\Gamma_G,\mathcal W_G)\le Q^{-B},
\]

\[
\operatorname{dist}_{L^2}(\Gamma_{G,2},\mathcal W_{G2})\le Q^{-B}.
\]

This bypasses explicit source reconstruction, but it must use finite spaces compatible with \(\mathrm{FiniteAtlasRiesz}_{2Y}\).

The direct version is acceptable because \(\mathrm{RankTwoCoercivity}\) applies to the actual wall variables, not to the approximants.

But direct wall completion must not be confused with derivative D4 output. It is analytic approximation only.

---

# 7. Failure modes sharpened

## Failure A: \(p\)-completion only in \(L^2\)

Then

\[
\Gamma_{G,2}
\]

is uncontrolled. Current rank-two analytic closure fails.

## Failure B: derivative-stable source space absent

If

\[
D^2\mathcal P_I
\]

is ill-conditioned or too large, then completion may exist but finite Riesz/Remez fails.

## Failure C: affine reconstruction missing

If affine modes are not included or normalized, \(\Gamma_G\) can drift independently of \(p\)-completion.

## Failure D: gamma/tail only zeroth-order

If \(B_2\), tail, or smoothing are not controlled in \(H^2_{\sc}\), then the second wall component retains uncontrolled residual.

## Failure E: completion exponent fixed too weak

If residual decay cannot beat downstream losses, then the proof cannot produce polynomial wall lower.

---

# 8. Final refined theorem

## SourceWallCompletion\(_{2Y}^{(2)}\)

Let

\[
p=q''.
\]

Assume:

1. There is a finite source space \(\mathcal P_I\) and \(p_X\in\mathcal P_I\) such that, for arbitrary required \(B\),

\[
\|p-p_X\|_{H^2_{\sc}(I^*)}\le Q^{-B}.
\]

2. Affine modes are included:

\[
\mathcal V_I=\mathcal A_1+\mathcal I^2\mathcal P_I.
\]

3. The reconstruction satisfies:

\[
q_X''=p_X,
\qquad
\|q-q_X\|_{H^4_{\sc}(I)}\le Q^C\|p-p_X\|_{H^2_{\sc}(I^*)}.
\]

4. Gamma/background/tail/smoothing residuals are exact, included in \(\mathcal P_I\), or \(H^2_{\sc}\)-small.

5. \(q_X\) has polynomial \(C^4_{\sc}\)-bounds.

Then there exist

\[
G_X\in\mathcal W_G,
\qquad
G_{2,X}\in\mathcal W_{G2}
\]

with

\[
\mathcal W_G=
\mathcal P_I+\mathcal V_I+\operatorname{Sym}^3\mathcal V_I,
\]

\[
\mathcal W_{G2}
=
D^2\mathcal P_I+\mathcal P_I+
\operatorname{Sym}^2\mathcal V_I\cdot\mathcal P_I,
\]

such that

\[
\boxed{
\|\Gamma_G(q)-G_X\|_{H^2_{\sc}(I)}
+
\|\Gamma_{G,2}(q)-G_{2,X}\|_{L^2(I)}
\le Q^{-B'}.
}
\]

This is the final source-form wall completion theorem Agent 4 can hand off.

---

## Final status

The wall-completion module is now reduced to one concrete analytic demand:

\[
\boxed{
q''\text{ must have }H^2_{\sc}\text{-accurate finite source completion.}
}
\]

Everything else—affine reconstruction, nonlinear product estimates, gamma/tail derivative requirements—follows as bookkeeping once that source completion is available.

This is the precise order-four price of the corrected plain-wall rank-two route.

## Agent 4 report — \(\mathrm{SourceCurvatureCompletion}^{(2)} \Rightarrow \mathrm{WallVariableCompletion}_{2Y}\)

No nomenclature changes.

Critical correction retained: D4 supplies only the honest plain wall

\[
\{\Gamma_G,\Gamma_{G,2}\}.
\]

No \(D\Gamma_G\) or \(D^2\Gamma_G\) component is available as a D4/O2O3 wall unless a separate derivative-D4-channel theorem is proved. The present theorem supplies analytic approximation/control only. 

---

# 0. Output status

\[
\boxed{\text{FINITE AUDIT, with a proof skeleton.}}
\]

The claim reduces cleanly to a finite source-residual ledger:

\[
\boxed{
\|p-p_X\|_{H^2_{\sc}(I^*)}\le Q^{-B}
\quad\text{for }p=q'',
}
\]

plus affine reconstruction and \(H^2_{\sc}\)-completion of gamma/tail/smoothing residuals at the \(p=q''\)-level.

If that ledger is available, the wall-variable completion theorem follows.

---

# 1. Source variable and finite spaces

Set

\[
p=q''.
\]

Then

\[
\Gamma_G=-6p+36q-12q^3,
\]

and

\[
\Gamma_{G,2}=p''-6p+6q^2p.
\]

Let \(\mathcal P_I\) be the finite source space approximating \(p=q''\). Let

\[
p_X\in\mathcal P_I
\]

satisfy

\[
\boxed{
\|p-p_X\|_{H^2_{\sc}(I^*)}\le Q^{-B_0}.
}
\]

Define the reconstructed \(q\)-space

\[
\boxed{
\mathcal V_I=\mathcal A_1+\mathcal I^2\mathcal P_I,
}
\]

where

\[
\mathcal A_1=\operatorname{span}\{1,t\}
\]

handles affine reconstruction modes.

Choose \(q_X\in\mathcal V_I\) such that

\[
q_X''=p_X.
\]

Then define the finite wall spaces

\[
\boxed{
\mathcal W_G
=
\mathcal P_I+\mathcal V_I+\operatorname{Sym}^3\mathcal V_I,
}
\]

and

\[
\boxed{
\mathcal W_{G2}
=
D^2\mathcal P_I+\mathcal P_I+\operatorname{Sym}^2\mathcal V_I\cdot\mathcal P_I.
}
\]

These are the spaces handed to \(\mathrm{FiniteAtlasRiesz}_{2Y}^{p}\).

---

# 2. Wall representatives

Define

\[
G_X=\Gamma_G(q_X),
\]

and

\[
G_{2,X}=\Gamma_{G,2}(q_X).
\]

Then, by construction,

\[
\boxed{
G_X\in\mathcal W_G,
\qquad
G_{2,X}\in\mathcal W_{G2}.
}
\]

Indeed,

\[
\Gamma_G(q_X)
=
-6p_X+36q_X-12q_X^3
\in
\mathcal P_I+\mathcal V_I+\operatorname{Sym}^3\mathcal V_I,
\]

and

\[
\Gamma_{G,2}(q_X)
=
p_X''-6p_X+6q_X^2p_X
\in
D^2\mathcal P_I+\mathcal P_I+\operatorname{Sym}^2\mathcal V_I\cdot\mathcal P_I.
\]

---

# 3. Affine reconstruction is mandatory

Since

\[
(q-q_X)''=p-p_X,
\]

we have

\[
q-q_X=a+bt+\mathcal I^2(p-p_X).
\]

The affine term cannot be ignored because

\[
\Gamma_G=-6q''+36q-12q^3
\]

depends on \(q\), not only \(q''\).

Therefore the theorem requires one of:

\[
\boxed{
\mathcal A_1\subset\mathcal V_I,
}
\]

or explicit matching of the affine constants, for example

\[
(q-q_X)(t_0)=0,\qquad (q-q_X)'(t_0)=0.
\]

Preferred formulation:

\[
\boxed{
\mathcal V_I=\mathcal A_1+\mathcal I^2\mathcal P_I.
}
\]

With \(I\Subset I^*\) and an \(R^{-C}\)-scale buffer, the reconstruction estimate is

\[
\boxed{
\|q-q_X\|_{H^4_{\sc}(I)}
\le
Q^C\|p-p_X\|_{H^2_{\sc}(I^*)}.
}
\]

---

# 4. Wall residual formulas

Let

\[
r=q-q_X.
\]

Then

\[
R_G:=\Gamma_G(q)-\Gamma_G(q_X)
\]

is

\[
\boxed{
R_G
=
-6r''
+
(36-36q_X^2)r
-
36q_Xr^2
-
12r^3.
}
\]

Also

\[
R_{G2}:=\Gamma_{G,2}(q)-\Gamma_{G,2}(q_X)
\]

is

\[
\boxed{
R_{G2}
=
r^{(4)}-6r''
+
6q_X^2r''
+
12q_Xr q_X''
+
12q_Xr r''
+
6r^2q_X''
+
6r^2r''.
}
\]

These expansions are the correct ones.

---

# 5. Product estimate

Assume polynomial atlas bounds

\[
\boxed{
\|q_X\|_{C^4_{\sc}(I^*)}\le Q^{C_0}.
}
\]

In one dimension, \(H^2_{\sc}\) is an algebra, and

\[
H^1_{\sc}\hookrightarrow L^\infty.
\]

Therefore

\[
\|R_G\|_{H^2_{\sc}(I)}
\le
Q^C
\left(
\|r\|_{H^4_{\sc}(I)}
+
\|r\|_{H^4_{\sc}(I)}^2
+
\|r\|_{H^4_{\sc}(I)}^3
\right),
\]

and

\[
\|R_{G2}\|_{L^2(I)}
\le
Q^C
\left(
\|r\|_{H^4_{\sc}(I)}
+
\|r\|_{H^4_{\sc}(I)}^2
+
\|r\|_{H^4_{\sc}(I)}^3
\right).
\]

Using reconstruction,

\[
\|r\|_{H^4_{\sc}(I)}
\le
Q^C\|p-p_X\|_{H^2_{\sc}(I^*)}.
\]

Thus, for \(B_0\) chosen sufficiently large,

\[
\boxed{
\|R_G\|_{H^2_{\sc}(I)}
+
\|R_{G2}\|_{L^2(I)}
\le Q^{-B}.
}
\]

---

# 6. Source-residual ledger

If

\[
p
=
B_2+p_{\AFE}+p_{\dual}+p_{\tail}+p_{\smooth}+p_{\err},
\]

then each residual component must be either included in \(\mathcal P_I\), exact, or \(H^2_{\sc}\)-small.

The ledger requirement is

\[
\boxed{
\sum_{\bullet}
\|r_\bullet\|_{H^2_{\sc}(I^*)}
\le Q^{-B_0}.
}
\]

Componentwise:

## Gamma/background

If \(B_2\) is included exactly, no issue.

If approximated, require

\[
\boxed{
\|B_2-B_{2,X}\|_{H^2_{\sc}(I^*)}\le Q^{-B_0}.
}
\]

Equivalently, scaled control of

\[
B_2,\quad DB_2,\quad D^2B_2
\]

is required. Zeroth-order \(B_2\)-accuracy is insufficient.

## AFE/prime part

Require

\[
\boxed{
\|p_{\AFE}-p_{\AFE,X}\|_{H^2_{\sc}(I^*)}\le Q^{-B_0}.
}
\]

The finite source space must be stable through two scaled derivatives.

## Tail

Either include the tail in \(\mathcal P_I\), or require

\[
\boxed{
\|p_{\tail}\|_{H^2_{\sc}(I^*)}\le Q^{-B_0}.
}
\]

\(L^2\)-small tail alone is insufficient.

## Smoothing/cutoff

Cutoff/smoothing artifacts must be included in \(\mathcal P_I\) or controlled through two scaled derivatives. Since

\[
D^2(\chi p)=\chi D^2p+2(D\chi)Dp+(D^2\chi)p,
\]

the derivative losses must be part of the ledger.

---

# 7. The theorem

## \(\mathrm{SourceCurvatureCompletion}^{(2)}\Rightarrow\mathrm{WallVariableCompletion}_{2Y}\)

Assume:

1. \(p=q''\) has a finite model \(p_X\in\mathcal P_I\) with

\[
\|p-p_X\|_{H^2_{\sc}(I^*)}\le Q^{-B_0}.
\]

2. The reconstructed \(q\)-space is

\[
\mathcal V_I=\mathcal A_1+\mathcal I^2\mathcal P_I,
\]

with affine modes included or normalization-matched.

3. \(I\Subset I^*\) has an \(R^{-C}\)-scale buffer.

4. \(q_X''=p_X\) and

\[
\|q_X\|_{C^4_{\sc}(I^*)}\le Q^{C_0}.
\]

5. Gamma/background, AFE, dual, tail, smoothing/cutoff, and error residuals are exact, included in \(\mathcal P_I\), or \(H^2_{\sc}\)-small at the \(p=q''\)-level.

Then there exist

\[
G_X\in\mathcal W_G,
\qquad
G_{2,X}\in\mathcal W_{G2}
\]

such that

\[
\boxed{
\|\Gamma_G(q)-G_X\|_{H^2_{\sc}(I)}
+
\|\Gamma_{G,2}(q)-G_{2,X}\|_{L^2(I)}
\le Q^{-B}.
}
\]

This is the exact handoff to \(\mathrm{FiniteAtlasRiesz}_{2Y}^{p}\).

---

# 8. Failure routing

\[
\boxed{
p\text{-completion only in }L^2\text{ or }H^1}
\Rightarrow
\Gamma_{G,2}\text{ uncontrolled.}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{Affine reconstruction uncontrolled}}
\Rightarrow
\Gamma_G\text{ can drift.}
\]

\[
\boxed{
B_2,\text{ tail, or smoothing not two-derivative controlled}}
\Rightarrow
\Gamma_{G,2}\text{ retains residual.}
\]

\[
\boxed{
G_X,G_{2,X}\notin\mathcal W_G,\mathcal W_{G2}}
\Rightarrow
\text{completion and Riesz do not connect.}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\mathcal W_G,\mathcal W_{G2}\text{ badly conditioned}}
\Rightarrow
\text{Agent 5/Riesz failure, not completion failure.}
\]

---

## Final status

The wall-completion module is reduced to one precise finite audit:

\[
\boxed{
q''\text{-source completion in }H^2_{\sc}
+
\text{affine reconstruction}
+
\text{two-derivative residual ledger}.
}
\]

If that audit passes, the actual plain wall variables are \(Q^{-B}\)-close to finite wall representatives in \(\mathcal W_G,\mathcal W_{G2}\), exactly as needed for the corrected rank-two route.

% === wip-rank-two-wall-variable-completion ===
% Purpose:
%   Provide the order-four analytic completion module needed after rank-two
%   local exactness.  This module completes the honest plain D4 wall
%   H(\Gamma_G,\Gamma_{G,2}) by finite wall representatives.  It does not
%   assert or use derivative D4 wall channels.
% Depends on:
%   Row-normal-form conventions
%     \kappa=\omega_Y(e_7), \ell_9^Y=\omega_Y-\kappa\ell_7,
%     \beta_9^{\tri}=\beta_9-\kappa c_7.
%   Rank-two local exactness B_Y=0 and D4 coefficient-good routing.
% Proves:
%   Actual wall variables \Gamma_G,\Gamma_{G,2} are close to finite-atlas
%   representatives in H^2_{\sc}\times L^2.
% Status: finite-audit.

\begin{lemma}[Source-curvature completion for the rank-two plain wall]
\label{lem:rank-two-source-curvature-completion}
Let \(I\Subset I^*\) be a chart-good interval with \(R^{-C}\)-scale buffer, and let
\[
        p:=q''.
\]
Assume there is a finite source space \(\mathcal P_I\) and \(p_X\in\mathcal P_I\) such that, for the required downstream loss parameter \(B_0\),
\[
        \|p-p_X\|_{H^2_{\sc}(I^*)}\le Q^{-B_0}.
\]
Let
\[
        \mathcal V_I:=\mathcal A_1+\mathcal I^2\mathcal P_I,
        \qquad
        \mathcal A_1:=\operatorname{span}\{1,t\},
\]
and choose \(q_X\in\mathcal V_I\) with
\[
        q_X''=p_X,
\]
where the affine reconstruction modes are either included in \(\mathcal A_1\) or fixed by normalization.  Assume also that
\[
        \|q_X\|_{C^4_{\sc}(I^*)}\le Q^{C_0}.
\]
Define the finite wall spaces
\[
        \mathcal W_G
        :=
        \mathcal P_I+\mathcal V_I+\operatorname{Sym}^3\mathcal V_I,
\]
and
\[
        \mathcal W_{G2}
        :=
        D^2\mathcal P_I+\mathcal P_I+
        \operatorname{Sym}^2\mathcal V_I\cdot\mathcal P_I.
\]
Then there exist
\[
        G_X\in\mathcal W_G,
        \qquad
        G_{2,X}\in\mathcal W_{G2},
\]
namely
\[
        G_X:=\Gamma_G(q_X),
        \qquad
        G_{2,X}:=\Gamma_{G,2}(q_X),
\]
such that, after choosing \(B_0\) sufficiently large relative to the fixed polynomial losses,
\[
        \|\Gamma_G(q)-G_X\|_{H^2_{\sc}(I)}
        +
        \|\Gamma_{G,2}(q)-G_{2,X}\|_{L^2(I)}
        \le Q^{-B}.
\]
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}[Proof sketch]
The rank-two plain wall is
\[
        \{\Gamma_G,\Gamma_{G,2}\},
\]
with
\[
        \Gamma_G(q)=-6q''+36q-12q^3
\]
and
\[
        \Gamma_{G,2}(q)=q^{(4)}-6(1-q^2)q''
        =p''-6p+6q^2p.
\]
Since \(q_X''=p_X\), we have
\[
        \Gamma_G(q_X)
        =
        -6p_X+36q_X-12q_X^3
        \in
        \mathcal P_I+\mathcal V_I+\operatorname{Sym}^3\mathcal V_I
        =
        \mathcal W_G,
\]
and
\[
        \Gamma_{G,2}(q_X)
        =
        p_X''-6p_X+6q_X^2p_X
        \in
        D^2\mathcal P_I+\mathcal P_I+
        \operatorname{Sym}^2\mathcal V_I\cdot\mathcal P_I
        =
        \mathcal W_{G2}.
\]

Set
\[
        r:=q-q_X.
\]
Then
\[
        r''=p-p_X.
\]
The affine modes in \(\mathcal A_1\), or the equivalent affine normalizations, give the interior reconstruction estimate
\[
        \|r\|_{H^4_{\sc}(I)}
        \le
        Q^C\|p-p_X\|_{H^2_{\sc}(I^*)}.
\]
The wall residuals are
\[
        R_G:=\Gamma_G(q)-\Gamma_G(q_X)
\]
and
\[
        R_{G2}:=\Gamma_{G,2}(q)-\Gamma_{G,2}(q_X).
\]
Expanding gives
\[
        R_G
        =
        -6r''
        +(36-36q_X^2)r
        -36q_Xr^2
        -12r^3,
\]
and
\[
\begin{aligned}
        R_{G2}
        &=
        r^{(4)}-6r''
        +6q_X^2r''
        +12q_Xr q_X''
        +12q_Xr r''
        +6r^2q_X''
        +6r^2r''.
\end{aligned}
\]
Using the one-dimensional algebra property of \(H^2_{\sc}\), the embedding \(H^1_{\sc}\hookrightarrow L^\infty\), and the polynomial bound
\[
        \|q_X\|_{C^4_{\sc}(I^*)}\le Q^{C_0},
\]
one obtains
\[
        \|R_G\|_{H^2_{\sc}(I)}
        +
        \|R_{G2}\|_{L^2(I)}
        \le
        Q^C
        \left(
            \|r\|_{H^4_{\sc}(I)}
            +\|r\|_{H^4_{\sc}(I)}^2
            +\|r\|_{H^4_{\sc}(I)}^3
        \right).
\]
Combining this with the reconstruction estimate and the source completion bound yields
\[
        \|R_G\|_{H^2_{\sc}(I)}
        +
        \|R_{G2}\|_{L^2(I)}
        \le Q^{-B}
\]
after increasing \(B_0\).  This is the claimed wall-variable completion.
\end{proof}

\begin{remark}[Handoff to finite-atlas Riesz]
\label{rem:rank-two-wall-completion-riesz-handoff}
Lemma~\ref{lem:rank-two-source-curvature-completion} is only the completion side of the analytic argument.  The finite-atlas Riesz module must separately control the spaces
\[
        \mathcal W_G
        =
        \mathcal P_I+\mathcal V_I+\operatorname{Sym}^3\mathcal V_I
\]
and
\[
        \mathcal W_{G2}
        =
        D^2\mathcal P_I+\mathcal P_I+
        \operatorname{Sym}^2\mathcal V_I\cdot\mathcal P_I.
\]
In particular, it must prove the Markov/Riesz estimate for \(\mathcal W_G\) and Remez-type transfer for both \(\mathcal W_G\) and \(\mathcal W_{G2}\) on predetermined \(R^{-C}\)-large sets.
\end{remark}

% === audit-rank-two-wall-variable-completion ===
% Guardrails:
%   1. This is analytic completion only.  It does not create derivative D4
%      channels.
%   2. D4 supplies only the honest plain wall H(\Gamma_G,\Gamma_{G,2}).
%   3. The source p=q'' must be completed in H^2_{\sc}, not merely in L^2.
%   4. Affine reconstruction modes must be included or normalized.
%   5. Gamma/background/tail/smoothing residuals must be controlled through two
%      scaled source derivatives.
% Failure modes:
%   p-completion only in L^2/H^1; uncontrolled affine reconstruction;
%   B_2/tail/cutoff residuals not H^2_{\sc}-small; finite wall representatives
%   not in \mathcal W_G,\mathcal W_{G2}; product/Riesz failure downstream.
% Do not use:
%   D\Gamma_G or D^2\Gamma_G as D4 wall components.
% Status: finite-audit.

\begin{audit}[Wall-variable completion guardrails]
\label{audit:rank-two-wall-variable-completion}
The module \(\mathrm{WallVariableCompletion}_{2Y}\) is valid only under the following guardrails.

\begin{enumerate}
\item \textbf{Plain-wall-only guardrail.}
The rank-two D4 output, after row normal form and coefficient routing, is
\[
        F_{2Y}
        =
        H
        \begin{pmatrix}
        c_7\Gamma_G\\
        \beta_9^{\tri}\Gamma_G+\alpha_9\Gamma_{G,2}
        \end{pmatrix}
        \asymp
        H(\Gamma_G,\Gamma_{G,2}).
\]
It does not include \(D\Gamma_G\) or \(D^2\Gamma_G\).  Those derivatives may appear in analytic estimates only after a separate finite-atlas Markov/Riesz theorem.

\item \textbf{\(H^2_{\sc}\)-source completion.}
The source variable
\[
        p=q''
\]
must admit finite completion in the scale-normalized norm
\[
        \|p-p_X\|_{H^2_{\sc}(I^*)}\le Q^{-B}.
\]
Completion only in \(L^2\) or \(H^1\) leaves the term \(p''\) in
\[
        \Gamma_{G,2}=p''-6p+6q^2p
\]
uncontrolled.

\item \textbf{Affine reconstruction.}
Since
\[
        (q-q_X)''=p-p_X
\]
determines \(q-q_X\) only modulo affine functions, one must either include
\[
        \mathcal A_1=\operatorname{span}\{1,t\}
\]
in the \(q\)-atlas or impose two affine normalizations.  Otherwise \(\Gamma_G\) can drift even when \(p=p_X\).

\item \textbf{Two-derivative gamma/background ledger.}
If \(B_2\) or any gamma/background term is part of \(p=q''\), then it must be included exactly in \(\mathcal P_I\) or approximated through two scaled derivatives:
\[
        \|B_2-B_{2,X}\|_{H^2_{\sc}(I^*)}\le Q^{-B}.
\]
A zeroth-order approximation to \(B_2\) is not sufficient for \(\Gamma_{G,2}\).

\item \textbf{Tail, smoothing, and cutoff ledger.}
Every tail, smoothing, cutoff, dual, or AFE residual left outside \(\mathcal P_I\) must be \(H^2_{\sc}\)-small at the \(p=q''\) level.  In particular, cutoff terms such as
\[
        D^2(\chi p)
        =
        \chi D^2p+2(D\chi)Dp+(D^2\chi)p
\]
must be included in the ledger.

\item \textbf{Riesz handoff.}
The completion theorem only produces finite representatives
\[
        G_X\in\mathcal W_G,
        \qquad
        G_{2,X}\in\mathcal W_{G2}.
\]
It does not itself prove that \(\mathcal W_G,\mathcal W_{G2}\) have good Riesz, Markov, Remez, or leverage constants.  That is the responsibility of the finite-atlas Riesz module.
\end{enumerate}
\end{audit}

\begin{audit}[Failure routing for wall-variable completion]
\label{audit:rank-two-wall-variable-completion-failures}
Failures of \(\mathrm{WallVariableCompletion}_{2Y}\) route as follows.

\begin{enumerate}
\item If
\[
        \|p-p_X\|_{H^2_{\sc}}
\]
is unavailable, then the rank-two analytic closure stalls because \(\Gamma_{G,2}\) is not completed in \(L^2\).

\item If affine reconstruction is uncontrolled, then \(p\)-completion does not imply \(\Gamma_G\)-completion.  The repair is to add \(\mathcal A_1\) to \(\mathcal V_I\) or impose affine normalizations.

\item If \(B_2\), tail, smoothing, or cutoff residuals are not two-derivative controlled, then the second wall coordinate retains an uncontrolled residual.  The repair is exact inclusion in \(\mathcal P_I\) or an \(H^2_{\sc}\)-small residual estimate.

\item If \(G_X,G_{2,X}\) do not lie in the finite spaces
\[
        \mathcal W_G,\qquad \mathcal W_{G2},
\]
then the completion theorem does not hand off to the finite-atlas Riesz theorem.

\item If the spaces \(\mathcal W_G,\mathcal W_{G2}\) are badly conditioned, this is not a completion failure.  It is a \(\mathrm{FiniteAtlasRiesz}_{2Y}\) failure.
\end{enumerate}
\end{audit}

% === wip-rank-two-wall-variable-completion ===
% Purpose:
%   Complete the honest plain rank-two wall variables by finite-atlas representatives.
%   This module supplies analytic completion for \Gamma_G and \Gamma_{G,2};
%   it does not create derivative D4 wall channels.
% Depends on:
%   B_Y=0, row-normal-form conventions
%     \kappa=\omega_Y(e_7),
%     \ell_9^Y=\omega_Y-\kappa\ell_7,
%     \beta_9^{\tri}=\beta_9-\kappa c_7,
%   and D4 coefficient-good routing for the plain wall.
% Proves:
%   If p=q'' admits H^2_{\sc}-accurate source completion with affine reconstruction,
%   then \Gamma_G and \Gamma_{G,2} are close to finite wall spaces
%   \mathcal W_G and \mathcal W_{G2}.
% Status: finite-audit

\begin{lemma}[Source-curvature completion for the rank-two plain wall]
\label{lem:rank-two-source-curvature-completion}
Let \(I\Subset I^*\) be a chart-good interval with \(R^{-C}\)-scale buffer, and set
\[
        p:=q''.
\]
Assume that there is a finite source space \(\mathcal P_I\) and an element
\[
        p_X\in\mathcal P_I
\]
such that, for the required downstream loss parameter \(B_0\),
\[
        \|p-p_X\|_{H^2_{\sc}(I^*)}\le Q^{-B_0}.
\]
Let
\[
        \mathcal V_I:=\mathcal A_1+\mathcal I^2\mathcal P_I,
        \qquad
        \mathcal A_1:=\operatorname{span}\{1,t\},
\]
and choose \(q_X\in\mathcal V_I\) with
\[
        q_X''=p_X,
\]
where affine reconstruction modes are either included in \(\mathcal A_1\) or fixed by normalization.  Assume also that
\[
        \|q_X\|_{C^4_{\sc}(I^*)}\le Q^{C_0}.
\]
Define
\[
        \mathcal W_G
        :=
        \mathcal P_I+\mathcal V_I+\operatorname{Sym}^3\mathcal V_I
\]
and
\[
        \mathcal W_{G2}
        :=
        D^2\mathcal P_I+\mathcal P_I+
        \operatorname{Sym}^2\mathcal V_I\cdot\mathcal P_I .
\]
Then there exist
\[
        G_X\in\mathcal W_G,
        \qquad
        G_{2,X}\in\mathcal W_{G2},
\]
namely
\[
        G_X:=\Gamma_G(q_X),
        \qquad
        G_{2,X}:=\Gamma_{G,2}(q_X),
\]
such that, after choosing \(B_0\) sufficiently large relative to fixed polynomial losses,
\[
        \|\Gamma_G(q)-G_X\|_{H^2_{\sc}(I)}
        +
        \|\Gamma_{G,2}(q)-G_{2,X}\|_{L^2(I)}
        \le Q^{-B}.
\]
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}[Proof sketch]
The honest rank-two D4 wall is the plain pair
\[
        \{\Gamma_G,\Gamma_{G,2}\}.
\]
No derivative wall component is supplied by D4 in this module.  The wall functions are
\[
        \Gamma_G(q)=-6q''+36q-12q^3
\]
and
\[
        \Gamma_{G,2}(q)=q^{(4)}-6(1-q^2)q''
        =p''-6p+6q^2p.
\]
Since \(q_X''=p_X\), we have
\[
        \Gamma_G(q_X)
        =
        -6p_X+36q_X-12q_X^3
        \in
        \mathcal P_I+\mathcal V_I+\operatorname{Sym}^3\mathcal V_I
        =
        \mathcal W_G,
\]
and
\[
        \Gamma_{G,2}(q_X)
        =
        p_X''-6p_X+6q_X^2p_X
        \in
        D^2\mathcal P_I+\mathcal P_I+
        \operatorname{Sym}^2\mathcal V_I\cdot\mathcal P_I
        =
        \mathcal W_{G2}.
\]
Set
\[
        r:=q-q_X.
\]
Then
\[
        r''=p-p_X.
\]
Because affine modes are included or normalized and \(I\Subset I^*\) has polynomial buffer,
\[
        \|r\|_{H^4_{\sc}(I)}
        \le
        Q^C\|p-p_X\|_{H^2_{\sc}(I^*)}.
\]
The residuals are
\[
        R_G:=\Gamma_G(q)-\Gamma_G(q_X)
\]
and
\[
        R_{G2}:=\Gamma_{G,2}(q)-\Gamma_{G,2}(q_X).
\]
Expanding gives
\[
        R_G
        =
        -6r''
        +(36-36q_X^2)r
        -36q_Xr^2
        -12r^3,
\]
and
\[
\begin{aligned}
        R_{G2}
        &=
        r^{(4)}-6r''
        +6q_X^2r''
        +12q_Xr q_X''
        +12q_Xr r''
        +6r^2q_X''
        +6r^2r''.
\end{aligned}
\]
Using one-dimensional product estimates, \(H^2_{\sc}\)-algebra bounds, the embedding \(H^1_{\sc}\hookrightarrow L^\infty\), and
\[
        \|q_X\|_{C^4_{\sc}(I^*)}\le Q^{C_0},
\]
we obtain
\[
        \|R_G\|_{H^2_{\sc}(I)}
        +
        \|R_{G2}\|_{L^2(I)}
        \le
        Q^C
        \left(
            \|r\|_{H^4_{\sc}(I)}
            +\|r\|_{H^4_{\sc}(I)}^2
            +\|r\|_{H^4_{\sc}(I)}^3
        \right).
\]
Combining with the reconstruction estimate and increasing \(B_0\) yields the asserted wall-variable completion.
\end{proof}

\begin{remark}[Handoff to finite-atlas Riesz]
\label{rem:rank-two-wall-completion-riesz-handoff}
Lemma~\ref{lem:rank-two-source-curvature-completion} supplies finite representatives
\[
        G_X\in\mathcal W_G,\qquad G_{2,X}\in\mathcal W_{G2}.
\]
The finite-atlas Riesz module must separately prove Markov/Riesz/Remez control for
\[
        \mathcal W_G
        =
        \mathcal P_I+\mathcal V_I+\operatorname{Sym}^3\mathcal V_I
\]
and
\[
        \mathcal W_{G2}
        =
        D^2\mathcal P_I+\mathcal P_I+
        \operatorname{Sym}^2\mathcal V_I\cdot\mathcal P_I.
\]
Completion and finite-model control are separate gates: this lemma supplies the representatives; it does not prove product Riesz, leverage, or Remez bounds.
\end{remark}

% === audit-rank-two-wall-variable-completion ===
% Guardrails:
%   1. D4 supplies only H(\Gamma_G,\Gamma_{G,2}); no derivative wall components
%      are introduced here.
%   2. Completion is stated in the source variable p=q''.
%   3. p=q'' must be completed in H^2_{\sc}, not merely in L^2.
%   4. Affine reconstruction modes must be included in \mathcal V_I or fixed by
%      normalization.
%   5. Gamma/background/tail/smoothing residuals must be exact, included in
%      \mathcal P_I, or H^2_{\sc}-small.
% Failure modes:
%   p-completion only in L^2/H^1; uncontrolled affine reconstruction;
%   B_2/tail/cutoff residuals not controlled through two source derivatives;
%   finite representatives not lying in \mathcal W_G,\mathcal W_{G2};
%   bad product/Riesz conditioning in \mathcal W_G,\mathcal W_{G2}.
% Do not use:
%   D\Gamma_G or D^2\Gamma_G as D4/O2O3 wall components.

\begin{audit}[Guardrails for wall-variable completion]
\label{audit:rank-two-wall-variable-completion}
The module \(\mathrm{WallVariableCompletion}_{2Y}\) is valid only under the following guardrails.

\begin{enumerate}
\item \textbf{Plain-wall guardrail.}
After \(B_Y=0\) and row normal form, D4 gives
\[
        F_{2Y}
        =
        H
        \begin{pmatrix}
        c_7\Gamma_G\\
        \beta_9^{\tri}\Gamma_G+\alpha_9\Gamma_{G,2}
        \end{pmatrix}
        \asymp
        H(\Gamma_G,\Gamma_{G,2})
\]
under coefficient-good routing.  This module must not be read as producing a D4 wall with \(D\Gamma_G\) or \(D^2\Gamma_G\).

\item \textbf{Source-completion guardrail.}
The required analytic input is
\[
        \|p-p_X\|_{H^2_{\sc}(I^*)}\le Q^{-B},
        \qquad p=q''.
\]
Control only in \(L^2\) or \(H^1\) is insufficient, because
\[
        \Gamma_{G,2}=p''-6p+6q^2p
\]
contains \(p''\).

\item \textbf{Affine-reconstruction guardrail.}
Since \(p=q''\), reconstructing \(q\) from \(p\) leaves an affine ambiguity.  One must either include
\[
        \mathcal A_1=\operatorname{span}\{1,t\}
\]
in \(\mathcal V_I\), or impose two affine normalizations.  Otherwise \(\Gamma_G\) may drift even when \(p=p_X\).

\item \textbf{Gamma/background guardrail.}
If \(B_2\) is part of \(p=q''\), then \(B_2\) must be included exactly in \(\mathcal P_I\) or approximated in \(H^2_{\sc}\):
\[
        \|B_2-B_{2,X}\|_{H^2_{\sc}(I^*)}\le Q^{-B}.
\]
A zeroth-order approximation to \(B_2\) is not sufficient, since \(\Gamma_{G,2}\) sees two scaled derivatives of \(p\).

\item \textbf{Tail, smoothing, and cutoff guardrail.}
Tail, dual, smoothing, cutoff, and AFE residuals left outside \(\mathcal P_I\) must be \(H^2_{\sc}\)-small at the \(p=q''\) level.  In particular, for a cutoff factor \(\chi\),
\[
        D^2(\chi p)=\chi D^2p+2(D\chi)Dp+(D^2\chi)p
\]
must be accounted for either inside \(\mathcal P_I\) or in the \(H^2_{\sc}\)-small residual ledger.

\item \textbf{Finite-atlas handoff guardrail.}
The representatives \(G_X,G_{2,X}\) must lie in the exact finite wall spaces
\[
        \mathcal W_G
        =
        \mathcal P_I+\mathcal V_I+\operatorname{Sym}^3\mathcal V_I,
\]
\[
        \mathcal W_{G2}
        =
        D^2\mathcal P_I+\mathcal P_I+
        \operatorname{Sym}^2\mathcal V_I\cdot\mathcal P_I.
\]
Otherwise completion and finite-atlas Riesz do not connect.
\end{enumerate}
\end{audit}

\begin{audit}[Failure routing for wall-variable completion]
\label{audit:rank-two-wall-variable-completion-failure-routing}
Failures of \(\mathrm{WallVariableCompletion}_{2Y}\) route as follows.

\begin{enumerate}
\item If \(p=q''\) is completed only in \(L^2\) or \(H^1\), then \(\Gamma_{G,2}\) remains uncontrolled.  The rank-two analytic closure stalls.

\item If affine reconstruction is uncontrolled, then \(\Gamma_G\) may drift independently of \(p\)-completion.  The repair is to include \(\mathcal A_1\) in \(\mathcal V_I\) or impose affine normalizations.

\item If \(B_2\), tail, smoothing, or cutoff residuals are not controlled through two source derivatives, then \(\Gamma_{G,2}\) retains a residual not seen by the finite atlas.

\item If the wall representatives do not lie in \(\mathcal W_G,\mathcal W_{G2}\), then the completion theorem does not hand off to \(\mathrm{FiniteAtlasRiesz}_{2Y}^{p}\).

\item If \(\mathcal W_G,\mathcal W_{G2}\) are badly conditioned, this is not a completion failure.  It is a \(\mathrm{FiniteAtlasRiesz}_{2Y}^{p}\) or ProductRiesz failure.
\end{enumerate}
\end{audit}

## Agent 4 — \(\mathrm{SourceCurvatureCompletion}^{(2)} \Rightarrow \mathrm{WallVariableCompletion}_{2Y}\)

**Status: FINITE AUDIT, with proof skeleton.**

No nomenclature changes. Critical correction retained: D4 supplies only the honest plain wall

\[
\{\Gamma_G,\Gamma_{G,2}\}.
\]

The derivative quantities used below are analytic completion/Riesz objects, not D4 wall components. 

---

# 1. Target theorem

Let

\[
p=q''.
\]

Use

\[
\Gamma_G=-6p+36q-12q^3,
\]

and

\[
\Gamma_{G,2}=p''-6p+6q^2p.
\]

The desired theorem is:

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{SourceCurvatureCompletion}^{(2)}
\Longrightarrow
\mathrm{WallVariableCompletion}_{2Y}.
}
\]

Concretely, we need finite wall representatives

\[
G_X\in\mathcal W_G,\qquad G_{2,X}\in\mathcal W_{G2}
\]

such that

\[
\boxed{
\|\Gamma_G(q)-G_X\|_{H^2_{\sc}(I)}
+
\|\Gamma_{G,2}(q)-G_{2,X}\|_{L^2(I)}
\le Q^{-B'}.
}
\]

This is the handoff to \(\mathrm{FiniteAtlasRiesz}_{2Y}^p\).

---

# 2. Finite source and wall spaces

Let \(\mathcal P_I\) be the finite source space modeling

\[
p=q''.
\]

Choose

\[
p_X\in\mathcal P_I
\]

with

\[
\boxed{
\|p-p_X\|_{H^2_{\sc}(I^*)}\le Q^{-B}.
}
\]

Define the reconstructed \(q\)-space

\[
\boxed{
\mathcal V_I=\mathcal A_1+\mathcal I^2\mathcal P_I,
}
\]

where

\[
\mathcal A_1=\operatorname{span}\{1,t\}
\]

handles affine reconstruction modes. Choose

\[
q_X\in \mathcal V_I,\qquad q_X''=p_X.
\]

Then the finite wall spaces are

\[
\boxed{
\mathcal W_G
=
\mathcal P_I+\mathcal V_I+\operatorname{Sym}^3\mathcal V_I,
}
\]

and

\[
\boxed{
\mathcal W_{G2}
=
D^2\mathcal P_I+\mathcal P_I+
\operatorname{Sym}^2\mathcal V_I\cdot\mathcal P_I.
}
\]

Indeed,

\[
\Gamma_G(q_X)
=
-6p_X+36q_X-12q_X^3
\in \mathcal W_G,
\]

and

\[
\Gamma_{G,2}(q_X)
=
p_X''-6p_X+6q_X^2p_X
\in \mathcal W_{G2}.
\]

Set

\[
G_X:=\Gamma_G(q_X),\qquad G_{2,X}:=\Gamma_{G,2}(q_X).
\]

---

# 3. Affine reconstruction

Since

\[
(q-q_X)''=p-p_X,
\]

we only recover \(q-q_X\) modulo an affine function. This matters because

\[
\Gamma_G=-6q''+36q-12q^3
\]

depends on \(q\), not only \(q''\).

So the completion theorem requires either:

\[
\boxed{
\mathcal A_1\subset \mathcal V_I,
}
\]

or explicit affine normalizations such as

\[
(q-q_X)(t_0)=0,\qquad (q-q_X)'(t_0)=0.
\]

With \(I\Subset I^*\) and \(R^{-C}\)-scale buffer, the reconstruction estimate is

\[
\boxed{
\|q-q_X\|_{H^4_{\sc}(I)}
\le
Q^C\|p-p_X\|_{H^2_{\sc}(I^*)}.
}
\]

---

# 4. Wall residual expansions

Let

\[
r=q-q_X.
\]

Then

\[
R_G:=\Gamma_G(q)-\Gamma_G(q_X)
\]

equals

\[
\boxed{
R_G
=
-6r''
+
(36-36q_X^2)r
-
36q_Xr^2
-
12r^3.
}
\]

And

\[
R_{G2}:=\Gamma_{G,2}(q)-\Gamma_{G,2}(q_X)
\]

equals

\[
\boxed{
R_{G2}
=
r^{(4)}-6r''
+
6q_X^2r''
+
12q_Xr q_X''
+
12q_Xr r''
+
6r^2q_X''
+
6r^2r''.
}
\]

Assume polynomial atlas bounds

\[
\boxed{
\|q_X\|_{C^4_{\sc}(I^*)}\le Q^C.
}
\]

Then one-dimensional product estimates give

\[
\|R_G\|_{H^2_{\sc}(I)}
+
\|R_{G2}\|_{L^2(I)}
\le
Q^C
\left(
\|r\|_{H^4_{\sc}(I)}
+
\|r\|_{H^4_{\sc}(I)}^2
+
\|r\|_{H^4_{\sc}(I)}^3
\right).
\]

Using affine reconstruction,

\[
\|r\|_{H^4_{\sc}(I)}
\le
Q^C\|p-p_X\|_{H^2_{\sc}(I^*)}.
\]

Thus, after choosing \(B\) large enough,

\[
\boxed{
\|R_G\|_{H^2_{\sc}(I)}
+
\|R_{G2}\|_{L^2(I)}
\le Q^{-B'}.
}
\]

Therefore

\[
\boxed{
\|\Gamma_G(q)-G_X\|_{H^2_{\sc}(I)}
+
\|\Gamma_{G,2}(q)-G_{2,X}\|_{L^2(I)}
\le Q^{-B'}.
}
\]

---

# 5. Source-residual ledger

The finite audit is the \(H^2_{\sc}\)-ledger for

\[
p=q''.
\]

If

\[
p
=
B_2+p_{\mathrm{AFE}}+p_{\mathrm{dual}}+p_{\mathrm{tail}}+p_{\mathrm{smooth}}+p_{\mathrm{err}},
\]

then every component must be either exact, included in \(\mathcal P_I\), or \(H^2_{\sc}\)-small:

\[
\boxed{
\sum_\bullet
\|r_\bullet\|_{H^2_{\sc}(I^*)}
\le Q^{-B}.
}
\]

In particular:

\[
\boxed{
B_2\text{ must be exact or completed through }B_2,\ DB_2,\ D^2B_2.
}
\]

A zeroth-order \(B_2\)-approximation is not enough, because

\[
\Gamma_{G,2}=p''-6p+6q^2p
\]

sees \(p''\).

Similarly, AFE, dual, tail, smoothing, cutoff, and error residuals must be \(H^2_{\sc}\)-small at the \(p=q''\)-level, or included in \(\mathcal P_I\).

For cutoffs,

\[
D^2(\chi p)
=
\chi D^2p+2(D\chi)Dp+(D^2\chi)p,
\]

so cutoff derivative terms must be in the finite model or in the residual ledger.

---

# 6. Output theorem

## \(\mathrm{SourceCurvatureCompletion}^{(2)}\Rightarrow\mathrm{WallVariableCompletion}_{2Y}\)

Assume:

1. \(p=q''\) has finite source approximation

\[
p_X\in\mathcal P_I,\qquad
\|p-p_X\|_{H^2_{\sc}(I^*)}\le Q^{-B}.
\]

2. The reconstructed space is

\[
\mathcal V_I=\mathcal A_1+\mathcal I^2\mathcal P_I,
\]

with affine modes included or normalized.

3. \(I\Subset I^*\) has \(R^{-C}\)-scale buffer.

4. \(q_X''=p_X\) and

\[
\|q_X\|_{C^4_{\sc}(I^*)}\le Q^C.
\]

5. Gamma/background, AFE, dual, tail, smoothing/cutoff, and error residuals are exact, included in \(\mathcal P_I\), or \(H^2_{\sc}\)-small.

Then there exist

\[
G_X\in\mathcal W_G,\qquad G_{2,X}\in\mathcal W_{G2}
\]

such that

\[
\boxed{
\|\Gamma_G(q)-G_X\|_{H^2_{\sc}(I)}
+
\|\Gamma_{G,2}(q)-G_{2,X}\|_{L^2(I)}
\le Q^{-B'}.
}
\]

This is the precise finite-audit completion module needed by the corrected rank-two plain-wall route.

---

# 7. Failure routing

## NO-GO / obstruction cases

1. **Only \(L^2\) or \(H^1\) source completion.**

   If

   \[
   \|p-p_X\|_{H^2_{\sc}}
   \]

   is unavailable, then \(\Gamma_{G,2}\) is uncontrolled.

2. **Affine reconstruction uncontrolled.**

   If \(\mathcal A_1\) is not included or normalized, \(\Gamma_G\) can drift even when \(p=p_X\).

3. **\(B_2\), tail, or smoothing not controlled through two source derivatives.**

   Then \(\Gamma_{G,2}\) retains an uncontrolled residual.

4. **Wall approximants not in \(\mathcal W_G,\mathcal W_{G2}\).**

   Then completion and finite-atlas Riesz do not connect.

5. **Bad Riesz/ProductRiesz conditioning of \(\mathcal W_G,\mathcal W_{G2}\).**

   This is not Agent 4’s completion failure. It is an Agent 5 / \(\mathrm{FiniteAtlasRiesz}_{2Y}^p\) failure.

---

# 8. Final status

\[
\boxed{\text{FINITE AUDIT}}
\]

The module is closed if and only if the following finite source-residual ledger passes:

\[
\boxed{
q''\text{-source completion in }H^2_{\sc}
+
\text{affine reconstruction}
+
\text{two-derivative gamma/tail/smoothing ledger}.
}
\]

This supplies finite representatives in

\[
\mathcal W_G
=
\mathcal P_I+\mathcal V_I+\operatorname{Sym}^3\mathcal V_I
\]

and

\[
\mathcal W_{G2}
=
D^2\mathcal P_I+\mathcal P_I+
\operatorname{Sym}^2\mathcal V_I\cdot\mathcal P_I.
\]

It does not assume or create derivative D4 wall channels.

## Edit 1 — insert after `rem:ordinary-route-no-go-summary`

**Location:** In Section `\ref{sec:upstream-gate-one-arithmetic-inputs}`, immediately after:


**Insert:**

```latex
\begin{remark}[Dual form of O3: left-null polynomial nonorthogonality]
\label{rem:dual-left-null-polynomial-O3}
The O3 input can be put in an exact dual form.  Let
\[
F=Z_\alpha,\qquad G=q''_{\can},\qquad \mathcal D=\lambda\partial_m.
\]
At an admissible ordinary zero \(F(m_0)=0\), write
\[
f_k=(\mathcal D^kF)(m_0),\qquad
g_k=(\mathcal D^kG)(m_0).
\]
Let
\[
b=(f_0,\ldots,f_{R+1})^T,
\]
and let
\[
K=(g_{j+k})_{\substack{0\le k\le R+1\\0\le j\le R}}
\in\mathbf C^{(R+2)\times(R+1)}.
\]
Then O2 is the lower singular-value statement for \(K\), while O3 is the
Schur-complement statement
\[
\operatorname{dist}(b,\operatorname{col}K)\ge Q^{-A}\|b\|.
\]
Assume O2, so that \(K\) has rank \(R+1\).  Let \(u\) span the left nullspace of
\(K\), and define
\[
U(x)=\sum_{k=0}^{R+1}\overline{u_k}x^k.
\]
If \(L=L_{m_0,\alpha}\) denotes the zero-conditioned prime-log moment
functional and \(m=m_{\can}\) denotes the canonical curvature multiplier
relating \(q''_{\can}\) to \(Z_\alpha\) on the ordinary packet, then
\[
u^*K=0
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
L\bigl(U(x)m(x)P(x)\bigr)=0
\quad
\text{for all }P\in\mathbf C_R[x].
\]
Thus
\[
U\perp m\mathbf C_R[x]
\]
in the zero-conditioned complex moment geometry.  Moreover,
\[
u^*b=L(U).
\]
Consequently O3 is exactly the lower bound
\[
\boxed{
U\perp m\mathbf C_R[x]
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
|L(U)|\ge Q^{-A}\|U\|.
}
\]
Equivalently, O2 and O3 together are the augmented complex-frame lower bound
for
\[
\mathbf C\cdot1+m\mathbf C_R[x].
\]
This dual form should be used when comparing pointwise O2/O3 to aggregate
replacement inputs.
\end{remark}

\begin{remark}[O3 is not supplied by multiplier variation alone]
\label{rem:multiplier-variation-does-not-prove-O3}
The canonical multiplier \(m_{\can}\) must be non-scalar on the ordinary packet.
Indeed, if
\[
m_{\can}(x)\equiv c,
\]
then \(q''_{\can}\) is, at the level of this packet model, a scalar multiple of
\(Z_\alpha\), the first curvature column is proportional to \(b\), and O3 fails
identically:
\[
b\in\operatorname{col}K.
\]
More generally, if \(m_{\can}\) is too close to a scalar or if \(1/m_{\can}\)
is too well approximated by a degree-\(R\) polynomial, then the O3 residual is
small.

However, non-scalar multiplier variation is not sufficient.  The functional
\(L_{m_0,\alpha}\) is complex and oscillatory.  Even when \(m_{\can}\) is
genuinely nonconstant and \(L(1)=0\), a phase-relaxed model can arrange
\[
L\bigl(UmP\bigr)=0\quad(P\in\mathbf C_R[x]),
\qquad
L(U)=0,
\]
with \(K\) still full rank.  Therefore the true missing input is not ordinary
symbol variation but the zero-conditioned complex-frame theorem
\[
\boxed{
\inf_{(c,P)\ne0}
\left\|
\bigl(
L((c+m_{\can}P)x^k)
\bigr)_{0\le k\le R+1}
\right\|_2
\ge Q^{-A}(|c|+\|P\|).
}
\]
This is an actual prime-log arithmetic input, not a consequence of local
Wronskian algebra.
\end{remark}
```

```latex
\subsection{Aggregate ordinary replacement: Stack C2}
\label{subsec:aggregate-stack-c2-ordinary-replacement}

The pointwise ordinary package
\[
\mathrm{O1}+\mathrm{O2}+\mathrm{O3}
\]
is the cleanest local statement, but it is statistically stronger than the
aggregate information normally suggested by the actual prime-log model.  The
current aggregate replacement is Stack C2:
\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{Agg}L^2(q''_{\can})
+
\mathrm{JointAgg}_{1,23,\mathrm{wall}}
+
\mathrm{GoodSubsetExtraction}
+
\mathrm{GoodSubsetDownstream}.
}
\]
This is not a proved theorem in the present draft.  It is the preferred
aggregate reformulation of the ordinary branch if the pointwise O1/O2/O3
package is judged too strong.

\begin{definition}[Local augmented O2/O3 map]
\label{def:local-augmented-o2o3-map}
At an admissible ordinary center \(m\), let
\[
L_m
\]
be the zero-conditioned prime-log moment functional, including the full
canonical plus, folded-dual, background, and completion contributions.  Define
the augmented O2/O3 map
\[
\mathcal A_m(c,P)
:=
\left(
L_m\bigl((c+m_{\can}P)x^k\bigr)
\right)_{0\le k\le R+1},
\qquad
(c,P)\in\mathbf C\oplus\mathbf C_R[x].
\]
Let
\[
E_{23}(m):=\sigma_{\min}(\mathcal A_m)^2.
\]
Equivalently, if
\[
G_m:=\mathcal A_m^*\mathcal A_m,
\qquad
n:=R+2,
\]
define the harmonic exterior quantity
\[
\mathcal H_{23}(m)
:=
\frac{\det G_m}{e_{n-1}(G_m)}
=
\frac{1}{\operatorname{tr}(G_m^{-1})}
\]
when \(G_m\) is invertible, with the convention \(\mathcal H_{23}=0\) otherwise.
This is pointwise comparable to the smallest singular-value square:
\[
\frac1n\sigma_{\min}(\mathcal A_m)^2
\le
\mathcal H_{23}(m)
\le
\sigma_{\min}(\mathcal A_m)^2.
\]
\end{definition}

\begin{definition}[Gate--1/wall weighted joint aggregate]
\label{def:jointagg-123-wall}
Let
\[
W_2(m):=|\Gamma_G(m)|^2+|\Gamma_{G,2}(m)|^2.
\]
Let \(E_{\mathrm{wall,full}}(m)\) denote the full local wall eligibility factor:
it includes the plain wall factor, D4 coefficient-goodness, Gram/gauge/carrier
eligibility, and any chosen forbidden-channel visibility factor.  In the basic
plain-wall route this includes
\[
N_{\sm}|H|^2W_2.
\]
Let \(X_{\can}(m)\) be the canonical source vector used for aggregate Gate--1,
and let \(T_{\can}X_{\can}\) be the visible canonical projection.  The
wall-inclusive joint aggregate theorem is
\[
\boxed{
\sum_{m\in C}
\rho(m)
|T_{\can}X_{\can}(m)|^2
\mathcal H_{23}(m)
E_{\mathrm{wall,full}}(m)
\ge
Q^{-A}
\sum_{m\in C}
\rho(m)|X_{\can}(m)|^2.
}
\]
Equivalently, with
\[
d\mu(m):=\rho(m)|X_{\can}(m)|^2,
\qquad
E_1(m):=\frac{|T_{\can}X_{\can}(m)|^2}{|X_{\can}(m)|^2},
\]
the theorem reads
\[
\int_C
E_1(m)\mathcal H_{23}(m)E_{\mathrm{wall,full}}(m)\,d\mu(m)
\ge
Q^{-A}\mu(C).
\]
\end{definition}

\begin{lemma}[Joint aggregate extraction]
\label{lem:jointagg-good-subset-extraction}
Assume the joint aggregate theorem of
Definition~\ref{def:jointagg-123-wall}.  Assume also polynomial upper bounds
\[
0\le E_1(m)\le Q^C,\qquad
0\le \mathcal H_{23}(m)\le Q^C,\qquad
0\le E_{\mathrm{wall,full}}(m)\le Q^C.
\]
Then there is a subset \(G\subset C\) with
\[
\mu(G)\ge Q^{-C'}\mu(C)
\]
such that, on \(G\),
\[
E_1(m)\ge Q^{-C'},\qquad
\mathcal H_{23}(m)\ge Q^{-C'},\qquad
E_{\mathrm{wall,full}}(m)\ge Q^{-C'}.
\]
Thus the pointwise downstream machinery may be run on \(G\), after replacing
the original pointwise O1/O2/O3 inputs by aggregate joint extraction.
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
Let
\[
\eta=Q^{-A}
\]
be the product lower-bound scale and let \(B_i\le Q^C\) be polynomial upper
bounds for the three factors.  Choose thresholds \(a_i\) polynomially below
\(\eta/(B_1B_2B_3)\).  Outside the set where all three factors exceed their
thresholds, the product is at most a fixed fraction of the aggregate lower
scale.  Therefore the good set has polynomial \(\mu\)-measure.  This is the
standard product-energy extraction argument.
\end{proof}

\begin{remark}[Separate aggregate inputs do not imply intersection]
\label{rem:separate-aggregate-inputs-do-not-intersect}
Separate estimates
\[
\int_C E_1\,d\mu\ge Q^{-A}\mu(C),
\qquad
\int_C \mathcal H_{23}\,d\mu\ge Q^{-A}\mu(C)
\]
do not imply that the Gate--1-good set intersects the O2/O3-good set.  The two
polynomial-measure subsets can be disjoint.  Stack C2 therefore requires a
joint aggregate theorem, or an equivalent restricted theorem proving aggregate
O2/O3 on the Gate--1-good subset.
\end{remark}

\begin{remark}[Leave-one-out form of aggregate O2/O3]
\label{rem:leave-one-out-form-aggregate-o2o3}
Let \(v_0,\ldots,v_{R+1}\) be the columns of \(\mathcal A_m\).  Define
\[
\Delta_j^{\mathrm{loo}}(m)
:=
\operatorname{dist}
\left(
v_j(m),\operatorname{span}\{v_i(m):i\ne j\}
\right)^2.
\]
Then
\[
\mathcal H_{23}(m)
=
\left(
\sum_{j=0}^{R+1}
(\Delta_j^{\mathrm{loo}}(m))^{-1}
\right)^{-1}.
\]
The leave-one-out residual corresponding to the constant column \(v_0\) is the
O3 residual.  The remaining leave-one-out residuals are the O2 cofactor
residuals.  Thus aggregate O2/O3 can equivalently be read as the statement that
Gate--1 and wall mass do not concentrate on centers where one augmented column
is close to the span of the others.
\end{remark}

\begin{remark}[Determinant proxy and normalization]
\label{rem:determinant-proxy-normalization-o2o3}
The exterior determinant
\[
|\det\mathcal A_m|^2
\]
is algebraically expandable, but it controls \(\mathcal H_{23}\) only under
a strong normalization.  If the augmented map is represented in a basis with
\[
\|\mathcal A_m\|\le R^C,
\]
then
\[
\sigma_{\min}(\mathcal A_m)^2
\ge
|\det\mathcal A_m|^2\,R^{-CR}
\ge Q^{-C'}|\det\mathcal A_m|^2.
\]
If instead one only knows \(\|\mathcal A_m\|\le Q^C\), the conversion loses
\(Q^{O(R)}\), which is super-polynomial.  Thus determinant-energy versions of
Stack C2 must use an \(R^C\)-normalized Riesz basis for the augmented map.
\end{remark}

\begin{remark}[Status of Stack C2]
\label{rem:status-stack-c2}
Stack C2 is an aggregate replacement for the pointwise ordinary O1/O2/O3
package.  It is not proved in this draft.  Its central arithmetic input is a
zero-restricted, wall-weighted, exterior or harmonic lower-frame theorem for
the actual canonical packet
\[
q''_{\can}=B_2+P_2+D_2+E_2.
\]
It is dimensionally more plausible than pointwise O2/O3, but it remains a
major actual-zeta arithmetic theorem.
\end{remark}
```

## Edit 3 — insert after `rem:rank-two-no-go-register`

**Location:** In the rank-two architecture section, immediately after:

```latex
\begin{remark}[Rank-two no-go and guardrail register]
\label{rem:rank-two-no-go-register}
...
\end{remark}
```

**Insert:**

```latex
\begin{remark}[Finite D4 still needs a forbidden channel]
\label{rem:finite-d4-needs-forbidden-channel}
The rank-two local D4 row conversion is a visibility theorem unless it is paired
with a global or atlas-level forbidden identity.  Even after
\[
B_Y=0
\]
and after coefficient-good routing gives the plain wall
\[
H(\Gamma_G,\Gamma_{G,2}),
\]
finite D4 by itself only says that the local carrier plane is visible.  To
obtain a contradiction one still needs an operator \(\Lambda\) such that
\[
\Lambda V_{\glob}=0
\]
for actual global scalar rows, but
\[
\operatorname{rank}(\Lambda E_{\car}L_{\clean})\ge1
\]
on the D4 live plane.  Without such a \(\Lambda\), finite D4 remains local
visibility, not a contradiction channel.
\end{remark}

\begin{definition}[Two-chart transition--singular projection holonomy]
\label{def:two-chart-transition-singular-holonomy}
Let
\[
T:=T_{\tr\to\sing},
\qquad
T^{-1}:=T_{\sing\to\tr}
\]
be exact transition maps between the transition and singular chart jet spaces.
Let
\[
P_{\tr}:\mathcal J_{\tr}\to W_{\tr},
\qquad
P_{\sing}:\mathcal J_{\sing}\to W_{\sing}
\]
be finite carrier projections, with
\[
W_{\tr}=\operatorname{span}\{[\mathsf C_7]^{\tr},[\mathsf C_{Y12}]^{\tr}\}.
\]
Define the two-chart projection holonomy
\[
\boxed{
\Lambda_{\tr,\sing}^{(R)}
:=
P_{\tr}T^{-1}P_{\sing}TP_{\tr}-P_{\tr}.
}
\]
On \(W_{\tr}\), since \(P_{\tr}\) is the identity, this equals
\[
\Lambda_{\tr,\sing}^{(R)}w
=
-P_{\tr}T^{-1}(I-P_{\sing})Tw.
\]
Thus the finite holonomy is precisely the singular discarded component of a
transition carrier, transported back to the transition live plane.
\end{definition}

\begin{definition}[Atlas forbidden wall factor]
\label{def:atlas-forbidden-wall-factor}
Let
\[
K:=(I-P_{\sing})T_{\tr\to\sing}|_{W_{\tr}}.
\]
For
\[
c_7:=[\mathsf C_7]^{\tr},
\qquad
c_9:=[\mathsf C_{Y12}]^{\tr},
\]
define the \(2\times2\) forbidden Gram matrix
\[
G_{\forb}
:=
\begin{pmatrix}
\langle Kc_7,Kc_7\rangle & \langle Kc_7,Kc_9\rangle\\
\langle Kc_9,Kc_7\rangle & \langle Kc_9,Kc_9\rangle
\end{pmatrix}.
\]
The atlas-forbidden D4 wall factor is
\[
E_{\wall}^{\forb}(m)
:=
\frac{
|H(m)|^2\,
\Gamma(m)^*
L_{\clean}(m)^*
G_{\forb}(m)
L_{\clean}(m)\Gamma(m)
}{
\mathcal N_{\D4}(m)
},
\qquad
\Gamma=(\Gamma_G,\Gamma_{G,2})^T.
\]
If \(G_{\forb}\) has rank \(2\), the full rank-two D4 wall is visible to this
atlas channel.  If \(G_{\forb}\) has rank \(1\), one forbidden linear
combination of \(\Gamma_G,\Gamma_{G,2}\) is visible.  If \(G_{\forb}=0\), this
two-chart atlas channel is D4-blind.
\end{definition}

\begin{remark}[Atlas upper as singular discarded projection]
\label{rem:atlas-upper-singular-discarded-projection}
For an actual global row \(J_{\tr}(u)\), exact chart functoriality gives
\[
T^{-1}TJ_{\tr}(u)=J_{\tr}(u).
\]
The projected two-chart defect is
\[
\Lambda_{\tr,\sing}^{(R)}J_{\tr}(u)
=
-P_{\tr}T^{-1}(I-P_{\sing})TJ_{\tr}(u).
\]
Therefore a sufficient global upper for the atlas-forbidden channel is
\[
\sum_p
\|(I-P_{\sing,p})T_{\tr\to\sing,p}J_{\tr,p}(q''_{\can})\|^2
\le Q^{-B}\mu(C).
\]
Thus the atlas-\(\Lambda\) upper is a singular approximation/coherence theorem:
actual scalar source rows must be well approximated by the retained singular
carrier space.  D4 local carriers need not satisfy this approximation, and
their discarded singular component is exactly what the finite holonomy detects.
\end{remark}

\begin{remark}[Two-layer basis guardrail]
\label{rem:two-layer-basis-guardrail}
The analytic Riesz basis used to normalize the augmented O2/O3 map should not
be identified with the carrier projection basis used to define
\(\Lambda_{\tr,\sing}^{(R)}\).  A functorial basis good for analytic
normalization may commute with chart transitions and kill atlas holonomy.
The safe design is two-layered:
\[
\boxed{\text{Riesz basis for analytic O2/O3 normalization}}
\]
and
\[
\boxed{\text{carrier projection basis for finite atlas holonomy}},
\]
with an \(R^C\)-bounded interface on the D4 live plane.  If this interface is
not polynomially conditioned, an O2/O3 lower bound in one coordinate system may
not transfer to the D4 carrier coordinates.
\end{remark}
```

**Insert:**

```latex
\begin{remark}[Aggregate Stack C2 is an alternate upstream route]
\label{rem:terminal-ledger-stack-c2-alternate}
The terminal ledger above records the pointwise ordinary route through
\[
\mathrm{O1}+\mathrm{O2}+\mathrm{O3}.
\]
The aggregate Stack C2 route of
Subsection~\ref{subsec:aggregate-stack-c2-ordinary-replacement} is an alternate
ordinary upstream route.  It replaces pointwise O1/O2/O3 by the joint aggregate
input
\[
\mathrm{JointAgg}_{1,23,\mathrm{wall}},
\]
followed by good-subset extraction.  If Stack C2 is used, the ordinary input
ledger should be read as
\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{Agg}L^2(q''_{\can})
+
\mathrm{JointAgg}_{1,23,\mathrm{wall}}
\Longrightarrow
\text{ordinary good subset}.
}
\]
This alternate route does not weaken the downstream requirements: after
extraction, one still needs row-normal D4, coefficient-core routing,
wall-variable completion, finite-atlas Riesz, and scalar O2/O3 transfer on the
extracted subset.

At present, Stack C2 is also open.  Its central missing theorem is the
zero-restricted wall-weighted exterior or harmonic lower-frame estimate for the
actual canonical packet.
\end{remark}

\begin{remark}[Forbidden-channel status]
\label{rem:terminal-forbidden-channel-status}
The finite-D4 branch has an additional dependency not captured by the local
row-normal exactness statement alone.  The local D4 plane must be paired with a
global or atlas-level forbidden operator \(\Lambda\).  The current leading
candidate is the two-chart transition--singular projection holonomy
\[
\Lambda_{\tr,\sing}^{(R)}
=
P_{\tr}T_{\sing\to\tr}P_{\sing}T_{\tr\to\sing}P_{\tr}-P_{\tr}.
\]
The required finite audit is
\[
\operatorname{rank}
\left(
\Lambda_{\tr,\sing}^{(R)}
E_{\car}L_{\clean}
\right)\ge1
\]
on
\[
\operatorname{span}\{[\mathsf C_7],[\mathsf C_{Y12}]\},
\]
together with an aggregate upper showing that actual global rows have small
singular discarded projection.  If no such \(\Lambda\) exists, finite D4 remains
a local visibility theorem and does not by itself close the proof.
\end{remark}
```

**Location:** Immediately after:

```latex
\label{rem:rank-two-symbolic-o2o3-degree}
...
\end{remark}
```

**Insert:**

```latex
\begin{remark}[Aggregate O2/O3 degree and determinant normalization]
\label{rem:aggregate-o2o3-degree-determinant-normalization}
In the aggregate Stack C2 route, the augmented O2/O3 map
\[
\mathcal A_m:\mathbf C\oplus\mathbf C_R[x]\to\mathbf C^{R+2}
\]
must be written in an \(R^C\)-normalized Riesz basis if determinant or exterior
energy is used.  A bound of the form
\[
\|\mathcal A_m\|\le Q^C
\]
is not enough, since converting determinant energy to a smallest-singular-value
lower bound loses
\[
\|\mathcal A_m\|^{2(R+1)}.
\]
With only \(Q^C\)-control this is super-polynomial.  With
\[
\|\mathcal A_m\|\le R^C,
\]
the loss is
\[
R^{CR}=Q^{O(1)},
\]
which is compatible with polynomial bookkeeping.  Thus any determinant-based
aggregate O2/O3 theorem must be paired with finite-atlas Riesz normalization.
\end{remark}
```

## Edit 7 — insert in no-go archive after `subsec:no-go-archive-ordinary-wronskian` opening paragraph

**Location:** In Subsection:

```latex
\subsection{Ordinary Wronskian no-go archive}
\label{subsec:no-go-archive-ordinary-wronskian}
```

after the initial paragraph that starts:

```latex
The ordinary Wronskian no-go results are centralized...
```

**Insert:**

```latex
\begin{remark}[O3 as Hermite--Padé noncontact]
\label{rem:O3-hermite-pade-noncontact}
In monomial jet coordinates, exact O3 failure has the following equivalent
form.  Let
\[
F=Z_\alpha,\qquad G=q''_{\can},\qquad \mathcal D=\lambda\partial_m.
\]
At an admissible ordinary zero \(F(m_0)=0\), O3 fails if and only if there is
a differential polynomial
\[
A(\mathcal D)=\sum_{j=0}^{R}a_j\mathcal D^j
\]
such that
\[
\mathcal D^k\left(F-A(\mathcal D)G\right)(m_0)=0,
\qquad
0\le k\le R+1.
\]
Thus O3 is a zero-restricted Hermite--Pad\'e noncontact theorem for the pair
\[
(Z_\alpha,q''_{\can}).
\]
Differentiating this incidence in \(m\) or in an auxiliary parameter only
produces higher-order contact conditions; it does not contradict O3 failure.
\end{remark}

\begin{remark}[Pointwise O2/O3 is counter-random at polynomial threshold]
\label{rem:pointwise-o2o3-counter-random}
After O2/O3 are unified into the augmented matrix
\[
\mathcal A_m=[\,b\ K\,]\in\mathbf C^{(R+2)\times(R+2)},
\]
pointwise O2/O3 asks for
\[
\sigma_{\min}(\mathcal A_m)\ge Q^{-A}
\]
at every admissible ordinary zero.  Random complex-matrix and random
Euler-product heuristics predict polynomial small-singular-value tails.  Since
the number of zeros up to height \(T\sim e^Q\) is \(\asymp e^Q Q\), a universal
pointwise polynomial threshold is statistically mis-sized unless the actual
prime-log orbit supplies a strong counter-random theorem.  This is the main
motivation for the aggregate Stack C2 replacement in
Subsection~\ref{subsec:aggregate-stack-c2-ordinary-replacement}.
\end{remark}
```

## Edit 1 — strengthen the singular-discard D4 module with the two-chart projection-loss identity

**Location:** Immediately after:

```latex
\begin{definition}[Transition--singular forbidden discard operator]
```

**Insert:**

```latex
\begin{lemma}[Two-chart projection-loss identity]
\label{lem:two-chart-projection-loss-identity}
Let
\[
  T:=T_{\tr\to\sing},
  \qquad
  T^{-1}:=T_{\sing\to\tr},
\]
and define the two-chart projected holonomy on \(W_{\tr}\) by
\[
  \widehat\Lambda_{\tr,\sing}^{(R)}
  :=
  P_{\tr}T^{-1}P_{\sing}TP_{\tr}-P_{\tr}.
\]
Then, on \(W_{\tr}\),
\[
  \boxed{
  \widehat\Lambda_{\tr,\sing}^{(R)}
  =
  -P_{\tr}T^{-1}(I-P_{\sing})T.
  }
\]
Consequently the two-chart projected holonomy is exactly the singular discarded
component of a transition live carrier, transported back to the transition
carrier plane.

In particular, with
\[
  K_{\tr,\sing}:=(I-P_{\sing})T_{\tr\to\sing}|_{W_{\tr}},
\]
the finite D4 visibility of this channel is controlled by the Gram matrix
\[
  G_{\forb}
  =
  \begin{pmatrix}
  \langle K_{\tr,\sing}[\mathsf C_7],K_{\tr,\sing}[\mathsf C_7]\rangle
  &
  \langle K_{\tr,\sing}[\mathsf C_7],K_{\tr,\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]\rangle
  \\[2mm]
  \langle K_{\tr,\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}],K_{\tr,\sing}[\mathsf C_7]\rangle
  &
  \langle K_{\tr,\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}],K_{\tr,\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]\rangle
  \end{pmatrix}.
\]
Thus this channel has rank \(0,1,\) or \(2\) according as the two discarded
singular images of
\[
  [\mathsf C_7]^{\tr},\qquad [\mathsf C_{Y12}]^{\tr}
\]
span dimension \(0,1,\) or \(2\) in \(C_{\sing}\).
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
For \(w\in W_{\tr}\), \(P_{\tr}w=w\).  Therefore
\[
\begin{aligned}
  \widehat\Lambda_{\tr,\sing}^{(R)}w
  &=
  P_{\tr}T^{-1}P_{\sing}Tw-w  \\
  &=
  P_{\tr}T^{-1}P_{\sing}Tw-P_{\tr}T^{-1}Tw \\
  &=
  -P_{\tr}T^{-1}(I-P_{\sing})Tw.
\end{aligned}
\]
The rank statement follows by applying this identity to the two-dimensional live
plane \(W_{\tr}\).
\end{proof}

\begin{remark}[Positive projection-loss form]
\label{rem:positive-projection-loss-form}
If the transition map is Riesz-unitary up to polynomial constants and
\(P_{\sing}\) is orthogonal in the singular carrier norm, then the two-chart
defect is equivalent to the positive discarded projection energy
\[
  \|(I-P_{\sing})T_{\tr\to\sing}w\|^2.
\]
Thus the singular-discard channel is best viewed as a finite-element-type
projection-loss mechanism:
\[
  \text{actual global rows glue well through the singular retained space,}
\]
while
\[
  \text{local D4 carrier patterns may fail to glue after finite projection.}
\]
This is the current concrete candidate for converting carrier-quotient D4
visibility into a forbidden-channel contradiction.
\end{remark}
```

## Edit 2 — clarify the exact global upper required for singular-discard D4

**Location:** Immediately after:

```latex
\begin{theorem}[Finite audit for forbidden singular-discard D4]
\label{thm:forbidden-singular-discard-D4-audit}
...
\end{theorem}
```

**Insert:**

```latex
\begin{remark}[Actual-row upper for the singular-discard channel]
\label{rem:actual-row-upper-singular-discard-channel}
The global upper in Theorem~\ref{thm:forbidden-singular-discard-D4-audit} is not
a generic row-energy upper.  It is the specific approximation/coherence estimate
\[
  \sum_p
  \|(I-P_{\sing,p})T_{\tr\to\sing,p}J_{\tr,p}(q''_{\can})\|^2
  \le Q^{-B}\mu(\mathcal C).
\]
Equivalently, actual transition-chart source rows for
\[
  q''_{\can}=B_2+P_2+D_2+E_2
\]
must be well approximated by the retained singular carrier space.

This estimate is structurally parallel to the branch-complete \(L^2\)-Gate--1
completion/coherence estimate.  In favorable cases the same effective
branch-bandwidth theorem may serve two roles:
\[
  \boxed{
  \text{source }L^2\text{ observability}
  }
  \qquad\text{and}\qquad
  \boxed{
  \text{small singular-discard projection for actual rows.}
  }
\]
If the estimate above fails, the singular-discard channel cannot be used as a
forbidden identity even when it detects local D4 carriers.
\end{remark}
```

## Edit 3 — add the full-canonical graph-block formulation to the finite/O2O3 section

**Location:** In Subsection:

```latex
\subsection{Finite/O2O3\(_{23}\): analytic Riesz graph and exterior-pivot input}
\label{subsec:finite-o2o3-23}
```

Immediately after:

```latex
\begin{definition}[Riesz graph model for O2/O3]
\label{def:finite-o2o3-riesz-graph-model}
...
\end{definition}
```

**Insert:**

```latex
\begin{definition}[Full canonical graph-block model]
\label{def:full-canonical-graph-block-model}
The pure-branch graph model is a local analytic normalization device.  The full
canonical finite/O2O3 matrix must still be built from
\[
  q''_{\can}=B_2+P_2+D_2+E_2.
\]
At the level of branch symbols one should regard the full augmented space as
\[
  \mathcal V_{\full}
  =
  \mathbf C\cdot1
  +
  m_+\mathbf C_R[x]
  +
  m_-\mathbf C_R[-x]
  +
  \mathcal V_{\comp},
\]
where \(m_+\) and \(m_-\) are the branch-normalized prime and folded-dual
curvature multipliers, and \(\mathcal V_{\comp}\) contains the finite
background/completion directions.

The pure branch graph theorem controls, for example,
\[
  \mathbf C\cdot1+m_+\mathbf C_R[x]
  =
  m_+\bigl(\mathbf C\cdot(1/m_+)+\mathbf C_R[x]\bigr).
\]
It does not by itself control the full space \(\mathcal V_{\full}\).  For the
full canonical O2/O3 theorem one needs a branch-block survival statement:
\[
  \operatorname{dist}
  \left(
  1,\,
  m_+\mathbf C_R[x]+m_-\mathbf C_R[-x]+\mathcal V_{\comp}
  \right)
  \ge Q^{-A}
\]
in the relevant zero-conditioned moment geometry, or an aggregate
exterior-pivot substitute proving the same lower-frame conclusion.
\end{definition}

\begin{remark}[Pure branch graph is not enough]
\label{rem:pure-branch-graph-not-enough}
If
\[
  m_+(x)\simeq \kappa x^2
\]
on an ordinary subpacket, then \(1/m_+\) has a robust non-polynomiality
against \(\mathbf C_R[x]\).  This gives a strong analytic model for the pure
plus branch.  However, the full canonical augmented matrix contains plus,
folded-dual, background, and completion components.  The dual branch can help
approximate the constant direction unless the branch blocks are sufficiently
orthogonal, sector-compatible, or controlled by the exterior-pivot theorem.
Thus the pure branch graph model is a useful analytic submodule, not a complete
finite/O2O3 theorem for \(q''_{\can}\).
\end{remark}
```

---

## Edit 4 — add the determinant Cauchy–Binet / divided-difference goodie as an audit remark

**Location:** In the same finite/O2O3 subsection, immediately after:

```latex
\begin{definition}[Full determinant survival under Stack weight]
\label{def:full-determinant-survival-stack-weight}
...
\end{definition}
```

**Insert:**

```latex
\begin{remark}[Cauchy--Binet determinant coefficient and divided differences]
\label{rem:cauchy-binet-divided-difference-o2o3}
For a single branch with frequencies \(x_n=\lambda\log n\), write the augmented
columns as
\[
  h_0(x)=1,\qquad h_{j+1}(x)=m_{\can}(x)x^j,\quad 0\le j\le R.
\]
If
\[
  A_m=(L_m(h_jx^k))_{0\le k,j\le R+1},
\]
then Cauchy--Binet gives
\[
  \det A_m
  =
  \sum_{\substack{S\\ |S|=R+2}}
  C_S e^{-im\log N_S},
  \qquad
  N_S:=\prod_{n\in S}n.
\]
For a pure branch one has
\[
  C_S
  =
  \left(\prod_{n\in S}a_n\right)
  (\det V_S)^2
  \left(\prod_{n\in S}m_{\can}(x_n)\right)
  [x_n:n\in S]\left(\frac1{m_{\can}}\right),
\]
where \(V_S\) is the Vandermonde matrix of the nodes \(x_n\).  Thus the analytic
O3 content of the exterior determinant is the divided difference
\[
  [x_n:n\in S]\left(\frac1{m_{\can}}\right).
\]

In the favorable branch-normalized elliptic model
\[
  m_{\can,+}(x)=\kappa x^2(1+\varepsilon(x)),
\]
one compares \(1/m_{\can,+}\) to \((\kappa x^2)^{-1}\).  Since
\[
  \left(x^{-2}\right)^{(R+1)}
  =
  (-1)^{R+1}(R+2)!x^{-R-3},
\]
the model divided differences have fixed phase and non-small mass on
\(x>0\).  Therefore the analytic multiplier obstruction reduces to the finite
audit that
\[
  \left(
  \frac1{m_{\can,+}}
  -
  \frac1{\kappa x^2}
  \right)^{(R+1)}
\]
is perturbative relative to
\[
  \left(\frac1{\kappa x^2}\right)^{(R+1)}
\]
on the chosen branch-routed subpacket.
\end{remark}

\begin{remark}[Collision and branch-survival guardrail for determinant methods]
\label{rem:collision-branch-survival-determinant-methods}
A pure-branch determinant lower bound is not, by itself, a scalar finite/O2O3
theorem for the full canonical matrix.  The determinant expansion has product
frequencies
\[
  \log N_S=\sum_{n\in S}\log n,
\]
with \(|S|=R+2\), and therefore effective length \(e^{O(RQ)}\).  Exact and
near multiplicative collisions can occur among the products \(N_S\).  Moreover,
the full determinant also contains pure dual, mixed, background, and completion
exterior terms.

Thus any determinant route must prove both:
\[
  \boxed{\text{collision/near-collision noncancellation for the relevant
  exterior coefficients}}
\]
and
\[
  \boxed{\text{survival of the branch determinant inside the full scalar
  canonical determinant}.}
\]
Prime-labelled or otherwise collision-safe subpackets are useful only as
witnesses inside a sector-compatible component of the full determinant.  A
projected prime determinant alone certifies a projected map, not the scalar
canonical augmented matrix.
\end{remark}
```

---

## Edit 5 — add the Stack-weighted branch-count survival theorem target

**Location:** After Edit 4 above, or immediately after `rem:finite-o2o3-basis-and-no-go-register`.

**Insert:**

```latex
\begin{hypothesis}[Stack-weighted branch-count survival]
\label{hyp:stack-weighted-branch-count-survival}
Let
\[
  D_{\full}(m):=\det A_{\full}(m)
\]
be the determinant of the full scalar canonical augmented O2/O3 matrix.  Suppose
the exterior expansion admits a branch-count decomposition
\[
  D_{\full}(m)=\sum_{\beta}D_\beta(m),
\]
and let \(D_{\dom}\) be a branch-routed component for which sectorial
divided-difference mass and collision-safe exterior mass have been proved.

Under the active Stack-C2 measure
\[
  d\nu
  =
  F_1F_{\wall}^{\forb}N\,d\rho,
\]
assume
\[
  \boxed{
  \int |D_{\full}|^2\,d\nu
  \ge
  Q^{-A}
  \int |D_{\dom}|^2\,d\nu.
  }
\]
This hypothesis is the scalar-compatibility condition for determinant-based
finite/O2O3.  Without it, \(D_{\dom}\) is only a projected branch witness and
does not prove nondegeneracy of the full canonical augmented matrix.
\end{hypothesis}

\begin{remark}[Pure branch is extremal but the Stack weight may bridge it]
\label{rem:pure-branch-extremal-stack-weight}
In the branch-count expansion, the pure plus exterior determinant has extremal
signed branch count, and the pure dual exterior determinant has the opposite
extremal signed branch count.  Under an unweighted smooth measure, this suggests
orthogonality between pure and mixed branch-count components.  However the
Stack-C2 measure includes
\[
  F_1F_{\wall}^{\forb}N,
\]
which may itself carry plus/dual frequencies.  Therefore branch-count survival
must be proved under the actual Stack weight, not merely under raw smoothing.
Equivalently, the Stack weight must not bridge the one-branch frequency gap in
a way that cancels the pure branch exterior mass.
\end{remark}
```

## Edit 6 — add the finite/O2O3 Riesz graph simplification replacing stale cofactor worries

**Location:** In the finite/O2O3 subsection, after:

```latex
\begin{lemma}[Riesz-normalized determinant controls \(\mathcal H_{23}\)]
\label{lem:riesz-normalized-determinant-controls-H23}
...
\end{lemma}
```

**Insert:**

```latex
\begin{remark}[Cofactor denominator as a Riesz graph quantity]
\label{rem:cofactor-denominator-riesz-graph}
The cofactor denominator in
\[
  \mathcal H_{23}(A)
  =
  \frac{\det(A^*A)}{e_{n-1}(A^*A)}
\]
should not be treated as an independent unmatched-cofactor mystery once the
augmented space is put in a Riesz graph basis.  In the idealized model, write
\[
  f=\frac1{m_{\can}},
  \qquad
  f=P_Rf+r,
  \qquad
  r\perp \mathbf C_R[x],
\]
and assume the polynomial block is orthonormal.  In the basis
\[
  \{f,p_0,\ldots,p_R\},
\]
one has
\[
  \det G=\|r\|^2
\]
and
\[
  e_{n-1}(G)
  =
  1+\|P_Rf\|^2+(R+1)\|r\|^2.
\]
Hence
\[
  \mathcal H_{23}
  =
  \frac{\operatorname{dist}(1/m_{\can},\mathbf C_R[x])^2}
       {1+\|P_R(1/m_{\can})\|^2
        +(R+1)\operatorname{dist}(1/m_{\can},\mathbf C_R[x])^2}
\]
up to the \(R^C\)-distortion of the actual Riesz basis.  Thus the denominator
problem is controlled by:
\[
  \|1/m_{\can}\|_{\mathrm{Riesz}}\le R^C,
  \qquad
  \operatorname{dist}(1/m_{\can},\mathbf C_R[x])\ge Q^{-A}.
\]
The remaining difficulty is not a generic cofactor taxonomy, but the survival
of this graph-good branch inside the full canonical matrix.
\end{remark}
```

```latex
\begin{remark}[Deprecated routes after the post-closure cleanup]

The following routes are demoted in the current architecture.

\begin{enumerate}
\item The historical pointwise O1/O2/O3 route remains a valid alternate
conditional route, but it is no longer the active Gate--1 definition.  The
active Gate--1 object is scalar local \(L^2\)-energy of
\[
q''_{\can}=B_2+P_2+D_2+E_2.
\]

\item The derivative wall
\[
\{\Gamma_G,D\Gamma_G,D^2\Gamma_G,\Gamma_{G,2}\}
\]
is not a D4 wall.  D4 supplies only
\[
H(\Gamma_G,\Gamma_{G,2}).
\]
Derivatives enter only through finite-atlas Markov/Riesz estimates.

\item Pure branch determinant lower bounds do not prove scalar finite/O2O3
unless the branch component survives inside the full determinant.  Projected
branch determinants are source-family certificates, not scalar canonical
certificates.

\item A total row-energy or Bessel upper is not a forbidden identity.  Finite
D4 requires a concrete \(\Lambda\) with small global action and nonzero local
D4 action.  The current candidate is the transition--singular discarded carrier
projection.

\item Re-expanding \(E_2/R_{\lab}\) is not part of the local \(B_Y\)-audit unless
one exhibits atom-local live-fiber fitting or post-extraction feedback.
Fixed, coherent, and residual \(E_2/R_{\lab}\) terms route downstream.
\end{enumerate}
\end{remark}
```

## Edit 11 — optional: add a short “\(\alpha\)-twist caution” to the O2/O3 section

**Location:** After `rem:finite-o2o3-basis-and-no-go-register`.

**Insert:**

```latex
\begin{remark}[\(\alpha\)-twist caution]
\label{rem:alpha-twist-caution-o2o3}
A scalar \(\alpha\)-dependent phase multiplying an entire branch can help
separate branch-count terms in an averaged determinant expansion, but it does
not change the complex span of that branch:
\[
e^{i\Theta(\alpha)}V_-=V_-.
\]
Therefore such a scalar twist does not improve local graph/O2O3
nondegeneracy.  To improve the local augmented frame, \(\alpha\) must change
the branch subspace nontrivially, for example through a nonconstant tilt in
the frequency variable.  Otherwise \(\alpha\)-averaging is only a determinant
survival tool, not a pointwise O2/O3 cure.
\end{remark}
```

**Insert:**

```latex
\begin{remark}[Source-completion ledger is the order-four price]
\label{rem:source-completion-order-four-price}
The wall-variable completion theorem reduces the order-four analytic burden to
the source variable
\[
p=q''_{\can}.
\]
The exact price is
\[
\|p-p_X\|_{H^2_{\sc}(I^*)}\le Q^{-B},
\]
with affine reconstruction for \(q\).  Thus every component of
\[
p=B_2+p_{\AFE}+p_{\dual}+p_{\tail}+p_{\smooth}+p_{\err}
\]
must be exact, included in \(\mathcal P_I\), or \(H^2_{\sc}\)-small.  In
particular, if \(B_2\) is approximated rather than included exactly, then
\[
B_2,\quad DB_2,\quad D^2B_2
\]
must all be controlled at the required scale.
\end{remark}
```

## Edit 1 — replace “two-chart holonomy is the candidate” with “discarded singular projection is the candidate”

**Location:** In the downstream singular-discard / forbidden-channel subsection, find the definition or theorem that currently centers:

```latex
\Lambda_{\tr,\sing}^{(R)}
=
P_{\tr}T_{\sing\to\tr}P_{\sing}T_{\tr\to\sing}P_{\tr}
-
P_{\tr}.
```

**Action:** Keep it only as a derived diagnostic if desired, but insert the following **before** that definition, or replace the primary candidate definition with this.

```latex
\begin{definition}[Positive singular-discard forbidden operator]
\label{def:positive-singular-discard-forbidden-operator}
Let
\[
  T:=T_{\tr\to\sing}
\]
be the transition-to-singular realization map, and let
\[
  P_{\tr}:\mathcal J_{\tr}\to W_{\tr},
  \qquad
  P_{\sing}:\mathcal J_{\sing}\to R_{\sing}
\]
be the transition live-carrier projection and the singular retained/coherent
projection.  Define the local positive forbidden operator
\[
  \boxed{
  \Lambda_{\tr,\sing,p}
  :=
  (I-P_{\sing,p})T_{\tr\to\sing,p}P_{\tr,p}.
  }
\]
Its codomain is the discarded singular component
\[
  C_{\sing,p}:=(I-P_{\sing,p})\mathcal J_{\sing,p}.
\]
This operator is preferable to a full atlas loop when the proof only needs a
forbidden global upper and a local D4 lower: actual global rows should have
small discarded singular component, while formal D4 live carriers may have
nonzero discarded singular component.
\end{definition}

\begin{remark}[Relation with the two-chart projection loop]
\label{rem:singular-discard-vs-two-chart-loop}
The two-chart projected loop
\[
  \widehat\Lambda_{\tr,\sing}^{(R)}
  =
  P_{\tr}T_{\sing\to\tr}P_{\sing}T_{\tr\to\sing}P_{\tr}-P_{\tr}
\]
satisfies, on \(W_{\tr}\),
\[
  \widehat\Lambda_{\tr,\sing}^{(R)}
  =
  -P_{\tr}T_{\sing\to\tr}(I-P_{\sing})T_{\tr\to\sing}.
\]
Thus the loop is only the discarded singular projection transported back to the
transition carrier plane.  For a Type-I positive atomic forbidden channel it is
cleaner to use
\[
  (I-P_{\sing})T_{\tr\to\sing}P_{\tr}
\]
directly.  No return map is needed unless the target theorem specifically
requires a transition-plane holonomy.
\end{remark}
```

## Edit 2 — replace the finite D4 wall factor with the discarded-singular wall factor

**Location:** Wherever the current forbidden wall factor is defined using a generic \(\Lambda\), a two-chart loop, or a returned Gram matrix.

**Replace the definition with:**

```latex
\begin{definition}[Singular-discard D4 wall factor]
\label{def:singular-discard-D4-wall-factor}
Let
\[
  \Gamma=(\Gamma_G,\Gamma_{G,2})^T.
\]
The singular-discard D4 wall factor is
\[
  \boxed{
  E_{\wall}^{\forb}(m)
  :=
  \frac{
  |H(m)|^2
  \left\|
  (I-P_{\sing})T_{\tr\to\sing}
  E_{\car}^{\tr}L_{\clean}^{\tr}\Gamma(m)
  \right\|^2
  }{
  \mathcal N_{\D4}(m)
  }.
  }
\]
Equivalently, define the finite D4 transversality matrix
\[
  K_{\D4}
  :=
  (I-P_{\sing})T_{\tr\to\sing}
  E_{\car}^{\tr}L_{\clean}^{\tr}.
\]
Then \(K_{\D4}=0\) means this singular-discard channel is D4-blind.  If
\(\operatorname{rank}K_{\D4}=1\), the channel sees one forbidden linear
combination of \(\Gamma_G,\Gamma_{G,2}\).  If
\(\operatorname{rank}K_{\D4}=2\), it sees the full rank-two D4 wall.
\end{definition}
```
